It would anfwer little purpofe to fpecify the particular foe* ties ot drefs proper for all infants. Thefe will always vary in * This is by no means inveighing againft a thing that does not happen. In many parts of Britain at this day, a roller eight or ten feet in length, is applied tichtiv round the child's bodv as foon as it is born. * } OF CHILDREN*. 39 different countries, according to cuftom and the humour of pa- t"n*$. The great rule to be obfcrved i?, That a child have rmmort clothes than are neccfjiary to keep it warm, and that they be quite eafy for its body. Stays are the very bane of infants.^ A volume would not fuffice to point out all the bad effects of this ridiculous piece of drefs both on children and adults. ,The madnefs in favc ur of Stays feems however, to be fomewhat abated ; and it is to be hoped tne world will, in time, become wife enough to know, that the human fhape does not folely depend upon whale-bone and bend leather.* I fhall only add with respect to the clothes of children, that they ou -lit to be kept thoroughly clean. Children pi rfpire more than adults ; and if their clothes be not frequently changed, they become very hurtful. Dirty clothes not only g.il and fret the ten :er Skins of infants, but Iikewife occafion ill Smells; and what is worfe, tend to produce vermin and cutaneous diSenSes. CbanlineSs is not only agreeable to the eye, but tends great- ly to preServe the health of children. It promotes the perfpira- tion, and, by that means, frees the body from fuperflu us hu- mours, which, if retained, could not fail to occafion diiefes. No mother or nurfecan have any excule for allowing a child to be diry. Poverty mny oblige here to give it coarfe clothes ; but if fhe does not keep them clean, it muft be her own fault. Of the Food of Children. Nature not only points out the food proper for an infant, but actually prepares it. Tnis however, is not fufficient to prevent fome who think themfelves wifer tnan Natur-, from attempting to bring up their children without her provifion. Nothing can fiVw the difpofition whiclv mankind have to depart from Nature more than their endeavoring to bring up children without the breaft. The mother's milk, or that of a healthy nur'e, is un- questionably the beft food for an infant. Neither art nor nature can afford a proper Substitute for it. Children may feem to thrive for a few months without the breaft ; but when teething, the fmail-pox, and other difeafes incident to childhood, come on, they generally perifh. A child, foon after the birth, fhews an inclination to Suck ; and there is no reafon why it fhould not be gratified. It is true, the mother's milk does not always come immediately after the birth; but this is the way to bring it: befides, the firft milk that the child can fqueeze out of the brerft anfwers :he purpofe of cleanfing, better than all the drugs in the apothecary's (hop, and at the fame time prevents inflammations of the breaft, fev- ers, and other difeafes incident to mothers. * Stays made of bend leather are worn by all the women of lower fiation in many parts of England. I am forry to underftand, that there are ftill mothers mad enough to lace their daugh- ters very tight in order to improve their ihape. As reafoning would be totally loft up- on fuch people, 1 (hall beg leave juft to alk them, Why there are ten deformed women for oie man r and Iikewife to recommend to their ntr-ufal a fhort more! precept, which for- bids OS tO DETCRM THE HVM.'.N BCDV, 4© QF CHILDREN. It is ftrange how people came to think that the firft thing given to a child fhould be drugs. This is beginning w;th medr, cine by times, and no wonder if thev generally end with it. It Some- times happens, indeed, that a child does not difcharge the meconium. fo foon as could be wifhed ; this has induced phyficians, in fuch cafes, to give fomething of an opening nature to cleanfe the firft paffages. Midwives have improved upon this hint, and never rail to give fyrups, oils, &c. whether they be necefTary or not. Cramming an infant with fuch indigeftible ftuff as foon as it is born, can hardlv fail to make it fick, and is more likely to occa- fion difcafes than to prevent them. Children are feldom long af- ter the birth without having a paffage both by ftool and urine ; though th -fe evacuatio ^s may be wanting for fome thne without any aa-.g-r- But if children muft have fomething before they be allowed the breaft, let it be a little thin water pap, to which mav be added an equal quantity of new milk; or rather water alone, with the addition of a little raw fugar. If this be given without any wines or fpiceries, it will neither heat the blood, load the Stomach, nor occafion gripes. Upon the firft fight of an infant, almoft every perfon is -Struck with the idea of its being weak, feeble, and wanting Sup- port. This naturally fuggefts the need of cordials. According- ly wines are univerfally mixed with the firft food of children. A Nothing can be more fallacious than this way of reafoning» ; or more hurtful to infants than the conduct founded upon it. Children require very little food for fome time- after thebirt ; and wht they receive fhould be thin, weak, light, and of a coding quality. A very fmall quantity of wine is fufficient to heat and inflame the blood of an infant; but every perfon converfant in thefe matters muft know, that moft of the difeafes of infants proceed from the heat of their humours. If the mother or nurfe has enough of milk, the child will need little or no food before the third or fourth month. It will hen be proper to give it, once or twice a day, a little of fome food that is eafy of digeftion, as water-pap, milk-pottage, weak broth with bread in it, and fuch like. This will eafe the mother, will accuftom the child ty degrees to take food, and will render the weaning both lefs difficult and lefs dangerous. All great and fudden tranfitions are to be avoided in nurfing. For this pur- pofe, tie food of children ought not only tobefimple, but to refemble, as nenrly as poffible, the properties of milk. Indeed - milk itfelf fhould make a principal part of their food, not only before they are weaned, but for fome time after. Next to milk, we would recommend good light bread. Bread may be given to a child as foon as it fhews an inclination to chew; and it mav at all times be allowed as much plain bread as it will eat. The very ch wing of bread will promote the cutt'ng of the teeth, and the difcharge of faliva, while by mixing with the nurle's I milk in the ftomach, it will afford an excllent nourifhment. Chil. dren difcov^ r an early inclination to chew whatever is put into their hands. Parents obferve the inclination, but generally mif- OF CHILDREN. 4t take the object. Inftead of giving; the child fomething which Tmy at once exerciie its gums and aftor S it nourifhmenr, they com- monly put into its hands a piece of hard metal, or impenetrable ceral. A cruft of bread is the beft gum-ftck. it not only an- fwers the purpofe better than any thing elfe, but has the addi- tional properties of nourifhirg the child and carrving the ftriiva down to the ftomach, which is too vVlu^ble aliqucr to be 1ft. Bread, befides being ufed dry, may be many ways pre* pared into frod for children. One of the beft methods is toboii it in water, afterwards pouring the water off, and mix5 g with the bread a proper quantity of n<--w milk unboiled. Milk is both more wholefome and ncurifhing this way than boiled, and is 1-fa apt to occafion coftivenefs. For a child further advanced, bread may be mixed in veal or chicken broth, made into puddings or the like. Bread is a prOper food for children at all times, pro- vide*4 it be plain, made of wholefome grain, and well f rmented ; but when enriched with fruits, fugars, or fuch things, it becomes very unwholefome. It is foon enough to allow child «-en animal food when they have got teeth to eat it. They Should never tafle it till afrer they are weaned, and even then they ought to ufe it fparingly. In- deed, when children live wholly on vegetable food, it is apt to xfour on their Stomachs; but, on the other hand, too much flefh heats the body, and occafions fevers and other inflimmatory dif- eafes. This plainly points out a due mixture of animal and veg- etable food as moft proper for children. Few things prove more hurtful to infants than the common method of fweetening their food. It entices.them to take m< re than they ought to do., which makes them grow fat and bloated. It is pretty cc rtain, if the food of children were quite plain, that they would never take more than enough. Their exercifes are entirely owing to nurfes. If a child be gorged with food at all hours, and enticed to take it, by making it tweet and agreeable to the palate, is it any wonder that fuch a child fhould in time be indu-ed to crave more food than it om>ht to have ? Children may be hurt by too little as well as too rmrh food. After a child is weaned, it ought to be fed four or five time* a day; but fhould never be accuftomed to eat in riv night; Dei'her fhould it have too much at a time. Children thrive beft with fmall quantities of food frequently given. This neither over- loads the ftomach nor hurts tne digeftion, and is certainly moft agreeable to nature. Writers on nurfing have inveighed with fuch vehemence againft giving children too much f <od, that manv parents, by en- deavouring to fhunthat error, have run into the oppofite extreme, and ruined the constitutions of their children. But the error of pinching children in their food is more hurtful than the other ex- treme. Nature has many ways ot relieving herfelf when over- charged ; but a child, who is pincheo whh hung-r, will never become a ftrong or healthy man. That errors are frequently ( + ) ft OF CHILDREN. committed on both fides, we are ready to acknowledge; but where one child is hurt by the quantity of its food, ten tuner from the quality. This is the principal evil, and claims oui Strictest attention. Many people imagine, that the food which they themfelves love, cannot be bad for their children : but this notion is very abfurd. In the more advanced periods of life we often acquire an inclination for food, which when children we could not en- dure. Befides, there are many things that by habit may agree very well with the Stomach of a grown perfon * which would be hurtful to a child; as high-feafoned, fait, and fmoke-dried pro- visions, &c. It would alfo be improper to feed children with fat meat, ftrong broths, rich foups, or the like. All .ftrong liquors are hurtful to children. Some parents teach their children to guzzle ale, and others fermented liquors, at every meal. Such a practice cannot fail to do mifchief. Tiefe children feldom efcape the violence of the fmall-p-.x, me?.fles9 hooping-cough, or fome inflammatory dif.-rder. Milk, waer, butter-milk, or whey, are the moft proper for children to drink. If they have any thing ftronger, it may be fine fmall beer, or a little wine mixed with water. The ftomarh of children can di- geft well enough without the affiftance of warm ftimulants; be- tides, being naturally hot, they are eafily hurt by every thiDg of a heating quality. Few things are more hurtful to children than unripe fruits. They weaken the powers of digeftion, and four and relax the ftomach, by which means it becomes a proper neft for infects. Children indeed fhew a great inclination for fruit, and I am apt to believe, that if good ripe fruit were allowed them in proper quantity^ it would have no bad effects. We never find a nat- ural inclination wrong, if properly regulated. Fruits are gener- ally of a coonngnature, and correct the heat and acrimony of the humours. This is what moft children require; only care fhould be taken left they exceed. Indeed the beft way to pre- vent children from going to excefs in the ufe of fruit, or eating that which is bad, is to allow them a proper quantity of what Is good.* Roots which contain a crude vifcid juice fhould be fparingly given to children. They fill the body with grofs humours, ancl tend to produce eruptive difeafes. This caution is peculiarly necefTary for the poor; glad to obtain at a fmall price what will fill the bellies of their children, they fluff them two or three times a day with crude vegetables. Children had better eat a fmaller quantity of food which yields a wholefome nourifhmen*, than be crammed with what their digeftive powers are unable properly to affimilate. Butter ought Iikewife to be fparingly given to children. It * Children are always fickly in the fruit feafon, which may be thus account«d>for i Two ihirdsof the fruit which comes to market in this country is really unripe, and chil- dren not being in a condition to judge for themfelves, eat whatever they can lay their hand* upon, which often proves little better than poifon to their tender bowel*. Servants, an* others who have the care of children, Ihould be ftticUy forbid to giver them any fruit \*ith- -^ u t the knowledge of. their parents, OF CHILDREN. 4$ both relaxes the ftomach, and produces grofs humours. Indeed, moft things that are fat or oily have tins effect. Butter when falted becomes ftill more hurtful. Inftead of butter, fo liberally given to children in moft parts of Britain, we would recommend honey. Honey is not only wholefome, but cooling, cleanfing, and tends to fweeten the humours. Children who eat honey are feldom troubled with worms: they are alfo lefs fubject to cuta- neous difeafes, as itch, C-abbed head, &c. Many perfons err in thinking that the diet of children ought to be altogether moift. When children live entirely upon flops, it relaxes their Solids, renders them weak, and difpnfes them to the rickets, fcrophula, and other glandular difotders. Relaxation is one of the moft general caufes of the difeafes of children. Ev- ery thing therefore which tends to unbrace their folids, ought to be carefully avoided. We would not be understood by thefe obfervations as con- fining children to any particular kind of food. Their diet may be frequently varied, provided always that fufficient regard be had to fimplicity. Of the Exercife of Children* Of all the caufes which confpire to render the life of man fliort and miferable, none have greater influence than the want of proper Exercife: Healthy parents, wholefome food, and proper clothing, will avail little, where exercife is neglected. Suf- ficient exercife will make up for feveral defects in nurfing; but nothing can fupply the want of it. It is abfojutely necefTary to the -health, the growth, and the Strength of children. The defire of exercife is coeval with life itfelf. Were this principle attended to, many difeafes might be prevented. _ But, while indolence and fedentary employments prevent two thirds of mankind from either taking fufficient exercife themfelves, or giv- ing it to their children, what have we to expect bu' difeafes and deformity among their offspring ? The rickets, fo destructive to children, never appeared in Britain until manufactures began to flourifh, and people, attracted by t'~e love of gain, left the coun- try to follow fedentary employments in great towrs. L is amongft thefe people that this difeafe chiefly prevails, and not only deforms but kills many of their offspring. The conduct of other young animals fhews the propriety of giving exercife to children. Every other animal makes ufe of its organs of motion as Soon as it can,'and many of them, even when under no neceffity of moving in queft of food, cannot be restrain- ed without force. This is evidently the cafe with the calf, the lamb, and moft other young animals. If thefe creatures were not permitted to frifk about and take exercife, they would foon die or become difeafed. The fame inclination appears very early in the human Specie;; bur as they are not able to t<ike exercife themfelves, it is the bufinefs of their parents and nurfes to alii it them. Children may be exercifed various ways. The beft meth- od, while they are light, is to Garry them about in the nurSe's to OF CHILDREN. arms.* This gives the nurfe an opportunity of talking to tne chtl» and of p Vmti g out every thing that mav pleale and dengnt its fhncv. Befi4es, ir is much fafer ttr-m fwingmg an infant m a machine, or leaving if to the care cf fuch as are- not tit to take c**v» of themfelves. Nothing c- o be m»re abfurd than to let one child to keep anot. er; this conduct has pr-vecUaial to many in- fants, and has rendered others miferaWe for life. When children begin to walk, the iafeft and beft method of leading them about is bv the tonds. The common way, of fwmging them in leading f rings fixed to their backs, has feveral bad confequences. It m?k?s them throw their bodies forward, and prefs with their whr.le weight upon the ftomach and breaft; by this means the breathing is obftructed, the breaft flattened, and the bowels comprefl>d ; which muft hurt the digeftion, and occafion confirmed ms of the lungs, and other difeafes. It is a common notion, that if clvldren are fet upon their feet too foon, the'r legs will became crooked. There is reafon to believe, that the very reverie of this is true. Every member ac- quires ftrength in proportion as it is exfrcifed. The limbs of children are weak indeed, but their hodies are proportionably light; and had thev fkill to direct themfelves, they would foon be able to fupport their own weight. Who ever heard of any other animal rh*t became crooked by ufing its legs too foon ? In- deed, if a child is not permitted to make any ufe of its legs till a considerable time after the birth, and be then fet upon them with its whole weight at once, there may be fome danger ; but this proceeds -ntirelv from fhe child's not having been accuftomed to ufe its legs from the beginning. Mothers <^f the poorer fort think they are great gainers by making their children fie or fit wMlethey themfelves work. In this thev greatly miftake. Bv neglecting to give their chil- dren exercfe, they are obliged to k^ep them a long time before they can do anv thing frr themfelves, and to fpeud more on median" than w uld trve paid for proper care. To take care of their rhildre-"", is the moft ufeful bufinefs in which even th-* poor ran be emploved ; but alas ! it is not alwavs in thc-^r power. P vertv often obliges them to neglect th-ir off- fnriig in order to procure the n^ceff^ries of life. When this is th's c^f3, it becomes the intrreft as well ts the duty of the public to affift them. Ten thour°.nd times more benefit would accrue to the ftnte, bv enaNhg the poor to bring up their own chil 4ren, than from all the hofpitals t that ever can be erected for that purpofc. * The nurfe ought to be careful to keen the child in a proper pofition ; as deformity is often the confluence of inattention to this circum ance Its fituation ought alfo to br frequently changed. I have known a child's leg bent all on one fide, by the nurfe carrvinz it conftantly on O'.e a'm. f If it were made the intereft of the poor to keep their children alive, we fhould lofe very few of them. A frnall premium given annually to each poor familv. for eveiv child they had alive at theyear'send, would fa»emoie lives of infants th*n if thewhole r-v-nueof the nation w-re expended on hofpitals for this purpofe. This would make the poor elveem fertility a bleffmg ; whereas manv of them think it the greateft curfe that can befal them and in place of wfhing their children to live, fo far does poveity get the better of rafual afYe&ion, ttrat they are often very happy when they die. OF CHILDREN. 45 Whoever confiders the ftrudure of the human body will foon be convinced of the neceffity of exercife f>r the health of chu re2' • The bod7r is coniP^'ed of an infinite number of tubes, whole fluids cannot be/pufhed on without the acti -n and preffure of the mufcles. But, if the fluids remain inactive, obstructions muft happen, and the humours will of courfe be vitiated, which cannot fail to occafion difeafes. Nature r.as furnifhed both the veffels wh;ch carry the blood and lymph with numerous valves, in order that the action of every mufcle mig»:t pufh forward their contents; but without action,' thin admirable contrivance can have no effect. This part of the animal eon' my proves to a de- monftration t^.e neceffity of exercife for the prefervaoon of health. Arguments to fhew the importance of exercife might be drawn from every part of the anmal economy; w!thout exer- cife, the circulation of the blood cannot oe properly carried on, nor the different fecretbns duly performed; without exercife, the fluids cannot be properly prepared, nor the f fids rendered ftrong or firm. The action of the heart, the motion of the lungs, and all the vital functbns are greatly affifted by exercife. But to point out the manner in which thefe efivcts are produced, would lead us further into the economy of the human body, than moft of thofe for whom this treatife is intended would be ;ble to follow. We Avail therefore only add, that, wh.-n exercife is neglected, none of the animal functions can be duly p rf;rmed ; and when that is the cafe, the whole conftitution muft go to wreck. A good conftitution ought certainly to be our firft obj ct in the management of children. It lays a foundation for their be- ing ufeful and happy in life: and whoever neglects it, not only fails in his duty to his offspring, but to f ciety. One very common error of parents, by which they hurt the conftitutions of their children, is the fending tiem to.) young to School. This is often done folely to prevent trouble. When the child is at fchool, he needs no keeper. Thus the fchool-maf- ter is made the nurfe; and the poor child is fixed to a feat Seven or eight hours a-day, which time ough.t to be fpent in exercife and diverfions. Sitting fo long cannot fail to produce the worft effects upon the body ; nor is the mind lefs injured. Early am- plication weakens the faculties, and often fixes in the mincl an averfion to books, which continues for life.* But fuppofe this were the way to make children Scholars, it certainly ought not to be done at the expence of their conftitu- tions. Our anceftors, who Seldom went to lchool very young, were not lefs learned than we. But we imagine the boy's edu- cation will be quite marred, unleSs he be carried to icho'ol in his nurfe's arms. No wonder if Such hot-bed plants Seldom become. either Scholars or men! Not only tne confinement of children in public Schools, but their numoer, often proves hurtful. Children are much injured * Jtis undoubtedly the duty of parents to inftruft their childien, at leaft till they ares of an age proper to take fome care of themfelves. This,would tend much to confi-m the ties of parental tenderneis and filial affection, of the want of which there are at orefent lo many deplorable inftances. Though fsw fathers have time to in(bu<S their cUiidre.-i, yet moft nwners have, uad furcly they cannot bs better employed.. 46 OF CHILDREN. by being kept in crouds within doors; their breathing not only renders the place unwholefome, but if any one of them har> pens to be difeafed, the reft catch the infection. A imgle child has been often known to communicate the bloody flux, tne noon- ing cough, the itch, or other difeafe, to almoft every individual in a numerous fchuol. But, if fafhion muft prevail, and infants are to be fent to fchool, we would recommend it to teachers, as they value the in- terests of feciety, not to confine them too long at a time, but al- low them to run about and piay at fuch active diverlions as may promote their growth, and Strengthen their conftiiutions. Were boys, inftead of being whipped tor ftealing an hour to run, ride, Swim, or tne like, encouraged to employ a proper, part of tneir time in thefe manly and uleful exerciles, it would have many ex- cellent eff cts. It wouid be a great fervice to boys, if, at a proper age, they were taugnt the military exercife. This would increale their ftrength, inlpire them with courage, and when their country call- ed for tneir afliftance, would enable them to act in her defence, without being obliged to uudergo a tedious and troublefome courfe of iultructions, at a time wneu they are lefs fit to learn new motions, geftures, &c* An effeminate education will infallibly fpoil the beft natural conftitution, and if ooys are brought up in a more delicate man- ner man even girls ought to be, they will never be men. a or is the common education of girls lefs hurtful to the' conftitution than that of boys. Mifs is fet down to her frame before fhe can put on her clothes; and is taught to believe, that to ex el at the needle is the only thing that can entitle her to gen- eral efteem. It is unneceffary here to infift upon the dangerous confluences of o :liging girls to fit too much. rl hey are pretty well known, and are too often felt at a certain time of life. But fuppofing this critical period to Le got o^er, greater dangers ftill await them when they come to be motners. Women who have been early accuftomed to a Sedentary life, generally run great hazard in childrbed ; whib t.iofe who have been ufei to romp about, and take Sufficient exercife, are leldom in any danger. One hardly meets with a girl who can at the fame time boaft of early performances by tue needle, and a good conftitu- tion. Clofe and early confinement generally occalions indigeft- ions, head-achs, pale complexions, pain of the ftomach, lols of appetite, coughs, confumptions of the lungs, and deformity of body. The laft of thefe indeed is not to be wondered at, confid- ering the awkward poftures in which girls fit at many kinds of needle-work, and the delicate flexible State of their bodies in the early periods of life. Would mothers, inftead of having their daughters instructed in many trifling accomphfhments, employ them in plain work and OF CHILDREN, '4* houfewifery, and allow them fufficient exercife in the open air, they WQuld both make them more healthy mothers, and more ufeful members of fociety. I am no enemy to genteel accom- plifhments, but would have them only confidered as Secondary, and always difregarded when they impair the health. Many people imagine it a great advantage for children to be early taught to earn their bread. This opinion is certainly right, provided they were fo employed as not to hurt their health or growth; but, when thefe Suffer, fociety, inftead of being ben- efited, is a real lofer by their labor. There are few employments, except fedentary ones, bv which children can earn a livelihood ; and if they be fet to thefe too foon, it ruins their constitutions. Thus, by gaining a few years from childhood, we generally lofe twice as many in the la'ter period of life, and even render the perfon lefs ufeful while he does live. In order to be Satisfied of the truth of this obervation, we need only look into tne great manufacturing towns, where we fhall find a puny degenerate race of people, weak and fickly all their lives, feldom exceeding the middle period of life; or if they do, being unfit for bufinefs, they become a burden to fociety. Thus arts and manufactures, though they may increafe the rich- es of a country, are by no means favourable to the health of its inhabitants. Good policy would therefore require, that fuch people as labor during life, fhould not be fet too early to work. Every perfon converfant in the breed of horfes, or other working animals, knows, that if they be fet to hard labor too foon, they never will turn out to advantage. This is equally true with rel- pect to the human fpecies. Weakly children fhould always be put apprentices to trades whicn require their being moftly cut of doors. There are neverthelefs various ways of employing young people, without hurting their health. The eafier parts of gard- ening, hufbandry, or any bufinefs carried on without doors, are moft propsr. Thefe are employments which moft young people are fond of, and fome parts of them may always be adapted to their age, tafte, and Strength.* Such parents, however, as are under the neceffity of em- ploying their children whhin doors, ought to allow them fuffi- cient time for active diverfions without. This would both en- courage Mem to do more work, and prevent their conftitutions from being hurt. Some imagine, that exercife within doors is fufficient; but they are greatiy miftaken. One hour fpent in running, or any other exercife without doors, is worth ten within. When chil- dren cannot go abroad, they may indeed be exercifei at home. The be t memod of doing this, is to make them run about in a iarge roGm, or dance. This laft kind of exercife, if not carried to excefo, is of excellent fervice to young people. It cheers the fpirks^ promotes perfpiration, ftrengthens the limbs, &c. I know an eminent phyiician who ufed to fay, that he made his children * I have been told that in China, where the police it the beft in the world, all the children are employed in the eafier part of gardening :tid buibanury ■ at w:eiin,:, gather- Wg (tones oft '.he land, and fuci like* ^8 OF CHILDREN. dance, inftead of giving them phyfic. It were well if more peo- ple followed his example. Ihe cold bath may be considered as an aid toexercitev -By it the body is braced and Strengthened, the circulation and fecre- tio:s ifr promoted, and, were it conducted with prudence many difeafes, as the rickets, fcrophula, &c. might thereby be prevent- ed. The anc e:ts, who took every method to render children hardy a »d robuft, were no Stranger's to the ufe of the cold batn; and, if we may credit rep rt, the practice of immerfiog children daily in cold water muft have been very common among cur ancestors. The greateft objection to the ufe of toe cold ba'h arifes fr~m the fuperftitious prejudices of nurfes. Thefe are often fo ftrong, that it is mipoffibie to bring them to make a proper ute of it. 1 have known fome of them who would not dry a child's ikin af- ter bat "ing it, left it fhould deftroy the effects of the water. Oth- ers will even put clothes dipt in water upon the child, and either put it to bed, or fuffer it to go ab>. ut in that condition. Some believe t. at the wnole virtue of the water depends upon its be- ing dedicated to a particular faint; while others place their con- fidence in a certain number of dips, as three, feven, nine, or the like , and trie world could not perfuade them, if thefe do not fuc- ceed, to try it a little longer. Thus, by the whims of nurfes, c .iidren lole the benefit of tne cold bath, and the hopes of the ' pnyfidan from that medicine are often fruftrated. We ought not, however, entirely to fet afide the coid b?th, becauf.1 fome nurfes make a wrong ufe of i*. Every child wren in health, fhould at leaft have i s extremities daily wafhed in cold water. This is a partial ufe of the cold bath, and is better than none. In win er this may fuffice; but, in the warm feafon, if a child be relaxed, or feem to have a tendency to the rickets or fcro- phula, its whole body ought to be frequently immerfed in cold "water. Care however muft be taken not to do this when the body is hot, or the ftomach full. The child fhould be dipped only once at a time, fhould be taken out immediately, and have its ikin well rubbed with a dry cloth. The bad EffeEls of unwholefame Air upon Children. Few things prove more destructive to children than c ^nfined or unwholefome air. This is one reafon why fo few of thofe in- fants who are put into hofpitals, or parifh workhouSts, live. TneSe places are generally crouded with old, ficklv, and infirm people ; by which means the air is rendered fo extremely perai. cious, that it becomes a poifon to infants. Want of wholefome air is Iikewife destructive to nrny of the children born in great towns. There the poorer Sort of inhab- itants hve in low, dirty, confined houfes, to which thefrefh air has hardly any accefs. Though grown people, who are tr rdv and robuft, may live m fuch fituations, yet they generally prove fatal to their offspring, tew of whom arrive at maturity ind thofe who do are weak and deformed. As fuch people are not in a condition to carry tjieir children abrg^d mo tbe open air w OF CHILDREN, 4$ muft lay our account with lofing the greater part of them. But the rich have not this excufe. It is their bufinefs to fee that their children be daily carri-d abroad, a^d that hey be kept in t .e open air for a fufficient time. This will always fucce«.d better if the mother goes along with them. Servants ire often negligent in th -fe matters, and allow a child to fit or he on the damp ground, inftead of leading or carrying it about. 1 he mother Lrely needs air as well as her children; and how can fhe be better employed than in attending them ? A very bad cuftom prevails, of making children fleep in fmall apartments, or crowding two or three beds in one chamber. In iead of this, the nurfery cugM :lways to be the largeft and beft aired room in the houfe. When children are confined in final! apartments, the air not only becomes ucwnohlome, but the heat rehx-s their Solids, renders them delicate, and difpofes them to colds and many other diforders. No- is the cuftom of wrap- pingthem up too clofe in cradles lefs pernidi us. One would think that nurfes were afraid k-ft childr-m fhould Suffer by breath- ing free air, as many of them actually c var the child's face while afiV-p, and others wr p a covering over the wh le cradle, by which mean? the child is forced t ■ or athe he fame air over and over all tre time it fleeps. Cradles indeed are on many accounts hurtful to children, and it would be better if the ufe of them were totally laid afide.* A child is generally laid to fleep with all its clo'hes on; and if a number of others are heaped above them, it muft be over- heated ; by which means it cannot fail to catc-i cold -m being tak- en out of the cradie, and expofed to the open air wi;h only its ufual clothing, which is too frequently the cafe. Children who are kepf within doors all day, and fleep all night in warm clofe apartments, ma}?-, with great prpriety, be compared to plants, nnrfed in a hot-bcufe, bftead of the open air. Though fuch plants m»y by this means" b= kept alive for Some time, they will never arrive at that degree of Strength, vig- our, and magnitude, which they w.«ul: h:.ve a quired in the open a;r, nor would they be able to bear it afterwards, Should they be expofed to it. Children brought up in the country, who have been accuf- tomed to the open air, fhould not be too early lent to great towns, where it is confined and unwholefome. This is frequently done with a view to forward their education, but proves very hurtful to their health. All fchools and feminaries of learning ought, if poffible, to be fo Situated as to have frefh, dry, wholefome air, and fhould never be too much crowded. * It is amazing how children efcape fuffocation, confiriering the manner in which they are often rolled up in flannels, &c. I lately attended an infant, whom 1 found muf- fled up overhead and fts in many folds of flannel, though it was in the middle of June. 1 begged fora little free air to the poor babe ; but though ibis indulgence wa» granted dur- ing my ftay, 1 found it always on my return in the fame fit nation. Death, as might be t*xpe&ed, foon freed the infant from all its miferiet: but it was not in my power to free the minds of its parents from thofe prejudices v.hich proved fatal to the child. I was very latelv called to fee an infant which wjs I?id to be expiring in convul- fion fits. 1 defired the mother to ftrip the child, and wrap it in a loofe coveur.g. It had no more convw'-fion fit?. 50 OF CHILDREN. Without entering into a detail of the particular advantages of wholefome air to children, or of the bad confequences whrth ■, Srocied from the want of it, I Shall only obferve, that of fevera! - thoufands of children which have been under my care, I do not remember one inftance of a fingle child who continued healthy T in a clofe confined ftation; but have often known the molt ob- stinate difeafes cured by removing them from fuch a fituation to an open free air. Cf Nurfes. It is not here intended to lay down rules for the choice of ; nurfes. This would be* wafting time. Common fenfe will di- . rect every one to chufe a woman who is healthy, and has plenty of milk.* If fhe be at the fame time cleanly, careful, and good- , natured, fhe can hardly fail to make a pr per nurfe.f After all, however, the only certain proof of a good nurfe, is a healthy.^ child upon her breaft. But, as the mifcondudt f nu-fes often * proves fatal to children, it will be of importance to point out a few of their moft baneful errors, iu order to roufe the atten- tion of parents, and to make N them look more ftr ctly into the conduct of t&ple-'to whom rtieiysommit the care of their infant offspring. \ Though it admits of fame, exceptions, vet we mav. &y it down asi a general rule, That ever/woman who nurfes for hire fhould be carefully looked after, otherivifffhe/will not do her duty. For this rea- fon parents otfght always to-mave their children nurfed under their own eye, il" poffible; and where this cannot be done, they Should be extremely circumfpect in the choice of thofe perfons to whom they intruft them. It is folly to imagine that any woman, who abandons her own child to fuckle another for the fake of gain, fhould feel all the affections of a parent towards her nurf- ling ; yet fo neceffary aie the affections in a nurfe, that, but for them, the human race would foon be extinct. One of the moft common faults of thofe who nurfe for hire, is dofing children with ftupefactives, or fuch things as lull them afleep. An indolent nurfe, who does not give a child fuffi- cient exercife in the open air to make it ileep, and does not chufe to be disturbed by it in the night, will feldom fail to procure for it a dofe of laudanum, diacodium, faffrcn, or what anfwers the fame purpofe, a dofe of fpirits, or other Strong liquors. Thefe, though they be certain poifon to infants, are every day adminif- tered by many who bear the character of very good nurfes.J * I have often known people fo impofed upon, as to give an infant to a nurfe to be fuckled who had not one drop of milk in her breaft. f Next of importance to a healthy, cleanly, and good natured nurfe, is her d4et. On had changed their diet from entirely vegetable, to their takinV in a"„„.i,-. / •1 food. Nay, I have known inftanee/of children becom nf difordlred froVl »!"fr?J makiig a fingle meal of an umifaall, large portion of animalfoodI »' ""* U * i If a mother, on vifiting her child at nurfe, finds it always aileeD I would •»£';£ i,« t« remove it immediately j ocherwifc it will foon fleep its laft. C P> l %vou,d advifeher OF CHILDREN. 5x A nurfe who has njt mik enough is apt to imagine that this defect may be fupplied by giving the child wines, cordial waters, or other ftrong liquors. This is an egregious mistake. The only tring that nas any chance to fupply the place of the nurfe'smilk, muft be fome what nearly of tne Same quality, as cow's milk, afs's milk, or beef tea, wifh a iittie bread. It never c.n be done by the help of ftrong liquors. Thefe, inftead of nourifhing an infant, never fail to produce the contrary effect. Children are often hurt by nurfes Suffering them to cry long and vehemently. This itrams their tender bodies, and frequently occafions ruptures, iafidrnmationsof ihi throat, lungs,< &c. A child never continues to cry long without fome cauie, which might always be diicovered by proper attention ; and the nurfe who can hear an infant cry till it has almoft Spent itfelf, without endeavoring to pleaie it, muft be crud indeed, and is unworthy to be intruited witn the care of an human creature. Nurfes who deal much in medicine are always to be fufpect- ed. They truft to i', and neglect their duty. I never knew a good nurfe who had ner Godfrey's cordial, Daffy's elixirs, Dal- by's carminative, &e. at hand, ouch generally imagine, that a dole of medicine will make up for all detects in food, air, exercife, and cleaniinels. By errors of this kind, I will venture to fay, that one half the children who die aunuaUy in London lofe their fives. Allowing children to continue long wet, is another very per- nicious cuftom of indolent nuries. This is not only difagreeable, but galls and frets the infant, and., by relaxing the foiids, occa- fions fcrophulas, rickets, and other dileaies- A dirty nurfe is always to be fufpecTed. Nature often attempts to free the bodies of children from bad humours, by thro wing them upon the ikin: by this means fevers and other difcaSes are prevented. Nurfes are apt to mif- take fuch critical eruptions for an itcn, or Some other infectious diforder. Accordingly they take every method to drive them in. In this way many children lolie their lives ; and no wonder, as Nature is oppofed in the very method She takes to relieve them. It ought, to be a ruie, which every nurfe fhould obferve, never to ftop any eruption without proper advice, or beiny well affur- ed tnat it is not of a critical nature. At any rate, it is never to be done without previous evacuations. Loofe itools is another method by which Nature often pre- vents or carries off the difeafes of infants, if thefe proceed too far, no doubt they ought to be checked; but this is never to be done without the greateft caution. Nurfes, upon the lirft ap- pearance of loofe itools, frequently fly to the ufe of aftringents, or fuch things as bin i the body. Hence inflammatory fevers, and other fatal difeafes, are occasioned. A dole of rheubarb, a gentle vomit, or fome other evacuations, fhould always precede the ufe of aftringent medicines. One of the greateft faults of nurfes is, concealing the'dii- eafes of children from tfuir pa.ren,t,§, This they are extremely 51 THE LABORIOUS, THE SEDENTARY, ready to do, efpedally when the difeafe is the effect of their own negligence. Many inftances might be given of perfons who have been rendered lame for life by a fall from thenurfe's arms, which fhe, through fear, concealed tUl the misfortune was paft cure. Every parent who intrufts a nurfe with the care of a child, ought to give her the itricteft charge not to conceal ihe moft trifling diforder or misfortune that may befal it. We can fee no reafon why a nurfe, who conceals any mif- forrune which, happens to a child under her care, till it lof'es its life or limos, fhould not be punifhed. A few examples of this would f?ve the liv.es of many infants; but as there is little reafon to expect tha' ,it ever will be the cafe, we would earnestly recom- mend it to all parents to look carefully after their children, and not to truft fo valuable a treafure entirely in the hands of a hireling. No perfon ought to imagine thefe things unworthy of his attention. Onte proper management of children depend not only their health and uf-fuln fs in life, but Iikewife the fafety and prosperity of the fta'e to which they belong. Effeminacy ev r will pr. ve the ruin of any fhte where it prevails; and when its foundations are laid in infancy,, it can never afterwards be wholly eradicated. Parents who love thdr offspring, and wifh well to their country, ought therefore, in the management of their children, to avoid every thing that may have a tendency to make them weak or effeminate, and to take every method in their power to render their constitutions ftrong and hardy. " By arts like thefe " Laronia nurs'd at' old her hardy fons $ •* And Rome's uaconquer'd legions urg'd their way, " Unhurt, through every toil in every clime." Ahmstho*'6. CHAP. II. < OF THE LABORIOUS, THE SEDENTART, AND THE STUDIOUS. A HAT men are expofed to particular difeafes from the occupations which they follow, is a fact well kncwro.; but to remedy this evil is a matter of fome difficulty. Moft people are under the neceffity of following thofe employments to whicn the? have been bred, whether they be favorable to heal:h or not. For this reafon, inftead of inveighing, in a general way, as fome au- thors have done, againft thofe occupations which are hurtful w health, we Shall endeavor to point out the circumstances in each ot them from which the danger chiefly arifes, and to nropofe the molt rational methodst>f preventing it. ^t^cC^y^S,/?Uni2rS' forSs1rs,?ghfs"makers,andfeveral other artiits, are hurt by the unwnolefome air which they are obliged io oreathe. 1 his air is not only loaded with the noxious SS tions anfing from metals and minerals, but is fo chirirH with Phlogiston as to be rendered unfit for e^^t£ J™&t AND THE mfDlOUS. §* ficiently, and. anfwerbg the other important purpofes of ref- piration. Hence proceed afthmas, coughs, and consumptions of the lung?, fo incident to perfons who follow thefe employment?. To prevent fuch confequences, as far as poffible, the places where thefe occupations are carried on, ought to be constructed in fuch a manner as to difcharge the fmoke and other exhala- tions, and admit a free current of frefh air. Such artifts cught never to continue too long at work ; and when they give ov^r, they fhould fufler themfelves to cool gradually, and put on their clothes before they go into the open air. Tney ought never to drink large quantities of cold, weak, cr watery liquors, while their bodies are hot, nor to indulge in raw fruits, fallads, or any thing that is cold on the ftomach.* Miner?, and all who work under ground, are Iikewife hurt by unwholefome air. r! he air, by its Stagnation in deep-mines, not only lofes its proper fpring and other qualities necefTary for refpiration, but is often loaded with fuch noxious exhalations as to became a moft deadly poifon. The two kinds of air which prove moft destructive to mi- ners, are what they call the fire damp, and the choke damp. In both cafes the air becomes a poifonby its being loaded with p1 lo- gifton. The danger from the former may be obviated by mak- ing it explode before it accumulates in too great quantities; and the latter may be generally carried off by promoting a free circu- lation of air in the mine. Micers, are net only hurt by unwholefome air, but Iikewife by fhe particles of metal which adhere to their Skin, clothes, &c. Thefe are abforbed, or taken up into the body, and occafion pal- fi^s, vertigoes, and other nervous affections, which often prove fatal. F: llopius obTerves, that thofe who work in mines of mer- cury feldom live above three or four years. Lead, and feveral other metals, are Iikewife very pernicious to the health. Meiers ought never to go to work fading, n r to continue too long at work. Their food ought to be nourifhing, and their liquor generous: nothing more certainly hurts them than living too low. They fhculd by all means avoid coffcvenefs. This m?v either be done by chewirg a little rheubarb, or taking a fuf- ficient quantity of fallad oil. Oil not only opens the body, but fheathes avd defends the inteftines from the ill effects of the met- als. Ail who w~rk in mines or metals ought to wafh carefully, and to change their clothes as foon as they give over working. Nothing would tend more to preferve the health of fuch people^ than - Strict, and ?Imoft religious regard to cleanlinefs. _ Plumbers, painters, gilders, fmelters, makers of white lead, and ma^y o'hers who Work in metals, are liable to the fame dif- eafes as miners; and ought to cbferve the fame directions for avoiding them. Tallow-chandlers, boilers of oil, and all who work in putrid animal fubftances, are Iikewife liable to fufter from theunwheie- • When perfons. heated with labor h»ve drank cold water, they ought to continue it work for fome time after. $4 THE LABORIOUS, THE SEDENTARY, fome fmells or effluvia of thefe bodies. They ought to pajtjfc- fame regard to cleanlinefs as miners; and when they are affect- ed with naufea, ficknefs, or indigeftion, we would advife them to take a vomit or gentle purge. Such fubftanres ought alwavs to be manufactured as foon as poffible. When long kept, they not only become unwholefome to thofe who manufacture them, but Iikewife to people who live in the neighborhood. It would greatly exceed the limits of this part of our fubject, to fpedfy the difeaies peculiar to perfons of every occupation; we Shall therefore confider mankind under the general claffes of the Laborious, Sedentary, and Studious. The Laborious. Though thofe who follow laborious employments are in general the moft healthy of raavknd, yet the nature of their oc- cupations, and the places whrre they are ~arried on, expofe them more particubrly to fome difeafes. Hufbandmen, for example, are expofed to all the vicifiitudes of the weather, which, in this country, are often very great and fudden, and occafion colds, coughs, quinfies, rheumatifms, fev r?, and other arute diforders. They are Iikewife fored to work hard, and often to carry burdens above their Strength, which, by overftraining the veffel?, occafion afthmas, ruptures, pleurifies, &c. Thofe who labor wit: out doors are often afflicted with in- termitting fevers or agues, occasioned by the frequent viciffitudes of heat and cold, poor living, bad water, fitting or lying on the damp ground, evening dews, night air, &c. to which they are frequently expofed. Such as bear heavy burdens, as porters, laborers, &c. are obliged to draw in the air with much greater force, and ilfo to keep their lungs diftended with more violence than necefTary for common refpiration: by this means the tender veffels of the lungs are overstretched, and often burft, infomuch that a fpitting of blood or fever enfues. Hippocrates mentions an inftance to this purpofe, of a man, who, upon a wager, carried an afs, but •was foon after feized with a fever, a vomiting of blood and a rupture. Carrying heavy burdens is generally the effect of mere lazi- nefs, which prompts people to do at once what fhould be done at twice. Sometimes it proceeds from vanity or emulation. Hence it is, that the Strongest men are moft commonly hurt by heavy burdens, hard labor, or feats of activity. It is rare to find one who boafts of ftrength without a rupture, a fpitting of blood, or fome other difeafe, which he reaps as the fruit of his folly. One, would imagine the daily instances we have of the fatal effects of carrying great weights, running, wreftling, and the like, would be fufficient to prevent fuch practices. There are indeed fome employments which neceffarily re- - quire a great exertion of ftrength, as porters, blackfmiths, car- penters, &c. None ought to follow thefe but men of ftrong body ; and they fhould never exert their ftrength to the utmcft. AND THE STUDIOUS. 55 nor work too long. When the mufcles are violently Strained* frequent reft is necefTary, in order that they may recover their tone; without this, the Strength and constitution will foon be worn out, and a premature old age be induced. The eryGpeks, or St. Anthony's fire, is a difeafe very inci- dent to t e laborious. _ It is occafioned by whatever gives a fud- den check to the perfpiration, as drinking cold water when the body is warm, wet feet, keeping on wet clothes, fitting or lying on the damp ground, &c. It is impoffible for thofe who labor without doors always to guard againft thefe inconveniences; but it is known from experience, that their ill confequences might often be prevented by proper care. The iliac paffion, the cholic, and other complaints of the bowels, are often occafioned by the Same caufes as theeryfipelas; but they may Iikewife proceed from flatulent and indigestible food. Laborers generally eat unfermented bread, made of peas, beans, rye, and other windy ingmlients. They alfo devour great quantities of unripe fruits, baked, ftewed, or raw, with various kinds of roots and herbs, upon which they often drip.k. four milk, ftale fmall beer, or the like. Such a mixture cannot fail to fill the bowels with wind, and occafion difeafes in thofe parts. Inflammation, whitloes, and other difeafes of the extremities-, are Iikewife common among thofe who labor without doors- Thefe difeafes are often attributed to venom, or fome kind of poifon; but they generally proceed either from fudden heat after cold, or the contrary. When laborers, milk-maids, &c. come from the field, cold or wet, they run to the fire, and often plunge their hands in warm water, by which means the blood and other humours in thofe parts are fuddenly expanded, and, the veilels not yielding fo quickly, a Strangulation happens, and an inflam- mation or a mortification enfues. When fuch perfons come home cold, they ought to keep at a distance from the fire for fome time, to wafh their hands in c dd » water, and rub them well with a dry cloth. It fometimes hap- pens, that people are fo benumbed with cold, as to be quite de- prived of the ufe of their limbs. In this cafe the only remedy is to rub the parts affected with fnow, or where it cannot be had, with cold-water. If they be held near the fire, or plunged into warm water, a mortification will generally enfue. Laborers in the hot feafon are apt to lie down and fleep in the fun. This pra&ice is fo dangerous, that they often awake in a burning fever. Thefe ardent fevers, which Drove fo fatal about the end of the fummer and the beginning of autumn, are frequently occafioned by this means. When laborers leave oft work, wMch they ought always to do during the heat of the day, they fhould go home, or at leaft get under tome cover where they may repofe themfelves in fafety. m Many people follow their employments in the fields from morning till night, without eating any thing. Tnis cannot fail to hurt their health. However homely their tare be, they ought to have it at recular times; and the harder they work, the more 55 THF LABORIOUS, THE SEDENTARY, frequertly they Should e?». If the humours be not frequently replenished with freSh nourishment, they foon become putnd, and produce fevers of the very w rP kino. JVJany peafants r.re extremely crelefs with refpect to what' they eat r'r drink, and often, t rcugh mere i dolence, ufe un- wholefome food, when they mieht, for the fane expence, have that which is wholefome. In fome parts < f Britain, the peafants are *oo carelefs evan to take the trouble of dreffing their own victuals. Such people would live upon one meal a-day, inindo* lence, rather than labor, though it were to procure them the greateft affluence. Fevers of a very bad kind are often occafioned among la- borers by poor living. When the body is not fufficiently nour- ifhed, the humours become vitiated, and the fblids weak ; from whence the moft fatal confequences enfue. Poor living is Iikewife productive of many of f ofe cutaneous difeafes fo frequent among the lower chfs of people. It is remarkable that cattW, when pinched in their food, are generally affected with difeafes of the ikin, which feldom fail to difappear when they are put up- on a good pafture. This Shews how much a good ftate of the humours depends upon a fufficient quantity of proper nour- ishment. Poverty not only occrfions, but aggravates many of the dif- eafes of the laborious. Few of them have much foresight; ano\ if they had, it is feldom in their power to fave any thing. They are glad to make a Shift to live from day to day; and when any difeafe overtakes them, they are miferable indeed. Here the godlike virtue of charity ought always to exert itfetf. To reliesw the industrious poor in diftreSs, is Surely the moft exalted act of religion and humanity. They alone, who are witnefles of thofe fcenes of calamity, can form a notion of what numbers perifh in difeafes, for want of proper affiftance, and even for want of the neceffaries of life. Laborers are often hurt by a foolifh emulation, which prompts them to vie with one another, till they overheat them- I Selves to fuch a degree as to occafion a fever, or even to drop down dead. Such as wantonly throw away their lives in this manner, deferve to be looked upon in no better light than felf- murderers. ' The office of a foldier, in time of war, may be ranked among the laborious employments. Soldiers fuffer many hard- ships from the inclemency of feafons, long marches, bad provis- ions, hunger, watching, unwholefome climates, bad water, &c. Thefe occafion fevers, fluxes, rheumatifms, and other fatal dif* eafes, which generally do greater execution than t~e fword, es- pecially when campaigns are continued too late in the feafon- A few weeks of cold rainy weather will often prove mere fatal than an engagement. Thofe who have the command of armies fhould take care that their foldiers be well clothed and well fed. They ought al- fo to finifh their campaigns in due feafon, and to provide their AND THE STUDIOUS. & &en with dry and well aired winter quarters. Thefe rules, tak* ing care, at the fame time* to keep the fick at a proper distance from thofe in health* would tend greatly to prelerve the lives of the foldiery.* Sailors may alio be numbered among the laborious. t They undergo great hardships from the change of climate, the violence of the weather, hard labor * bad provisions, &c* Sailors are of fo gr*at importance that too much pains can never t e bef owed in pointing out the meuis of preferv ing their lives. One great fource of the difeafes of Seafaring people is? ex- cefs. When they get on fhore, after having been long at fea, without regard to the climate, or their own constitutions, they plunge.headlong into all manner of riot, and often perfift till a fe- ver puts an end to their lives. Thus intemperance, and n t the climate, is often the caufe why fo many of our brave fail rs die on foreign coafts. Such people ought not to.live too iow; but they viMd moderation the beft defence againft fevers and ma- ny other maladies. Sailors, when on duty, cannot avoid fometimes getting wet. When this happens, they fhould change their clothes as foon as they are relieved, and take every method to reftore the perfpira- tion. They Should not, ia this cafe, make too free with Spirits or other ftrong liquors, but fhould rather drink them diluted w;th warm water, and go immediately to bed * where a found fleep and gende Sweat would fet all to rights. But the health of failors fufters moft from unwholefome food* The cortftant ufe of falted provisions vitiates their hiunpurs, and occafions the fcurvy, and other obftihate maladies. It is no eafy matter to prevent this difeafe in long voyages; yet we cannot help thinking, that much might be done towards effecting fo dt> iirablfi an end,were due pains beftowed fpr that purpofe. Fcrex- ample, various roots,greens, and fruits, might be kept a long t.rae at fea, as onions, potatoes, cabbages, lemons, oranges, tamarinds, apples, &c. When fruits cannot be kept, the JuVes of them* either frefh or fermented, may. With thefe all the drink, and even the food of the Ship's company, ought to be acidulated ill long voyages. Stale bread and beer Iikewife contribute to vitiate tht hu- mours. Flour will keep for a long time on board, of which fr^fh bread might frequently be made. Malt too nrght be kept * It is indeed to be regretted, that ibid ie'rs fuffer not left from indolence and intemp- erance in the time of peace, than from hardships in time of war If menaie idle they will be vicious. Jt would therefore be of great importance, could a fcheme b- formeo for rendering the military, in times of peace, both more healthy and more ufeful. Thef de- rVab.'e objefts might, io oumpinian, be obtained, by employing them for form hours eve-^ ry day, and advancing their pay accordingly* By this mean*, idlers 1-, the morher of V:ce, might be prevented, the price of labor lowered, public works, as h bors. can-Is, turnpiker roa^is, tec. might be made without hutting manufactures } and fe r.iers might I* er biedt fo n-arrv and bring up children. A fcheme of this kind might aafiiy be condudler, to as not to d'eprefs the martial foirit, provided the men were only to vv rk four 01 five hours every d >y, and alway3 to work without doors ; no foldier* fhould be fuftered to work too long, orto follow any frdentary employment. Sedentary employments rendei mtr weak ?nd efTenv :ate, quite unfit for the hardships of War ; whereas working fora few hours ev* ery day without doors, would iniire :hem to the weather., brace then nettes, and inmate •,yn:r ft-i-tagth .tT»-! courage. i5) $8 THE LABORIOUS, THE SEDENTARY, and infufed with boiling water at any time. This liquor, wheb drank even in form of wort, is very wholefome, and is found to be an antidote againft the fcurvy. Small wines and cider might Iikewife be plentifully laid in; and mould they turn four, they would ftill be ufeful as vinegar. Vinegar is a great antidote againft difeaies, and Should be ufed by all travellers, efpecially at fea. It may either be mixed with the water they drink, or taken in their food. Such animals as can be kept alive, ought Iikewife to be car- iried on board, as hens, ducks, pigs, &c. Frefh broths made of portable foup, and puddings made of peas or other vegetables* ought to be ufed plentifully. Many other things will readily oc- cur to people converfant in theSe matters, which would tend to preferve the health of that brave and ufeful fet of men.* We have reafon to believe if due attention were paid to the diet, air, doathing, and above all things to the cleanlinefst of fea- faring people, they would be the moft healthy fet of men in the world; but wtien thefe are neglected, the very reverfe will happen. The beft medical antidote that we can recommend to failors or foldiers on foreign coafts, efpecially where dampnefs prevails, is the Peruvian bark. This will often prevent fevers, and other fatal difeafes. About a drachm of it may be chewed every day; or if this fh uld prove difagreeable, an ounce of bark, with half an ounce of orange peek and < wo drachms of Snake-root coarfery powdered, may be inmfed for two or three days ir an Englifh quart of brandy, and half a wine glafs of it may be taken twice or thrice a-day, when the ftomach is empty. This has been f und to be an excellent antidote againft fluxes, putrid, intermitting, and other fevers, in u; ihealthy climates. It is not material in what form this medicine is taken. It may either be infufed in water, wine, or fpirits, as recommended above, or made into an electuary with Syrups of lemons, oranges, or the like. The Sedentary. Though nothing can be more contrary to the nature of man than a Sedentary life, yet this clafs comprehends by far the great* er part of the Species. Almoft ihe whole female world, and in manufacturing countries, the major part of the males, may be reckoned fedentary4 * The celebrated Captain Cook has /hewn how far, by proper care and attention, the difeafes formerly fo fatal to feamen may be prevented. In a voyage of three years and eighteen days, during which he was expofed to every climate, from the 32 deg. north, to the 71 deg. of fouth latitude^ of one hundred and eighteen men, compofing the ftip's * company, he loft only one, who died of a Phthifis Pulmonalis, The principal means he ufed were, to preferve a Uriel attention to cleanlinefs, to procure abundance of vegetable and frefh provisions, efpecially good water, a,nd to allow his people fufficient time for reft. f £ regulation on board the United States' Navy, requiring every individual,at leaft once a wrefc to wain their feet clean, is worthy of general attention, as a means of pre- ferving health. a. t. i The appellation of fedentary hasgenerally been given onlv to the ffudious ; we ca» fee no reafon, however, for reftri<SHng it to them alone. Many artificers may, with »» much propriety, be denominated fedentary as the ftudious, with this particular difadvati- t>age,that they are often obliged, to. fit in very awkward poftures, which tht fiiniicut ntsj a« do, ujiiefs they pletfe;. 'AND THE STUDIOUS. & Agriculture, the firft and moft healthful of all employments, is now followed by few who are able to carry on anv other ' u- fmefs. But thofe who imagine that the culture of the ^arth is not fufficient to employ all its inhabitants, are greatly mift?ken. An ancient Roman, we are told, could maintain his family from the produce of one acre of ground. So might a modern Briton, if" he would be contented to live like a Roman. This Shews what an immenfe increafe of mhabitrmts Britain might admit of, and all of them live by the culture of the ground Agriculture is the great fource of domeftic riches. Where it is neglected, whatever wealth may be imported from abroad, pnverty and mifery will abound at home. Such is, and ever will be, the fluctuating ftate of trade and manufactures, that thoufands of people may be in full employment to-day and in beggary tomorrow. This can never happen to thofe who culti- vate the ground. They can eat the fruit of their labor, and al- ways by induftry obtain, at leaft, the neceiTaries of life. Though fedentary employments are neceflary, yet there feems to be no reafon why any perlon fhould be confined for life to thefe alone. Were fuch employments intermixed with the .more active and laborious, they would never do hurt, it is con* ftant confinement that ruins the health. A man may not be hurt by fitting five or fix hours a-day j but if he is obliged to fit tea or twelve, he will foon become difeafed. But it is not want of exercife alone which hurts fedentary people j they Iikewife fuffer from the confined air which they breathe. It is very common to fee ten or a dozen taylors,* or ftay-makers, fot example, crowded into one fmall apartment, where there is hardly room for one perfon to breathe freely. In this fituation they generally continue for many hours at a time, often with the addition of feveral candles, w.1 ich tend Iikewife to wafte the air, and render it lefs fit for respiration. Air that is breathed repeatedly becomes unfit for expanding the lungs, this is one caufe of the phthifical coughs, and other complaints of the breaft, fo incident to fedentary artificers. Even the perfpiration from a great number of perfons pent up together, renders the air unwholefome. The danger from this quarter will be greatly increafed, if any one of them happens to have bad lungs, or to be otherwife difeafed. Thofe who fit near him, being forced to breathe the fame air, can hardly fail to be infected. It would be a rare thing, however, to find a dozen of fedentary people all in good health. The danger of crowding them together muft therefore be evident to every one. Many of thofe who follow fedentary employments are con- stantly in a bending pofture, as fhoemakers, taylors, cutlers, &c. Such a Situation is extremely hurtful. A bending p-fture ob- * A nerfon of obfervation in that line of life told me, that moft taylors die of con- fumptions ; Which he airribured chiefly to the unfavorable poftures in which they fir, and the unwholefcmenefs of thofe places where their bufinefs is carried on. If more attention wa» not paid to profit than to the prelervation of human lives, this ev! m^ght be »afiiy remedied ; but while rru^r? only a >4 their own intereft, nothing wiU be dene fbi ;R«s farety of ihr'.r fervar.t;. £d THE LABORIOUS, THE SEDENTARY, iructs all the Vital motions, and of courfe muft deftroy the health* Accordingly we find Such artificers generally complaining of in- digestions, flatulencies, head-achs, pains of the breaft, &c The aliment in fedentary people, inftead of being pufhed forwards by an ere& pofture, and the action of the mufcles, is in a manner confined in the bowels. Hence indigeftion, coftivenefs, wind, and other hypcchondrical affections, the constant compan- ions of the fedentary. Indeed none of the excretions can be du- ly performed where exercife is wanting; and when the mttter which ought to be difcharged in this way is retained too long in the body, it muft have bad effects, as it is again taken np into the mafs of humours. A bending pofture is Iikewife hurtful to the rungs. When this organ is comprefled, the air cannot have free accefs in all its parts, (o as to expand them properly. Hence tubercles, adhe* fions, &c are formed, which often end in confumptions. Befides, the proper action of the lungs being abfplutely neceSFary for making go^d blood, when the organ fails, the humours foon become univerfaliy depraved, and the whole conftitution goes to wreck. Sedentary artificers are not only hurt by preffure on the bowels, but alfo on the inferior extremities, which obftructs the circulation in thefe parts,and renders them weak: and feeble. Thus taylors, fhoeraakersi Sec. frequently lofe the ufe of their legs al«. together *, befides the blood and humours are, by Stagnation, vi- tiated, and the perfpiration is obstructed; from whence proceed fhe fcab, ulcerous fores,, foul blotches, and other cutaneous dif- eafes fo common among fedentary artificers. A bad figure of body is a very common confequence of clofe application to Sedentary employments. The fpine, fcr example^ by being continually Dent, puts on a, crooked fhape, and gener- ally remaips lb ever after. But a bad fig ure of body has already been obferved to be hurtful to health, as the vital function*. are thereby impeded. A fedentary life feldom fails to occafion an univerfal relaxa- tion of the Solids. This is the great fource from whence racft of the difeafes of fedentary people flow. The fcrophuh, confump- tion,hyfterics, and nervous difeafes, nowfocommon, were very lit- tle known in this country before fedentary artificersbecametonu- merous ; and they are very little known ftill among fuch of our people as follow aciive emplovments without doors, though in great towns at leaft two thirds of the inhabitants are afflicted with them. It is very difficult to remedy thofe evils, becaufe many who1 have been accuftomed to a fedentary life, like ricketty children, lofe all inclination for exercife; we fhall, however, throw cut a few hints with refpect to the moft likely means for preferving the health of this ufeful fet of people, which fome of them, we hepe* will be wife enough to take.. It has been already obferved, that fedentary artificers are of- *eu hurt by their bending poftga. They ought therefore to AND THE STUDIOUS. 6% ftand or fit as erect as the nature of their employments will per- mit. They Should Iikewife change their pofture frequently, and Should never fit too long at a time, but leave off wnrk, and walk^ ride, run, or do any thing that witt promote the vital functions. Sedentary artificers are generally allowed too little time for exercife; yet Short as it is, they feldom employ it properly. A 5ou •■ eyraan taylor or weaver, for example, instead of walking abroa for exercife and frefh air, at his hours of leifure, chufes often to fbend them in a public houfe, or in playing at fome fed- entary game, by which he generally lofes both his time and his money. The awkward poftures in which many fedentary artificers work, feem rattier to be the effect of cuftom than neceffity. For example, a table might furety be contrived for ten or a dozen tay- lors to fit round with liberty for their legs either to hang down, or reft upoa a foot-board, as they Should chufe. A place might iikewife be cut out for each perfon, in fuch a manner that he might fit as conveniently for working as in the prefent mode of fitting crofs-legged. All Sedentary artificers ought to pay the moft religious regard to cleanlinefs. Both their Situation and occupations render this highly neceflary. Nothing Would contribute more to preferve their health, than a Strict attention to it; and Such of them as neglect it, not only run the hazard of lofing health, but of becom- ing a nuifance to their neighbors. Sedentary people ought to avoid food that is windy or hard of digeftion, and fhould pay the ftricteft regard to fobriety. A perfon who works hard without doors will foon throw off a de- bauch; but one who fits has by no means an equal chance,. Hence it often happens, that fedentary people are feized with fe- vers after hard drinking. When fuch perfons feel their Spirits low, inftead of running to the tavern for relief, they fhould ride or walk in the firld. This would remove the complaint more effctually than ftrong liquor, and would never hurt the confti- tution. Instead of multiplying rules for preferving the health of the fedentary, we Shall recommend to them the following general plan, viz. That every perfon who follows a fedentary employ- ment Should cultivate a piece of ground with his own hands. This he might dig, plant, fow, and weed at leifure hours, fo as to make it both an exercife and araufement, while it produced many of the neceffaries of life. After working an hour in a gar- den, a man will return with more keennefs to his employment within doors, than if he had been all the while idle. Laboring the ground is every way conducive to health. It not only gives exercife to every part of the body, but the very fmell of the earth and frefh herbs revives and cheers the Spirits, whilft the perpetual profpect of fomething coming to maturity, delights and entertains the mind. We are fo formed as to be always pleafed with fomething in profpect* however diftant or npwever trivial. Hence the happineCs that moft men feel in 6z THE LABORIOXfS, THE SEDENTARY, planting, fowmg, building, &c. Thefe feem to have been the chief employments of the more early ges; and, when kings and conquerors cultivated the ground, there is reafon to believe that they knew as well wherein true happinefs confuted as we do. It may feem romantic to recommend gardening to manufac- turers in greit towns} but obfervation proves that the plan is very practicable. In t e towu of Sheffield, in Yurkfhire, where the great iron manufacture is carried on, there is hardly a journ. " eyman cutler who -oes not p ffefs a piece of ground, which he cultivates as a garden. This practice has many falutary effects. It not only induces thefe pe pie to take exercife without doors, but alfo to eat many greens, roots, &c. of their own growth, which they would never think of purch^fing. There can be no reafon why manufacturers in .any ether town in Great-Britain fhould not follow the fame plan. It is indeed to be regretted, that in fuel! a place as Lo .don a pkm of this kind is not practica- ble ; yet even there, fedentary artificers may find opportunities of taking air and exercife, if they C;;ufe to embrace them. Mechanics are too much inclined to croud into great towns. The Situation may have fome advantages, but it has Iikewife ma- ny difadvantages. All mechanics who live in the country have it in their power to cultivate a piece of ground ; which indeed moft of them do This not only gives them exercife, but enables them to live more comfortably. So far at leaft as my obferva- tion extends, mechanics who live in the country are far more hippy than thofe in great towns. They enjoy better health, live in greater affluence, and feldom fail to rear a healthy and numer- ous offspring. In a word, exercife without doors^in one Shape or another, is abfolutely neceflary to health. Thofe who neglect it, though they may fur a while drag out life, can hardly be faid to enjoy it. Weak and effeminate, they languifh for a few years, and foon drop into an untimely grave. The Studious. Intenfe thinking is fo destructive to health, that few instan- ces can be produced of ftudious perfons who are ftrong and heal- thy. Hard Study always implies a fedentary life; and when in- tenfe thinking is joined to the want of exercife, the confequences muft be bad. We have frequently known even a few months of clofe application to ftudy ruin an excellent conftitution, by in- ducing a train of nervous complaints, which could never be re- moved. Man is evidently not formed for continual thought more than for perpetual action, and would be as foon worn out by the one as the other. So great is the power of the mind over the body, that, by its influence, the whole vital motions may be accelerated or re- tarded, to almoft any degree. Trus cheerfulnefs and mirth quicken t: e circulation, and promote all the fecretions; whereas fadnefs aid profound thought never fail to retard them. Hence :t would appear, that even a degree of thougbtlsffnef? is necej§« AND THE 9T&DI0US. 6i ry to health. Indeed theffcjerpetual thinker feldom enjoys either health or Spirits ; while the perfon who can hardly be faid to think at all, generally enjoys both. Perpetual thinkers, as they are called, feldom think long. In a few years they generally become quite Stupid, and exhibit a melancholy proof how readily the greateft blefliugs may be abu- fed. Thinking, like every thing elfe, when carried to extreme^ becomes a vice; nor can any thing afford a greater proof of wiU dom, than for a man frequently and feafonably to unbend his mind. This may generally be done by mixing in cheerful com- pauy, active diversions, or the like. Inftead of attempting to inveftigate the nature of that con- nection which fubfifts between the mind and bo.liy, or to inquire into the manner in which they mutually affect each ether, we Shall only mention thofe difeafes to which the learned are more peculiarly liable, and endeavor to point out the means of avoid- ing them. Studious perf >ns are very fubject to the gout. This pain- ful difeafe in a great meafure proceeds from indigeftion, and an obstructed perfpiration. It is impofli:>le that tht: nun who fits from morning till night Should either digeft his food, or have any of the fecretions in due quantity. But when tp.at matter which fhould be thrown off by the fkin, is retained in toe body, and the humours are not duly prepared, difeafes muft enfue. The Studious are likewiSe very liable to the itone md gravel. Exercife greatly promotes both tre fecr^tion and difcharge of the urine ; consequently a fedentary life muft have t e contrary .effect:. Any one may be fatisfied of this by obferving that he paffes much more urine by day tfcah in the night, and alfo when he walks or rides, than wnea he fits. The circulation in the liver being fiVw, obstructions in that organ can hardly fail to be the confequence of inactivity. Hence fedefrtary people are frequently afflicted with fchirrous livers. But the proper fecretion and difcharge of the bile is fo neceffary a part of the animal economy, that wuere thefe are not duly per- formed, the health muft form be impaired. Jaundice, indigeftion, lofs of appetite, and a wafting of the whole body, feldom fail to be the confequences of a vitiated ftate of the liver or obstructions of the bile. Few difeafes prove more fatal to the Studious than confump- tions of the lungs. It has already been obferve!, that this organ cannot be duly expanded in thofe who do not take pr ;per exer- cife; and where this is the cafe, obstructions and adftefions will enfue. ■ Not only want of exercife, but the pofture in wfrch Studious perfons generally fit, is very hurtful to the lungs. Thofe who read or write much' are ready to contract a habit of bend- ing forwards, and often pr^fs with their breaft upon a table or bench. This oMture cannot fail to hurt the lun*;s. The functions of the heart may Iikewife by this means be injured. I remember to have feen a man opened, whole peri- cardium adhered to the breaft-bone in fuch a manner as tooV 0> THE LABORIOUS* THE SEDENTARY, ftruct. the motion of tne heart, and occafion his death. The on* ly pr wv >le caufe that could be affigned for this fingular Symptom was, tnat the man, whoSe bufinefs was writing, ufed constantly to it in a lending pofture, with his breaft preffing upon the edge of a plain table. No perfon can enjoy health who does not properly digelt his f->od. But intenfe thinking and inactivity never fail to weak- en the powers of d;g,'ftion. Hence the hum- urs become crude an 1 v'ti^i, the folids weak a d relaxed, and the whole confti» tution goes to ruin.
