Long and intenfe thinking often occafions grievous head- achs, which bring on virtigoes, apoplexies palfies, and other fa al ditbrders. The beft way to prevent thefe is, never to ftudy too ioog at one time, and to keep the body regular, either by pro? r food, or taking frequently a little of fome opening med- icine. Thofe who read or write much are often afrVcted with fore ey°s. Sf n ?ying by candle-hght is peculiarly hur'ful to the fight.- T s ught to be practifed as feldom as poffible. When it is un- av i able, the eyes fhould be Shaded, r.nd the head Should not be held *oo low. When tne eyes are weak or painful, they Should Be -athed every nigj.t and morning in cold water, to which a lit- tle brandy may be added. Ir has already been obferved, that the excretions are very defective in the Studious. The dropfv is often occafioned by the retenti n of thofe humours which ought to be carried off in this wav. Any perfon may obferve, that fitting makes his leg6 Swell, and that this goes off by exercife; which clearly points out the method of prevention. Fevers, efpecially of the nervous kind, are often the effect of ftudy. N thing affects the nerves fo much as intenfe thought. It in a manner unhinges the whole human frame, and not only hurts the vital motions, but difbrders the mind itfelf. Hence a deliriu , melancholy, and even madnefs, are often the effect of elofe application to Study, in fine, there is no difeafe which can proceed ei'her from a bad ftate of the humours, a defect: of the ufual fecretions, or a debility of the nervous Syftem, which may not be induced by intenfe thinking. But the moft afflicting of rdl the difeaies which attack the < ftudious is the hypocondriac. This difeafe feldcm fails to be the companion of deep thought. It may rather be called a complica- tion of maladies Than a Single one. To what a wretched condi- tion are the beft of men often reduced by it \ Their Strength and mppetite fail; a perpetual gloom hangs ever their minds; they live ia the conftant dread of death, and are continually in Search, ©f relief from medicine ; where, alas! it is not to be fc-und. Thofe who labor under this disorder, though they are often made the Subject of ridicule, juftly claim our higheft fympathy and compaffion. Hardly anv thng can be more prepofterous than for a per- iga to mjake itudy &§ lole. bvfee^, A mere Student \\ Severn. AND THE STUDIOUS. ^ an ufeful member of foeiety. He often nfegfecls the moft im- portant duties of life, in order to pnJ-fue ftudiesof a very trifling nature. Indeed it rarely happens that any ufeful invention is the effect of mere ftudy. The farther men dive into profound refearches, they generally deviate the more from common fenfe* and too often lofe fight of it altogether. Profound Speculations, inftead of making men wifer or better, generally render them abfolute fceptics, and overwhelm them with doubt and uncer- tainty. All that is neceftary for a man to know, in order to be happy, is eafily obtained; and the reft, like the forbidden fruit, ferves only to mcreafe his mifery. Studious perfons, in order to relieve their minds, muft not only discontinue to read and write, but engage in fome employ- ment or diverfion that will not fo far occupy the thought as to make them forget the bufinefs of the clofet. A folitary ride or walk are fo far from relaxing the mind, that they rather encour- age thought. Nothing can divert the mind when it gets into a train of 'erious thinking, but attention to fubjects of a more triv- ial nature. Thefe prove a kind of play to the mind, and confe- quently relieve it. Learned men often contract a contempt for what they call trifling company. They are afhamed to be feen with any but philofophers. This however is no proof of their being philofo- phers themfelves. No man deferves that name who is afhamed to unbend his mind, by aflbciating with the cheerful and gay. Even the fociety of children will relieve the mind, and expel the gloom which application to ftudy is too apt to occafion. As Studious people are neceffarily much within doors, they fhould make choice of a large and well aired place for ftudy. This woul \ not only prevent the bad effects which attend confin- ed air, but would cheer the Spirits, and have a moft happy influ- ence both on the body and mind. It is laid of Euripides the tra- gedian, that he ufed to retire to a dark cave to colnpofe his trag- edies, and of Oe.nofthenes the Grecian orator, that he chofe a place for a ftudy where nothing could be either heard or feen. Wlti1. ail deference to fuch venerable names, we cannot help con- demning their tafte. A m;n may furely think to as good pur- p feii an elegant apartment as in a cave; and may have as hap- py conceptions where the all-cheering rays of the fun render the air wholefome, as in places where they never enter. T" ofe who read or write much Should be very attentive to their pofture. They ought to fit and Stand by turns, always keeping as nearly in an erect pofture as poffible. Thofe who cte'.t", na?.y io it walking. It has an excellent effect frequently to read or fp?ak aloud. This not only exercifes the lungs,%but almoft the whole body. Hence Studious people are greatly ben- efited by deliver', og difcourfes in public. Public fpeakers, indeed, lb tetimes hurt thenfelve?, by overacting their part; but that is th°:r own fault. The martyr to mere vociferation merits not our Sympathy- The morning has, by all medical writers, been reckoned th? ■66 THE LABORIOUS, THE SEDENTARY, be. beft time for ftudy. It is fo. But it is alfo the moft proper feafon for exercife, while the ftomacn is empty, and the Spirits refreshed with Sleep. Studious people Should theref *e fome-i times Spend the morning in walking, riding, or fome manly diver- ,fions without doors. This would make them return to ftudy with greater alacrity, and would be of more ferv;ce than twice the time after their Spirits are worn out with fatigue. It is not fufficient to take diverfion only when we can think no longer. Every Studious perfon Should make it a part of his bufinefs, and mould let nothing interrupt his hours of recreation more than ihofe of ftudy. Mufic has a very happy effect in relieving the mind when fatigued with Study. It would be well if every Studious perfon were fo far acquainted with that fcience as to amufe himfelf after Severe thought, by playing fuch airs as have a tendency to raife y Z the fpirits, and infpire cheerfulnefs and good humour. ' ^ It is a reproach to learning, that any of her votaries, to re- jj fieve the mind after flu 'y, Should betake themfelves to the ufe '] of Strong liquors.* This indeed is a remedy ; but it is a defper- ate one, and always proves destructive. Would Such perfons, when their Spirits are low, get on horfeback, and ri e ten or a dozen miles, they would find it a more effectual remedy than any cordial medicine in the apothecary's fhop, or all the ftrong liquors in the world. The following is my plan, and I cannot recommend a better to others. When my mind is fatigued witi Study, or other feri- ous bufinefs, I mount my horfe, and ride ten or twelve miles in- to the country, where I fpend a day, and fomeiimes, two with a .* cheerful friend ; after which 1 rev^r fail to return to town with JB new vigor,and to purfue my Studies or bufinefs with frrih alacrity, "^m It is much to be regretted, tha* learned men, while in health,- :<■ pay fo little regard to thefe things! There is not any thing more 1 common than to fee a miferaMe object over-run with nervous dif- eafes, bathing, walking, riding, and, in a word, doing every thing for health after it is gone; yet, if any me had recommended thefe things to him by way of preventior, the advice would, in all probability, have been treated with contempt, cr, at leaft, with neglect. Such is the weaknefs and folly of mankind, and fuch the want of forefight, even in thofe who ought to be wifer than others 1 With regard to the diet of the Studious, we fee no refon why they fhould abstain from any kind of food that is whole- fome, provided they ufe it in moderation. They ought, however, to be fparing in the ufe of every things that is windy, rancid, or hard of digeftion. Their Suppers fhould always be light, or taken foon in the evening. Their drink may be water, fine malt • *' To fuch perfons," fays Dr. Rufli,«« it may be a difcovery to know, that tea is a much better remedy for that purpofe. By its grateful and gentle ftimulus, it reraovei fa- ligue, reftores the excitement of the mind, and invigorates the whole fyftem. I am no advocate for the exceffive ufe of tea. When taken too ftrong, it is hurtful, efpecially to ihe female conftitution ; but, when taken of a moderate degree of ftrength, a'nd in moder- ate quantities, with fugar and cream, or mi'k, I believe it is, in general, innoxious and at •i!! t;mc?, to be pressed to ardent fpirits, as a cordial for ftudious me^y ' ^. J? OF ALIMENT. 6j fiquor, nottoo ftrong, good cyder, wine and water, or, if troub- led with acidities, water mixed with a little brandy, rum, or any other genuine Spirit. We Shall only obferve, with regard to thofe kinds of exer- cife which are moft proper for the Studious; that they Should not be too violent, nor ever carried to the degree of exceSIive fa- tigue. They ought Iikewife to be frequently varied So as to give action to all the different parts of the body; and Should, as often as poffible, be taken in the open air. In general, riding on horfe- back, walking, working in a garden, or playing at fome a.ctive diversions, are the beft. We would likewile recommend the ufe of the cold bath to the Studious. It will, in fome meafure, Supply the place of exer- cife, and Should not be neglected by perfons of a relaxed habit, efpecially in the warm feafon. No perfon ought either to take violent exercife or to Study immediately after a full meal. CHAP. III. OF ALIMENT. U N WHOLESOME food, and irregularities of diet, oc- cafion many difeafes. .There is no doubt but the whole confti- tution of body may be changed by diet alone. The fluids may be thereby attenuated or condenfed, rendered mild or acrimoni- ous, coagulated or diluted, to almoft any degree. Nor are its effects upon the folids lefs confiderable. They may be braced or relaxed, have their fenfibility, motions, &c. greatly increafed or diminished, by different kinds of aliment. A very Small atten- tion to ti»e(e things will be fufficient to Shew, how much the pre- ferv^fion of health depends upon a proper regimen of the diet. Nor is an attention to diet neceffary for the preservation of health only; it is Iikewife of importance in the cure of difeaf- es. Every intention in tie cure of many difeafes, may be anf- wered by diet ahne. Its effects, mdeed, are not always fo quick as thofe of medicine, bui they are generally more lafting ; beiides, it is neither So difagreeable to the patient, nor fo dangerous as medicine, and is always more eafily obtained. % Our intention here is not to inquire minutely into the nature and properties of t'-1 ? various kinds of aliment in ufe amone; manki'-d; nor to fhew their effects upon the different constitu- tions of the human bo y; but to mark fome of the moft perni- cious errors which people are apt to fall into, with refpect both to the qua-.tity and quality of their food, and to point cut their influence upon health. It is not indeed an eafy matter to afcertain the exact quanti- ty of food proper for every age, fex, and conftitution ; but a Scru- pulous nicetv here is bv no means neceffarv. fhe be? rule is to avoid all extreme.?. Mankind \vere never intended to weigh and* $g ®F ALIMENI. meafure their .food. Nature teaches every creature when it has enough; and the calls of thirft and hunger are fufficient to inform them when more is neceffarv. Though moderation is the coief rule with regard to the quan- tity, yet the quality of food merits a farther consideration. There are many ways by which provifions may be rendered unwhole- fome. Bad feafons may either prevent the ripening of grain, or damage it afterwards. . Thefe, indeed, are acts or Providence, and we muft Submit to them; but Surely no puniShment can be too feverefor thofewhofufferprovifions to fpoil by hoarding them, on purpofe to raife the price, or who promote their own intereft by adulterating the neceffories of life.* Animal, as well as vegetable food, may be rendered un- wholefome, by being kept too long. All animal fubftancs have a conftant tendency to putrefaction ; and, when that has pro- ceeded too far, they not only become offenfive to the fenfes, but hurtful to health. Difeafed animals, and fuch as die of them- felves, ought never to be eaten. It is a common practice, how- ever, in fome grazing countries, for fervants and poor people to eat Such animals as die of any difeafe, or are killed by accident. Poverty, indeed, may oblige people to do this; but they had bet- ter eat a fmaller quantity of what is found and wholefome: it Would both afford a better nourifhment, and be attended with lefs danger. The injunctions given to the Jews, not to eat any creature which died of itfelf, feemed to have a Strict regard to health; and ought to be obferved by Christians as well as Jews. Animalg never die themfelves without fome previous difeafe; but how a difeafed animal Should be wholefome food, is inconceivable: even thofe which die by accident muft be hurtful, as their blood is mixed with the flefh, and Soon turns putrid. Animals which feed grofsly, as tame ducks, hogs, &c. are neither fo eafily digested, nor afford Such wholefome nourishment as others. No animal can be wholefome which does not take fufficient exercife. Moft of our Stalled cattle are crammed with grofs food, but not allowed exercife nor free air; by which means they indeed grow fat, but their juices not being properly prepared or affimilated, remain crude, and occafion indigestions, grofs humours, and oppreifion of the Spirits, in thofe who feed upon them. Anim-ds are often rendered unwholefome by being overv heated. Exceffive heat caufes a fever, exalts the animal falts, and mixes the blood fo intimately with the flefh, that it cannot be fep-rated. For this reafon, butchers fhould, be Severelypun- lfhed who over-drive their cattle. No perfon would chufe to eat the fleSh of an animal which had died in a high fever; yet this is the cafe with all over-drove cattle; and the fever is often raifed even to the degree of madnefs. .• .* 7!le Pi°ur* •indeed' are ^"."""y tbe fi,ft who f«ter by unfound provifions; but the hves of the laboring pour .re or great irapoitance to the ftate , befides, difeafes occafioned by unwholeiome food often prove infectious, bv which means they reach people in evert be^ofed toVa'"ef0rethe'ntereft °f *" t0 ■toke-Mie UWt n0 {loilt P™fi°«WanySd OF ALIME&n $ Bnt this is not the only way by which butcfeers render meat unwholefome. The abominable cuftom of filling the cellular membrane with air, in order to make them appear fat, is every day pracVtfed. This not only Spoils the meat, and renders it un- fit forkeeping, but is fuch a dirty trick, that the very idea7 cf it is fufncient to difguft a perfon of any delicacy at every thing which comes from the fha bles. Who can bear the thought of eating meat which has been blown up with air from the lungs of a dirty fellow, perhaps laboring under the very worft of -difeafes T Butchers have 1 kewifea meth d of'filling the cellular mem- branes of animals with blood. Thi* makes the meat feem fatter, and Iikewife weigh more, but is notwirMending a very pernicious- Cuftom, as it both renders the meat unwholefome and unfit for keeping. I feldom fee a piece of meat from the Shambles, where the blood is not diffufed through the cellular texture* I Shall not fay that this is always the effect of defign r but am certain it is not the cafe with animals that are killed for domefdc ufe, and properly blooded. Veal feems to be moft frequently Spoilt in this way. Per-, haps that may in fome meafure be owing to the practice of car- rying calves from a great diftance to market, by which mema their tender flefh is bruifed, and many of their veffels burft. No people in the world- eat fuch quantities of animal food as the Engmh, which is one reafon why they are fb generally tainted with the Scurvy, and its numerous train of confequehcep, indigeftion, low Spirits, hypochondriacifm, &c. Animal food was furely defigned for man, and with a proper mixture of veg- etables, it will be found the moft wholefome', but to gorge beef, mutton, pork, filh, and fowl, twice or thrice a-day, is certainly too much. All who value health ought to be contented with making one meal of flefh in twenty-four hours, and this ought to confift of one kind only. The moft obstinate fcurvy has often been cured1 by a veget- able diet; nay, milk alone will frequently do more in that dif- eafe than any medicine. Hence it is evident, that if vegetables and milk were more ufed in diet, welhouldhavelefs fcurvy. and Iikewife fewer putrid and inflammatory fevers. Frefh vegetables, indeed, come to be daily more ufed in diet; this laudable prac- tice we hope will continue to gain ground. Our aliment ought neither to be too moift nor too dry. Moift aliment relaxes the folids, and renders the body feeble. Thus we fee females, who live much on tea and other watery diet, generally become we*k aid unable to digeft Solid food ; heno#proceed hyfterirs, and all their dreadful confequences. On the other hand, food that is too dry, renders the fclids in a manner rigid, snd the humours vifcid, which diSpcSes the body to inflammatory fevers, Scurvies, and the like. Much has been faid on the ill effects of tea in diet. They are, no dcubt, numerous; but they proceed rather from theira- orudent ufe of it, than from anv bad qualities in the tea itfelf. Tea 'is new the univerfol breakfaft in this part of the world v bn^ 70 OF ALIMENT. the morning k furely the moft improper time of the day for drink- ing it. Moft delicate perfons, who, by the bye, are the great- eft tea drinkers, cannot eat any thing in the morning. If fuch perfons, after fafting ten or twelve hours, drink four or five cups of green tea without eating fcarcely any bread, it muft hurt them. Good tea, taken in a moderate quantity, not too ftrong, nor too hot, nor drank upon an empty ftomach, will feldom do harm; but if it be bad, which is often the cafe, or fubftituted in the room of folid food, it muft have many ill effects. The arts of cookery render many things unwhoiefome, which are not fo in their own nature. By jumbling together a number of different ingredients, in order to make a poignant fauce, or rich foup, the composition proves almoft a poifcn. All high feafoning, pickles, &c. are only incentives to luxury, and never fail to hurt the ftomach. It were well for mankind, if cookery, as an art, were entirely prohibited!. Plain roafting or boiling is all that the Stomach requires. Thefe alone are fuffi- cient for people in health, and the fick have ftill lefs need of a cook. The liquid part of our aliment Iikewife claims our attention. ""Water is not only the bafis of moft liquors, but alfo compofes a great part of our folid food. Good water muft therefore be of the greateft importance in diet. The beft water is that which is moft pure, and free from any mixture of foreign bodies. Wa- ter takes up parts of moft bodies with which it comes into con- tact ; by this means it is often impregnated with metals or min- erals of a hurtful or poifoneus nature. Hence the inhabitants of fome hilly countries have peculiar difeafes, which in all prob- ability proceed from the water. Thus the people who live near the Alps in Switzerland,and the inhabitants of the Peak of Derby in England, have large tumors or wens on their necks. # This difeafe is generally imputed to the fnow water; but there is more reafon to believe it is owing to the minerals in the mountains through which the waters pafs. When water is impregnated with foreign bodies, it generally appears bv its weight, color, tafte, Smell, heat, or fome other fen- fible quality. Our bufinefs therefore is to chufe fuch wa.er, for common ufe, as is lighteft, and without any particular color, tafte, or Smell. In moft places of Britain the inhabitants have it in their power to make choice of their water, and few things would contribute more to health than a due attention to this article. But mere indolence often induces people to make ufe of the wa- ter that is neareft to them, without confidering its qualities. Before water is brought into^ great towns, the ftricteft at- tention ought to be paid to its qualities, as many difeaSes may be occafioned or aggravated by bad water ; and when once it has been procured at a great expence, people are unwilling to give ft up. The common methods of rendering water clear by fil- tration, or Soft, by expofing it to the fun and air, &c. are fo gen- erally known that it is unneceffary to fpend time in explaining them. We fhall only, in general, advife all to avoid waters widen OF ALIMENT. 7* ftagnate long in finall lakes, ponds, or the like, as fuch waters of- ten become putrid, by the corruption of animal and vegetable bodies with which they abound. Even cattle frequently fuffer by drinking, in dry feafons, water which has Stood long in Small refervoirs, without being fupplied by fprings, or frefhened with Showers. All wells ought to be kept clean, and to have a free communication with the air. As fermented liquors, notwithstanding they have been ex- claimed againft by many writers, ftill continue to be the common drink of almoft every perfon who can afford them; we Shall rath- er endeavour to affift people in the choic* of thefe liquors, than pretend to condemn what cuftom has fo firmly eftabhfhed. It is net the moderate ufe of found fermented liquors which hurts mankind: it is excefs, and ufiug fuch as are ill prepared or vi- tiated. Fermented liquors, which are too ftrong, hurt digeftion; and the body is fo far from being Strengthened by them, that it is weakened and rrlaxed. Many imagine that hard labor could not be Supported without drinking ftrong liquors; this is a very er- roneous notion. Men who never tafte ftrong liquors are not on*- ly able to endure more fatigue, but alfo live much longer than thofe who ufe them daily. But, fuppofe ftrong liquors did ena- ble a man to do more work, they muft nevertheless wafte the powers of life, and occafion premature old age. They keep up a conftant fever, which exhaufts the fpirits, inflames the blood, and difpofes the body to numberlefs difeafes. But fermented liquors may be too weak as well as too ftrong i when that is the cafe, they muft either be drank new, or they be- come four and dead: when fuch liquors are drank new, the fer- mentation not being ever, they generate air in the bowels, and occafion flatulencies ; and, when kept till dale, they turn four on the Stomach, and hurt digeftion. For this reafon all malt-liquor, cider, &c. ought to be of fuch ftrength as to keep till they be ripe, and then they fhould be ufed. When fuch liquors are kept too long, though they fhould not become lour, yet they generally con- tract a hardnefs which renders them unwholefome. All families, who can, .ought to prepare their own liquors. Since preparing and vending of liquors became one of the molt general branches of bufinefs, every method has been tried to adul terate them. The great object b-rth to the makers and venders of liquors is, to render it intoxicating, and to give it the appear- ance cf age. But it is well known that this may be done by oth- er ingredients, than thofe which ought to be ufed for making it ftrong. It w ->uld be imprudent even to name thofe things which are daily made ufe of to render liquors heady. Suffice it to fay, that the pr tctice is very common, and that all the ingredients ufed for this purpofe are of a narcotic or ftupefactive quality. But a. all opiates are poifonous, it is eafy to fee what muft be the con- fequence of their general ufe. Though they do not kill fudden- ly, yet they hurt the nerves, relax -and weaken the itcmach, an" Spoil the digeftion. 7i OF ALIMENT. Were fermented liquors fafchfuHy prepared, kept to a pmjfc er aee, and ufed in moderation, they would prove real blellmg* to mankind. But, while they are ill prepared, various ways adulterated, and taken to excefs, they muft have many pernicious We would recommend it to families, not only to prepare their own liquors, but Iikewife their bread. Bre;>.d is fo neceffary a part of diet, that too much care cannot be beftowed in order to have it found and wholefome, For this purpofe, it is not on- ly neceffary that it be made of good gfain, but Iikewife properly prepared, and kept free from all unwholefome ingredients, fnis, however, we have reafon to believe is not always the cafe with bread prepared by thofe who make a trade of vending it. Their object: is rather to pleafe the eye, than to conSult the health. The beft bread is that which is neither too coarSe nor too fine; well fermented, and made of wheat flower, r rather of wheat and rye mixed together. To fpecity the different kinds of aliment, to explain their na- ture and properties, and to point Out their effects in different constitutions, would far exreed the limits of our defign. Inftead of a detail of this kind, which would not be generally understood, and of courfe little attended tr, we Shall only mention the fol- lowing eafy rules with refpect to the choice of aliment; PerSons whoSe Solids are weak and relaxed, ought to avoid all vifcid food, or fuch things as are hard of digeftion. Their di- et, howevf r, ought to be nourishing; and they Should take fuffi-< tient exercife in the open air. Such as abound with blood Should be fparing in the ufe of every thing that is highly nouri thing, as fat meat, rich wines, Strong ale, and Such like. Their food fhould confift chiefly of bread and other vegetable fubftances; and their drink ought to be water, whey or fmall beer. Fat people fhould not eat freely of oily nourishing diet. They ought frequently to ufe horfe-radaMfh, garlic, Spices, or fuch things as are heating and promote perfpiration and urine. Their drink Should be water, coffee, tea, or the like; and they ought to take much exercife and little fleep. TV fe who are too lean muft follow an oppofite courfe. Such as are troubled with acidities, or whofe food is apt to four on the ftomach, fhould live much on animal food; and thofe who are afflicted with hot billious eructations,ought to ufe a diet confuting chiefly of acid vegetables. People who are afflicted with the g'.ut. low fpirits, hypo- chondriac or hyfteric diforders, ought to avoid all flatulent food, every thing that is vifcid, or hard of digeftion, all falted crfmrke- dried prorfions, an4 whatever is au*'ere, acid, or apt to turn four on the ftomacn. Their food fhould be light, Spare, cool, and of an opening nature. The diet ought not only to be Suited to the age and constitu- tion, but alfo to the manner of Ufe ; a Sedentary or ftudieus per-, Son fhould live more fparingly than one who labors hard without OP ALIMENT. ft cjpors. Many kinds of food will nourish a peafant very well which would be almoft indtgeftible to a citizen j and the latter will live upon a diet on which the former would ftarve. Diet ought not to be too uniform. The conftant ufe of One kind of food might have fome bad effects. Nature teaches us this, by the great variety of aliment which Ihe has provided for man, and Iikewife by giving him an appetite for different kinds of food. Thofe who labor under any particular difeafe, ought to avoid Such aliments as have a tendency to increafe it; for exam- ple, a gouty perfon fhould not indulge m rich wines, ftrong Soups, or gravies, and fhould avoid all acids. One who is troubfed with the gravel ought to fhun all auftere and aftringent aliments; and thofe who are fcorbutic fhould be fparing in the ufe of felted provifions, &c. In the firft period of life, oor food ought to be light, but nourishing, and frequently taken. Food that is folid, with a fuf* ficient degree of tenacity, is moft proper for the ftate of manhood. The diet suited to the laft period of life, When nature is upon the decline, approaches nearly to that of the firft. It Should be lighter and more fucculent than that of vigorous age, and like* wife more frequently taken. It ts not only neceffary for health that our diet be wholefome, but alfo that it be taken at regular periods. Some imagine that long rafting will atone for excefs; but this, inftead of mending the matter, generally makes' it worfe. When the ftomach ana inteftines are Over diftended with food, they lofe their proper tone, and, by long fafting, they become weak, and inflated with wind. Thus, either gluttony or fafting destroys, the powers of digeftion. The frequent repetition of aliment is not only neceffary for >epairing the continual wafte of our bodies, but Iikewife to keep the fluids found and fweet. Our humours, even in the moft healthy State, have a conftant tendency to putrefaction, which can only be prevented by frequent Supplies of frelh nourifhment: when that is wanting too long, the putrefaction often proceeds fa far as to occafion very dangerous fevers. From hence we may learn the neceffity of regular meals. No perfon can enjoy a good ftate of health, whofe veffels are either frequently overcharg- ed, or the humours long deprived of frefh fupplies of chyle. Long fafting is extremely hurtful to young people ; it not only vitiates their humours, but prevents their growth. Nor is it lefs injurious to the aged. Moft perfons, in the decline of life, are afflicted with wind: this complaint is not edy increafed, but even rendered dangerous, and often fataLrfby long fafting. Old people, when their Stomachs are empty? are frequently feized with giddinefs, head-achs, and faiutnefs. Ihefe complaints may gen-. erally be removed by a piece of bread and a glafs of wine, or tak- ing any other folid food; which plainly points Out the me«fcc4 of preventing them. !t is mere t&an probable, that many of the faddfn deaths, (6) tf of aliment: ..... v which happen in the advanced periods of life, are occafionea1 bjrf fafting too long, as it exhaufts the fpirits, and iills the bowels'; with wind: we would therefore advife people in the decline ot life, never to allow their ftomachs to be too long empty. Many people take nothing but a few cups of tea and a little bread, from ' nine o'clock at night till two or three next afternoon. Such may. be faid to fait almoft three fourths of their time. This can hard- ly fail to ruin the appetite, vitiate the humours, and fill the bow- els with wind ; all which might be prevented by a folid. breakfaft. It is a very common practice to eat a light breakfaft and a, heavy fupper. This cuftom ought to be reverfed. When peo- ple fup late, their fupper Should be very light; but the breakfaft., ought always to be folid. If any one eats a light fupper, goes foon fo bed, and rifes betimes in the morning, he will be fure to find an appetite for his breakfaft; and he may freely indulge it. * The ftrong and hedt^y do not indeed Suffer fb much from fafting as the weak and delicate; but they run great hazard from its oppofite, viz. repletion. Many difeafes, efpecially fevers, are the effect of a plethora, or tco great fullnefs of the veflels. Strong people, in high health, have generally a great quantity of blood and other humours. "When thefe are fuddenly increafed, by an overcharge of rich and nourifhing diet, the veffels become too' much diftended, and obstructions and inflammations enfue. Hence fo many people are feized with inflammatory and eruptive- fevers, apoplexies, &c. after a feaft or debauch. All great and Sudden changes in diet are dangerous. What rhe ftomach has been long accuftomed to digeft, though lefs wholefome, will agree better with it than food of a more faluta- ry nature to which it has not been ufed. When therefore a Change becomes neceffary, it ought always to be made gradually; a Sudden tranfition from a poor and low, to a rich and luxurious^ diet, or the contrary* might fo difturb the functions of the body as to endanger health, or even to occafion death itfelf. When we recommend regularity .in diet, we would not be underlined as condemning every fmall deviation from it. It is ,next to irrmoffiMe for people at all times to avoid fome degree of excefs, and living too much by rule might make even the fmalleft deviation dangerous. It may therefore be prudent to vary a lit- , tie, famettmes taking more, Sometimes lef?, than the ufual quan- tity of meat and drink, provided always that a due regard be had to moderation. $ Notwithstanding our author's omiffion of a general account of the *qusditie$*>f "fijk different kinds of animal and vegetable food -^oft commonly ofcd in diet, we think the following not unr worthy attention. - •. ■■ .... ..••,, " Beef."Wh^n this is the flefh of a bullock of middle age, it affoH's go^d'ft»^ftr-cnigmoiiriShment,an4,is peculiarly well adap- ted to thofe who labor, or take much exerrife.' It will often fit eafv upon the'ftomachs that can digeft no other kind o. food ; and its flit is almoft as eafily digeft«d as thatot vtal. OF ALIMENT. 7S " Veal is a proper food for perfons recovering from an indif- pofition, and may even be given to febrile pa-ients in a very weak ftate, but it affords lefs nourifhment than the flefh of the fame animal in a ftate of maturity. The fat of it is lighter than that of any other animal, and fhows the leaft difpofition to putref- ceccy. Veal is a very fui table food in coftive habits; but of all meat it is the leaft calculated for removing an acid from the ftomach. " Mutton, from the age of four to fix years, and fed on dry pafture, is an excellent meat. It is of a middle kind between the firmnefs of beef and the tendernefs of veal. Th' lean part of raut- , ton, however, is the moft nourifhing, an 1 conducive to health; the fat being hard of digeftion. The h. ? d of fe fheep, efpecially when divefted of the Skin, is very tender; and the feet, on account.of the jelly they contain, highly nutritive. " Lamb is not fo nourifhing as mutton; but it is light, and ex- tremely fuitable to delicate Stomachs. " Houfe-lamb, though much. efteemed by many, poffeffes the bad qualities common to the flefh of all animals reared in an unnat- ural way. i " Pork affords rich and fubftantial nourifhment; and its juices are wholefome when properly fed, and when the animal enjoys pure air and exercife. But the flefh of hoes reared in towns is Soth hard of digeftion and unwholefome. Pork is particularly improper for thofe who are fiable to any foulnefs of the Skin. It is almoft proverbial, that a dram is good for promoting its digeftion: but this is an erroneous notion : for, though a dram may give a momen- tary Stimulus to the coats of the ftomach,it tends to harden the flefh, and of courfe, to make it more indigestible. " Smoakcd-hams are a ftrong kind of meat, and rather fit for a rel- ifh than for diet. It is the quality of all Salted meat' that the fibres become rigid, and therefore more difficult of digeftion; and when to this is added finoaking, the heat of the chimney occafions the fait to concentrate, and the fat between the mufcles to become rancid. " Bacon is alfo of an indigestible quality, and is apt to turn ran- cid on weak Stomachs. " The flefh of goats is hard and indigestible; but that of kids is tender, as wpll as delicious, and affords good nourifhment. " Venifm, or the flefh of deer, and that of hares, is of a nourifh- ing quality, but is liable to one inconvenience; which is, that though much difpofed to putrefcency of itfelf, it muft be kept for a little time before it becomes tender. " The blood of animals is ufed as aliment by the common peo- ple : but they could not long fubfift upon it unlefs mixedwiti; oat- meal, &c. for it is not foluble alone by the digestive powers of the hu- man ftomach, and therefore cannot be nourifhing. " Milk is of very different confiftence in different animals; bur that of cows being the kind ufed in diet, is at prefent the object ot"; our attention. Milk, where it agrees with the ftomach, affords e-V cellent nourifhment for thofe who are weak, and cannot digeft other- aliments. Though an animal production, it does not readily t>e* 7<S OF ALIMENT. come putrid, as being poffefled of the properties of vegetable ali- ment ; but it is apt to become four on the ftomach, and thence to produce flatulence, the heart-burn, or gripes, and, in fome constitu- tions, a loofenefs. The beft milk is from a cow at three or four years of age, about two months after producing a calf. It is lighter, but more watery, than the milk of Sheep and goats; while, on the other hand, it is more thick and heavy than the milk of affes and mares, which are the next in confiftence to human milk. " On account of the acid which is generated after digeftion, milk coagulates, in all Stomachs; but the cafeous or cheefy part is again difiolved by the digeftive juices, and rendered firJbr the pur- pofe of nutrition. It is however, improper to eat ado1 fubftances with milk, as thefe would tend to prevent the due digeftion of it. " Cream is very nourishing, but on account of its fatnefs is dif- ficult to be digefted in weak Stomachs. Violent exercife, after eat- ing it, will in a little time convert it into butter. " Some writers inveigh againft the ufe of Butter as univerfally pernicious; but they might with equal reafon condemn all vegeta- ble oils, which form a considerable part of diet in the fouthern cli- mates, and feem to have been beneficently intended by nature for that purpofe. Butter, like every other oily fubftance,has doubtlefs a relaxing quality, and, if long retained in the ftomach, is liable to be- come rancid ; but, if eaten in moderation, it will not produce thofe effects in any hurtful degree. It is, however, improper in bilious* constitutions. The worft confequence produced by butter, when eaten with bread, is, that it obstructs the difcharge of the faliva in the act of maftication or chewing; by which means the food is not fo readily digefted. To obviate this effect, it would be a commen- dable practice at breakfaft, firft to eat fome dry bread, and chew it well, till the Salivary glands were exhausted, and afterwards to eat it with butter. By thefe me:ins fuch a quantity of faliva might be carried into the ftomach as would be Sufficient for the purpofe of digeftion. " Cheefe is Iikewife reprobated by many as extremely unwhole. fome. It is doubtlefs not eafy of digeftion; and, when eaten in a great quantity, may load the ftomach; but, if taken fparingly, its tenacity may be diffolved by the digeftive juices, and it may yield a wholefome, though not a very nourifhing chyle. Tpafted cheefe is agreeable to moft palates, but is rendered more indigestible by that procefs. " The flefh of Birds differs in quality according to the food on which they live. Such as feed on grain and berries afford, in gen- eral, good nourifhment, if we except geefe and ducks, which are hard of <ligeftion. A young hen or chicken is tender and delicate food, and extremely well adapted when the digeftive powers are weak. jut of all tame fowls the capon is the moft nutritious. " Turkies, as well as Guinea or India fowls, afford a Substantial aliment, but are not fo eafy of digeftion as the common domeftic fowls. In all birds thofe parts are the moft firm which are moft ex- ercifed: in the fmall birds, therefore, the wings, and in the large? kinds, the legs, are commonly the moft difficult of digeftion. OP ALIMENT. 77 " The flefh of wild birds, in general, though more eafily digeft- ed, is lefs nourishing than that of quadrupeds, as being more dry, on account of their almoft conftant exercife. Thofe birds are not wholefome which fubfift upon worms, infects, and fifties. *' Eggs- in tne kft clafe of teiTeftrial animal food we may rank the eggs of birds, which are a fimple and a wholefome aliment. ThoSe of the turkey are fuperior in all the qualifications of food. The white of eggs is diffolved in a warm temperature, but by much heat it is rendered tough and hard. The yolk contains much oil, and is highly nourishing, but has a ftrong tendency to putrefaction; on which account eggs are improper for people of weak Stomachs* efpecially when they are not quite frefh. Eggs hard boiled or fried are difficult of digeftion, and are rendered ftill more indigestible by the addition of butter. All eggs require a fufficient quantity of fait, to promote their folution in the ftomach. *;' Fifb, though fome of them be light, and eafy of digeftion, af- ford lefs nourifhment than vegetables, or the flefh of quadrupeds, and are of all animal tribes the moft difpofed to putrefaction. Salt- water fifh are, in general, the beft; but when Salted, though lefs difpofed to putrefcency, they become more difficult of digeftion. Whitings and flounders are the moft eafily digefted. Acid Sauces and pickles, by refifting putrefaction, are a proper addition to fifh, both as they retard putrefcency, and correct the relaxing tendency of butter, fo generally ufed with this kind of aliment. "* Oyflers are eaten both raw and dreffed ; but in the former ftate they are preferable; becaufe heat diflipates considerably their nutritious parts, as well as the falt-water, which promotes their* di- geftion in the ftomach; if not eaten very fparingly, they generally prove laxative. " Mufcles are far inferior to oyfters, both in point of digeftion and nutriment. Sea mufcles are by fome fuppofed to be ot a poi- fonous nature; but though this opinion is not much countenanced by experience, the fafeft way is to eat them with vinegar, or fome Other vegetable acid. " Bread. At the head of the vegetable clafs Stands bread, that article of diet, which, from general ufe, has received the name of theflaff of life. Wheat is the grain chiefly ufed for the purpofe in this country, and is among the moft nutritive of 'ill the farinaceous kinds, as it contains a great deal of mucilage. Bread is very prcperly eat- en witfet animal food, to correct the difpofition to pu'rtfcency ; but is moft expedient with Such articles in diet as contain much nourish- ment in a fmall bulk, becaufe it then ferves to give the ftomach a proper degree of expanficn. But as it produces a flimy chyle, and difpofes to coftivenefs, it ought not to be eaten in a large quantity. To render bread eafy of digeftion, it ought to be well fermented and baked; and it never Should be ufed till it has Stood twenty-four hours after being taken out of the oven, otherwise it is apt to occa- fion various'complaints in thefe Who have weak bowels •, fuch as flatulence, the heart-burn, watchfulnefs, and the like. The cuftom of eating butter with bread hot from the oven is compatible ©nly with ftrong digeftive powers. 78 OF ALIMENT. " Paflry, efpecially when hot, has all the difadvantages of hot bread and butter; and even buttered toaft, though the bread is Stale, is Scarcely inferior in its effects on a weak ftomach. Dry toaft without butter is by far the wholefomeft breakfaft. " Bread made of Rye is apt to four on the ftomach, and to ex. cite heart-burn in certain constitutions—is of a laxative nature, and, therefore, better Suited to coftive habits, either alone, or mix- ed with wheat: But on account of its difpofition to acefcency, f r- Hientation, and flatulency, may not be fo well adapted for perfons of choleric temperaments, and thofe afflicted wito dyfpeptic, hypo- chondriac, and hyfteric Symptoms: yet, it is the beft to prevent or cure the Scurvy. " That made of Indian Com appears tp agree well with moft people who like it; and when mixed with wheat or rye, or both, it makes them palatable, a/id keeps moift a considerable time. " Buckwheat being Somewhat liable to an acefcent fermentation in the ftomach, does not agree well with all conftitutions. The grain fhould, previous to being ground, be freed from the duft and grit. It is fuppofed that its ufe occafions itchings and cutaneous eruptions—and conftandy ufed, is not thought fo wholefome as other bread. " Oats, when deprived of the hufk, and particularly barley, when properly prepared, are each of them fattening, and afford wholefome and cooling nourifhment. Rice Iikewife contains a nu- tritious mucilpge, and is lefs ufed in this country than it deferves, both on account of its wholefbmenefs and economical utility. The notion of its being hurtful to the fight is a vulgar error. In fome conftitutions if tends to make them coftive; but this feems to be owing chiefly to flatulence, and may be corrected by the addition of fome fpice, fuch as caraway, anife feed, and the like. " Potatoes are an agreeable and wh.lefome food, and yield as much nourishment as any of tlv roots ufed in diet. The farina- ceous or mealy kind is in general the moft eafy of digeftion; and they are much improved by beir.g roafted. " Green peafe and Turkey beans, boiled in their fr-fh State, are .both agreeable to the tafte, and wholefome; being neither near So flatulent, nor difficult of digeftion, as in their ripe ftate; in which they referable the other leguminous vegetables. French beans pof- frfs much the f :me qualities, but yield a more watery juice, and ha e a greater diSpofition to produce flatulence. The leguminous vegetables in general ought to be eaten with Some Spice. . •* Sallads, being eaten raw, require good digeftive powers, ef- pecially thofe of the cooling kind; and the addition of oil and vin- egar, though qualified with muft rd, hardly renders the free ufe of » them confiftent with the weak ftomach. " Soinage affords a foft lubricating aliment, but contains little nourifhment. In weak Stomachs it is apt to produce acidity, and frequently a loofenefs. To obviate thefe eff fo, it ought always to be well beaten, and but little butter mixed with it. " Afparagus is a nourishing article in diet, and promotes urine; but, in common with the vegetable clafs, difpofes a little to flatulence. UF-, ALIMENT* 7> i: Artichokes referable afparagus in their qualities, but feem to be more nutritive and lefs diuretic. " White cabbage is one of the moft confpicuous plants in the garden. It does not afford much nourifhment, but is an agreeable addition to animal food, and not quite fo flatulent as the common greens. It is Iikewife diuretic, and fomeWhat laxative. Cabbage has a Stronger tendency to putrefaction than moft other vegetable Substances ; and, during their putrefying ftate, Sends fcnh an often- five fmell, much refembling that of putrefying animal bodies. So far, however, from promoting a putrid difpofition 'in the human body, it is, on the contrary, a "Wholefome aliment in the true pu- . aid fcurvy. " Turnips are a nu'ritious article of vegetable food, but not very eafy of digeftion, and are flatulent;. This effect is, in a great mea- fure, obviated by preffing the water out of them before they are eaten. " Carrots contain a confid--ravle quantity of nutritious juice, but are am ng the moft flatulent of vegetable productions. " Parfnips are more nourifhing and lefs flatulent than carrots, which they alfo exceed in the fweetnefs of their mucilage. By boil- ing them in two d fferent waters, they are rendered lefs flatulent. but their other qualities are thereby diminished in proportion. " Parflcy is of a Stimulating and aromatic nature, \veil calcula- ted to make agreeable fauces. It is alfo a gentle diuretic, but pre- ferable in all its qualities when boiled. " Celery affords a root both wholefome and fragrant, but is dif- ficult of digestion in its raw ftate. \t gives an agreeable tafte to Soups, as well as renders them diuretic. " Onions, garlic, and fhallots, are all of a flimulating nature, by which they afiift digeftion, diffolve Slimy humours, and" expel flatu- lency. They are, however, moft fiiitabie to perfons of a c®ld and phlegmatic constitution. " Radfhes of all kinds, particularly the horfe-ra^ifh, ?gree with the three preceding articles in powerfully diflblving Slimy humours. They excite the difcharge of air lodged m the inteftines; but this proceeds from the expulfioo of air contained in themfelves. " Apples are a wholefome vegetable aliment, and ia irony cafes medicinal, particularly in dileaSes of the breaft and complaints ari- sing from phlegm. But, in g-ner. 1, tv ey agree beft with the ftom- ach when eatc i either roafted or boiled- The more aromatic kinds of apples are the fittelt for eating raw. " Pears referable much in their effects the Sweet kind of apples, but have more of a iaxadve quality, and a greater tendency to.flau ulence. " Cherries are, in general, a wholefome fruit, when they agree with the ftomach, and they are bentfici?.l in many difeafes, efpecially thofe of the putrid kind. " Plumbs are nourifhing, and have befides an attenuating, as well as a laxative quality; but are apt to produce flatulence. If eaten frefh, and before ti ey are quite ripe, efpecially in large quantities^ they occafion cholics and other complaiots-of the bowels. 8p OF ALIMENT. " Peaches are not of a very nourifhing quality, but they abound in juice, and are ferviceable in billions complaiots. " Apricots are more pulpy than peaches, but are apt to ferment and produce acidities in weak ftomachs. Where they do not difa- gree they are cooling, and tend Iikewife to correct a tendency to putrefcency. " Goofeberries, as well as carrants? when ripe, are fimilar in their qualities to cherries, and, when ufed in a green ftate, they are agree- ably cooling. " Strawberries are an agreeable, cooling aliment, and are ac- counted good againft the gravel. " Cucumbers are cooling, and agreeable to the palate in hot weather; but to prevent them from proving hurtful to the ftom- ach the juice ught to be fqueezed out after they are diced, and vinegar, pepper, and fait) afterwards added. " Ted. By Some the ufe of this exotic is condemned in terms the moft vehement and unqualified, while others have either afferted its innocence, or gone fo far as to alcrbe to it falubricus and even extraordinary virtues. The truth feems to lie between thefe ex- tremes : there is however an effential difference in the effects of green tea and of black» or bohea; the former of which is much jnore apt to affect the nerves of the ftomach than the latter, efpec- ially wheu drunk without cream and Iikewife without bread and butter. That when taken in alarge quantity, or at a later hour than "tifual, it often produces watchfulnefs, is a point which cannot be de* niei ; but if ufed in moderation, and accompanied with the addi- tion juft now mentioned, it does not fenfibly difcover any hurtful effects, but greatly relieves an oppreffion of the ftomach, and abates a pain of the head, It ought always to be made of a moderate de- gree of ftrength; for, if too weak it certainly relaxes the ftomach.. As it has an aftringent taite, which feems not very confiftent with a relaxing power, there is ground for aScribing this effect not So much to the herb itSelf, as to the hot water, whitf not being im- pregnated with a fufficient quantity of tea to correct its own emrl- sent tendency, produces a relaxation unjuftly imputed to fome nox- 5ous quality of the plant, But tea, like every other commodity, as liable to damage, and when this happens, it may produce effects not neceffarily connected with its original qualities. " Coffee. It is allowed that coffee promotes digeftion, and ex- hilarates the animal fpirits; befides which, various other qualities are afcribed to it, fuch as dispelling flatulency, removing dizzineSs of the head, attenuating vifcid humours, incfeafing the circulation of the blood, and confequently perfpiration; but if drank too Strong it affects the nerves, occafions watchfulnefs, and tremor of the hands, though in fome phlegmatic conftitutions it is apt to produce Sleep. Indeed it is to perfons of that habit that coffee is well accommoda- ted ; for to people of a thin and dry habit of body it feerhs to be injurious. Turkey coffee is greatly preferable in flavor to that of the Weft-Indies. Drunk only in the quantity of one difh after dinner to promote digeftion, it anfwers beft without either fugar or milk; but if taken at otteer times it fhould have both, or in place of OF AIR. 81 the latter rather cream, which not only improves the beverage but tends to mitigate the effect of coffee upon the nerves. "■Chocolate is a nutritive and wholefome compofition if taken io fmall quantity, and not repeated too often; but is generally hurtful to the ftomach of thofe with whom a vegetable diet disa- grees. By the addition of vanilla and other ingredients it is made too heating, and fo much affects particular conftitutions as to ex- cite nervous Symptoms, efpecially complaints of the head. CHAP. IV. OF AIR. Lj NWHOLESOME air is a very common caufe of dif- eafes. Few are aware of the danger arifing from it. People gen- erally pay fome attention to what they eat or drink, but Seldom re- gard what goes into the lungs, though the latter proves often more Suddenly fatal than the former. Air, as well as water, takes up parts of moft bodies with which it comes in contact, and is often fo replenished with thofe of a noxious quality, as to occafion immediate death. But fuch viol- ent effects feldom happen, as people are generally on their guard againft them. The lefs perceptible influences of bad air prove more generally hurtful to mankind; we Shall therefore endeavor to point out fome of thefe, and to fhew whence the danger chiefly arifes. Air may become noxious many ways. Whatever greatly al- ters its degree of heat, cold, moifture, &c. renders it unwholefome: for example, that which is too hot diflipates the watery parts of the blood, exalts the bile, and renders the.whole humors adult and thick. Hence proceed billious and inflammatory fevers, cholera morbus, &c. very cold air obstructs the perfpiration, conftringes the Solids, and condenfes the fluids. It occasions rheumatifms, coughs, aid catarrhs, with other difeafes of the throat and breaft. Air that is too moift deftroys the elasticity or fpring of the folid?, induces phlegmatic or lax conftitutions, and difpofes the body to agues, or intermitting fevers, dropfies, &c. Wherever great numbers of people are crowded into one place, if the air has not a free circulation, it foon becomes unwhole- fome. Hence it is that delicate perfons are fo apt to turn fick or faint in^crowded churches, affemblies, or any place where the air is injured by breathing, fires, candles, or the like. In great cities fo many things tend to contaminate the air, that it is no wonder it proves So fatal to the inhabitants. The air in cities is not only breathed repeatedly over, but is Iikewife loaded with fuiphur, fmoke, and other exhalations, befides the vapors con- tinually arifing from innumerable putrid fubftances, as dunghills, flaughter houfes, &c. All poffible care fhould be taken to keep the ftreets of large towns open and wide, that the air may have a free current through them. They ought Iikewife to be kept very clean. Nothing tends more to pollute and contaminate the air of a city than dirty ftreets. g2 OF AIR. It is very common in this country to have church-yards in the middle of populous cities. Whether this be the effect of ancient fuperftition, or owing to the increafe of Such towns, is a matter of no confequence. Whatever gave rile to this cuftom, it is a tad one. It is habit alone which reconciles us to thefe things; by means of which the moft ridiculous, nay pernicious cuftoms, often become facred. Certain it is, that thoufands of putrid carcafies, fo near the furface of the eaith, in a place where the air :s confia- ed, cannot fail to taint it; and that fuch air, when breathed into the lungs, muft occafion difeafes.* Burying within churches is a practice ftill more deteftable. The air in churches is Seldom good, and the effluvia from putrid carcaf- es muft render it ftill worfe. Churches are commonly old build- ings with arched roofs. They are feldom open above once a w ek, are never ventilated by fires nor open windows, and rarely kept clean. This occafions that damp, mufty, unwholefome Smell which one feels upon entering a church, and renders it a very mifafe place for the weak and valetudinary. Thefe inconvenienc-s might, in a great meafure, be obviated, by prohibiting all perfons from burying within churches, by keeping them clean, and permitting a Stream of frefh air to pafs frequently through them, by opening oppofite doors and windows.f Wherever air Stagnates long, it becomes unwholefome. Hence the unhappy perfons confined in jails not only contract malignant fevers themfelves, but often communicate them to others. Nor arc many of the holes, for we cannot call them houfes, poffeffed by the poor in great towns, much better than jails. Thefe low dirty habi- tations are the very lurking places of bad air and contagious dif- eafes. Such as live in them feldom enjoy good health; and their children commonly die young. In the choice of a houle, thofe who have it in their power ought always to pay the greateft attention to open free air. The various methods which luxury has invented to make houfes clofe and warm, contribute not a little to render tnem unwholefome. No houfe can be wholefome unlefs the air has a free paffage through it. For which reafon houfes ought da ly to be ventilated by open- ing oppofite windows, and admitting a current of frefh air into ev- ery room. Beds, inftead of being made up as foon as people rife out of them, ought to be turned down, and expofed to the frefh air from the open windows through the day. This wojild expel any noxious vapour, and could not fail to promote the health of the Inhabitants. In hofpitals, jails, Ships, Sec. where that cannot be conveniently done, ventilators Should be ufed. The method of expelling foiil and introducing frefh air, by means of ventilators, is a moft faluta- ry invention, and is indeed the moft ufeful of all our modern medi- cal improvements. It is capable cf univerfal application, and is * J" mo«eaft«rn countries ><■ *»» euftomtry to bury the dead at fome diftance from wr town. As this praft-ce obtained among the Jews, the Greeks and ali« ?h,. In all places, where numbers «-f people are crowded togeth- er, ventilation becomes absolutely neceffary. Air which Stagnates in muv-s, wells, cellars, &c. is extremely noxiou*. That kind of air is to be avoided as the moft deadly poi- fon. It often kills almoft as quickly as lightning. For this reafon, people Should be very cautious in ope; <ing-cellars that have been long Shut, or going down into deep wells or pits, efpecially if they have been kept clofe covered.* Many people who have fplendid houfes, chufe to Sleep in fmali apartments. This conduct is very imprudent. A bed-chamber ought always to be well a;red ; as it is generally occupied in the night only, when all doors and windows are Shut. If a fire be kept in it, the danger fr m a fmall room becomes ftill greater. Numbers have be,jn Stifled when afleep by a fire in a Small apartmer t, which is al- ways hurtful. Thofe who are obliged, on account of bufinefs, to fpend the day in clofe towns, ought if poffible, to fleep in the country. Breath- ing free air in the night will, in fome meafure, make up for the want of it through the day. This practice would have a greater ef- fect in preServing the "health of citizens than is commonly imagined. Delicate p rf ns ought, as much as poffible, to avoid the air of great towns. It is peculiarly hurtful to the afthmatic and confump- tive. Such perfons Should avoid cities as they would the plague. The hypochondriac are Iikewife much hurt by it. 1 have often feen perfons Co much afflicted with this malady while in town, that it Seemed impoffible for them to live, who, upon being removed to the country, were immediately relieved. The fame obfervation holds with'regard to nervous and hyfleric women. Many people, indeed, have it not in their power to change their Situation in queft of better air. All we can fay to fuch perfons is, that they Should go as often abroad into the oppn air as they can, that'they fhould admit frefh air frequently into their houfes, and take core to keep them very clean. It was neceffary in former times, for fafety-, to furround cities, colleges, and even fingle houfes, with high walls. Thefe, by ob- structing the free current of "air, never fad to render fuch ploce-: damp and unwholefome. As fuch walls are now, in moft parts of this country, become ufelefs, they ought to be pulled down, and ev- ery method laken to admit a free pafiage to the air. Proper atten- tion to Ah* and Cleanlinefs would tend more to pr^ferve the health of mankind, than all the prescriptions of the faculty Surrounding houfes t )o clofely with planting or thick woods, like- wife tends to render the air unwholefome. Wood not only ob- structs the free current of-the air, but fends forth great quantities of moift exhalations, which render it cOnftantly damp. Wood i very agreeable at a prop?r diftance from a ho ufe, but Should never be planted too near it, efpecially in a flat country. , Many of the * Wc "have dai'y accounts of nerfons who lofe their lives by going down into d?ep wclli and other places wrier.'the air ftagnates ; all thefeaccidents might be prevented by only let- ting down a lighted candle before them, and flopping when they perceive ir en °-'-: ?~r- '^■•' precaution, fimplc as it i», is filer, ufed. #4 OF EXERCIS&* gentlemen's feats in England are rendered very unwholefome from the great quantity of wood which furrounds them. Houfes Situated in low marfhy countries, or near large lakes ot Stagnating water are Iikewife unwholefome. Waters which Stagnate notonly render the air damp but load it with putrid exhalations, which produce the moft dangerous and fatal difeafes. Thofe who are obliged to inhabit marShy countries, ought to make choice of the dryelt fituations they can find, to live generoufly, and to pay the ftricteft regard to cleanlineis...... If freSh air be neceffary for thofe in health, it is Still more fo for the fick, who often loSe their lives for want of it. The notion that fick people muft be kept very hot, is fo common, that one can hard- ly enter a chamber where a patient lies,without being ready to faint, by reafon of the hot fuffocating fraell. How this muft affect the fick, any one may judge. No medicine is fo beneficial to the fick as frefh air. It is the moft reviving of all cordials, if it be admin* iftered with prudence. We are not however, to throw open doors and windows at random upon the fick. Frefh air is to be let into the chamber gradually, and, if poffible, by opening the windows of fome other apartment. The air of a fick perfon's chamber may be greatly frefhened, and the patient much revived, by fprinkling the floor, bed, &c. fre* qumtly with vinegar, juice of lemon, or any other ftrong vegeta- ble acid. In places where numbers of fick are crowded into the fame houfe, or, which is often the cafe, into the fame apartment, the frequent admiffion of frefh air becomes absolutely neceffary. Infirmaries, hofpitals, &c. are often rendered fo noxious, for want of proper ventilation, that the fick run more hazard from them than from the difeafe. This is particularly the cafe when putrid fevers, dyfenta- ries, and other infectious difeafes prevail. Phyficians, furgeons, and others who attend hofpitals, ought, for their own Safety, to take care that they be properly ventilated. Such perfons as are obliged to fpend the moft of their time araongft the fick, run great hazard of being themfelves infected when the air is bad. All hofpitals, and places of re:eption for the fick, ought to have an open fituation, at fome distance from any great town, and fuch patients as labour under any infectious difeafe ought nev- er to be Suffered to come near the reft.* CHAP. V. OF EXERCISE. JVAaNY people look upon the neceffity man is under of earning his bread by labor, as a curfe. Be this as it may, it is evi. dent from, the Structure of the body, that exercife is not lefs necef- fary than food for the prefervation'of health: thofe whom poverty * A year feldom paffes that we do not hear of fome hofpital phyfician or furgeon having ,'oft his life by an hofpital fever, caught from his patients. For this they have themfeW" alone to brame. Their patients are either in an improper fituation, or the* are too caieltf' wirb regard to their own conduft, ' OF EXERCISE, *s obliges to labor for daily bread, are not only the moft healthy, but generally the moft happy part of mankind. Induftry feldom rails to place them above want, and activity ferves them inftead of phy* fie. This is peculiarly the cafe with thofe who live by the culture of the ground. The great increafe of inhabitants in infant colonies, and the longevity of fuch as follow agriculture, every where evi- dently prove it to be the moft healthy as well as the moft ufeiui employment. The love of activity Shews itfelf very early in man. So ftrong is this principle, that a healthy youth cannot be restrained from ex- ercife, even by the fear of punifhment. Our love of motion isfure- ly a ftrong proof of its utility. Nature implants no difpofition in vain. It Seems to be a catholic law throughout the whole animal creation, that no creature, without exercife, Should enjoy health, or be able to find fubfiftence. Every creature, except man, takes as much of it as is neceffary. He alone, and fuch animals as are under his direction, deviate from this original law, and they Suffer accordingly. ' Inactivity never fails to induce an univerfal relaxation of the folids, which difpofes the body to innumerable difeafes. When the folids are relaxed, neither the digeftion nor any of the fecretions can be duly performed. In this cafe the worft confequences muft enfue. Flow can perfons who loll all day in eafy chairs, and fleep all night on beds of down, fail to be relaxed ? Nor do fuch great- ly mend the matter, who never Stir abroad but in a coach, fedan, or fuch like. Thefe elegant pieces of luxury are become fo common, that the inhabitants of great towns feem to be in fome danger or lofing the ufe of their limbs altogether. It is now below any one to walk, who can afford to be carried. How ridiculous would it fsem, to a perfon unacquainted with modern luxury, to behold the young and healthy fwinging alongonthefhouldersof their feHow-creatures! or to fee a fat carcafe, over-run with difeafes occafioned by inactiv- ity, dragged through the ftreets by half a dozen horfes.* Glandular obftru£tions, now f j common, generally proceed from inactivity. Thefe are the moft obftinate maladies. So long as the liver, kidnies, and other glands, duly perform their fun&ions, health is feldom impaired; but when they fail, nothing can reftore it. Exercife is almoft the only cure we know for glandular ob- structions ; indeed it does not always fucceed as a remedy: but there is reafon to believe that it would feldom fail to prevent thefe complaints, were it ufed in due time. One thing is certain, that amongft thofe who take fufficient exercife, glandular difeafes are v-ry little known; whereas the indolent and inactive are ieldom free from them. . *.«,.„ vr^\. Weak nerves are the conftant companions ot inactivity. No#i- Uig but exercife and open air can brace and Strengthen the nerves, 1hers to comply with a ridjculoui fafh'rcu. Q6 OF EXERCISE. or prevent the endlefs train df difeafes which proceed from a relaxed ftate of thefe organs. We feldom hear the active or laborious com* plain of nervous difeafes; thefe are refervedfor the fonsof eafe.and affluence. Many have been completely cured of thefe diforders by being reduced, from a ftate of opulence, to labor for their daily bread. This plainly points out the fources from whence nervous difeafes flow, and the means by which they may b- prevented. , It is absolutely impoffible to enjoy health, where the perfpira- tion is not duly carried on: but that can never be the cafe where exercife is neglected. When the matter which ought to be thrown , off by perfpiration is retained in the body, it vitiates the humours, and occafions the gout, fevers, rheumatifm, &c. Exercife alone would prevent many of thofe difeafes which cannot be cured, and would remove others where medicine proves ineffectual. . A late author,* in his excellent treatife on health, fays that the weak and valetudinary ought to make exercife a part of their reli- gion. We would recommend this, not only to the weak and vale- tudinary, but to all whom bufn efs does not oblige to take fufficint exercife, as fedentary artificers,! fhcpkeppers, Studious per- fons, &c. Such ought to ufe exercife as regularly as they take food. : This might generally be done without any interruption to bufinefs or real lofs of time. No piece of indolence hurts the health more than the modem .. cuftom of lying a-bed too long in a morning. This is the general practice in great towns. The inhabitants of cities feldom rife be- Jk fore eight or nine o'clock ; but the morning is undoubtedly the "beft time for exercife, wfrle the ftomach is empty, and the body re- frefhed with Sleep. Befides the morning air braces and Strength- ens the nerves, and in fome meafure anfwers'the purpofe of a cold bath. Let any one who has been accutdomed to lie a-bed till eight or nine o'clock, rife by fix or feven, fpend a couple of hours in walking, riding, or any active diversion without doors, and he will find his fpirits cheerful and Serene thrown the day, his appetite keen, and his body braced and Strengthened. Cuftom foon renders early rifipg agreeable, and nothing contributes more to the preferv- ation of health. The inactive are continually complaining of pains cf the fto- mach, flatulencies, indigeftiors, &c. Thefe complaints, which pave the way to many others, are nof to be removed by medicines. They can only be cured by a vfgorous courfe of exercife, to which indeed they feldom fail to yield. Exercife, if poffible, ought always to be taken in the open air, • Cbeyne. f Sedentary occupations ought chiefly to be followed by women. They bear confine- Bent much better than men, and are fitter for every kind of bufinefs which does not require sruch ftrength. It is ridiculous enough to fee a lufty fellow making pins, needles, or watch \tteels, while many of the laborious parts of hulbandry are carried on by the other fex. The fsft is, we want men for laborious employments, while one half of the other fex are rendered uelefsfor want of occupations fu'ted to their ftrength, &c. Were girls bred to mechanical enployments, we mould not fee fuch numbers of them proftitute themfelves for bread, not fiid fueh a want of men for the important purpofesof navieation, agriculture tec. An «m- iiznt filk manufacturer told me, that he found women anfwe- better for that bufinefs than m:n ; and that he had lately taken a ereat tait-y girls apprentice* at filh weavers.
