Vomiting jfultf, ' ■H2 OF THE HOOPING COUGH, 6c. ever be ftrong ; gentle vomits frequently repeated, are Doth left dangerous, and more beneficial than ftrong ones. The body ought to be kept gently open. The belt medicines for this purpofe are rhubarb and its preparations, as the fyrup, tinaure, &c. Of thefe a tea-fpoonful or two may be given to an infant twice or thrice a-day, as there is occafion. To fuch as are farther advanced, the dofe muft be proportionally mcreafed, and repeated till it has the defired effect. .Thofe who cannot be brought to take the bi-ter tinaure, may have an infufion of fenna and prunes, fweetened with mmna, coarfe fugar, or honey ; or a few grains of rhubarb mixed with a tea-fpoonful or two of fyrup, ct currant jelly, fo as to difguife the tafte. Moft children are fond of fyrups and jellies, and feldom refufe even a difagreeable medi- cine when mixed wih them. Many people Del eve that oily, peaoral, and balfamic medi- cines poffefs wonderful virtues for the cure of the chin-cough, and accordingly exhibit them plentifully to patients of every age aod conftitution, without confi'ering that every thing of this nature muft load the ftomach, hurt the digeftion, and of courle aggravate the diforder.* The millepedes, or wood-lice, are greatly recommended for the cure of a chin-cough. Thofe who chufe to make ufe of thrfe in- fects, may infufe two ounces of them bruifed in a pint of fmall white-wine for one night. Afterwards the liquor may be ftraiied through a cloth, and a table-lpoonful of it given to the pitient three or four times a-day. Opiates are fometimes neceffaly to allay the violence of the cough. For this purpofe a little of the fyrup of poppies, or five, fix, or feven drops of laudanum,according to the ag-. of the p? tient may be taken in a cup of hyffop or penny-royal tea, and repeated occafionally.t The garlic ointment is a well-known remedy in Ncrth-Britain for the chin-cough. It is made, by beating in a mortar, garlic, with an equal quantity of hog's lard. With this the foles of the feet may be rubbed twice or thrice a-day ; but the belt method is to fpread it upon a r^g, and apply it in the form of a plafter. It fhould be renewed every night and morning at leaft, as the garlic foon lofes its virtue. This is an exceeding good medicine both in the chin-cough,"! and in moft other coughs of an obftinate nature. It ought not however to be ufed when the patient is very hot or feverifh, left it fhould increafe thefe fymptoms. The feet fhould be bathed once every two or three days in lukewarm water ; and a Burgundy-pitch plafter kept constantly * Dr. Duplanil fay*, he has feen many good effects from the kermea mineral in thi« complaint, the cough being frequently alleviated even by the firft dofe. The dofe for a chili of one irearoy, is a quarter of a grain dilTolvsd in a cup of any liquid, repeated two or three times a-day. For a child of two years, the dofe is half a grain ; and the quantity muft be thus mcreafed in proportion to the age of the patient. + Some recommend theextrad of hemlock as an eatraordinary remedy in the hooping- cough } but fo far as I have been able to obferve, U is in no way fuperior lo opium, whicfi, Siforder? adminiftered» w'11 ofte» rd,evc fon" of ^ ™<>ft troublefome fymptoms of that •u fe;S Soft $£ fcSIKara ™Mi » tbiak *■* «** ■*■■**• *» INFLAMMATION OF THE STOMACH, 6c. 2i3 between the fhoulders. But when the difeafe proVes very violent it will be neceflary, inftead of it, to apply a bliftering-plafter, ana* to keep the part open for fome time with mue-ointment. When the difeafe is prolonged, and the patient is free from a tever, the Peruvian bark, and other bitters, are the moft proper medicines. The bark may either be taken in fubftance or in a decoaion or infufion, as is moft agreeable. For a child' ten fif- teen, or twenty grains, according, to the age of the patient, may be giyen three or four time9 a-day. For an adult, half a drachm or two fcruples will be proper. Some give the extraa of the bark with cantharides ; but to manage this requires confiderable atten- tion. It is more fafe to give a few grains of caftor along with the bark. A child of fix or (even years of age may taks feven or eight grains of caft< r, with fifteen grains of powdered bark, for a dofe. This may be made into a mixture with two Or three ounces of any fimple diftilled water, and a little fyrup, and taken three or four times a-day. CHAP. XXXII. * INFLAMMATION OF THE' STOMACH, AND OTHER VISCERA. jf\.LLinflammationsofthebowelsaredangerous,andrequire the moft fpeedy aififtance; as they frequently end in a fuppuration, and fometimt5s in a mortification, which Ulcer tain death. CAUSES.—An inflammationof the ftomach may proceed from any of the caufes which produce an inflammatory fever; as cold liquor drank while the body is warm, obftruaed perfpiration, or the fudden ftriking in of any eruption. It may Iikewife proceed from the acrimony of the bile, or from acrid and ftimulating fub- ftances taken into the ftomach; as ftrong vomits or purges, corro- five poifons, and fuch like. When the gout has been repelled from the extremities, either by cold or improper applications, it often oc- cafions an inflammation of the ftomach. Hard or indigeftible fub- ftances taken into the ftomach, as bones, the ftones of fruit, &c. may Iikewife have that effea. SYMPTOMS .—It is attended with a fixed pain and burning heat in the ftomach; gr£at reftleffnefs and anxiety; a fmall, quick, and hard pulfe; vomiting, or, at leaft, a naufea and ficknefs; ex- ceffive thirft; ccldnefs of the extremities ; difficulty of breathing; cold clammy fweats; and Ibmetimes convulfions and fainting fits. The ftomach is fwelled, and often feels hard to the touch. One of the moft certain figns of this difeafe, is the fenfe of pain, which the patient feels upon taking any kind of food or drink, efpecially if it be either too hot or too cold. When the patient vomits every thing he eats or drinks, is ex tremely reftlefs, has a hickup, with an intermitting pulfe, and fre- quent fainting fits, the danger is very great. REGIMEN.—All acrimonious, heating and irritating uxxl and drink, are carefully to be avoided* The woknefs of t>,e pa- 2H INFLAMMATION OF THE STOMACH, 6c. tient may deceive the byftanders, and induce them to give wines, fpirits, or other cordials; but thefe never fail to Increafe the dif- eafe, and often occafion fudden de?th. The inclination to vomit may Iikewife impofe on the attendants, and make them think a vomit neceffary; but that too is almoft certain death. The food muft be light, thin, cool, and eafy of digeftion. It muft be given in fmall quantities, and fhould neither be quite cold. nor too hot. This gruel made of barley or oatmeal, light toafted bread diffolved in boiling water, or very weak "chicken broth, is the moft proper. The drink fhould be clear whey, barley-water, water in which toafted bread has been boiled, or decoctions of emollient vegetables, as liquorice and marfh-mallow roots, far- faparilla, or the like. * MEDICINE.—Bleeding in tMs difeafe is abfolutely neceffary, and is almoft the only thing that can be depended on. When the difeafe proves obftinate, it will often be proper to repeat this ope- ration feveral times, nor muft the low ftate of the pulfe deter us ' from doing fo. ^ The pulfe indeed generally rifes upon bleeding, and. as ioifg as that is the cafe, the operation is fafe. Frequent fomentations with lukewarm waiter, or a dec^aioiiof emollient vegetables, are Iikewife ben^fica!. Flannel cloths dipped in thefe muft be applied to the regi*i of t< e ftomach, and remov- ed as they grow cool. They muft ne; ther be applied too warm, nor be fuffered to continue till thev become quite cold, as either of thefe extremes would aggravate the difeafe. The feet and legs ought Iikewife to be frequently bsthed in lukewarm water, and warm bricks or poultires may be applied^to the foles of the feet?, The warm bath, if it can be conveniently - ufed, will be of great fervice. In this, and nil other inflatrinvvio'-s of the bowels, an epifpaftic, or bliftering-plafter, applied over the part affeaed, is one of the beft remedies I know. I have often ufed it, and do not recollea one inftance wherein it did not give relief to the patient. The only internal medicines which we fhall venture to recom- mend in this difeafe, are mild clyfters. Thefe may be made of warm water, or thin water-gruel; and if the patient i« coftive, a little fweet oil, honey, or manna, may be added. Clyfters anfwer the purpofe of an internal fomentation, while thev keep the body" open, and at the fame time nourifh the patient, who is often in this difeafe una- ble to retain any food upon his ftomach. For thefe rearons tlw muft not be negleaed, as the patient's life may depend on them. INFLAMMATION OF THE INTESTINES. .T^1? j? °ne of the m°ff painful and dangerous difeafes that mankind is liable to. It generally proceeds from the frme caufes as. the inflammation of the ftomach ; to whic- nrv be added c ftive- nefs, worms, eating unripe fruits; or great quantities of nuts, drink- ing hard, windy malt liquors, as fble bo'tled beer cr ale, four wine Cyder, &c. It may hkewife be occafioned by a rupture, by fchirr- ous tumours of tne inteftines, cr by their cppcfite fides growing tc-gether. & ° INFLAMMATION OF THE INTESTINES* 2151 The inflammation of the inteftines is denominated Siacpaflion9 Enteritis, 6c. 1 according to the name of the parts affeaed. The treatment however is nearly the fame whatever part of the inteft- inal canal be the feat of the difeafe; we mail therefore omit thefe diftinaions, left they fhould perplex the reader. The fymptoms here are nearly the fame as in the foregoing dif- eafe ; only the pain, if poffible, is more acute, and is fituated low- er. The vomiting is Iikewife more violent, and fometimes even the excrements, together with the clyfters, are difcharged by the mouth. The patient is continually belching up wind, and has often an ob- struction of his urine. - While the pain fhifts, and the vomiting only returns at certain intervals, and while the clyfters pafs downwards, there is ground for hope;.but when the clyfters and faces are vomited, and the pa- tient is exceeding weak, with'a low fluttering pulfe, a pale coun- tenance, and a difagreeable or rankirg breath, there is great reafon to fear that the confequences will prove fatal. CI immy fweat, black foe id fto >h, with a fmall i termit ing pulfe, and a to'al ceffation of pain, are figns of a mortification already begun, and of an approach- ing death. REGIMEN.—The regimen in'"this difeafe* is in general the fame as in an inflammation of the ftomach. The patient muft be kept quiet, avoiding cold, and all violent paffions of the mind. His food ought to be very light, and given in fmall quantities; his drink •lireA and dikring, as clear whey, barley-water, and fuch like. MEDICINE.—Bleeding in this, as^ell as in the inflamma- tion of the ftomach, is of the greateft imfiortance. It fhould be performed as foon as tf e fympt ms appear, and muft be repeated accordingto the ftrength of the patient, and thefaolenee of the difeafe. A bliftering plafter is here Iikewife to be applied immediately over the part where the moft violent pain is. This not only relieves the pain of the bowels; but even clyfters and purgative medicines, which before had no effect, will operate when the bfifter begins to rife. , . « Fomentations and laxative clyfters are b-y no mean s to be omi t- ted. The patient's feet and legs fhould frequently be bathed in warm water; and clothes dipped into it applied to his belly. Blad- ders filled with warm water may Iikewife be applied to the region of the naval, and warm bricks, or bottles filled with warm water, to the foles of the feet. The clyfters may be made of barley-water or thin gruel with fait, and foftened with fweet. oil or frefh butter. Thefe may be adminiftered every two or three hours, or oftener, if the patient continues ccftive. If the difeafe does not vield to clyfters and fomentations, re- courfe muft be had to pretty ftrong purgatives: but as thefe, by irri- tating rhe bowels, often increafe their contraaion, and bv that means fmftrate their own indention, it will be neceffary to join them with opiat s, which by allaying the pain, and relaxing theSpas- modic contraaions of the guts, greatly affift the operation of pur- gatives »n this cafe. ... What anfwers the purpofe cf opening the body v*ry well, - 2i5 INFLAMMATION OF THE INTESTINES. a folution of the bitter puiging falts. Two ounces of thefe may be diffolved in an Englifh pint of warm water, or thin gruel, and a tea-fpooriful of it taken every half hour till it operates. At the fame time fifteen, twenty, or twenty-five drops of laudanum may be giv. en in a glafs of peppermint or fimple cinnamon-water, to appeafe the irritation, and prevent the vomiting, &c. Acids have often a very happy effea in ftaying the vomiting, and appeafing the o'her violent fymptoms of this difeafe. It will there- fore be of ufe to fharpen the patient's drink with cream of tartar, juice of lemon; or, when thefe cannot be obtained, with vinegar. But it often happens that no liquid whatever will ftay on the ftomach. In this cafe the patient muft take purging pills- I have generally found the following anfwer very well: T?ke jalhp in pow- der, and vitriolated tartar, of each half a drac m, opium one gram, Caftile foap as much* as will make the mafs fit for pills. Thefe muft be taken at one dofe, and if they do not operate in a few hours, the dofe may be repeated. If a ftool cannot be procured by any of the above means, it will be neceffary to immerfe the patient in warm water up to the breaft. I have often feen this fucceed when other means had been tried in vain. The patieht muft continue in the water as long as he can eafily bear it without fainting, and if one immerfion has not the defired effea, it may be repeated as foon as the patient's ftrength and fpirits are recruited.- It is more fafe for him to go frequently into the bath, than to continue too long at a time, and it is ofterf neceffary to repeat it feveml times before it has the defired effea. It has fometimes happened, after all other means of procuring a tool had been tried to no purpofe, that tlrs was brought about by immerfing the patient's lower extremities in cold water, or making him walk on a wet pavement, and dafhing his legs and thighs with the cold wat^r. This method, when others fail, at le?ft merits a trial. It is indeed attended with fome danger; but a doubtful rem- edy is better than none. In deiperate cafes it is common to give quickfilver. This may be gwen to the quantity of feveral ounces, or even a pound, but fhould not exceed that.* When there is reafon to fufpea a morti- fication of the guts, this medicine ought not to be tried. In that cafe it cannot cure the patient, and will only haftep his death. But when the obftruaion is occafioned by any caufe that can be remov- ed by force, quickfilver is not only a'proper medicine, but the beft that can be adminiftered, as it is the fitteft body we know for mak- ing its way through the inteftinal canal. If the difeafe proceeds fr m a rupture, the patient muft be laid with his head very low, and the inteftines returne * by gentle press- ure with the hand. If this, with fomentations and clyfters, fhould not fucceed, recourfe muft be had to a furgical operation, which may give the patient relief. •When quickfilw is given in two large quantities, it defeats its own intention, as it drags down the bottom or the ftomach, which prevents'its getting over the Pylorus, in '&«lei b/^moth? * hUnfi UP b? *•***> ,a °rdCr th" the *»■«**&« **' » VF THE COLIC. „ 217 Such as would avoid this excruciating and dangerous difeafe, muft take- care never to be too long without a ftool. Some who have died of it have had feveral pounds of hard dry.faces taken out of their guts. They fhould hkewife beware of eating too freely of four or unripe fruits, or drinking ftale windy liquors, fee. I have known it brought on by livi g too much on baked fruits, which are feldom good. It Iikewife proceeds frequently from cold caught by wet cloths, &c- but efpecially from wet feet. OF THE COLIC. THE colic has a great refemblance to the two preceding dif- eafes, both in its fymptoms and method of cure. It is generally attended with coftivenefs and acute pain of the bowels; and re- quires diluting diet, evacuations, fomentations, &c. Colics are vanoufly denominated according to their caufes, as the flatulent, the bilious, the hyfleric, the nervous, &C. As each -f thefe require a particular metnod of treatment, we fhalr point out their moft general fymptoms, and the means to be ufed for theit relief. The flatulent, or wind-colic, is generally occafioned by an in- difcreet ufe of.unripe fruits, meats of hard digeftion, windy veget- ables, fermenting liquors, and fuch like. It may Iikewife proceed from an obftruaed perfpiration, or catching cold. Delicate people, whofe digeftive powers are weak, are moft liable to this kind 01 colic. \ The flatulent colic may either affeifcthe ftomach or inteftines* It is attended with a painful ftretchingW the affeaed part. The patient feels a rum'.ling in his guts, and is generally relieved by a difcharge of wind, either upwards or downwar Js. The pain is fel- dom confined to any particular part, as the vapour wanders from one divifion of the bowels to another till it finds a vent. When the difeafe proceeds from windy liquor, green fruit, four herbs, or the like, the beft medicine on thefirit appearance of the fymptom is a dram of brandy, gin, or any good fpirits* The patient ftnuld Iikewife fit'with his feet upon a warm hearth-ftone* or apply, warm bricks to them; and warm cloths may be 3pplied to his ftomach and bowels. This is the only colic wherein ardent fpirits,. fpiceries, or any thing of a hot nature* maybe ventured upm. Nor indeed are they to be ufed here unlefs at'the very beginning, before any fymp* toms of inflammation appear. We have reafon to believe, that a cmc occafioned by wind or flatulent food might always be cured by fpirits and warm liquors, if they were taken immediately upon perceiving the firft uneafmefs; but when the pain has continued for a confiderable time, and there is reafon to fear an inflammation of the bowels is already begun, all hot things are to be av:ided as poi- fon, and the patient is to be treated in ihe fame manner as for the inflammation of the inteftines. Several kinds of food, as honey, eggs, Se<-. occaGon colics 10 fome particular conftitutions. I have generally foufid the beftmeih- od of cure for thefe, was to drink plentifully of fmall cUlutiDg li-jiwrs. ('5) » 2i8 OF THE dOlfC. as water-gruel, fmall poflet, water with toafted bread foaked in it, &c Colics which proceed from excefs and indigeftion generally cure themfelves by occafioning vomiting or pnrgmg. Ihefe diicharges are by no means to be flopped, but promoted by drinking plenti- fully of warm water, or weak poflet. When their violence is over, the patient may take a dofe of rhubarb, or any other gentle purge, to carry off the dregs of his debauch. Colics which are occafioned by wet feet, or caching cold, may generally be removed at the beginning, by bathing the feet and legs in warm water, and drinking fuch diluting liquors as will promote the perfpiration, as weak whey, or water-grue., with a fmall quan- tity of fpirits in it. Thofe flatulent colics, which prevail fo much among country neople, might generally be prevented were they careful to change their clothes when they get wet. They ought Iikewife to take a dram, or to drink fome warm liqmor after eating any kind of green trafh. We do not mean to recommend the praaice of dram drink- ing* but In th:s cafe ardent fpirits prove a real medicine, and indeed the beft that can be adminiftered. A glafs of good peppermint-wa-. ter will have neariy the fame eff^a as a glafs of brandy, and in fome cafes is rather to be preferred. The bilious colic is attended with very acute pains about the re- gion of the naval. The patient complains of great thirft, and is g"nerally coftive. He vomits a hot, bitter, yellow coloured bile, which being difcharged, feems to afford fome relief, but is quickly followed by the fame vicJJAjt pain as before. As the diftemper ad* vances, the propenfity to vrlmit fometimes increafes fo as to become, almoft continual, and the proper motion of the inteftines is fo far perverted, that there are all the fymptoms of impendir g iliac paifi®n. If the pitient be young and ftrong, and the pulfe full and fre- quent, it will be pr per to bleed, after which clyfters may be ad- miniftered. Clear whey or gruel, fharpened with the juice of lemon, Or cream of tartar, muft be drank freely. Small chicken broth, with a little manna diffolved in i% or a flight decoaion of tamarinds, are Iikewife very-proper, or any other thin, acid, opening liquor. Befides bleeding and plentiful dilution, it will be neceffary to foment the belly with cloths dipped in warm wa< er, and if 'this fhould not fucceed, the patient muft be immerfed up to the breaft in warm water. In the bilious colic the vomiting is often very difficult to re- ftraia. When {his happens, the patient mav drink a decoaion of toafted bread, or an infufion of garden mint in boiling water. Should thefe not have the defired effea, the faline draught, with a few drops of laudanum in if, may be given, and repeated according to the urgency of the fymptoms.. A fmall quantity of Venice treacle may be ipread m form of a cataplafm„and applied to the pit of the itomnch. Clyfters, with a proper quantity of Venice treacle or li- quid laudanum m them, may Iikewife be frequently adminiftered. Ltehv/lenc colic bears a great refemblance to the bilious. It *s attended with acute pains about the region of the ftomach, vomit- ing, &c What the patient vomits in this cafe is commonly of a UP Irtlt, COLIC. 2tg greetiifh colour. There is a great finking of the fpirits, with de- jeaion of m nd and diuvulty of breathing, which are the eharaa- criftic fympt.ms of this diforder. Sometimes it is accompanied with the jaundice, but this generally goes off of its own accord in a few days. In this colic all evacutions, as bleeding, purging, vomiting, &c. do hurt. Every thing that weakens the patient, or finks the fpirits, is to be avoided. If however the vomiting fhould prove violent, luke warm water, or fmall poffet, may be drank to cleanfe the ftom- ach. Afterwards the patient may take fifteen, twenty, or twenty- five drops of liquid laudanum io a glafs of cinnamon-water. This may be repeated every ten or tw4ve hours till the fymptoms abate. The patient may Iikewife take four or five of the foetid pills every fix hours, a id drink a cup of penny-r,>y?.l tea aft=r them. If afafcetida fhould prove difagreeable, which is fometimes the cafe, a tea-fpoonful of the tina&re of caftor in a cupof penny-royal *ea, or thirty or forty drops of the balfam of Peru dropped up- m a bit of lotf fugar, may be taken in its ftead. The anti-nyfteric plafter may alfo be ufed, which has often a good effea.* The nervous colic prevails among .miners, fmelters of leaq^ plumbers, the manufaaurers of white lead, Sec. It is very common in the cyder counties of England, and is fuppofed to be occafioned by the leaden veffels ufed in preparing that liquor. It is Iikewife a frequent dileafe in the Weft-Indies, where it is termed the dry belly-ache. . . ' . . No difeafe of the bowels is attended with more excruciating pain than this. Nor is it foon at art end. I h we known it con- tinue eight or ten days with very little interm ffion, the body all the while continuing bound in fpite of medicine, yet at length yield, and the patient recover.? It generally however, leaves the patient weak, and often ends in a palfy. _ The general treatment of this difeafe is fo nearly the lame with that of t^e iliac paffion, or inflammation of the guts, that we fhall not infit upon it. The body is to be opened by nrld purgatives given in fmall dofes, and frequently repeated, and their operation muft be aflifted by fof c oily clyfters, fomentations, &c. 1 he caitor- oil is reckoned peculiarly proper in this difeafe. It may both be mixed with the clyfters and given by the mouth.J m The Barbadoes tar is faid to be an efficacious medicine m this complaint. It may be taken to the quantity of two drachms three times a-day, or oftener if the ftomach will bear it. This tar, mix- ed with an equal quantity of ftrong rum, is Iikewife proper for rub- bing the fpine, in cafe any tingling or other fymptoms qt the palfy, are felt. When the tar cannot be obtained, the back may be rub- bed with ftrong fpirits, or a little oil of nutmegs, or of rofemary. If the patient remains weaic and languid atter this diieale, he * See Appendix, Anti-Hyfteric Plafter. + As the frookeof tobacco thrown into the bowels wilt often procure a ftool when all 2 2o INFLAMMATION OF THE KlDJShia. muft t:.kc exercUb on horfeback, and ufe an infufion of the Peru* vian-bark in wine. V.' hen the difeafe ends in a palfy, the Bat;.-wa- ters are found to be extremely proper. , ■ „ . To avoid thiskind of colic, people muft fhun all four fruits, acids, and auflere liquor?, Sec. Thofe who work in lead outfit neve* to go to their hufinefs faftmg, and their tood fhould be oily. or far. They m?y take a glafs of fclad oil, v. ;th a little brandy or' rum, every rooming, but uVuld never^?ke fpiri s alone. Liquid riirr.ent is beft for them; as fat broths, Sec. but low living is ta<f* They fhould frequently go a little out of the tainted air; and fhould never fuffer themfelves to be coftive. In the Weft-Indies and on the coaft of Guirea, it has been found of great uf' f.>" preventing this ch lie, to wear-a piece of flannel round the waift, and to drink an infufion of ginger bv way of »ea. Sundry c:hcr kind's of .:lvs difeofe might be mentioned, but too mar3 diftjn&ions would tend only to perplex ihe reader. Thofe al- ir?:ly mentioned are the moft material, and fhould indeed be at- tended to, as their treatment is very different- But even perfons who are not in a coriditi n"to diftinguifh very accurately in thefe matters, may nevertheiefs be of great fervice to patients in colics of f^ery kiod, by only obferving the following general rul s, viz. To '' e the feet and legs in warm water; to apply bladders filled with wiry . Should tWe notfucceed,tie natientcuglit'tobeimmerkdinwan* ■ftater. . '-* INFLAMMATION OF THE KIDNEYS. C AUSES.—This difeafe may proceed from any of thofe coufea^ **<■ , j Which produce an inflammatory fever. It may Iikewife be occafion-* ed by wounds or bruifes of the kidneys; fmall ftonesor grrvel lodg- ing within them; 'Jy ftrong diuretic medicines, as fpirits of turpen- tine, tinaure of cantharides, &c. Violent motion, as hard riding or walking, efpecially in hot weathei, or whatever drives the blood too forcibly into the kidneys, may occafion the malady. It may Iikewife proceed from Ivirg too foft, too much en the back, invol- untary contractions, or fpafms in the urinary veffels, Sec. SYMPTOMS.—There is a fharp pain about th e region of the kidneys,, with fome degree of fever, and aftupor or dull pain in the thigh qf the nftecred fide.. * The urine is-at firft clear, and afterwards • of a reddifh colour; but in the worft kind of the difeafe it generally continues pale^.ispaffed with difficulty, and commonly in fmall quan- tities at a time. The patient feels great uneafin°fs when he enaea v- ours to walk or fit upright. He lies with moft eafe on t^e affea- ed fide, and has generally a naufea or vomiting, refembling that Which hvppeos in the cMic. This difeafe however may be diftinguifhed from the colic by the pain being feated farther back, and bv the difficulty of puffing Orice wrh whic it is conftantlv attended. < REGIMEN.—Every thing of a heating or ftimulating nature INFLAMMATION OF THE KIDNEYS. **i is to be avoided. The food muft be thin" arid l'gr.t; as panado, fmall broths, with miH vegeta* les, and the like. Em 'lient and thin liquors muft be plentifully drank; as clear whey, orbalm»tea fweet- ened with honey ^decoaion of marfh- mallow roots: withbafleyand liquorice, &c. The patient, nc^ithftandinfe the vomiting, muft ccnftantly keep fipping fmalNiuaut'ties of th^fe or other diluting li- quors. Nothing io fafely* ant! 'certainly abatesthe inflammation,and' exo-ls the otftruaing caufe, as copiv us dilution. The patient muft be kept eafy, qu;et? and free from cold, as long as-any fymptoms of inflammation remain. • , MEDICINE.—Bleeding is generally necefTary, efpecially at the begining, -Ten or twelve ounces may be let from the arm cr foot with a lan-'et, and.if the pahraiid inflammation continue, the operation maybe repeated in twenty-four hoevs, efpecially if'the patient be of a ft ill habit. Leeches may likewrle be applied to the 'hemorrhoidal vt:i.o£; as a dhchar^i from thefe will greatly relieve the patient. CI ths dipped in warm water, or-bladders'filled with it, rhuft be applied as near as p.-.-ffibtt'to tne part affected, and renewed as they grow cool. If the bladders be hlied wtim a decoction of malr lows and camomile 'flowersy n which a 'littfesfaffron is added, and mixed with about a third part of new milk, it j^ill "be ftill more bene- ficial. . . :* ' - L- ; Emollient clvfersought frequently to lW adminiftered; and it thefe do not open the body, a little fait andPhoney cr manna'may be ad*ed to th<m. • -': "'.'*, The fame comfe is to be followed wh ".rgjrra vol or ftone is lodg- ed in the kidney, but whmthe gravel or ftohe is f parated from the kidney, and lodg js in the Ureter,* it will be-proper, befides the fomentations, to rub the fmall of the back-wi'm fweet oil, tind to give 'gentle diuretics; asjiuiiper-wter, fweeieued with the fyrin>of marfh-mallows: a toa-fpooo,ful of the fweet" fpirits of nitre, with a few dr-'ps of laudanum, may now and then be put in a cup of the patient's drink. He ought Iikewife to take exercife on horfe-back, or in a carriage, if he be abl- to br.ar it. When the difeafe is protracted beyond the feventh or eighth- day, and the patient complains of a ftupor and heavinefs of the part, has frequent returns of chillnefs,'fhivenug, &c. there is real' n to fufpect that matter is forming in the kidney, and that an abfeefs will enfue. When matter in the urine fhews that an ulcer is already form- ed in the kidney, the patient muft be careful to abftain from all ac- rid, four and faked*provifions, and to live chiefly upon miid mucil- aginous herbs and fruits, together with the broth of young animals, made with barley, and common pot-herbs, &c. His drink may be whey, and butter milk that Is not four. The latter is by fome reck- oned a fpecific remedy in ulcers of the kidneys, To anfwer this charaaer however, it muft be drank for a confiderable time. Cha- * The ureters are too long and fmall canals, one on each fide which carry tbe «»e from the bafon or the kidneys to the bladder. They are fometimes obftrucled pv Miau Fie^ csi of grate! falling dowa from the kaincv, an i lodging in them. 3-tt INFLAMMATION OF THE BLADDER. lybeate waters have Iikewife been found beneficial in this difeafe This medicine is eafily obtained, as it is found in every part of Great- B itain. It muCe Iikewife be ufed for a confiderable time, in order to produce any falutary effeas. Thofe who are liable to frequent returns of inflammation, or obftruaionSjOf the kidneys, muft abftain from wines, efpecially fuch as abound with tarfar; and their food oughtto be light, and of eafy digeftion. They fhould ufe moderate exercife, and fhould not lie too hot, nor too much on their back. . INFLAMMATION OF THE BLADDER. 'THE inflammation of the bladder proceeds, in a great meaf- ure, from the fame caufes as that of the kidneys. It is known by an acute pain towards the bottom of the belly, and difficulty of paffing urine, with fome degree of fever, a conftant inclination to go to ftool, and a perpetual defire to make water. This difeafe muft be treated on t^e fame principles as the one im- mediately preceding. The diet muft be light and thin, and the drink of a cooling nature. ;, Bleeding is very proper at the beginning, and in robuft conftitntions it will often be neceffary to repeat it. The lower part of ,the.ielly fhould be fomented with warm water, or a decoaion of mild, vegetables; and emollient clyfters ought fre- quently to be adminin^rea, &c. The patient fhould abftain from every thing that is of a hot, acrid and ftimulating quality, and fhould live entirely upon fmall broths, gruels, or mild vegetables! . A ftoppage of urine may proceed from other caufes befides an inflammation of the bladder; as a fwelling of the haemorrhoidaj., veins, hard faces lodged in the rectum, a ftone in the bladder, ex- crefcences in the urinary paffages, a palfy of the bladder, h\fteric affeaions, &c. Each of thefe require a particular treatment, which does not fall under our prefent confideration. We fhall only ob- ferve, that in all of them mild and ger.tle applications ar<° the fafeft, as ftrong diuretic medicines, or things of an irritating nature, gen- erally increafe the danger. I have known ■ feme periods kill them- felves by introducing probes into the urinary paffages, to remove, as they thought, fomewhat that obftruaed the difcharge of urine, and others bring on a violent inflammation of the bladder, by ufe- ing ftrong diuretics, as oil of turpentine, &c- for that purpofe. INFLAMMATION OF THE LIVER. THE liver is lefs fubje& to inflammation than moft of the oth- er vifcera, as in it the circulation is flower; but when an inflamma- tion does happen, it is with difficulty removed, and often ends in a fuppuration or fcirrhus. CAUSES.—Befides the common caufes of inflammation, we may here reckon the following, viz. exceffive fatnefs, a fcirrhus of the liver itfelf, violent fhocks from ftrong vomits when the liver was before unfound, an aduft or atrabifiarian ftate of the blood, any thing that fuddenly cools the liver after it has been greatly heated, ibse: obitruaing the courfe of the bile, drinking ftrong wines and INFLAMMATION OF THE LIVER. 22j fpirituous liquors, ufihg h:>t fpicy aliment, obftinate hypochondria- cal aflfeaicns, &c. SYMP1OM3.-—Th's difeafe is known by a painful tenfion of the right fide under the falfe ribs, attended with fome degree of fe- ver, a fenfe of weight or fulneis of the part, difficulty of breathing, loathing of food, great thirft, with a pale or yeliowifh colour of the fkin and eyes. The fymptoms here are various, according to the degree of in- flammation, and Iikewife according to the particular part of the liv- er where the inflammation happens. Sometimes the pain is fo incon- fiderable, that an inflammation is not fo much as fufpeaed; but when it happens in the upper or convex part of the liver, the pain is more acute, the pulfe quicker, and the patient is often troubled witn a dry cugh, a hickup, and a pain ex'ending to the fhoulder, with difficulty of lying on the left fide, &c. Tnis difeafe may be diftinguifhed from the pleurify, by the pain being lefs violent, fuatedurder t e falfe ribs, the pulfe sot to h.:rd, and by the difficulty of lying on the left fide, it may be dift- ingmihed from the hyfteric and hypochondriac diforders by the de- gree cf fever with which it is always attended. This difeafe if prop :rly treated, is feldom mortal. A conftant hickuping, violent* fever, and exceffive. thirft, are bad fymptoms. If it ends in a fuppuration, and the matter cannot be difcharg.-d out- wardly, the dang'-r is great. When the fcirrhus of the liver en- fues, the patient, if he observes a proper regimen, mav neverthelefs live a number of .ye r0- tolerably eafy; but if he indulges in animal food and ftrong liqu :rs, or take medicines of an acrid or irritating nature, the fcirrhus will be converted into a cancer, which-muft in- fallibly prove fatal. REGIMEN.—The fame regimen is to be obferved in this as in other inflammatory diforders. All hot things are to be careful-* jy avoided, and cool diluting liquors, as whey, barley water, &:c. drank freely. The food muft be light and thm, and the body, as well as he mind, kept eafy and quiet. MEDICINE.—Bleeding is prr .perst the beginning of this dif- eafe, and it will often be neceffary, even trough the pulfe fhould not feel hard, to repeat it. All violent purgatives are to be avoid- ed; thebodyhowevermuitbekeptgentlyopen. A decoaion of tama- rinds, with a little honey or manna, will anfwer this purpofe very well. The fide affeaed muft be fomented in the manner direaed in the foregoing difeafe. Mild laxative clyfters fhould be frequent* ly admin'ftered; and, if the pain fhould notwithstanding continue violent, a bliftering plafter may be applied over the port affeaed; or rather a plafter made of gum ammoniac and vinegar of fquills. Me ficines which promote the ferrotion of urine have a very good effea here. For this purpofe half a drachm of purified nitre, or a tea-fpoonful of the fweet fpirits of nitre, may be taken in a cup of the patient's drink three or four times a-day. When there is an inclination to fweat, it ought to be promo- ted, but not by warm fudorifics. The only thing to be ufed for that purpofe. i* nfentv of diirfvng liquors drank about tahe warmth 22* OF THE CHOLERA MORBUS, 6c. of the human blood. Indeed the patient in this cafe, as well as in all other topical inflammations, ought to drink nothing that is cold- er than the blood. «f the fto Is fhould be loofe, and even ftreaked with blood, no means muft he ufed to ftop them, unlefs they be fo frequent as to weaken the patient. Loofe ftools often prove critical, and carry off t" e difeafe. If an aofcefs or impofthume is formed in the liver, all methods fhould be tried to make it break and difcharge itfelf outwardly, as fomentations, the appliration of poultices, ripening cataplafms, &c. Sometime indee^'the malt -t of ao abfcefs comes away in the urine, and fometimes it is difcharged by ftool, but thefe are efforts of na- ture winch no means c?.n promote. When the abfcefs burfts into the cavity of the abdomen at large, death muft enfue, nor will the event be more favomabfe when the abfcefs is opened by an incis- ion, unlefs in cffes'where the liver adheres to the peritonaum, fo as to form a bag for the matter, arid prevent i? from falling intothe, cavity of the abdomen; in which cafe open ug the abfcefs by a fuffici? entiy lar-e incifion will probably fave the patient's life.* If the diforder, infpite of all endeavours to the contrary, fhould end in a fcrrhus, the patieut muft be careful to regulate his diet, &c. in fuch a manner as not to aggravate the difeafe. He muft not in* dulge in flefh, fifh, ftrong liquors, or any highly feafoned or falted provifions; but fhould, for the moft part, live on mild vegetables; as fruits and roots; taking gentle exercife, and drinking whey, bar* ley-water, or butter-milk. If he takes any thi"g ftronger, it fhould be fine mild ale, which is lefs heating than wines "or fpirits. We fhall take no notice of inflammations of th - other vifcera. They muft in general be treated upon the fame principles as thofe already mentioned. The chief rule with refpeft to all of them, is to let blood, to avoid every thing that is ftroog, or of a heating na- ture, to apply warm fomentations to the parts affeaed, and to caufe the patient to drink a fufficient quantity of warm diluting liquors. 4 CHAP. XXXIII. OF THE CHOLERA MORBUS, AND OTHER EXCESSIVE DISCHARGES FROM THE STOMACH AND BOWELS. r"|i X. HE cholera morbus is a violent purging and vomiting, at- tended with gripes, ficknefs, and a conftant defire to go to ftooh It comes on fuddenly, and is moft common in autumn. Th. re is hardly any difeafe that kills more quickly than this, when proper means are not ufed in due time for removing it. CAUSES.—It is occafioned by a redundancy and putrid ac- rimony of the bile; cold, food that eafily turns rancid or four on the ftomach; as butter, bacon, fweet-meats, cucumbers, melons, * ! k,n°w V BMKlemap «Ho has hud feveral abfceflei ot the liver opened, aad is now a ercas a£3 tea * iy man, though z:v:s eight? • cu* c; ag:. OF THE CHOLERA MORBUS, 6c. #225 cherries, sn^ Other cold fruits.* It is fometimes the efpa of flrong acrid purges or vomits, or ?f poifonous fubftances taken into the ftomach. It may lilcewife proceed from violent paffions or affec- tions of the mind ; as fear, anger, &c. & Y MP TOMS.—It is generally preceded by a cardialgia, or heart bur^, four belchings, and flatulencies, with pain of tne fto- mach and inteftines. To thefe fucceed exceffive vomiting and purging of green, yellow, or blackifh colored bile, with a" diftentioh of tne ftomach, - ni vi lent gwping pains. There is hkewife a great thirft, with a very quick unequal pulfe, and often a fixed acue pain about the region of the navel. As the difeafe advances, the pulfe often finks fo low as to become quite imperceptible, tne ex- tremities grow cold or cramped, and are often covered with-a clam- my fweat, the urine is obftruaed, and there is ajpalpitation of the heart. Violent hickupiug, fainting, and convulfions, are the figns of approaching death. . MEDICINE.—At the beginning of mis difeafe, the efforts cf Nature to expel the offending caufe ffioul :i be afmied, by promo- ting the purging and vomiting. For this purpofe the patient muft drink freely of dilu ing liquors; as woey, butter- nilk, warm water, thin water gruel, fmall poflet, or, what is perhaps ^referable to-any of them, very weak chicken broth. T is fhould not only be drank plentifully to promote the vomiting, out a clyfter of it g'.ven, every hour in order to promote the purging. After thefe evacuations have been continued for fome time, a decoaion of toafted oat-bread mav be drank to ftop the vomiting.' The bread fhould be toafted till it is of a brown colour", and after- wards-boiled in fpring water. If oat-oread cannot be had, wheat- bread, or oat meal well toifted, may be ufed in its ftead. If this does not put a ftop to the vomiting, two table-ipoonsful of the faline • julep, with ten drops of laudanum, may oe taken every hour till it ceafes. ^ The vomiting and purging however ought never to be flopped too foon. As long as thefe discharges do not weaken, tne pari -nt, they are falutary, and.may be allowed to go cv, cr rather ou;-ht to be promoted. But when the patient is weakened oy te evacua- tions, which may. be known from the finking of his ptilf*, &o.' re- courfe muft immediately be had to opiates, :js recommended aoove; to which may be added ftrong wines, with fp'ntu,us-ciinamon wa- ters, and other generous cordials. Warm ivgus, or ftrong wine- whey, will hkewife be neceflary to fupper- the patent's fpmts, and promote* the perfpiration. His legs fcVuLi be baclv d in warm water, and afterwards rubbed with iLnnel cloths, or wrapped in ,varm blank .-ts, and warm bricks apphed to tne fbles cf a is feet. 1-ian- nels wrung cut of warm fpirituous fomentations fhould iavc voe be applied to the region of the ftomach. , - When tl*violence of the difeafe is over, to privent a rehple, > will be neceflary for fome ti r.e to continue -the ufe of f'-nail dofes * * of laudanum. Ten or twelve drops may be taken in a glafs of v, me, * I have been twice brought .0 the gates cr ac*'-'- ,;y this d;!'?afe, ar»<' fcr'-h njr.'". '-T -*V QZ-.zCunei by eating ranuifbacoiu N ii6, OF A DIARRHOEA, OR LOOSENESS. at leaft twice a-day, for eight or ten days. The patient's food ought to be nourifhing, but taken in fmall quantities, and he fhould ufq moderate exercife. As the ftomach and inteftines are gen-rally much weakened, an infufion of the bark, or other bitters, in fmall wine, fharpened with the elixir of vitriol, may be drank for fome time. "Though phyficians are feldom called in due time in this difeafe, they ought not to defpair of relieving the patien1 even in the moft defperate circumftances. Of this 1 lately law a very ftriking proof in an old man and his fon, who had been both feized with' it about the middle of the night. 1 did not fee fhem till next morning, when they had much more the appearance of dead than of living men. No pulfe could be felt; the extremities were cold and rigid, the counte- nance was ghaftly, and the ftrength almoft quite exhaufted. Yet from this deplorable condition they were both recovered by the ufe of opiates and cordial medicines. OF A. DIARRHOEA, OR LOOSENESS. A LOOSENESS, in many cafes, is not to be confidered as a difeafe, but rather as a faliitary evacuation. It ought therefore never to be flopped, unlefs when it continues too long, or evident- ly weakens the patient. As this however fometimes happens, we fhall point cut the moft common caufes of a loofenefs, with a prop- er method of treatment. When a loofenefs is occafioned by catching cpld, or an. ob- ftruaed perfpiration, the patient ought to keep warm, to drink freely of weak diluting liquors, to bathe his feet and legs, frequent- ly in luke-warm water, to wear flannel next his fkin, and to take every other method to reftore the perfpiration. In a loofenefs which proceeds from excefs or repletion, a vom- it is the proper medicine. Vomits not only cleanfe the ftomach, but promote all the fecretions, which renders them of great impor- tance in carrying off a debauch. Half a drachm of ipecacuanha in powder will anlwer this purpofe very well. A day or two after the vomit, the fame quanti y of rhubarb may be taken, and repeat- ed two or three times, if the loofenefs continues. The patient ought to live upon light vegetable food of eafy digeftion, and to drink whey, thin gruel, or barley-water. A loofenefs occafioned by the obftruaion of any cuftomary evacuation, generally requires bleeding. If that does not fucc-ed, other evacuations may be fubftituted in the room of thofe which are ofcftrufted. At the fame time, every method is to be taken to reftore the ufual difcharges, as not only the cure of the difeafe, but the patient's life, may depend on this. A periodical loofenefs ought never to be flopped. It is alway9 an effort Of Nature to carry off fome offending matter, which, if retained in the body, might have fatal effeas. Ch*ren are very liable to this kind of loofenefs, efpecially while teething. If is how- ever fo far from being hurtful to them, that fuch children gener- ally get their teeth with leaft trouble. If thefe loofe ftools fhould at any time prove four or griping, ajtea-fpoouful of maguefia alia, OF A DIARRHOEA, OR LOOSENESS. 227 with four or five grains of rhubarb, may be given to the child in a little panado, or any other food. This, if repeated three or four times, will generally correa the acidity, and carry ol the griping ftools. A diarrhoea, or loofenefs, which proceeds from violent raffions or aff-'dions of t e mind, muft be treated w:th t e greateft caution. Vomits in this cafe are highly improper. Nor are purges fefe, un- lefs they be very mild, and given in fmall qua- -i itie?. Opiates, and ether antifpafmodic medicines, are moft proper. Ten or twelve dr p of liqu'd laudanum may be taken in a cup of valerian or pen- ny-roval tea every eight or ten hoursrriil the fymptoms abate. Eafe, cheerfulnefij, and tranquility of mind are here ot the great.-::t im- portance. "* % ^* When a loofenefs proceeds from acrid or poifonous r,a ftao-p:, taken into the ft moch, the patient muft drink la-ge quantise*, of diluting liquors, with oil or fat broths, to promote vomiting and purging. Afterwards, if there be reafon to fufpea Th t •! e bowels are inflamed, bleeding wilibe neceffary. Sma 1 0 it . iaadaiium may Iikewife be taken to remove their irritation. When the gout, repelled from the -xtreimr es£ * c fions a loofe- nefs, it ought to be promoted by gentle d^fes ^f r ob^rb ~r rther mild purgatives, ., The gouty matter is "ekjwhie be oiicited to- wards the extremities by warm frmentarions, cata. lafms, Sec. The perfpiration ought at the f. me time to be pro- -'-.? by warm dilut- ing liquors; as wine-whey with fpirits of hart: cm, cr a few drops of liquid laudanum, in it. When a loofenefs proceeds from worms, which may be kn-.wn from the fliminefs of the ftools, mixed with pieces of decayed w^rms, Sec. medicines muft be given to kill and carry off thefe vermin, as the powder of tin with purges of rhubarb and cJ-'eel. After- wards lime-water, eit er alone, or w;th a fmall quantity cf rhubarb infufed, will be proper to ftrengthen the bowels, and pr-v.:nt the new generation of worms. A loofenefs is often occafioned by dri>okir?^ b id Water. When this is the cafe, the difeafe generally pr oves : pi■'ev.ic.l. When there is reafi n to beiieve that t' is :-r any other difeaf- p; oceeds from the ufe of unwholefome water, It ougnt immedi t'-lv to be changed, or. 't' that cannot be done,, it may be correa d by mixing with it quick lime, chalk, or' the like. in people whofe ftomachs are weak, vHent exercife immediate- ly after eating will occafion a loofepefs. Though the cure of this is obvious, yet it will be proper, befides avoidtig vi lent exercife, to ufe fuch medicines as tend to brace and .'Vngmon the ftomach, as infufions of the ' >ark, with other Hrier a d ^ftrinzem i o^redients, in wi ite wine. Such perfons ought likew fe to take frequently a glafs or two of old red port, or good claret. From whatever caufe a loofenefs proceeds, when it U found neceffary to check it, the diet ought to confift of rice b^ilf-d with milk, and flav< ured with cinnamon; rice-jelly, fago with red p^rt; and the lighter forts of flefh-meat reafted. The drink may be thin water-gruel, rice-water, cr weak broth made from lean ver.?. cr with l28 OF VOMITING. I a fheep's head, as beingmore gelatinous than mutton, beef, or chick- j en-broth. „ , . . . 1 Perfons who, from a peculiar weaknefs, or too great an irnta- J bility of the bowels, are liable to frequent returns of this difeafe>l fhould live temperately, avoiding crude fummer fruits, all unwhole-^1 fome foods, and mems e i hard digeftion. They ought Iikewife to I beware of cold moi 11 urc,. cr whatever may obft i uft the perfpirat ion, * and fhould wear flann -1 next the fkin. All violent paffions, as fear, ' anger, &c. are Iikewife carefully to be guarded rgainft. OF VOMITING. VOMITING may proceed from various caufes; as excefs in eating and drinking; foulnclfc of the ftomach; the acrimony of the ! aliment; a tranflation of the morbific matter of ulcers, of the gout, % the eryfipelas, or other difeafes, to the ftomach. It may Iikewife ; proceed from a loofenefs having been too fuddently flopped; from ^ the ftoppage of any cuftomary evacuation, as the bleeding piles, the xr.enfes, Sec. from a weaknefs of the ftomach, the colic, the iliac paf- fion, a rupture, a fit of the gravel, worms; or from any kind of poi* fon taken into Ihe ftomach. It is an ufual fymptom of injuries done to the brain; as contufions, compreflions, Sec. It is Iikewife a fymptom of wounds or inflammations of the diaphrzgm, inteft- ] hies, fpleen, liver, kidneys, Sec. Vomiting may be occafioned by unufual motions, as failing,, . being drawn backwards in & carriage, Sec It may Iikewife be ex- cited by violent paffions, or by the idea, of naufeous or difagreea*^ bleobjeas, efpecially of fuch things as have formerly produced vom- iting. Sometimes it proceeds from a regurgitation of the bile into ««, the ftomach: in this cafe, what the patient vomits is generally of a yellow or greenifh colour, and has a bitter tafte. Perfons who are fubjea to nervous affeaions are often fuddenly feized with violent iits of" vomiting. Laitly, vomiting is a common fymptom of preg- nane v. In this cafe it generally comes on about two'weeks after ..lie ftoppage of the mevfes, and continues during the firft three cr four months. * : When vomiting proceeds from a foul ftomach or indigeftion, ; k is not to be confidered as a difeafe, but as the cure of a dileafe. It ought therefore to be pr moted by drinking hub. warm water, * ©r thin gruel.. If this does not put a ft- p to the vomiting, a dofe of ipecacuanha may be taken, and worked off with weak camomile-tea* ■ When the retroceflion of\the gout, or the obftruction of c.:ft- ornary evacuations, occafi. n vomiting, all means muft be ufed to reftcre trefe difcharges; or, if that cannot be effeaed, their place ' muffbe fupplied by others, as bleeding, purging, bathing the ex- tremities in w irni water, opening iffues,fetc ns,perpetualblifters,&c. m When vomiting is trie effect of pregnancy, it may generally be mitigated by bleeding, and keeping the body gently open. The bleeding ho, ver ought to be in fmall quantities at a time, and the purgatives in ei.1 be of the mildeft kind, asfigs,ftewed prunes, man- " na, •: fenna. Pregnant women are moft apt to vomit in the morn- U'S iiLmeuijtily after getting out of bed, which Is owing partly to ___' _____»_________ _____________ : Ot VOMITING. 229 the change of pofture, but more to the emptinefs of the ftomach. It may generally be prevented by taking a difh of coffee, tea or fome light breakfaft in bed. Pregnant women who are affliaed with vomiting, ought to be kepj^eafy both in body and mind. They fhould neither allow their ftomachs to be quite empty, nor fhould they ear much at on~e. Cold water is a very proper drink in this c?.fe; if the ftomach be weak, a little brandy may be added to it. If the fpirits are low, and the perfon apt to faint, a fpoonful of cin- namon-water, with a little marmalade of quinces or oranges, may be tnken. If vomiting proceeds from weaknefs of the ftomach, bitters will be of fervice. Peruvian bark infufed in wine or brandy, with as much rhubarb as will keep the body gently open, is an excellent medicine in this cafe. The elixir of vitriol is alfo a good medicine. It may be taken in the dofe of fifteen or twenty drops, twice or thrice a-day, in a glafs of wine or water. Habitual vomitings are fometimes alleviated by making oyfters a principal part of diet. A vomiting which proceeds from acidities in the ftomach, is relieved by alkaline purges. The beft medicine of this kind is the magneto* alba, a tea-fpoonful of which may be taken in a difh of tea or a little milk, three or four times a-day, or of tener if neceffa" ry, to keep the b">dy open. * When vapiting proceeds from violent paffions, or affeaiens of the mind, all evacuants muft be carefully avoided, efpeciaHy vom- its. Thefe are exceedingly dangerous. The patient in this cafe ought to be kapt perfeaiy eafy and quiet, to have the mind footh- ed, and to take fome gentle cordial, as negus, or a little brandy and water, to which a few drops of laudanum may occafionally be added. When vomiting proceeds from fpafmodic affections of the fto- mach, mufk caftor, and other antifpafmodic medicines are of ufe. Warm and aromatic plafters have Iikewife a good effea. The ftc- mach-plafter of the London or Edinburgh difpenfatory may be ap- plied to the pit of the ftomach, or a plafter of theriaa, which will anfwer rather better. Aromatic medicines may Iikewife be taken inwardly, as cinnamon or mint tea, wi e with fpiceries boiled in it. Sec. The region of the ftomach may be rubbed with aether, or if that cannot be had, with ftrong brandy, or other fpirits. The belly fhould be fomented with warm water, or the patient immerfed up to the breaft in a warm bath. I have always found the faline draughts taken in the act of effer- vefcence, of fingular ufe jn flopping cf vomiting, from what -ver caufe it proceeded. Thefe may be prepared by diffolving a dracKm of 1 he fait of tartar in an ounce and a half of frefh lemon juice, and adding to it an ounce of pepper-mint water, the fame quantity of fimple cinnamon water, and a little white fugar. This draught muft be fwallowed before the efferv:fcenceis quite over, and may be repeat- ed ^every two hours, ©r oftener, if the vomiting be violent. A vio- hit vomiting has fometimes been flopped by cuppiog on the region r of the ftomach after all other means had failed. As the leaft motion will often bitog on the vomiting again, evm afies it has been itopued, the patient mult avoid all manner of a-.t- 23o OF THE DIABETES, \*fc. ion. T^e diet muft oe fo regulated as to fit eafy upon the ftomach, and' ot ing fhmla be taken that is hard of digeftion. We do not how ■■ vr.r mean that the patient fhould live entirely upon flops. Sol- id food, in tnis cafe, often fits eafier on t.ie ftomach than liquids. CHAP. XXXIV. OF THE DIABETES,- AND OTHER DISORDERS OF THE KIDNEYS AND BLADDER. HP j^ HE diabetes is a frequent and exceffive difcharge of urine. It is felJom to be met with am ng young people; but often attacks perfons in the decline of life, efpecially thofe w.io follow the more violent employments, or have been hard drinkers in their youth. CAUSES.—A diabetes is often the confequence of acute dif- eafes, as fevers, fluxes, Sec. where the patient has fuffered by ex- ceffive evacuations; it may alf be occafioned by great fatigue, as riding long journies upon, a hard trotting horfe.^ carrying heavy bur- dens, Sec. It may be brought on by hard drinking, or the ufe of ftrong ftimulating diuretic medicines, as ti"cture of canttorides, fpir- its of turpentine, and fuch like. It is often t e efiOffc of drinking too great quarftities of mineral waters. Many imagine that thefe will do them no fervice unlefs they be drank in great quantities, by which miftake it oftm happens that they occafion worfe difeafes than thofe they were intended to cure. In a word, this difeafe m y eith- er proceed from too great a laxity of the organ, which fecrete the urine, from fomething that ftimulates th? kidneys too much, t from a thin diffolved ftate of the blood, which makes too great a quan- tity of it run off by the urinary paffages. SYMPTOMS.—In a diabetes, the urine generally exceeds in quantity all the liquid food which '.he patient takes. It is hin and pale, of a fweetifh tafte, and an agreeable fmell. The patient h.s a continued thirft, with fome degree of fever; his mouth is dry, and he fpits frequently a frothy fpittle. The ftrength fa'ls, the appnre decays, and the flefh waftes away till the patient is reduced to fkin and bone. There is a heat of the bowels, and frequently the loins and feet are fwelled. This difeafe may generally be cured at the beginning; but after it hascontinued long,thecure becomes very difficult. In drunk- ards, and very old people, a pcrfeft cure is not to be expected. REGIMEN.—Every th:ng that ftimulates the urinary paffa- ges, or tends to r lax the habit, muft be avoided. For this reafon the patientfhould live chi* fly on folid food. His thirft maybe quench- ed with acids; as forrel, juice of lemon, or vinerar. The mucilagin- ous vegetables, as rice, fago, and falop, with milk, ara the moft pro- per &od. Of animal fnbftances, fhell fifh are to be preferred; as oyfters, crabs, &c. ,r The drink may be Briftol-water. When that cannot be obtain- ed, lime-water, in which a due proportion of oak-bark has been ur inn. uuiD&TES, &rV. 231 macerated, may be ufed. The white decoaion,* with ifinglafs dif- folved in it, is Iikewife a very propor drink. The pi tient ought daily to take exercife, but it fhould be fo gentle as not to fatigue him. He fhould lie upon a hard bed or mat- rafs. Nothing hur s the kidneys more than lying too foft. A warm, dry air, the ufe of the flefh -brufti, and every thing that promotes perfpiration, is of fervice. For this reafon the patient ought to wear flannel next to his fkin. A large ftrengthening plafter may be ap* plied to the back; or, what will anfwer better, a great part of the body mav be wnpped in plafter. MEDlC'tNE.—Gentle purges, if the patient be not too much weakened by the difeafe, have a good effea. They may confift of rhubarb, with cardamum feeds, or any other fpiceries, infufed in wine, and may be taken in fuch quantities as to keep the body gent- ly open. The patient muft next have recourfe to aftringents and cor- roborants. Half a drachm of powder made of equal parts of alum and the infpiflated juice commonly called Terra Japonka, may be ta- ken four times a-day, or oftener, if the fbmacfi will bear it. The alum muft firft be melted in a crucible, afterwards they may both be pounded together. Along with every dofe of this powder the patient may take a tea-cupful of the tincture of rofes.t If the patient's ftomach cannot bear th ? alum in fubftance, whey may be made of it, and taken in the dofe of a tea-cupful three or four times a-day. The alum whey is prepared by boiling two Eng- lifh quarts of milk over a flow fire, with three drachm3 of alum, till the curd feparates. Opiates : re of fervice in this difeafe, even though the patient refts well. They take off fpafm and irritation, and at the fame time leffen the force of the circulation. Ten or twelve drops of liquid laudanum may be taken in a cup of the patient's drink three or four times a day. The beft corroborants which we know,are the Peruvian bark, and wine. A drachm of bark may be taken in a glafs of red port or claret three times a-day. The medicine will be both more effi- cacious and lefs difegreeable, if fifteen or twenty drops of the acid elixir of vitriol be addcd to each d ^le. Such as cannot take the bark in fubftance may ufe the decoaion, mixed with an equal quan- tity of red wine, and fharpened as above. There is a difeafe incident to labouring people in the decline of life, called incontinency of urine. But this is very different from a diabetes, as the water paffes offinvMmvary by drops, and does not exceed the ufual quantity. This difeafe is rather trouble- fome than dangerous. It is owing *o a relaxation of the fphinaar of the bladder, and is often the effea ^f a palfy. Sometimes ft pro- ceeds from hurts or injuries occafioned bv blows, bruifes, preternat- ural labours, Sec. Sometimes it is the effea of a fever. It may Iikewife be occafioned by a long ufe of ftrong diuretics, or of i.imu-. lating medicines injea<°d into the bladder. This difeafe may be mitig ited by the ufe of aftrir gent and cor- • See Appendix, iybi;edtco(Hix. t See Appendix, Tint'tun 0/JcVi;. 732 OF A SUPPRESSION OF URIN£i. •ooorating medicines, fuch as have be; n mentioned aoove; but we do no* remember ever to have feen it cured. In an incontineacy of urine, from whatever caufe, a piece of fuooge ought to be worn, or a bladder applied in fuch a manner as to prevent the urine from galling and excoriating the parts.* OF A SUPPRESSION OF URINE. IT has already been obferved, that a fuppreffion of urine, may proceed from various caufes; as,an inflammation of the kidneys, or bb ier; fmall ftones or gravel lodging in'the urinary paffages, hard faces-h ing in the retlum, pregnancy, a roafm or ^ontraaion of the nock ot the bladder, clotted blood in the bladder itfelf, a fwelling of the hcemorrhoidal veins, Sec. Some of thefe c'fes require the catheter, both to remove the obftruah g matter, and to draw off t eurce; but as thi inftrument can only be managed with faf. ty by perfons fkilled in furgery, we fhall f-y not h gfurrher of its ufe. A bougie ini.'y De ufed by a :y cautious hand, ahd will often fuccee * bett r than the catheter. We would chiefly reepmvend, in all ohftrua ons" • f urir.^fo- mentationsandevecuatii ns. Bleeding, as far as the patienT'sftrength will permit, is neceflary, efpecially wrere there are fymptoms of top- icae inflammation. Bleeding in this cafe not only abates thf fever, by leflening the force of thecrculati n, but, by-rel xng the folids, it takes off the fpafm or ftructure up n the veflels wh;ch cccafi n- led the obftruaion. After bleedings, fomentations muft be ufed. Thefe may eith* er confift of warm water -^lone, or of decoai ns of mild vegetables! as mallows, came mile-flowers, &c. Cloths dipped in thefe may eith- er be applied to the part affected, or a large bladder filled with the decoaion may be Kept continually upon it. Some put the herbs thtmfelves into a flannel-bag, and apply them to the part, Winch is far from being a bad method. Thefe continue longer warm than cloths dipped in the decoaion, and at the fame time keep the part equally moift. in all obftruaions of urine, the body ought to be kept open, This is not however to be attempted by ftrong purgatives^ but by emollient clyfters, or gentle infufions of fenna and mr.nna. Clyft- ers in this cafe not only opm the body, but anfwer the purpofe of an internal fomentation, and greatly affift in removing the fpafms of the bladder and parrs :d:acont. The food muft be light, and taken in fmall quantities. The drink may be weak broth, or decoaionsand infufioes cf rnucibp- mous vegetables, as marfh-mallow roots, lime-tree buds, &rc. A tea-fpoonful of the fweet fpirits cf nitre, or a drachm of caftile foap, may be frequently put into the patient's drink; and if there be no inflammation, h< may drink fmall gir-purxh. • Perf ms fubjea t < a fuppreffion of urine ought to live very tem- perate. Their diet fhould be light, and the:r l*qm r diluting. " Thev lhoi Id avoid all acids and auitere wines, fhould take fufficient excr- nl<\ he hard, and avoid ftudy and fedentary occupations. -■ A bottle'trudc of the IrKian rubber ami piopetlj applied, anfwers this purpofe beft. 233 OF THE GRAVEL AND STONE. WHEN fmall ftones are lodged in the kidneys, or difcharged along with the urine, the patient is faid to be affliaed with the grav- el. If one of thefe ftones happens to make a lodgirtent in the blad- der for fome time, it accumulates frefh matter, and at length be- comes too large to pafs qff with the urine. In this cafe the patient is faid to have the ftone.' CAUSES.—The ftone and gravel may be occafioned by high v living; the ufe of ftrong aftringent wines; a fedentary life; !ying too hot, foft, or too much on the back; the conftant ufe of water impregnated with earthy or ftony particles; aliments of an aftrm- g nt or windy nature, Sec. It may Iikewife proceed from an hered- ' tary difpofition. Perfons in the decline of life, and thofe who have been much affliaed with the gout or rheumatifm, are moft liable to it. SYMPTOMS.—Small ftones or gravel in the kidneys occafion , pain in the bin; ficknefs; vomiting; and fometimes bloody urine. When the ftone defcends into the ureter, and is too large to pafs along ' with eafe, all the above fymptoms are increafed; the pain extends towards the bladder; the thigh and leg of the affeaed fide are be- numbed ; the tefticles aredrawn upwards; and the urine is obftruaed. A ftone in the bladder is known from a pain, at the time, as well as before and after making water; from the urine coming away by drops, or flopping fuddenly, when it was running in a full ftream; by a violent pain in the neck of the bladder upon motion, efpecially on horfeback, or in a carriage on a rough road; from a white, thick,copious,ftinking, mucous fediment m the urine; from an itching on the top of the penis; from bloody urine; from an in- clination to go to ftool during the difcharge of urine; from the pa- tient's paffinghis urine more eafily when lying than in an erea poft- ure ; from a kind of convulfive motion occafioned by the fharp pain in difcharging the laft-drops of the urine; and laftly, from found- ingor fearching with the catheter. „ .. REGIMEN.—Perfons affliaed with the gravel or ftone fhould avoid aliments of a windy or heating mture, as fait meats, four fruits, ac. Their diet ought chiefly to'confift of fuch things as tend to promote the fecretion of urine, and to keep the body open. Ar- tichokes, afparagus, fpinnage, lettuce, parfley, fuccory, purilane, turnips, potatoes, carrots, and radifhes, may be lately eaten. Un- ion^ leeks, and cellery are, in this cafe, reckoned medicinal. Ihe moft proper drinks, are whey, butter-m Ik, milk and water, bariey- water; decoaions or infufions of the roots of marfh-mallows, pari- iev liquorice, or of ether mild mfkilaginous vegetables, as lmleed, i'me-tree buds cr leaves, &c. If the mtient has be^n accuftomed to generous liquors, he may drink gin and water not too ftrong. Gentfe ex*rcife is proper; but violent motion is apt to occahoa bbody urine. We would therefore advife that it fiiould be token in moderation. Perfons affliaed with gravel often pafs a great num- ber of ftones after riding on horfeback, or in a carriage;Jmt thole w^o *?ve a ftone <n the bladder are feUbm able to bear thefe kinds (if*) lu OF THE GRAVEL AND STON*E. of exercife. Where there isan hereditary tendency to this difeafe, a1 fedentary life ought never to be indulged. Were people carefu1, upon the firft fymptoms of gravel, to obferve a proper regimen of diet, and to take fufficient exercife, it might often be earned off, or at leaft prevented from ipcreafing; but if the fame courfe which oc- cafioned the difeafe is perfifted in, it muft be aggravated. MEDICINE.—In what is called a fit of the grwel, which is commonly occahoned bv a ftone flicking in the ureter or fome part of the urinary paffages, the patient mint babied, w?rm fomentations - lhould Iikewife be applied to the part -ff t\ed, emollient clyfters ad- miniftered, and diluting mucilaginous liquors drank, Sec. The treat- ment of this cafe has been fully painted out under the articles, in- flammation of the kidneys and bladder, to which we refer. Dr. Whyte advifes patients who are fubj a to frequent fits of gravel in the kidneys, but have no ftone in the bladd-r, to drink every morning, two or three h airs before breakfaft, an Englifh pint of oyfter or cockle-fhell lime-water. The Doaor very juftly pb- lerves, that though this quantity might be too fmall to have any fenfible effea in diffolving a ftone in the bladder, yet it may very probably prevent its growth. When a ftone is formed in the bladder, the Doftor recom- mends Alicant foap, and oyfter • r cockle-fhell lime-water,* to be taken in the following manner: The patient muft fwallow everyday, in'anv form that is leaft difagreeable, an ounce of the internal part of Alicant foap, and drink three or four Englifh pints of oyfter or cockle-fhell lime-water. The foap is to be divided into three dofes; the largeft to be taken fafting in the morning early; the fecond at noon; and the third at feven in the evening; drinking above each dofe a large draught of the lime-water; the remainder of which he may take any time betwixt dinner and fupper, inftead of other li- quors. The patient fhould begin with a fmaller quantity of the lime- water and foap than that mentioned above; at firft an Englifh pint of the former, and three drachms of the latter, may be taken daily. This quantity, however, he may increafe by degrees, and ought to perfevere in the ufe of thefe medicines, efpecially if he finds any abatement of his complaints, for feve/al months; nay, if the ftonebe very large, for years. It may Iikewife be proper for the patient, if he be feverely paned, not only to begin with the foap and lime- water in fmall quantities, but to take the fecond or third lime-water inftead of t1 e fir ft. However, afrer he has been for fome time ac- cuftomed to thefe medicines, he may not only take the firft water, but, if he finds he can eaiily bear it, heighten its diffolving power ftill more by pouring it a fecond time on frefh calcined ihells. The caiutic alkali, or foap lees is the medicine chiefly in vogU2 at prefent for the ftone. It is of a very acrid nature, and ought therefore to be given in feme gelatinous or mucilaginous liquor; as veal broth, new-milk, linfeed-tea, a folution of gum-arabic, or a decoaion of marfh-mallow roots. The patient muft begin with * See Appendix, Lime-water. Involuntary discharges, 6c £3 4 finatl dofes of the lees, as thirty or forty drops, and increafe by de- grees, as far as the ftomach can bear it.f Though the foap-lees and lime-water Jare the moft powerful medicines which have hitherto been difcovered for the ftone; yet there are fome thiugs of a more fimple nature, which in certain cafes are found to be beneficial, and therefore deferve a trial. An infill (ion of the feeds ofdaucus fylveflris, or wild carrot, fweetened with honey, has been found to give confiderable eafe in cafes where the ftomach could not bear any thing of an acrid nature. A decoaion of raw coffee-berries, taken morning arid evening, to the quantity of eight or ten ounces, with ten drops of fweet fpirits of nitre, has1 Iikewife been found very efficacious in bringing away largequanti- ties of e.irthy matter in flakes. Honey is Iikewife found to be of confiderable fervice, and may be taken in gruel, or in any other forni that is more agreeable. The only other medicine which we fhall mention is the uva urfi. It has been greatly extolled of late both for the gravel and ftone. It feems however to be in all refpeas inferior to the foap and lime-wa- ter; but it is lefs difagreeable, and has frequently to my knowledge, relieved gravelly complaints. It is generally taken in powder from half a drachm to a whole drachm, two or threS times a day. It may however be taken to the quantity ot feven Or eight drachms a-day, with great fafety and good effea. CHAP. XXXV. OF INVOLUNTARY DISCHARGES OF BLOOD. ■ "OTONTANEOU3 or involuntary difcharges of blood often happen from various parts of the body. Thefe, however, are fo far from being always dangerous, that- they often prove falutary, When fuch difcharges are critical, which is frequently the cafe in fevers, they ought not to be flopped. Nor indeed is it proper at any time to ftop them, unlefs they be fo great as to endanger the patient's life. Moft people, afraid of the fmalleft difcharge of blood from any part of the body, fly immediately to the ufe of ftyptic and aftringent medicines, by which means an iaflammatipn of the brain, Or fome other fatal difeafe, is occafioned, which, had the difcharge been allowed to go on, might have been prevented Periodical difcharges of blood, from whatever part of the body they proceed, muft not be flopped. They are always the efforts of Nature to relieve herfelf; and fatal difeafes have often been the confequence of obftruaing them. It may indeed be fometimes neceffary to check the violence of fuch difcharges ; but even this requires the greateft caution. Inftances might be given where the ftopping of a fmall periodical flux of blood, from one of the fingers, has proved fatal to the health. In the early period of life, bleeding at the nofe is very com- f The cauftic alkali rpay be prepared by mixing two parts of quick-lime with one of pot- aJhei, anil flittering them to ftihd till Ihe lixivium be formed, which muft be carefully fi!t»- ttd before it be ufed. If the folution dots not happen raadiljr, a fori ^uannty of water may be added to the mixture. 236 INVOLUNTARY DISCHARGES, 6c. mon. Thofe who are farther advanced in years are more liable to haemoptoe, or difcharge of blood from the lungs. After the middle period of life, hemorrhoidal fluxes are moft common ; and in the decline of life, difcharges of blood from the urinary paflages. Involuntary fluxes of blood may proceed from very different, and quite oppoiite caufes. Sometimes they are owing to a particu- lar conftitution of the body, as a fanguine temperament, a laxity of the veffels, a plethoric habit, &c. At other times they proceed from a determination of the blood towards one particular part, as the head, the haemorrhoidal veins, Sec. They may Iikewife pro- ceed from an inflammatory difpofition of the blood, in which cafe there'is generally fome degree of fever ; this Iikewife happens when the flux is occafioned by an obftruaed perfpiration, or a ftriaure upon the fkin, the bowels, or any particular part of the fyftem. But a diffolved ftate of the blood will Iikewife occafion haemor- rhages. Thus, in putrid fevers, the dyfentery, the fcurvy, the malignant fmall pox7, &c. there are often very great difcharges of blood from different parts of the body. _ They may Iikewife be brought on by too liberal an ufe of medicine, which tends to dif- folve the blood, as cantharides, the volatile alkaline falts, &c.— Food of an acrid or irritating quality may Iikewife occafion haemorrhages; as alfo ftrong purges and vomits, or any thing that greatly ftimulates the bowels. Violent paffions or agitations of the mind will Iikewife have this effea. Thefe often caufe bleeding at the nofe, and I haye known them fometimes occafion an haemorrhage in the brain. Vi- olent efforts of the body, by overftraining or heating the veffels,. may haye the fame effea, efpecially when the body is long kept in an unnatural pofture, as hanging the head very low, &c. The cure of an haemorrhage muft be adapted to its caufe.— When it proceeds from too much blood, or a tendency to inflam- mation, bleeding, with gentle purges and other evacuations, will be neceffary. It will Iikewife be proper for the patient in this cafe to live chiefly upon a vegetable diet, to avoid all ftrong liquors, and food that is of an acrid, hot, or ftimulating quality. The body fhould be kept cool and the mind eafy. Wher%.n haemorrhage is owing to a putrid or diffolved ftate f of the blood, the.patient ought to live chiefly upon acrid fruits with milk and vegetables of a nourifhing nature, as fago, falop, &o— His drink may be wine diluted with water, and fharpened with the juice of lemon, vinegar, or fpirits of vitriol. The beft medicine in this cafe is the Peruvian bark, which maybe taken according to the urgency of the fymptoms. When a flux of blood is the effea of acrid food, or of ftrong ftimulating medicines, the cure is to be effected by foft mucilagin- ous diet. The patient may Iikewife take frequently about ther bulk of a nutmeg of Locatelli's balfam, or the fame quantity of fpermaeeti. ^ Vtken an obftruaed perfpiration, or a ftriaure upon any par*: the fyftem, is the caufe cf an haemorrhage, it m3y be remove «i OF BLEEDING AT THE NOSE. 237 by-drinking warm diluting liquors, lying a-bed, bathing the ex- tremities in warm water, &c. OF BLEEDING AT THE NOSE. BLEEDING at the nofe is commonly preceded by fome degree of quicknefs of the pulfe, flufhing in the face, pulfation of the temporal arteries, heavinefs in the head, dimnefs of the fight, heat and itching of the noftrils, Sec. To perfons who abound with blood this difcharge is very fal- utary. It often cures a vertigo, the head-ach, a phrenzy, and even an epilepfy. In fevers, where there is a great determination of blood towards the head, it is of the utmoft fervice. It is Iikewife beneficial in inflammations of the liver and fpleen, and often in the gput and rheumatifm. In all difeafes were bleeding is neceffary, a fpontaneous difcharge of blood from the nofe is of much more fer- vice than the fame quantity let with a lancet. In a difcharge of blood from the nofe, the great point is to determine whether it ought to be flopped or not. It is a common praaice to ftop the bleeding, without confidering whe&er it be a difeafe, or the cure of a difeafe. This condua proceeds from fear; but it has often bad, and fometimes fatal confequences. When a difcharge of blood from the nofe happens in an inflam- matory difeafe, there is always reafon to believe that it may prove falutary ; and therefore it fhould be fuffered to go on,, at leaft as long as the patient is not weakened by ft. When it happens to perfons in perfe® health, who are full of blood, it ought not to be fuddenly flopped, efpecially if the^ fymp- toms of plethora, mentioned above, have preceded it. In this cafe it cannot be flopped without rifking the patient's life. In fine, whenever bleeding at the nofe relieves any bad fymn- torn, and does not proceed fo far as to endanger the patient's lif^ it ought not to be flopped.
