Such as cannot take this infufion, may ufe the de- coaion of feneka-root, which is both diuretic and fudorific. I ave known an obftinate anafarca cured by an infufion of the afhes of broom in wine. The above courfe will often cure an inc*dental dropfy, if the' conftitution be good ; but when the dileafe proceeds from a bad habit, or an unfound ftate of the vifcera, ftrong purges and vomits are not to be ventured upon. In this cafe, the fafer curie is to pal- liate the fymptoms by the ufe of fuch medicines as promote the fe- cretions, and to fupport the patient's ftrength by warm and nourifh- ing cordials. The fecretion of urine may be greatly promoted by nitre.— Brookes fays, he knew a young woman who was cured of a dropfy by taking a drachm of nitre every morning in a draught of ale, after fhe had been given over as incurable. The powder of fquills :s like- wife a good diuretic. Six or eight grains of it, with a feruple of nitre, may De given twice a-day in a glafs of ftrong cinnamon- water. Ball fays, a large fp ^onful of unbruifed muftard-feed tak .n every night and morning,and dri king half an Englifh piat of the decoaion of the tops of gr en broom after it, has performed a cure after other powerful medicines had proved ineffeaual. I have fometimes feen good effeas from cream of tartar in this dTeafe. It promotes the difch"rg-°s by ftool and urine, and will at leaft palliate., if it does not perfe rm a cure. . The patient may begin by taking an ounce every fecond or third day, and may increafe the quantity to two or even to three ounces, if the ftomach will bear it. This quantity is not however to be taken at once, but di- vided into three or four dofes. To promote perfpiration, the patient may ufe the dec ^ct'on cf feneka-root, as direaed above ; or he may take two table-fpoons- ful of Mindererus' fpirit in a cup of wine-whey three or four times a-day. To promote a difcharge of urine, the'following infufion of the London hofpitals will Iikewife be beneficial: Take of zedoary-root two drachms; dried fquills, rhubarb, and juniper-berries bruifed,of each a drachm ; cinnamon in powder three drachms ; fait of worm-wood, a drachm and a half; infufe in an Englifh pint and a half of old hock wine, and when fit for ufe, filter the liquor. * A wine-glafs of it may be t. ken three or four times a-day. , In the anafarca it is ufual to fcarify the feet and legs. By this means the water is often difcharged; but the operator muft be cui- tious not to make the incifions too deep; they ought barely to pierce through the fkin, and efpecial care muft be taken, by fpintu- ous fomentations and pr-per digeftives, to prevent a gangrene. In an afcittti when the difeafe does not evidently and fpeedily a54 OF THE GOUT. cive way to purgative and diuretic medicines, the water ought to be fe\ offbv tappin|. This is a very fimple and fafe operation, and would often fucceed, if it were performed in due time; but if it be delaved till the humours are vitiated, or the bowels fpoiled, by long leaking in water, it can hardly be expeaed that any permanent relief will be procured.* . After the evacuation of the water, the patient is to be put on a courfe of ftrengthening medicines ; as the Peruvian bark ; the elixir of vi'riol; warm aromatics, with a due proportion of rhu- barb, infufed in wine, and fuch like. His diet ought to be dry and nourifhing, fuch as is recommended in the beginning of the chap- ter, and he fhould take as much; exercife as he can bear without fatigue. He fhould wear flannel next his fkin, and make daily ufe of the flefh-brufh. CHAP. XL. OF THE GOUT. _ HERE is no difeafe which fhews more the imperfeaion of medicine, or lets the advantages of temperance and exercife in a ftronger light, than the gout. Excefs and idlenefs are the true four- ces from whence it originaly fprung, and all who would avoid it muft be atlive and temperate. Though idlenefs and intemperance are the principal caufes of the gout, yet many other things may contribute to bring on the dif- order. and to induce a paroxyfm in thefe who are fubjea to it; as interne ftudy; too free an ufe of acidulated liquors; night-watch- ing ; grief or uneafinefs of mind; an obftruaion or defea of any ofthe cuftomary difcharges, as the menfes, fweating of the feet, per- fpiration, Sec. SYMPTOMS.—A fit of the gout is generally preceded by indigeftion, drowfinefs, belching of wind, a flight head-ach, ficknefs, and fometimes vomiting. The patient complains of wearinefs and dejeaion of fpirits, and has often a pain in the limbs, with a fe-fa- tion as if wind or cold water were pairing down the thigh. The appetite is often remarkably keen a day ^r two before the fit, and there is a flight pain in pafling urine, and frequently an involuntary fhedding of tears. Sometimes thefe fymptoms are much more vio- lent, efpecially uponthe near approach of the fit; and it has been ob- ferved, that as is the fever which ufhers in the gout, fo will the fit be; if the fever be fhort and fharp, the fit will be fo Iikewife; if it be feeble, long and lingering, the fit will be fuch alfo. But this ob- fervation can only hold with relpea to very regular fits of the gout. The regular gout generally makes its attack in the fpring or be- ginning of winter, in the following manner: About two or three in the morning, the patient is feized with a pain in his gr-at toe, fbme- * The very name of an operation is dreadful to moft people, and «hey wifh to try every thing before they have recourse to it. This is the reafon why tapping fo feldom fucceeds to our wifh. 1 have had a patient who was reyularly tapped once a month for feveral years, and who ufed to eat her dinner as well afterthe operation as if nothing had happened. She d>ed at laft rather worn out by age than the difeafe. OF IHE GOUT. 265 times in the heel, and at other times in the ancle or calf of the leg. This oain is accompanied with 1 fenfation as ifcoldwa'er were pour- ed upon the part, which is fucceeded by a fhivering, with fome de- gree of fever. Afterwards the pain increafes, an", fixing among the fmall b nes of the foot, the patient feeh all *^e different kinds of torture, as if the part were ftr.-tched, burnt, fqueezed, gnawed, or torn to pieces. Ihe part at length becomes fo exquifitely fenfible, that the patient cannot bear to have it touched, or even fuffer any perfon to walk acrofs the room. The patient is generally in exquifife torture for twenty-four * hours from the time of the coming on of the fit; he then becomes eafier, the part begins to fwell, appear red, and is cohered with a little moifture. Towards morning he drops afleep, and generally falls into a gentle breathing fweat; This terminates the firft par- oxyfm, a number of which conflitutes a fit of the gout; which is longer or fhorter, according to the patient's age, ftrength, the feafon of the year, and the difpofition of the body to this difeafe; The patient is always worfe towards nights and eafier in the morning. The paroxyfms however generally grow milder every day, till at length the difeafe is carried off by perfpiration, urine and the otrrr evacuations. In fome patients this happens in a few days ; in others it requires weeks, and in fome, months, to finuh the fin Thofe whom age and frequent fits of the gout ha*ve great- ly debijitated, feldom get free from it before the approach of fum- mer, and fometimes not till it be pretty far advanced. REGIMEN.—As there are no medicines yet known that will cure the gout, we fhall confine our obfervations chiefly to regimen, both in and out of the fit. In the fit, if the patient be younjr, and ftrong, his diet ought to be thin and cooling, and his drink of a diluting nature; "but where the conftitution is weak, and the patient has been accuftomed to live high, this is not a proper time to retrench. In this cafe he muft keep nearly to his ufual diet, and fhould take frequently a cup of ^ ftrong negus, or a glafs of generous wine. Wine-whey is a very " proper drink in this cafe, as it promotes the perfpiration without grea'ly heating the patient. It will anfwer this purpofe betterif a tea-fpoonful oFfat volatile oleofum, or fpirits of hartshorn, be put into \ a cup of it twice a-day. It- will Iikewife be proper to give at bed- time a teaTfpoonful of the volatile tinaure of guaiacum in a^ large draught of warm wine-whey. This will greatly promote perfpiration through the night. As the molt fafeand efficacious method of difcharging the gouty matter, is by perfpiration, this ought to be kept up by all means, efpecially in the affeaed part. For th;s purpofe theleg and foot fhould be wrapt in foft flannel, fur, or wool. The laft is moft readily ob- tained, and feems to anfwer the purpofe better than any thing elfe. The people of Lancafhirelook upon wool as a kind of fpecific in the gout. They wrap a great quantity of it about the leg and foot af- feaed, and cover it with a fkin of foft drefled leather. This they fuffer to continue for eight or ten days, and fometimes for a fort- night or Uhree weeks, or longer, if the pahrdoes not ceafe* I nsv« (18) ,^ OF THE GOUT. er knew anv external application anfwer fo well in the gout. I have ofren feen'it applied when the iwellmg and inflammation we*e ™ea" withPvPiolent pain, and have found all thefe fymptoms re- lieve! by it in a few days. The wool which they ufe is generally gSed/and carded or combed. They choofe the fofteft which c n be had, and feldom or never remove it HI the fit be entirrly gone ot. The patient ought Iikewife to be kept quiet and eafy during the fit. Everv thing that affeas the mind difturbs the pamxvfm, and tends to throw the gout upon the nobler parts. All external appli- cations that repel the matter are to be avoided ~s death. I ih°y do not cure the difeafe, but remove it from a fafer to a more q; nger- ous part of the body, where it often prove.- fatal. A fit of the gout is to bo co:-fidered as Nature's method of removing-fomething that might prove deftruaive to the body, and all that we can do, with fafety, is to promote her intentions, and to "flift her in expelling the enemy in h r own way. Evacuation by bl eding, ftool, &c. are iikewife to be ufed with edition, they do not r move the caufe of the difeafe, and fometimes by weakening the patient, prolong the ./ fit; but where the co fcltution is able to bear it, i will be of ufe to keep the body gently open bv diet, or very mild laxative medicines, i Many things will indeed thorten a fit of the g >ut, and f me will drive it off altogether: but not ing has yet been f u d wltic will do this with fafety to the patient. In pain we eagerly grafp at any thing that promifes immediate eafe, and even hazard "life i; f elf for a temporary relief. This is the *rue reafon why fo many mfa'liaols remedies have been propofed for the gout, and why furh numbers have loft their lives by the ufe of them. It w---uld be as prudent to ftop the fmall-pox from rifing, and to drive thorn hto the blood, as to attempt to repel the gouty matter after it Irs been thrown upon the extremities. Thelater is as much, an effort .of Nature t free herfelf from an offending caufe as the former, a-d ought equally to be promoted. - When the pain however is very great, and the patient is reft- iefs, thir-y or forty drops of laudanum, more or lefs, acceding' to - the violence of the fymptoms, may be taken at bed-tme. This will eafe the pain, procure reft, promote perfpiration, and forward the crifis of the difeafe. Aff er the fit is over, the patient ought to take a gentle dofe or two of the bitter tinaure of rhubarb, or fome othsr worm ftomach* ic purge. He fhould alfo drink a weak infufion of ftomachir biriers in fmall wine or ale, as the Peruvian bark, with cinnamon, Virginia fnake-roo% and orange-peel. The diet at this time fhould be l?ght but nourifrnng, and gentle exercife ought to be taken on horfeback, or in a carriage. Out of the fit, it is ia the patient's power to do many things* towards preventing a return of the diforder, or rendering the fit, if it fhould return, lefs fevere. This, however, is not to be attempted by medicine. I have frequently known the gout kept off for fev- eral years, by the Peruvian bark and other af rirgent medicines; but in all the cafes where I'had occafion to fee this tried, the perfons died fuddenly, and, to all appearance, for want of a regular fit of OF IHE GOUT. &>} Ihe gout. One would be apt, from hence, to conclude, that a fit of the gout to fome conftitutions, in the decline of life, is rather lalu^ tary than hurtful. „ „ „ T , .. Though it may be dangerous to ftop a fit of the gout by medi- cine, yet if the conftitution can be fo charged by diet and exercife, as to leffen or totally to prevent its return, there certainly can be no danger in following fuch a courfe. It is well known that the whole habit mav be fo altered by a proper regimen, as quite to eradicate this difeafe; and thofe only who have fufficient refolution to perfilt in fuch a courfe have reafon to exnea a cure. The courfe which we would recommend for preventing the gout, is as follows : In the firft'place, univerfal temperance. In the next place fufficient exercife* By this we do not mean fauntering abourin an iridol nt manner, but labour, fweat, and toil. I hefe cnly can render the humours wholefome, and keep them fo. Going ear- ly to bed, and rifing betimes, are alfo of great importance. It is Iikewife proper to avoid night ftud;es, and all intenfe thought. The fupper fhould be light, and taken early. All ftrong liquors, efpe- cially generous wines and four punch, are to be avoided. We would 1 kewife recommend fome dofes of magnefla alba, and rhubarb to be taken every Spring and autumn; and afterwards a ■ courfe of ftomachic bitters, as ^anfy or water-trefoil tea, an infufion of gentian and camomile flowers, or a decoaion cf burdock root, t Sec. Any of thefe, or an infufion of any wholefome bitter fhat is more agreeable to the patient, may be drank for two or three weeks in March and Oaober, twice a-day. An iffue or perpetual bhlt- er has a great tendency to prevent the gout. If thefe were more generally ufed in the decline of life, they would nrt only of ten pre- vent the gout, but alfo other chronic maladies. Such as can afford to go to Bath, will find great benefit from baling aad drmkmg the water. It both promotes digeftion and mvgorates the habit'. Though there is little room for medicine during a regular htof the gout, yet when it leaves the extremities, and falls on <ome of the internal parts, proper applications to recall and fix it, become abio- * lately neceffary. When the g-ut affeas the head, the pain of the joints ceafes, and the fwelling difappears, while either fevere head- ach, drowfiuefs, trembling, gi Idinefs, convulfions, or dchrium come on. When it feizes the lungs, er-at oppreffion, with cough and difficulty of breathing, enfue. If it attacks the ftomach, extreme ficknefs, vomiting, anxiety, pain in the epigraftnc region, and total lofs of ftreng'h will fucceed. . „ When the gout attacks the ^ead or kings, every method muft be taken to fix ft in the feet. They muft be frequently ba;hed m warm water, and acrid cataplafms applied to the teles. Bhftei ng plafters ought Iikewife to be applied to the ancles or calves o: the legs. Bleeding in the feet or ancles is alfo neceffary, and warm Sachic purees. The patient ought to keep m bedtor the moft part, if there be anv figns of inflammation, and fhould be vs*y care- ful not to catch cold. , *Somemakea frcret of curing the gout by Macula* '""""j wWrw'u howeWr, is as old as Celfu,, who ttrongly recommends that m.,«,e of cure:; ar.d whoever w.U libm:t toivn the fulleft extent, may etpefl Co -mo M:i *rt permanent a..^ .a,- £68 OF THE RHEUMATISM. If it attacks the ftomach with a fenfe of cold, the moft warm cordials are neceffary ; as ftrong wine boiled up with cinnamon or other fpices; cinnamon-water; peppermint-water; and even brandy or rum.* The patient fhould keep his bed, an ! endeavour to pro- mote a fweat, by drinking warm liquors; and if he fhor.ld be troub- led with a naufea, or inclination to vomit, he may drink camomile- tea, or any thing that will make him vomit freely. When the gout attacks the kidneys, and imitates gravel-pains, the patient ought to drink freely of a decoaion of marfh-mallows, and to have the parts fomented with warm water. An emollient clyfler ought Iikewife to be given, and afterwards an opiate. If the pain be very viofent, twenty or thirty drops of laudanum may be taker, in a cup of the decoction. Perfons woo hav^ hai the gout fhould be very attentive to any complaints that may happen to them about the time when they have re-fon to expea a return of the fit. The gout imitates many other diforders, and by being miftaken for them, and treated improperly^ is often diverted from its regular courfe, to the great daDger of the patient's life. Thofe who never had the gout, but who, from their conftitution or manner of living, have reafon to expea it, ought Iikewife to be very circumfpea with regard to its firft approach. If the difeafe, by wrong condua,or improper medicines, be diverted from its regu- lar courfe, the miferable patient has a chance to be ever after tor, mented with head-aches, coughs, pains.of the ftomach and inteftines; and to fall at laft a viaim to its attack upon fome of the more noi ble parts.f OF THE RHEUMATISM. THIS difeafe has often a refemblance to the gout. It gene- rally attacks the joints witnexquifite'pain, and is fometimes attend- ed with inflammation and fwelling. it is moft common in the fpring, and towards the end cf autumn. It is ufually diftinguifhed into acute and chronic; or the rheumatifm with or without a fever. CAUSES.—The caufes of a rheumatifm are frequently the fame as thefe of an inflammatory .fever, viz. an obftruaed perfpi- ration, the immoderate ufe of ftrong liquors, and the like. Sud* den changes of tfle weather, and all quick Iranfitions from heat to c(*d, are very apt to occafion the rheumatifm. The moft extra- ordinary cafe of a rheumatifm that I ever faw, where alnoft every joint of the body was diftorted, was a man who ufed tO'V^rk one part of the day by fir% and the other part of it in water. Very oMtinate rheumatifms have Iikewife been brought on by perfons not accustomed to it, allowing t.eir feet to continue long wet. The lame effects are often produced by wet clothes, damp beds, fitting or lying on the damp ground, travelling in the night, &c. 1 he rheumatifm may Iikewife be occafioned by exceffive evac- uations, or the ftoppage of cuftomary difcharges. It is often the 1 '"S'V'V f°V d t0 be an efficac-.ou- remeffy in this cafe. cafe PiJfaitol&^X'S^ P'" dS VrX-] °! ^ P**'1*' for *e cu« of this dif- fUining from Jverv JJ 5&fn wfa™.wat" '» f1™ honrs, a glaf, everv teTj aU T» Fraicc. OF THE RHEUMATISM. 260 fcffea of chronic difeafes, which vitiate the humours; as the fcurvy, the lues venerea, obftinate autumnal agues, &c. The rheumatifm prevails in c>ld, damp, marfhy countries. It is moft common among the poorer fort of peafants, who are ill ctotli- ed, live in low damp houfes, and eat coarfe and unwholefome food, which contains but little nourifhment, and is not eafily digefred. SYMPTOMS.—The acute rheumatifm commonly begins with wearinefs, fhivering, a quick pulfe, reftleffnefs, thirft, and other fymp- toms of fever. Afterwards the patient complains of flying pains, which are increafed by the leaft ra tiro. Thefe at length fix in the^ joints, which are cften affeaed with fwelling and inflac maton. If blood be let in this difeafe, it has generally rhe fame appearance as in the pleurify. In this kind of rheumatifm the treatment of the paoent is near- ly th-a fame as in an acute or inflammatory fever. If he be ycung and ftrong, bleeding is neceflary, which may be repeated according to the exigencies of rhe cafe. The bo ly ought Iikewife to be kept open f)y emollient clyfters, or cool opening liquors; as deccaions of tamarinds, cream of tartar, whey, fenna-tea, and the like. The d^etfhould be light, and in fmallquantity, ccnofting chiefly of roaft- ed apples, groat-gruel, or weak caicken broth. After the feverifh fy nptoms have abated, if the pain ftill continues, the patient muft keep his bed, and take fuch things as promote perfpiration, as wine- whey, with fpiritits Mindereri, Sec. He may Iikewife take, for a few nights, at bed-time, in a cup of wine-whey, a drachm of the cream of tartar, aod alf a drachm of gum guaiacum in powder. Warm bathing, after proper evacuations, has often an exceed- ing good effea. The patien t may either be put into a bath of warm water, or have cloths wrung out of it applied to the parts affeaed. Great care muft be tak-n that he do not catch cold after bathing. T ie chronic rheumatifm is feldom attended with any confider- able degree .f fever, and is generally confined to fome particular part of the body, as the fhoul iers, the back, or the loins. There is feldom any inflammation or fwelling in this cafe. Perfons in the decfne of life are moft fubjea to the chronic rheumatifm. In fuch patie tsit often proves extremely obftinate andfometimes incurable. Iu this kind of rheumatifm the regimen• fhould be nearly the fame as in the acute, Cool and diluting diet, confifting chiefly of vegetable fubftances, as ftewed prunes, coddted apples, currai ts or goofe-berries boil d in milk, is moft proper. Arbuthnot fays, " If there be a fpecific in aliment for the rheumatifm, it is certainly whey;" and adds, that he " knew a perfon fubject to this difeafe, who could never be cured by any other mrthod but a diet of whey and bread." He Iikewife fays, that " cream of tartar h water- gruel, taken for feveral days, will eafe rheumatic pains confidera- blv." This I have often experienced, but found it always more efficacious when joined with gum guaiacum, as already direaed.— In this cafe the patient may take the dofe formerlv mentioned, twice a-day, and Iikewife a tea-fpoonfull of the volatile tinaure of gum guaiacum, at bed-time, in wine-whey.^ This courfe may be contin*qed for a week, or longer, if the a70 OF THE RHEUMATISM. cafe proves obftinate* and the patient's ftrength will permit. It ougat ihen to be omitted for a few days, and repeated again. At the fame time leeches, or a bUftering plafter may be applied to the part jffeaed What I have generally found anfwer better than eith- er of thefe, in obftinate fixed rheumatic pains, is the warm plafter.^ I have Iikewife known a plafter of Burgundy pitch worn for fome tim. on the part affeaed, give great relief in rheumatic pains. My ingenious friend Dr. Alexander, of Edinburgh, fays, he has fre- quendy cured very obftinate rheumatic pains, by rubbing* the part affeaed, with tinaure of cantharides. When the common tinaure did not fucceed, h? ufed it cf a double or treble ftrength. Cupping upon the part affeaed, is Iikewife often very beneficial, and fo is the application of leeches. Though this difeafe may not feem to yield to medicines for fome time, yet they ought ftill to be perfifted in. Perfons who are fub- jea to frequent returns of the rheumatifm, will often find their ac- count in ufing medicines, whether they be immediately affeaed with the difeafe or not. The chr nic rheumatifm is fimilar to the gout in this refpea, that the moft proper time for ufing medicines to ex- tirpate it, is when the patient is moft free from the diforder. To thofe who can afford the expenfe, I would recoommend the warm baths of Buxt m or Mail ck in Derbyshire. Thefe have, of- ten, to ray knowledge, cured v.ry obftinate rheumatifms, and are always fafe either in or out of the fit. When the rheumatifm is complicated with fcorbutic complaints, which is not feldom the cafe, the Han owgate waters, and thofe of Moffat, are proper. They fhould both be drank and ufed as a warm bath. There are feveral of our own domeftic plants which may be ufed with advaiv age in the rheumatilm. One of the beft is the white muflard. A table-fpoonful of the feed of this plant may be taken twice or thrice a-day, in a glafs of water or fmall wine. The wa- ter-trefoil is Iikewife of great ufe in this complaint. It may be in- fufed in wine or ale, or drank in form of tea. The ground-ivy, camomile, and feveral other bitters, are alfo beneficial, and may be ufed in t :e fame manner. No benefit however is to beexpeaed from thefe unlefs they be taken for a confiderable time. Excellent medicines are often defpifed in this difeafe, becaufe'they do not per- form an immediate cure; whereas nothing would be more certain than their effea, were they duly perfifted in. Want of perfever- ance in the ufe of medicines, is one reafon why chronic difeafes are fo feldom cured. Cold ..athing, efpecially in falt-water, often cures the rheuma- liim. We would alfo recommend riding on horfeback, and wear- ing flannel next the fkin. lffues are-likewife very proper efpecial- ly in chronic cafes. If the pain aftefts the fnoulders, an iffue may be made m the arm; but if it affeas the loins, it fhould be put into the leg or thigh. * Perfons affliaed with the fcurvy are very fubjea to rheumatic complaints. 1i.e beft medicines in this cafe are bitters and mild purgatives. Xaele may either be taken fepaiately or together, as * Sec Appendii, Warm Plafter. QF THE SCURVY. 271 the patient inclines. An ounce of Peruvian bark, and half an ounce of rauoarb in pow ler, may be infufed in a bottle of wine; and one, twn, or three wine-glaffes of it taken daily, as fhall be found neceffary for keeping the body gently open- In cafes where jthe bark itfelf proves fufficiently purgative, the rhubarb may be omtted. % Such as are fubjea to frequent attacks of the rheumatifm, ought to make choice of a dry, warm fituation, to avoid the night- air, wet clathfs, and wet feet, as much as poffible. Their clothing - fh uld be warm, and t ey fhould wear flannel next their ikin, and make frequent ufe of the flefh brufh. CHAP. XLI. OF THE SCURVY. X HIS difeafe prevails chiefly in cold northern countries, ef- pecilly in 1 w damp fituations, near large marfhes, or great quan- titi s of ft gnating water. Sedentary people, of a dull melancholy difp fition, are moft fubj a to it. It proves often fatal to failors on long v yages, particularly in (hips that are not Droperly ventilated, have many p- pie on board, or where cleanlinefs is negfeaed. It is not neceffary to mention the different fpecies into which this difeafe has been d v ded, as they differ from one anather chiefly in degree* What is called t; e land fcurvy, however, is feldom at- tended with th fe highly putrid fymptoms which appear in patients who have been long at fea, and which, we prefume, are rather ow-. ing to confioed air, want of exercife, and the unwholefqme food eat- en by fail rs ou long voyages, than to any fpecific oifference in the difeafe. CAUSES.--The fcurvy is occafioned by cold moift airj by the long ufe of falted or fmoke dried provifions, or any kind of food that is hard of digeftion, and affords little nourifhment. It may alfo proceed from the fuppreffion of cuftomary evacuations; as the men* fes, hsemorrh idal flux, &c. It is fometimes owing to an heredit- ary taint, in which cafe a very fmall caufe will excite the latent dif- order. Grief, fear, and other depreffing paffions, have a great ten- dency both to excite a.nd aggravate this difeafe. The fame obfer- vation holds with regard to neglea of cleanlinefs; bad clothing; the wmtofpr per exercife; confined air; unwholefome food; or any difeafe which greatly weakens the body, or vitiates the humours. SYMPTOMS.—This difeafe maybe known by unufual weari- nefs, heaviaefs, and difficulty of breathing, elpecidly after motion; r-ttennefs cf the gums, which are apt to ble-don the flighteft touch; a flanking breath; frequent bleeding at the nofe; crackling of the ioiots; difficulty of • walking; fometimes a fwelling and fometimes a falling away of the legs, on which there are livid, yellow, or violet coloured fpots; the face is generally cf a pale or leaden colour. As the difeafe advances, other fymptoms come on; as rottennefs of the teeth, haemorrhages or difcharges of blood from different parts of the bodv, foul obftinate ulcers, pains in various parts, efpeciahy 272 OF THE SCURVY. about the breaft, dry fca.iy eruptions all over the body, Sec ' At laft a w-fting i r heaic fever comes on, and the miferable patient is often car ied :-ff by a dyfentery, a diarrhoea, a dropfy, the palfy, fainting fit.*, or a mortification of fome of the bowels. CURE.—We know no way of curing this difeafe but by pur- fuing a plan direaiy oppofite fo that which brings it on. It proceeds from a vitiated ftate of the humours, occafioned by errors in diet, air, or exercife; and this cannot beitmoved but by a proper attenr tion to thefe important articles. If the patient has been obliged to breathe a cold, damp, or con- fined air, ho fhould be removed, as foon as poffible to a dry, open, and moderately warm one. If there is reafon to believe that the difeafe proceeds from a fedentary life, or depreffing paffions, as grief, fear, Sec. the patient muft take daily as much, exercife in the open air as he can bear, and his mind fhould be diverted by cheerful company and other amufements. Nothing has a greater tendency either to prevent or remove this d feafe, than conftant che< rfulnefs. and good humour. But this, alas! is feldom the lot of perfcas af7 ftiaed with the fcurvy; they are generally furly, peevifh and morofe. '"Wli.il the fcurvy has been brought on by a long ufe of falted proviflons, the proper medicine is a diet confifting chiefly of frefh vegetables; as oranges, apples, lemons, limes, tamarinds, water- crefTts, fcurvy-grafs, brook-line, &c The ufe of thefe, with milk, pot-herbs, new bread,' and frefh beer or cyder, willfeldom failto re- move a fcurvy of this kind, if taken before it be too far -dvanced, but to have this effea they muft be perfifted in a confiderable time. When frefh yegetables cannot be obtained, pickled or preferved ones may be ufed; and wnere thefe are wanting, recourfe muft be had to the chyrnical acids. All the patient's food and drink fhould in this cafe be fharpened with cream of tartar, elixir of vitriol, vin- egar, or the fpirii cf fea-f-.lt. Thefe things however will more certainly prevent than cure the fcurvy, for which reafon lea-faring people, efpecially on long voya, ges, ought to lay in plenty of them. Cabbages, onions, goofeber- nes, and many ether vegetables, mav be kept along time, by pickling^ preferving, Sec. and when thefe fail, the chyrnical acids, recommen- ded above, which will keep for any length of time, may be ufed. We have reafon to believe, if lhips were wall ventilated, had got itore of fruits, greens, cyder, &c. laid in, and if proper regard were paid to cleanlinefs and warmth, that failors would be the moft healthy people in the world, and would feldom fuffer either from the fcurvy or putrid fevers, which are fo fatal to th-t ufeful fet of men; but it is too much the temper of fuch peonk to defpife all pre- caution; they will not think of any calamity till it overtakes them, when it istoo late to ward off the blow. ' newer to mlkP *&* °Ted'that mS?y £thera have [t not ">their ftTthe nW % K°-V,'fl0n T are fpealPng of 5 but in this cafe it is tne duty cf their employer to make t for them- and no man^ought to engage in along voyage without havh^ tnefe articled I have often feen very extraordinary effeas in the land fcurvy OF THE SCUKVY. . 273 from a milk diet. This preparation of nature is a mixture of ani- mal and vegetable properties, which of all Others is the moft fit for^ reftoring a decayed conftitution, and removing that particular acri- " mony of the humours, which feems to conflitute the very effence of. the fcurvy, and many other difeafes. But people defpife this whole- fome and nourifhing food, becanfe it is cheap, and devour with greedinefs, flefh and fermented liquors, while milk is only deemed fit for their hogs. The ifcoft proper drink in the fcurvy, is whey or butter-milk. When thefe cannot be had, found cyder, perry, or ipruce-beer, may be ufed. Wort has Iikewife been found to be a proper drink in the fcurvy, and may be ufed at fea, as malt will keep during the longeft voyage. A decoaion of the tops of the fpruce fir is iikewife prop- Ier. It may be drank in the quantity of an Englifh pint twioe a-day. Mar-water may be ufed for the fame purpofe, or decoaions of any of the mild mucilaginous vegetables: as farfaparilla, marfh-mallow roots, &c. Infufions of the bitter plants, as ground ivy, the leffer centaury, marfh-trefoil, &c. are Iikewife beneficial. I have feen j . peafants in fome parts of Britain exprefs the juice of the laft men- t tioned plant, and drink it with good effea in thofe foul fcorbutic eruptions, with which they are often troubled in the fpring feafon. Harrowgate water is certainly an excellent medicine in the land- fcurvy. I have often feen patients who had been reduced to the moft deplorable condition by this difeafe, greatly relieved by drink- ing the fulphur-water, and bathing in it. 'Fhe chalybeate-water may alto be ufed with advantage, efpecially with a view to brace the ftomach after drinking the fulphur water, which though it fharpens : the appetite, never fails to weaken the powers of digeftion. A flight degree of fcurvy may be carri ;d offby frequently fuck- ing a little of the juice of a bitter orange or lemon. When the dif- eafe affeas the gums only, this praaice, if continued for lbmetime, will generally carry it off. We would however recommend the bilter orange as greatly preferable to lemon, it feems to be as good a medicine, and is not near fo hurtful to the ftomach. Perhaps our own forrel may be little inferior to either of them. All kinds of falad are good in the fcurvy, and ought to be eat- en veryplentifullv,asfpinnage, lettuce^ pariley, celery, endive, rad- ifh, dandelion, &c. It is amazing to fee how foon frefh vegetables in the fpring, cure the brute animals of any fcab or foulnefs which is upon their fkins. It ia reafonable to fuppofe that their effeas would be as great upon the human fpecies, were they ufed in prop- er quantities for a fufficient length of time. I have feen good effeas in fcorbutic complaints of very long ftaading, from the ufe of a decoaion of the roots of water dock. It is ufually made by boiling a pound of the frefh root ia fix Englifh pints of water, till about one third of it be confumed. The dog is f. from half a pint to a whole pint of the decoaion every day. But ' *m all the cafes where I have feen it prove beneficial, it was made much ftronger, and drank in larger quantities. The fafeft way, however, is for the patient to begin with fmall dofes, and mcreale them both in ftrength and quantity as he finds his ftomach w bear 5?4 OF THE SCROPHULA, OR it. It muft be ufed for a confiderable time. I have known fome take it for many months, and have been told of others who had ufed it for feveral years, before they were fenfible of any benefit, butrwho neverthelefs were cured by it at length. The 1 profy, which was fo common in the country long ago, feems to have been near a-kin to the fcurvy. Perhaps its appear- ing f" feldom now, may be owing to the inhabitants of Britain eat- ing more vegetable food than formerly, living more upon tea and other dilu ing diet, ufing lefs falted meat, being more cleanly, bet- ter lodged andcloathed, &c. For toe cure of this difeafe we would recommend the fame courfe of diet and medicine as in the fcurvy. OF THE SCROPHULA, OR KING'S EVIL. THIS difeafe chiefly affeas the glands, efpecially thofe of the neck. Children and young perfons of a fedentary life are very fub- iea to it. It is one of thole difeafes which may be removed by proper regimen, but feldom yields to medicine The inhabitants of cold, damp, marfhy countries, are moft liable to the fcrophula. CAUSES.— this difeafe may proceed from an hereditary taint, from a fcorphulous nurfe, &c. Cnildren who have the misfortune to be born of fickly parents, whofe conftitutions have been greatly injured by the pox, or other chronic difeafes, are apt to be ffeaed wi'.h the fcrophula. It may Iikewife proceed from fuch difeafes as weaken the habit or vifiate the humours, as the fmall pox, meafles, Sec. External injuries, as blows, bruifes, and the like, fometimes produce fcrophulous ulcers; but we have reafon to believe, when this happens, 'that there has been a predi'pofition in the habit to this difeafe. In fhort, whatever tends to vkiate the humours or relax the folids, paves the way to the fcr p'. ula; as the want of proper exercife, too much heat or cold, c nfined air,unwholefome food, bad water, the long ufe of poor, weak, watery aliments, the negfea of cleanlinefs, &c. Nothing tends more to produce tas difeafe in children, than allowing them to c ntinue long wet.* SYMPTOMS.—At firft fmall knots appear under the chin, or, behind the ears, which gradually increafe in numrer and fize, till they form one large hard tumour. This oftan continues for a long time without breaking, and when it does break, it only difehar- ges a thin fanies, cr watery humour. Oiher parts of t e body are Iikewife liable to its attack, as tae aro-pits, groins, feet, hands, eyes, breafts, Sec. Nor are the internal parts exempt fro a it. It often affeas the lungs, liver, or fpleen; and I have fr. quenrly feen the glands of the myfentery greatly enlarged by it. Thofe obftinate ulcers which bre.k out uo n fe feet andhan^s with fwelling, and little or no rednefs, are of 'the fcrophul us kind. They fejdom difcharge good matter, and are exceedingly oifficult to cur j* i?e u'hite fwellinSs cf the )omts feem Iikewife to be of this kmd. I hey are with difficulty brought to a fuppuration, ano when open?d, they only difcharge a thia ichor. There is not a more gen; eral lymptom ot thefcrcphula than a fwelling of the upper lip and nofe. * The fcrophula, as well as the rickets, is found to prevail in larep man„f,A.„;»- towns, whe.e people live grofs, and lead fedenurv lives. 8C manufacturing Ot- KING'S EVIL. 27c REGIMEN.—As this difeafe proceeds, in a great meafure, from relaxation, the diet ought to be generou: and nourifhing, but at the fame time light and eafy of digeftion: as wall fermented bread, made of fou- d grain, the flefh and broth of young animals, with now and then a glafs of generous, wine, or good ale. Tne air ought to be open, dry and not too qojjd, and the patient fnould take as much exercife as he can bear, This is of the utmoft importance. Child- ren who have fufficient exercife, are feldom troubled with the fcro- phula, MEDICINE,—The vulgar are remarkably credulous with re- gard to the cure of the fcrophula; many of them believing in the virtue of the royal touch, that of the feventh fon, &c. The truth is, we know but little either of the nature and cure of this difeafe, and where reafon or medicines fail, fupc-rftition always comes in their place. Hence it is, that in difeafes which are moft difficult to un- derftand, we generally hear of the greateft number of miraculous cures being performed, Here, however, the deception is eafily ac- counted for. The fcrophula, at a certain period of life, often cures of itfelf; and if the patient happens to be touched about this time, the cure is imputed to the touch, and not to nature, who is really the phyfician. In the fame way the infignificant noitrums of quacks and old women, often gain applaufe wnen they deferve none. There is nothing more pernicious than the cuftom of plying children in the fcrophula with ftrong purgative medicines. People imagine it proceeds from humours which muft be purged off, with- out confidering that thefe purgatives increafe the debility, and ag- gravate the difeafe. It has indeed been found, that keeping tne body gently open for ferae time, efpecially with fea-water, has a good effea; but this fhould only be given in grofs habits, and in fuch quantity as to procure one, or at moft two ftools every day. Bathing in the fait-water has Iikewife a very good effea, efpe- cially in the warm feafon. I have often known a courfe of bathing in falt-water, and drinking it in fuch quantities as tokeip the body gently open, cure a fcrophula, after many other medicines had been tried in vain. Wh&n fait water cannot be obtained, the patienc may be bathed in frefh water, and his body kept open by fmall quanti- ties of fait and water, or fome other mild purgative. Next to cold bathing, and drinking the fait water, we would recommend the Peruvian bark. The cold bath may be ufed in fum- mer and the bark in winter. To an adult half a drachm of the bark in powder may tfe given in a glafs of red wine, four or five timer, a-day. Children j and fuch as cannot take it in fubftance, may ufe the decoaion, made in the following manner: Boil an ounce of the Peruvian bark, and a drachm of Winter s bark both grofsiy powdered, in an Englifh quart of water to a pint: towards the end, half an ounce cf fl'.ced liquorice-root, and a hand- ful of raifins may be added, which will both render the decoaion lefs difagreeable, and make it take up more of the bark. The liquor muft be ftrained, and two, three, or four table fpoonsful, accord- ing to the age of the patient, given three times a-day. The Moffat and Harrowgate * waters, efpecially the latter, :•:>e 2?6 &F THE ITCH. Iikewife very proper medicines in the fcrophula. They ought not, however, to be drank in large quantities, but ftiould be taken fo as to keep the body gently open, and muft be ufed for a ■confidera- ble tirEe* n r, 1 -1 J • , The hemlock may fometimes be med with advantage in the fcrophula. Some lay it down as a general rule, that the fea-water is moft proper before there are any fuppuration or fymptoms of tabes: the Peruvian bark, when there are running fores, and a degree of heaic fever; and the hemlock in old inveterate cafes, approaching to the fcirrhous or cancerous ftate. Either the extraa, or the frefh juice of this plant may be ufed. The dofe muft be fmall at firft, and increafed gradually as far as the ftomach is able to bear it. External applications are of little ufe. Before the tumour breaks nothing ought to be applied to it, unlefs a piece of flannel, or fome- thing to keep it warm. After it breaks, the fore may be dreffed with fome digeftive ointment. What I have always found to anfwer beft, was the yellow bafilicon mixed with about a fixth cr eighth part of its weight of red precipitate of mercury. The fore may be dreffed with this twice a-day; and if it be very fungous, and does not digeft well, a large proportion of the precipitate may be added, Medicines which mitigate this difeafe, though they do not cure it, are not to be defpifed. If the patient can be kept alive by any means till he arrives at the age of puberty, he has a great chance to get well; but if he does not recover at this time, in all probability he never will. There is no malady which parents are fo apt to communicate to their offspring as the fcrophula, for wh ch reafon people ought to beware of marrying into families affeaed with this difeafe. For the means of preventing the fcrophula we muft refer the reader to the obfervations on nurfing at the beginning of the book. OF THE ITCH. THOUGH this difeafe is commonly communicated by' infec- tion, yet it feldom prevails where due regard is paid to cleanlinefs, frefh air and wholefome diet. It generally appears in form of fmall watery puftules, firft about the wrifts or between the fingers; aft- r- wards it affeas the arms, legs, thighs, &c. Thefe puftules are at- tended with an intolerable itching, efpecially when the patient is warm in bed, or fits by the fire. Sometimes indeed the fkin is cov- ered with large blotches or fcabs, and at other times with a white fcu£'^ ,ly eruPtion- Tms laft is called the dry itch, and is the moft difficult to cure. The itch is feldom a dangerous difeafe, uulefs when it is ren- dered lo by negfea or improper treatment, if it be fuffered to con- tinue too ong, it. may vitiate the whole mafs of humours; and if it be mddenly drove m5 without proper evacuations, it may occafion fevers, inflammations of the vifcera, or ether internal diforders rhe beftr medicine yet known for the itch i8 fulphur, which oughtto be ufed both externally and internally. The DartsTrnoft affeaed may be rubbed with an ointment madeof thfflour 0f fui phur,twoouncts;crudefalammomacfmelypowdered,twodrachm^i Of THE ITCH. 277 hog's lard, or butter, four ounces. If a fcruple or half a drachm of the effence of lemon be added, it will entirely take away the dif- agreeable fmell. About the bulk of a nutmeg of this may be rub- bed upon the extremities at bed-time twice or thrice a week. It is feldom neceffary to rub the whole body; but when it is, it ought not to be done all at once, but by turns, as it is dangerous to ftop too many pores at the fame time. Before the patient begins to, ufe the ointment, he ought, if he „ be of a full habit, to bleed or take a purge or two. It will Iikewife be proper, during the ufe of it, to take every night and morning, as much of the flour of brimftone and cream of tartar, in a little trea- cle or new milk, as will keep the body gently open. He fhould .be- ware of catching cold, fhould wear more clotnes than ufual, and take every thing warm. The fame clothes, the linen excepted, ought to be worn all the time of ufing theointment; and fuch clothes as have been worn while the patient was under the difeafe, are not to be ufed again, unlefs they have been fumigated with brimftone, and thoroughly cleanfed, otherwile they will communicate the in- > , feaion anew.* '' I never knew brimftone, when ufed as direaed above, fail to the itch; and I have reafon to believe, that if duly perfifted in, it never will fail, but if it be only ufed once or twice, and cleanlinefs neglea^d, it is no wonder if the diforder returns. The quantity of ointment mentioned above will generally be fufficient for the cure of one perfon; but if any fymptoms of the difeafe fhould appear again, the medicine muft be repeated. It is both more fafe and efficacious when perfifted in for a confiderable time than when a large quantity is applied at once. As moft people diflike the fmell of ful- phur, they may ufe in its place the powder of white hellebore root made up into an ointment, in the fame manner, which will feldom fail to cure the itch. Peopleoughtto be extremely cautious left they take other erup- tions for the itch; as the ftoppige of t^efe may be attended with fatal confequences. Many of the eruptive diforders to which chil- dren are liable, have a near refemblance to this difeafe; and I have often known infants killed by being rubbed with greafy ointments * mat make thefe eruptions ftrike fuddenly in, whichnature had thrown out to preferve the patient's life, or prevent fome other malady. Much mifchief is Iikewife done by the ufe of mercury in this difeafe. Some perfons are fo fool-hardy as to wafh the parts af- fected with a ftrong folution of the corrofive fublimate. Others ufe the mercurial ointment, without taking the leaft care either to avoid cold, keep the body open, or obferve a proper regimen. Th° confe- quences of fuchcondua maybe eafily gueffed. I have known even the mercurial girdles produce bad effeas, and would advife every perfon, as he values his he lth, to beware how he ufes them. Mer- cury ought never to be ufed as a medicine without the greateft care. * * Sir John Prinqle o'jferves, that though this difeafe may feem trifling, there is ns one in the army that is mire troublefome to cur*, as the irifetlion ofren lurks in clothes, &c and breaks out a fecond, or even a thirJ timi'. Ihe fz'.n: incontenierii y occurs in private families, unlefs particular regard is paid to tl r changing or Waning cf t'ncir ;!jthcs; which ,'all i« by m ratals an ea^y o'ention. u73 OF THE ASTHMA. Ignorant people look upon thefe girdles as a kind of charm, with- out confidering that the mercury enters the body. It is not to be told what mifchief is done by ufing mercurial ointment for curing the itch and killing vermin; yet it is unnecef- fary for either: the former may be always more certainly cured by fulphur, and the latter will never be found where due regard is paid to cleanlinefs. Thofe who would avoid thisdeteftable difeafe ought to beware of infeaed perfons, to ufe wholefome food, and to ftudy univerfal cleanlinefs.* CHAP. XLII. < OF THE\ ASTHMAi JL HE afthma is a difeafe of the lungs, which feldom ad- mits of a cure. Perfons in the decline of life are moft liable to it. It is diftinguifhed into the moift and dry, or humeral and nervous. The former is attended with expeaoration or fpk ing ; but in the latter the patient feldom fphs, unlefs fometimes a little tough phlegm by the mere force of coughing. CAUSES.—The aflhma is fometimes hereditary. It may like- wife proceed fr ;m a bad formation of fhe breaft ; the fumes of metals or minerals taken into the lungs; violent exercife, efpecial- ' ly running ; the obftruaion of cuftomary evacuations, as th men- fes, hemorrhoids, Sec. the fudden retroceffion of the gouf, or ftriking in of eruptions, as the fmall-pox, meafles, Sec. vHeot paf- fions of tie mind, as fudden fear or furprife. In a word, the dftV. eafe may proceed from any caufe that either impedes the circula- tion of the blood through the lungs, or prevents their being duly expanded by the air. SYMPTOMS.—An anfthma is known by a quick laborious breathing, which is generally performed with a kino* of wheezing noife. Sometimes the difficulty of breathing is fo great, that the patient is obliged to keep in an erea poftur-, otherwife he is in danger of being fnffocated. A fit or paroxvifm of the afthma gen- erally happens afte- a perfon has been expofed to ccld eafterly win-h, or has been abroad in thick foggy weather, cr has got wet, cr continued long in a damp plaee under ground, or has taken fome food which the ftomach could not digeft, as pafterks, toafted cheefe, or th * hke. „ . The paroxyfm is commonly ufhered in with liftleffnefs, want ot fieep hoarfenefs, a cough, belching cf wind, a feafe of heavinefs" aoout the breaft, and difficulty of breathing. To thefe fueled heat, fever pain of the head, ficknefs and naufea, great oppreffion of the breaft, palpitation of the heart, a weak and fometimes inter- * Theitrh is no*by cleanlinefs banilhed from everygenteel family :n BrMin It *iM SS^raSd^TfifhH? fT fj^™ ^ 4'^' anH ^onfthe manufaa- nrers in Lngland. 1 hefe are not only fufficient to keep the feeds of the dileafe alive but to fpread the .nfe#.on among other.. It were to be willed that W effeiu,' me thoi could £ dev.fed for «t>rpat,ng ,t altogether. Several country clergymen have told me tStlv Prttlnl fuch „ were mfefted cured and ftrongly recommending an attention tTcleanlineff thev h,»c ba^ci the itch came!/ out of their parifhei. ASTHMA. mitting pulfe, an involuntary flow of tears, bilious vomitings, Sec All the fymptoms grow worfe towards n:ght; the patient is eafier When up than in bed, and is very defirous of c~«ol air. REGIMEN.—The food ought to be light, and of eafy digef- tion. Beiled meats are to be prefered to roafted, and the flefh of young animals to that of old. All windy food, and whatever is apt to fwell in the ftomach, is to be avoided. Light puddings, white broths, aod ripe fruits baked, boiled, or roafted are proper. .Strong liquors of all kinds, efpecially malt-liquor, are hurtful. The patient mould eat a very light fupper, or rather none at all, and fhould nev- er fuffer himfelf to be long coftive. His cl thing fh uldbe warm, efpecially in the winter feafon. As all diforders of the breaft are much relieved by keeping the feet warm, and promoting the per- fpiration, a fl nnel fhirt or waiftcoat, and thick fhoes, will be of fiagular fervice. But nothing is of fo great importance in the afthma, as pure and moderately warm air. Afthmatic people can feldom bear eith- either the clofe heavy air of a large towa,or the fharp, keen atmcf- phere of a bleak hilly country ; a medium therefore, between thefe is to be chofen. The air near a large town is often better than at at a diftance, provided the patie t be removed fo far as not to be affeaed by t efmoke. Some afthmatic patients indeed, breathe ea- fier in town than in the country ; but this is feldom the cafe, efpe- cially in towns where much coal is burnt. Afthmatic perfons who are obliged to be in a town all day, ought at leaft to fle :p out of it. Even this will often prove of great fervice. Thofe who can aff rd it ought to travel into a warmer climate. Many afthmatic perfons who cannot live in Britain, enjoy very good health in the fouth of France, Portugal, Spain, or Italy. Exercife is Iikewife of very great importance in the afthma, as it promotes the digeftion, preparation of the blood. Sec. The blood of afthmatic perf ms is feldom duly prepared, owing to the proper aaion of the lungs being impeded. For this reafon fuch people oug^t d?ily to take as much exercife, either on foot, horfeback, or in a c°rria?;e, as they cm bear. MEDICINE__Almoft all that can be done by medicine In this difeafe, is to relieve the p?tient when feized wi'h a violent fit. rI his indeed requires the greateft expedition, as the difeafe often proves fuddenly fatal. In the paroxyfm or fit, the body is gener- ally bound; a purging clyfter, with a folution of afafcetida, ought therefore to be adminiftered, and if there be occafion, it may be re- peated two or three times. The patient's "feet and legs ought to be immerfed in warm water, and afterwards rubbed with a warm hand or dry cloth. Bleed ing* unlefs extreme weaknefs or old age fhould forbid it, is h:ghly pr per. If t^ere be a violent fpafm about the breaft or ftomach, warm fomentations, or bladders filled with warm milk and water, may be applied to the part affeaed; and warm cataplafms to the foles of the feet. The patient muft drink freely of diluting h'quors, and may take a tea-fpoonful of the tinaure of caftor and of faffron mixed together, in a cup of valerian tea, twice or thrice a-day. Sometimes a vomit has a very 28o OF THE APOPLEJCY. good effea, and fnatches the patient, as it were, from the jaws of death. This however will be more fafe after other evacuations have been premifed. A very ftrong infufion of roafted coffee is faid to give eafe in afthmatic paroxyfms. In the moift afthma, fuch things as promote expeaoration Or fpitting, ought to be uled; as the fyrrup of fquills, gum ammoniac, and fuch like. A common fpoonful of the fyrup or oxymel of fquills, mixed with an equal quantity of cinnamon water, may be taken three or four times through the day, and four or five pills made of equal parts of afafcetida and gum ammoniac, at bed-time.* For the convulfive or nervous afthma, antifpafmodics and bra- cers are the moft proper medicines. ^ The patient may take a tearfpoonful of the paregoric elixir twice a-day. The Peruvian bark is fometimes found to be of ufe in this cafe. It may be taken in fubftance, or infufed in wine. In fl?ort, every thing that braces the nerves, or takes off fpafm, may be of ufe in a nervous afthma. It is often relieved by the ufe of affes milk; I have Iikewife known cow's milk drank warm in the morning, have a very good effea in this cafe. In every fpecies of afthma, fetons and iffues have a good effea; they may either be fet in the back or fide, and fhould never be allowed to dry up. We fhall here, once for all, obferve, that not only in the afthma, but iumoft chronic difeafes, iffues are extreme- ly proper. They are both a fafe and efficacious remedy ; and though they do not always cure the difeafe, yet they will often pro- long the patient's life. CHAP. XLIII. OF THE APOPLEXY. A HE apoplexy is a fudden lofs of fenfe and motion, dur, ing which the patient is to all appearance dead; the heart and lungs however ftill continue to move. Though this difeafe proves often fatal, yet it may be fometimes removed by proper care. It chiefly attacks fedentary perfons of a grofs habit, who ufe a rich and plen- tiful diet, and indulge in ftrong liquors. People in the decline of hte are moft fubjea to the apoplexy. It prevails moft in winter, efpecially in rainy feafons, and very low ftates of the barometer. *^AUbiiJb.--Ihe immediate caufe of an apoplexv is a com- preffion of the brain, occafioned by an excefs of blood, or a ejec- tion of watery humors. The former is called zfanrune and the latter a/T, apoplexy. It may be occafioned by Iny thine that increafes the circulation towards the brain, or prevents the return ,.™ \After C°P'°"» evacuations, large dofes of aether have been found vPrv ,ffi, ,• • removmg a fit of the afthma. I have Iikewife known the follow" ne rnWn,?, effic,aC,0UJ ,B fappy efficls: To four or five ounces of the folution of gum-ammoni^ LJ7 "" V"V fimple cmnamon-water, the fame quantity of balfamic fyrup znihlT,' tW°r °unCes V eUxir. Of tfiiS> two tablefpoon^l ma/be takeaTver/ three hourS. °UnCC°f pareB°^ OF THE APOPLEXY. 281 of the blood'' from the head; as intenfe ftudy; violent paffions,f viewing objefts for a long time obliquely 5 wearing any thing too tight about the nec"k; a rich and luxurious diet $ fuppreffion of urine; fuffering the body to cool fuddenly after having been great- ly heatewd ; continuing long in a warm or cold bath; the exceffive nfe of fpiceries, or high feafoned food ; excefs of venery ; the fud- den ftriking in of any eruption ; fuffering iflues, fetons, Sec. fud- denly to dry up, or the ftoppage cf any cuftomary evacuation; a mercurial falivation pufhed too far, or fuddenly checked by cold; wounds or bruifes on the head; long expofure to exceffive cold; poilbnou^ exhalations, Sec. SYMPTOMS, and method of cure.~~The ufual forerunners of an apoplexy are giddinefs, pain and fwimraing of the head ; lofs of memory; drowfinefs, noife in the ears, the night mare, a fpon- taneous flux of tears, and laborious refpiration. When perfons of an apopkaic make obferve thefe fymptoms, they have reafon to fear the approach cf a fit, and fhould endeavor to prevent it by bleeding, a flender diet, and opening medicines. Io the fanguine apoplexy, if the patient does not die fuddenly, the countenance appears florid, the face is fwelled or puffed up, and the blood veffels3 efpecially about the neck and temples, are turgid; the breathing is difficult, and performed with a fhorting noife. The excrements and urine are often voided fpontaneoufly, aud the patient is fometimes feized with vomiting. In this fpecies of apoplexy every method muft be taken to lef- fen the force of the circulation towards the head. The patient fhould be kept perfeaiy eafy and cool. His head fhould be raifed pretty high, and his feet frittered to hang down. His clothes Ought to be loofened, efpecially about the neck,and frefh air admitted in- ro his chamber. His garters fnould be tied pretty tight, by which means the motion of the blood from the lower extremities will be retarded. As loan as the patient is placed in a proper pofture, he fhould be bled freely in the neck or arm, and, if there be occafion, the operation may be repeated in two or three hours. A laxative clyfter, with plenty of fweet oil, or frefh butter, and a fpoonful or two of common fait in it, may be adminiftered every two hours ; and bliftering-plafters applied between the fhculders, and to the calves of the 1 ,gs. As foon as the fymptoms are a little abated, and the patient is able to fwallow, he ought to drink freely of fome diluting open- ing liquor, as a decoaion of tamarinds and liquorice, rream-tartar whey, or comnon whey with cream cf tartar diffolved in it. Or he may take any cooling purge, as Glauber's falts, man~:a diffolved in an infufion of fenna, or the like. All fpirits and other ftrong li- quors are to be avoided. Even volatile falts held to the nofe do mifchief. Vomits, for the fame reafon, ought not to be given, cr f I knew a woman^ who in a violent fit of anger w»3 feized with a fanguine apop'e*y. She at firft complained of extreme pain, " as if daggers had been thruft through her head,? as (he expreffed it. Afterwards ihe became eomatofe, her pulfe funk neryjow, and was ex- ceeding flow. By bleeding, bliftering and other evacaations, fhe was kept alive for about a fortnight. When ber head wai opened, a large quantity of tJEtravafated Wood wa» found in rile left ventricle of tb« train, (19) 2g2 OF COSTIVENESS, 6c. any thing that may increafe the motion of the bloocf towards the head. r In the ferous apoplrxy, the fymptoms are nearly the fame, on- ly the pulfe is not fo ftrong, the countenance is lefs florid, and the breathing lefs difficult. Bleeding is not fo neceffary here as in the former cafe. It mav, however, generally be performed once with fafetv and advantage, hut fhould not be repeated. The patient fhouid be placed in the fame pofture as direaed above, and fhould have bliftermg-plaiters applied, and receive opening clyfters in the fame manner. Purges here are Iikewife neceffary, and the patient may drink ftrong balm-tea. If he be inclined to fweat, it ought to be promoted by drinking fmall wine-whey, or an infufion of car- duus benediaus. A plentiful fweat kept up for fome confiderable time, has often carried off a ferous apoplexy. When apopleaic fymptoms proceed from opium, or other narco'ic fubftances taken into' the ftomach, vomits are neceffary. The patient is generally relieved as foon as he has difcharged the poifon in this way. Perfons of an apopleaic make, or thofe who have been attack- ed by it, ought to ufe a very fpare and flender diet, avoiding all ftrong liquors, fpiceries, and high-feafoned food. They ought like- wife to guard againft all violent paffions, and to avoid the extremes of heat and cold. The head fhould be fhaved, and daily wafhed with cold water. The feet ought to be kept warm, and never fuf- fered to continue long wet. The body muft be kept open either by food or medicine, and a little blood may be let every fpring and fall. Exercife fhould by no means be negfeaed ; but it ought to be taken in moderation. Nothing has a more happy effea in pre- venting an apoplexy than perpetual iffues or fetons; great care however, muft be taken, not to fuffer them to dry up, without opening others in their ftead. Apopleaic perfons ought never to go to reft with a full ftomach, or to lie with their heads low, or to wear any thing too tight about their necks. CHAP. XLIV. OF COSTIVENESS, AND OTHER AFFECTIONS OF THE STOMACH AND BOWELS. VV E do not here mean to treat of thofe aftriaions of the bowels, which are the fymptoms of difeafe, as of the colic, the iliac pailion, &c. but only to take notice of that infrequency of ftools ^r\?l°metmf haSR:c!' and which » fome Particular conflitn- tions may occafion difeafes. ft*w^flwlfel?rmay proceed from drink»ng *cugh ^d wines, or ?t mavikew^ U1UOrSi L°° ffiU?h exerci{"e' efpecially on horfeback. It may hkewife proceed from a long ufe of cold infipid food, which in, toThe^lTn^?nUlate the intefUne?- Sometimes ft iTow- mg to the bile not defcending to the inreftines, as in the iaundice - and at other times * proceeds from difeafes of the *££££themi OF COSTIVENESS, 6c. 281 felves, as a palfy, fpafms, torpor, tumours, a cold dry ftate of the inteftines, &c. Exceffive coftivenefs is apt to occafion pains of the head, vom- iting, c dies, and other complaints of the bowels. It is peculiarly hurtful to hypochondriac and hyfteric perfons, as it generates wind and other grievous fymptoms. Some people howevc-r, can bear coftivenefs to a great degree. I know perfons who enjoy pretty good health, ye' do not go to ftool above once a week, and others not above once a fortnight. Indeed I have heard of fome who do not go above once a month. Perfons who are generally coftive, fhould live upon a moiften- ing and laxative diet, as roafted or boiled apples, pears, flewed prunes, raifins, gruels wi'h currants, butter, honev, fugar,'and fuch like. Broths with fpinnage, leeks, and other foft pot herbs, are Iikewife proper. Rye-bread, or that which is madeof a mix- ture of wheat and rye together, ought to be eaten. No perfon troubled with coftivenefs, fhoulcl eat white bread alone, efpecially that which is made of fine flour. The beft bread for keeping the" body foluble, is what in fome parts of England they call meflin. It is made of a mixture of wheat and rye, and is very agreeable to thofe who are accuftomed to it. Coftivenefs is increafed by keeping the body too warm, and by every thing that promotes the perfpiration; as wearing flannel, ly- ing too long a-bed) &c. Intenfe thought, and a fedentary life, are •Iikewife hurtful.. All the fecretion and excretions are promoted by moderate exercife without doors, and by a gay, cheerful, fprightly temper of mind. The drink fhouid be of an opening quality. All ardent fpirits, auftere and aftringent wines, as port, claret, Sec ought to be avoid- ed. Malt liquor that is fine, and of a moderate ftrength, is verv proper. Butter-milk, whey, and other watery liquors, are Iikewife proper,and maybe drank in turns,as the patient's inclination direas* Thofe who are troubled with coftivenefs, ought if poffible to remedy it by diet, as the conftant ufe of medicines for that purpofe is attended with many inconveniences, and often with bad confe- quences.* I never knew any one get into a habit of taking medi- cine for keepina; the body open, who could le we it off. In time the cuftom becomes necefTary, an 1 generally ends in ?. total relax-! ation of the bowels, indigeftion, lofs of appetite, waging of the ftrength, and death. * The learned Dr. Arbuthnot advifes thofe who afe troub'ed w'th coftivenefs to ufe animal oils, a3 frefh butter, cream, marrow, fat broths, ef. ecially thofe made of the interna! partj of animal?, as the liver, heart, midriff', &<. R-. Pkewife recommends the exoreffed oil, of mild vegetables, as olives, almonds, paftaches, a:id the fruits themfelves} all oily and mild fruits, as fies; decoft'tons of mealy vegetables 5 thefe lubricate the inteftmes; fome faoonaceous lubftances which ftimulate gently, as honey, hydrome!, or boiled honey and water, unrefined fug.ir, &C. The Dodlor obferves, that fuch lenitive fubftances are proper for perfons of dry atra- "Silarian conftitutions, who are fubj< ft to aftrlftion of the belly, and the piles, and will oper- ate when ftronger medicinal fubftances are fometimes ineffectual ; but that fuch lenitive diet hurts thofe whofe bowels are weak and las. He Iikewife obferves, that all watery fubftances are lenitive, and that even common water, whey, four milk, and butter-milk have that ef- feft: that new milk, efpecialv affes milk, ftimulates ftill more.when it fi-nrs on the fto- mach ; ai.d that whey turned four, will purge ftrongly-:—That moft garden fruits are like- wife lasatOe; and that fome of them, as grapes, will throw fu;h as take them immoderately, in:-j i the tra r-?rbus, 01 incurable diarrhoja. ag4 WANT OF APPETITE.—OF THE HEART-BURN When the bodv cannot be kept open without medicine, we would recommend gentle doks of rhubarb to be taken twice or thrice a-week. This is not near fb injurious to the ftomach as aloes, jalap, or the other drafttc purgatives fo much in ufe. Infu- fions of fenna and manna may Iikewife be taken, or half an ounce cf foluble tartar diffolved in water-gruel. About the fize of a nut- meg of lenitive efeauary,taken twice or thrice a-day, generally an- fwers the purpofe very well. WANT OF APPETITE. THIS may proceed from a foul ftomach; indigeftion; the want of free air and exercife ; grief; fear ; anxiety ; or any of the de- preffing paffions; exceffive heat; the ufe of ftrong broths^ fat meats, or any thing that palls the ap etite, or is hard of digeftbn; the immoderate ufe of ftrong liquors, tea, tobacoo, opium, Sec The patient ought, if poffible, to make choice of an open dry air; to take exercife daily on horfeback or in a ra irirue ; to rife betimes; and to avoid all intenfe thought. He foiad ofe a diet of eafy digeftion; and fhould avoid exceffive heat and great fa- tigue. If want of appetite proceeds from errors in diet, cr any other part of the patient's regimen, it ought to be changed. If naufea and retchings fhew that the ftomach is loaded with crudities, a vomit will be of fervice. After this a gentle purge or two cf rhubarb, or any of the bitter purgiig falts, may be taken. The patient ought next to ufe fome of the flomachic bitters infufed in wine. Though gentle evacuations be neceffary, yet ftrong purges and vomits are to be ayoiddH, as tney weaken the ftomach and hurt digeft-on. Elixir of vitriol is an excellent medicine in moft cafes of indi- geftion, weaknefs of the ftomach, or want of appetite. From twenty to thirty drops of it may be taken twice or thrice a-day in a glafs of wine or water. It may iikewife be mixed with the tinc- ture of the bark, one drachm of the former to an ounce of the lat- ter, and two tea-fpoonsful of it taken in wine and water, as above. The chalybeate waters,.if drank in moderation, are generally Oj confiderable fervice in this cafe. The fait-water has Iikewife good effeas; but it muft not be ufed too freely. The waters of Har- rowgate, Scarborough, Moffit, and moft other fpas in Britain, may be ufed with advantage We would advife all who are affliaed with mdig^tion and want of appetite, to repair to thefe places of pubhc rendezyaus. The very change of air, and the cheerful company, will be of fervice, not to mention the exercife, amufe- ments, &c. OF THE HEART-BURN. WHAT is commonly, called the heart-burn, is not a difeafe of r„», K V v-~'*'"%l':r *-a"<ru u;c near^ourn, is not a cneaie or Af f^s\ \ un?.rY fe.nfa/t.1 ^ of heat or acrimony, about the ot tne ftomaon, which is fometimes attended with anxiety, 1 aat crgan pi: of the naufea, and vomiting. <L ,£ maJ- prGC?ed fr??.<k«7 of the ftomach, indigeftion, bile, fce abounding of an aad in the ftomach, &c> Perfons who are: liS OF THE MR A7?T- BURN. ble to this complaint, ought to avoid ftale liquors, acids, windy or greafy aliments, and fhould never ufe violent exercife foon after a hearty meal. 1 know many perfons who never fail to have the heart-burn if they ride foon after dinner, provided they have drank ale, wine, or any fermented liquor : but are never troubled with it when they have drank rum, or brandy and water, without any fugar or acid. \y hen the heart-burn proceeds from debility of the ftomach, or indigeftion, the patient ought to take a dole or two of rhubarb; afterwards he may ufe infufions cf the Peruvian bark; or any oth- er of the flomachic bitters, in wine or brandy. Exercife in the open air -will iikewife be of ufe, and every thing that promotes di- geftion. When bilious humors occafion the heart-burn, a tea-fpoonful of the fweet fpirit of nitre in a glafs of water, or a cup of tea, will generally give eafe. If it proceeds from the ufe of greafy aliments, a dram of brandy or rum may be taken. If acidity or fournefs of the ftomach occafions the heart-burn, abforbents are the proper medicines. In this cafe an ounce of pow- dered chalk, half an ounce of fine fugar, and a quarter of an ounce of gum-arabic, may be mixed in a quart of water, and a tea-cupful of it taken as often as is neceffary. Such as do not chufe chalk may take a tea-fpoonful of prepared oyfter-fhells, or of the powder called crabs-eyes, in a glafs of cinnamon or peppermint-water. But the fafeft and belt abforbent is magnefla alba. This not only aas as an abforbenr, but Iikewife as a purgative; whereas chalk and other abforbents of that kind are apt to lie io the inteftines, and occafion obftruaions. This powoer is not difagreeable, and may be taken in a, cup of-tea, or a glafs of mint-water. A large tea- fpoonful is the ufual dofe ; 'but it may be taken in a much greater quantity when there is occafion. Thefe things are now generally made up into lozenges for the conveniency of being carried in the pocket, and tal^en atpleafure. If wind be the caufe of this complaint, the moft proper medi- cines are thofe called carminitives; as anifeede, juniper berries, gin- ger, canella alba, cardamom feeds, Sec. Thefe may either be chew- ed, or infufed in wine, brandy or ether fpirits. One of the fafeft medicines of this kind is the tinaure made by infuficg an ounce of rhubarb, and a quarter of an ounce of the ieffer cardamom feeds, in an Englifh pint of brandy. After this has digeft ed for two or three days, it ought to be ftrained, and four ounces of white fugar- candy added to it. It muft ftand to digeft a fecond time till the fu- gar be diffolved. A table-fpoonful of it may be taken cccafionaily for a dofe. I have frequently known the heart-burn cured, particularly in pregnant women, by chewing green tea. Two table-fpoonsful of what is called the milk of gum-ammoniac, taken once cr twice a-day will fometimes cure the heart-burn. 286 CHAP. XLV. OF NERVOUS DISEASES. (Jf all difeafes incident to mankind, thefe of the nervous kind are the moft complicated and difficult to cure. A volume would not be fufficient to point out their various appearances. 1 hey imitate almoft every difeafe; and are feldom alike in two different perfons, or even the fame perfon at different times. Proteus-like, t>y arecontinu lly changing fhape ; and upon every frefh attack, the patient thinks ne feels fvmptoms which he never experienced be, fore. Nor do :hey nly affea the body; the mind hkewife fuffers, and is thereby rendered weak and peevifh. The low fpirits, tim- oroufnefs, melancholy, and ficklen;as of temper, w ich generally attend nervous diforders, indu' ""Tiany to believe th n they are en- tirely difeafes of t'eHnd: Kit this co.ange of temper is rather a confequence, <man the can[e of nerve us* uileaf-s. CAUSES.—Even-- th ? r-.at tends to relax^or weaken the body, difpefes'i* tonerv-ns difeafes, as indolenc', excefiiv. venery, drinking too much tea}orotner weak watery liquors, wjrm frequent bleeding, purging, vomiting, &c. Whatever hurts the digeftion, or prevents the proper affinailatlon of the food, has Iikewife this ef- fea ; as long fafting, excefs in eating or drinking, the ufe of windy, crude or unwholefome aliments, an unfavourable p.fture of the body, &c. Nervous diforders often proceed from intenfe application to fWy. Indeed few ftudious perfons are entirely free from them. Nor is this at all to be wondered at; intenfe thinking not only (" preys upon the fpirits, but prevents the perfotl from taking proper exercife, by which means the digeftion is impaired, the nourifhment prevented, the folids relaxed, and the whole mafs of humors vitiated. Grief and difappointment Iikewife produce the fame effeas. I have known more nervous patients who dated the commencement cf their diforders from the lofs of a hufband, a favourite child, or from fome difappointment in life, than from any other caufe. In a word, whatever weakens, the body, or depreffes the fpirits, may occafion nervous diforders, as unwholefome air, want of fleep, great fatigue, difagreeable apprehenfions, anxiety, vexation, &c. SYMPTOMS.—We fhall only mention fome of the moft general fymptoms of thefe diforders, as it would be both an ufe- lefs and an endlefs tafk to enumerate the whole. They generally begin with windy inflations or distentions of the ftomach and intef- tines ; the appetite and digeftion are ufually bad ; yet fometimes there is an uncommon craving for food, and a quick digeftion. The iood often turns four on the ftomach ; and the patient is troub- led with vomiting of clear water, tough phlegm, or a blackifti col- ored 1< quor refembling the j: rounds of coffee. Excruciating pains are often felt about the navel, attended with a rumbling or mur- muring noife in the bowels. The bodv is fometimes locfe, but more commonly bound, which occafions a retention of wind ?nd great uneauncis. OF NERVOUS DISEASES. $87 The urine is fometimes in fmall quantity, at other times very copious and quite clear. There is a great ftraightnefs of the breaft, with difficulty of breathing; violent palpitations of the heart; fud- den flufhings cf hea*' . vario Is parts of the body; at other times a fenfe of cold, as if water were po&r€d on them ; flying pains in the arms and limbs, pains in the ha,ck and belly, refembling thofe occafioned by the gravel; the pulls' very variable, fometimes un- commonly flow, and at other times very quick; yawning, the hick- up, frequent fighing, and a fenfe of fuftbcation, as if from a ball or lump in the throat; alternate fits of crying and convulfive laugh- ing ; the fleep is unfound, and feldom refrefhing ; and the patient is often troubled with the night-mare. As the difeafe increafes, the patient is naolefted with head-achs, eraraps, and fixed pains in vtrious p?.rts of the body; the eyes . are clouded, and often affeaed with pain and drynefs ; there is a noife in the ears, and often a dullnefs of hearing ; in fhort the whole animal funaions are impaired. The mind is difturbed on the moft trivial occafions, and is hurried into the moft perverfe commo- tions, inquietude, terror, fadnefs, anger, diffidence, &c. The pa- tient is apt to entertain wild imaginations, and extravagant fancies j the raemorv becomes weak, and the judgment fails. Nothing is more charaaeriftic of this difeafe than a conftant dread of death. This renders thofe unhappy perfons who labour under it, peevifh, fickle, impatient, and apt to run from one phyii- cian to another ; which is one reafon why they feldom reap any benefit from medicine, as they have not fufficient refolution to per- fift in any one courfe till it has time to produce its proper effeas. They are Iikewife apt to imagine that they la our under difeafes from which they are quite free; and are very angry it any one at- tempts to fet them right, or laugh them out of their ridiculous no- REGIMEN.—Perfons affiled with nervous difeafes ought never to fait long. Their food ffrould be folid and nourifhing, but of eafy digeftion. Fat meats and heavy fauces are hurtful. All excefs fhoald be carefully avoided. They ought never to eat more at a time than they can eafilv digeft ; but if they feel themfelves weak and faint between meals, they ought ta eat a bit ot breacl, and drink a glafs of wine. Heavy f uppers are to be avoided. Though wine in excefs enfeebles the body, and impairs the facul- ties of the mind, yet taken in moderation it ftrengthens the ftomach, aud promotes digeftion. Wine and water is a very proper drink at meals; but if wine fours on the ftomach, or the patient is much troubled with wind, brandy and water will anfwer b-.tter. Eve- ry thing that is windy or hard of digeftion muft be avoided All weak and warm liquors are hurtful, as tea, coffee, punch, &c People may find a temporary relief in the ufe of thefe, but they always increafe the malady as they weaken the flomach, and hurt digeftion. Above all things, drams are to be avoided. Whatev- er immediate eafe the patient may feel from the ufe of ardent fpir- its, they are fure to aggravate the malady, and prove certain poi- fons at laft. Thefe cautions are the more neceflary ; as molt nerv- 288 OF NERVOUS DISEASES. ous people are peculiarly fond of tea and ardent fpdrlts, to fhe ufe of which many of them fall viaims. m „ . . „., Exercife in nervous diforders is fuperior to all medicines. Kid- ing on horfeback is generally eileemed the beft, as it gives motion to the whole body without fatiguing it. 1 have known feme pa- tients, however, with whom walking agreed better, and others who were moft benefitted by riding in a carriage. Every one ought to ufe that which he finds moft beneficial. Long fea-voyages have an excellent effea; and to thofe who have fuificient refolution,' we would by all means recommend this courfe.
