OF BUBOES. • VENEREAL Buboes are hard tumours feated in the groin, .occafioned by the venereal poifon lodged in this par'-. They are of two kinds, viz. fuch as proceed from a recent infeaion, and -fuchas accompany a confirm d iues. The cure of r cent buooes, that is, fuch as appear feon after impure ?biti n, may be firft attempted by dfperfon, and, if that fhould not fucceed, by fuppuration. To promote the difir-rfion of a buboe, the fame regimen muft '*e obferved as was directed in the firft ftage of a gonorrhoea. I he p^tie^t muft li.tew :.fe ' e ble ', and take fome cooling purges, as the decoaion of amarnd - and fenna, Ghuber's falts, and the like. If, by this eour j, the fwelling and other inflammatory fymotoms abate, we may fafely pr. c ed to the ufe of mercury, which muft be continued till the venereal virus is fubdued.t But if the buboe fhould, from the beginning, be attended with great heat, pain, and palfa'ion, it will be proper to promote its fup- puration. For this purpofe the patient may be allowed to ufe his ordinary dhf, and to take now and then a glafs of wi.:e. Emolli- ent cataplafms, confifting of bread and m Ik foftened with oil or frefh butter, may be applied to the part ; and in cold conftitutions, where the tumour advances flowly, white lily-roots, bailed, or fr- eed onions raw, and a fufficient quantity of yellow bafilicon, fnay be added to the poultice. When the tumour is ripe, which may be known by its conical figure, the foftnefs of the fkin, and a fluctuati.n of matter plainly to be felt under the finger, it may be opened either by a cauftic or a lancet, and afterwards dreffed with digeftive ointm nt. It fometimes however happens, that buboes can neither be dif- * The extract of hemlock may be made into pills, and taken in the manner diredted under the article Cancer. fFor the difperfion of a bubo, a number of leeches applied to the part afTefled will be found equally, efficacious as intheinfl*med ieft:cle» 33«5 OF CHANCRES. perfed nor brought to a fuppuration, but remain hard, indolent tu- mours. In this cafe the indurated glands muft be confumed by cauftic; if they fhould become fcirrhous, they muft be diffolved by the application of hemlock, both externally, and internally as di- refted in the fcirrhous tefticles. OF CHANCRES. CHANCRES are fuperficial, callous, eating ulcers; which may hdppwii either with or without a gonorr cea. They are com- moaiy ieaied about the glans, and make their appearance in the fol- lowing manner: Firft a little red pimple arifes, which foon be- comes pointed at top, and is filled with a whitifh matter inclining to yellow. This pimple is hot, and itches generally before it breaks; afterwards it degenerates into an obftinate ulcer, the bot* torn of which is ulually covered with a vifcid mucus, and whofe edges gradually become hard and callous. Sometimes the firft ap- p.arance reiemblesa fimple excoriation of the cuticle ; which, how- ever, if the caule be veuereal, loon becomes a true chancre. V A chancre is fometimes a primary affeaion ; but it is much oftener fymptomatic, and is the mark of a confirmed lues. Pri- mary chancres difcover themfelves foon after impure coition, and are generaijyieated in parts covered with a thin cuticle, as the lips, the nipples of women, theglaus penis of men, &c* When a chancre appears foon after impure Coition, its treat- ment is nearly fimilar to that of the virulent gonorrhoea. The pa- tieut mult obferve the coding regimen, lole a little blooAnd take fome gentie dofes of lalts and manna. The parts affeaed ought frequently to be bathed, or rather loaked in warm milk and water, and, if the inflammation be gi eat, an emollient poultice or cata- plafm may be applied to tnem. This courfe will, in moft cafes be iuffident to abate the inflammation, and prepare the patient for the life of the mercury, , Symptomatic chancres are commonly accompanied with ulcers in the throat, no&urnal pains, feurvy eruptions about the roots of %:.e ha.r, and other fymptoms of a confirmed lues. Though they p.ay e feated h any 6^' the parts mentioned above, they common* ly appear upon the private parts, or the infide of the thigh. They are alio lefs painful, but frequently much larger and harder than primary chancres. As their cure muft depend upon that of the pox, ot which they are only a fymptom, we fhall take no further police of tnem, till we come to treat of a confirmed lues-t Ihus we have related moft of the fymptoms which accompany or fucceed a violent gonorrhoea, and have alfo given a fhort view or their proper treatment there are, however, feveral others which * When venereal ulcers are feated in the lips, the infection may be communicated by • u8" u e feen ver"/ ol>ftinate venereal ulcers in the lips, which 1 have all the reafon in the world to believe were communicated in this manner. Nurfesought to beware of fuckl'inginfecled children, or having theiT bseafls drawn by peYlons tainted with the venerea' difeafe. This caution is very neceflary for nurfes who re- fide in the neighbourhood of great towns. -..t I have round it anfwer extremely well to fprinkle chancres twice a-dav with calomel. i his will otun perform a cure without any other application whatever. lf-the chancres ?J^R^,e,£i;-•N.l, tJey mi,yfce warned withjniik and water, a little warm, aj}d afcer^ Wards the cal9rr.el may be applied as »bo»e, . VKCHANCRES. 33,7 iometimes attend this difeafe, as n ftrangury, ox obftruaion Of urine, a phymofls, paraphymofis, 6c. A ftrangury may either be occafioned by a fpafmodic coaftric tion, or ail inflammation of the urethra and parts about the neck of the bladder. In the former cafe, the patient begins to void his urine with tolerable eafe; but, as foon as it touches the galled or inflamed urethra, a fudden conftriaion takes place, and the urine is voided by fpurts, fometimes by drops only. When the ftrangury is owing to an inflammation about the neck of the bladder, there is a conftant heat and uneafinefs of the part, a perpetual defire to make water, while the patient can only render a few drops, and a troublefome tencfmus, or conitant inclination to go to ftool. When the ftrangury is owing to fpafms, fuch medicines as tend to dilute and blunt the falts of the urine will be proper. For this purpofe, befides the common diluting liquors, foft and cooling emulfions, fweetened with the fyrup of poppies, may be ufed.— Should thefe not have the defired effea, bleeding and emollient, fomentations, will be neceffary. When the complaint is evidently owing to an inflammation about the neck of the bladder, bleeding muft be more liberally per- formed, and repeated according to the urgency of the fymprems.—- After bleeding, if the ftrangury ftill continues, foft clyfters, with a proper quantity of laudanum in them, may be admmiftered, and emollient fomentations applied to the region of the bladder. At the fame time, the patient may take every four hours a tea-cupful of barley water, to an Englifh pint of which fix ounces of the fyrup *of raarfh-mallows,four ounces of the oil of fweet almonds, and half an ounce of nitre, may be added. If thefe remedies fhould not re- lieve the complaint, and a total fuppreffion of urine fhould come en, bleeding muft be repeated, and the patient let in a warm bath up to the middle. It will be proper in this cafe to difcontinue the diuret- ics, and to draw off the water with a catheter; but as the patient is feldcm able to bear its being introduced, we would rather re- commend the ufe of mild bougies. • Thefe often lubricate the paf- fage, and greatly facilitate the difcharge of urine. Whenever they begin to ftimulat^ or give any uneafinefs, they may be withdrawn. The phymofls is fuch a conftriaion of the prepuce over the glans, as hinders it from being drawn backwards; the paraphymofis, oh the con1 rary, is fuch a conftriaion of the prepuce behind the glans, as hinders it from being brought forward. The treatment cf thefe fymptoms is fo nearly the feme with that of the virulent gonorrhoea, that we have no occafion to en- large upon it. In general, bleeding, purging, poultices, and emolli- ent fomentations are fufficient. Should thefe however fail of re- moving the ftridure, and the parts be threatened with a mortifica- tion, twenty or thirty grains of ipecacuanha, and one grain cf emetic tartar, may be given for a vomit, and may be worked off with warm water or ihm gruel. It fometimes happens, that, 1.1 fpite of all endeavours to the contrary, the inflammation goes en, and the fymptoms of a begin- ning mortification appear. Wh-?a this is the cafe, the prepuce muff 338 OF A CONFIRMED LUES. befcarifiedwitha lancet, and, if neceflary, divided, in order to prevent a ftrangulation, and fet the lmprilonei glans at liberty.—. We fhall not defcribe the mannerof performing this operation, as ( it ought always to be done by a furgeon. When a mortification j has actually taken place, it will be neceffary befides performing the above operations, to foment the parts frequently with cloths wrung out of a ftrong decoaion of camomile flowers and bark, and to give the patient a drachm of the* bark in powder -every two or three hours. With regard to the priapifm, chordee, and other diftortions of the penis, their treatment is no way different from hat of the gon- orrhce. When they prove very trou lefome, the paiie.it may take a few drops of laudanum at night, efpecially alter the operation of a purgative through the day. i" ■■*• OF A CONFIRMED LUES. * » WE have hitherto treated cf thofe affeaions in w^ich the venereal poiion is luppofed to be confined chiefly to the particular parrs'by which it was received, and fnall next take a view of the lues in its confirmed ftate ; that is, when the poifon is aauaily re- ceived into the blood, and circulating,with it*through every part of j the body, mixes with tne feveral fecredons, and renders tre whole habit tainted. The fymptoms of a confirmed lues, are, buboes in the groin, pain of the head and joints, which are peculiarlv iroublefome in the night, or when the patient is warm in bed ; fcabs and fcurfs ia# various parts of the body, efpecially on the head, of a yeilowifh" colour, refemblinga honey-comb ; c-Trodmg'ulc^s in various parts ^ i of the body, which generally begin about-the throat, from whence *■. \ they creep gradually, by the palate, towards'he cartilage of the cole, which they deftroy; excrefceno=s or exoftofes arife in the mid- dle of the bones, and their fpongy ends become brittle, and break upon the leaft accident; at offter times they are foft and bend like wax ; the conglobate glands become hard and callous, and £ rm, in the neck, armpits, groin, and mefentary, hard moveable tumours, like the king's evil; tumours of differ re " kb:ds are Iikewife form- ed in the lymphatic veffels, tendons, hgaments, and nerves, as the gummata, ganglia, nodes, tophs, Sec. ; the eyes are aifea d with itch- ing, pain, r-dnefs, and fome imes with topal blinan fs, and the ears with a finging noife, pain, and deafnefs, whiift tleir i. ternal fub- lianceis ulcerated and rendered cari us ; at length ?11 the animal, vital, and natural funftions are dep:avr-d ; the face becomes pale and livid; the body amaciated and unfit for motion, and the mife- rable patient falls into an atrcphy cr wafting confump'ion. Women have fymptoms peculiar to tne fex.; as cancers of the breaft ; a fuppreffion or overflowing of the menfes; the whites ; hyfteric affeaions ; an inflammation, .abfe-fs, fchirrhcus, gangrene, cancer, or ulcer of the womb ; they are gener.db either barren or fubjea to abortion ; or, if they bring children into the world, th: y have an univerfal eryfipelas, are halt rotten, and covered with ulcers; OF A CONFIRMED LUES. 339 Such is the catal gue of fymptoms attending this dreadful dif- eafe in its confirmed ftate. Indeed they are feldom all to be m-t with in the fame perfon, or at the fame time; fo many of them, however, are generally.prefent as arc fufficient to alarm the pa- tient ; and it he has reafon to fufpea the infeai n is lurking in his body, he ught immediately to fet about the expulfion of it, other- wife the muft tragical confequences will enfue Ihe only certain remedy hitherto kuown in Europe, for the cure of this difeafe, is mercury, w. i'h may be ufed in a great vari- ety of forms, with nearly the fame fuccefs.* Some time ago it was reckoned impoffible to cure a confirmed lues with ut a fal vatioa. T. is method is now however preity generally laid afiJe, and mer- cury is found to be as efficacious, or rataer more fo, in expelling t e venereal poifon, when adminiftered in fuch a manner as not to run off by the falivary glands. Though many are of opinion, that the mercurial ointment is as efficacious as any other preparation of that mintral ; yet expe- rience has taught me to t ink otherwife. I have often feen the moft obftinate venereal cafes, where gr^at quantities of mercurial ointment had been ufed in vain, yield to the faline preparations of mercury. Nor am i fuguhr in this opinion : * My ingenious friend, Mr. Cbre, an eminent furgeon of this city, affures me, that for fome time paft he has employed, in venereal cafes, a faline prepar- ation of mercury with moft happy fuccefs. This preparation rub- bed with a fufficient quantity of any mild powder, he appli -s, in fmall porti. ns to the toague, where, with a gentle degree of fric- tion, it is immediately abforbed,'and produces its full effea upon fhe fyftem, without doing the leaft injury to the ftomach or bowels ; a matter of the greateft importance in the application of this moft aaive and powerful remedy. It is impoffible *o af ertam either the exaa quantity of medi- cines that muft be taken, or the time they ought t > be continued, in order to perform a cure. Thefe will ever vary according to the % conftitution of the patient, the feafon of the year, fe degree of in- feaion, c.e time it has lodged in the body, Sec But though ir is difficult, as Aftruc obferves, to determine a prirori, fyhat quantity of mercury will, in the whole be neceffary to cure this diltemper completely ; yet it may be judged fa pofleriorU from he abatement and ceafing of the fymptoms. The fame author adds, that com- monly not lefs than two ounces of the ftrong mercurial ointment, is fufficient, and not more than three or four ounces neceffary. The only chemical preparation of mercury which we fhall take notice of, is the corrofive fuMimate. This was fome time ago brought into ufe for the venereal difeafe, in Germany, by the il- luftrious B::ron VanSwieien ; and was foon after introduced into Britain by the learned Sir John Pringle, at that time phyfician to the army. The method of giving it is as follows : One grain of corrofive fublimate is diffolved in two ounces of French brandy or malt fpirits ; and of this feluiioii, an ordinary table-fpoonful, or the 'The preparations which I now chiefly of?, in the Confirmed lues; are calomel aad calci- ged mercury. 34o [OF A CONFIRMED LUES. quantity of half an ounce,is to be taken twice a-day, and to be con- tinued as long as any fymptoms of the diforder remain. To thofe whofe ftomach cannot bear the folution, the fublimate may be given in form of a pill.* Several roots, woods, and barks, have been recommended for curing the venereal dileafe ; but none of them have been found, upon experience, to anfwer the high encomiums which, had been beftowed upon ihem. Though no one of thefe is to be depended upoa alone, yet, when joined with mercury, fome of them have been found to be very beneficial in promoting a cure. One of the beft we know yet is larfapariUa, which may be prepared and taken according to the direaions in the Appendix, f The mezervm-root is Iikewife found to be a, powerful affiftant to the fublimate or any other mercurial. It may either be ufed along with farfaparilla, as direaed in the Appendix, or by itfelf.— Thofe who choofe to ufe the'mezeron by itfelf, may boil an ounce of the frefh bark, taken from the root, in twelve Englifh pints of water to eight, adding towards the end an ounce of liquorice. The dofe of this is the fame as of the decoaion of farfaparilla. We have been told that the natives of America cure the vene- real difeafe, in every ftage, by a decoaion-of the root of a plant called Lobelia. It is ufed either frefh or dried ; but we have no certain accounts with regard to the proportion. Sometimes they mixother roots with it, as thofe of the ranunculus, the ceanothus, | >; &c. but whether thefe are defigned to difguife or affift it, is. ' doubtful. The patient takes a large draught of the decoaion r •$ early in the morning, and continues to ufe it for his ordinary drink through the day4 Many other roots and woods might be mentioned, which have been extolled for curing the venereal difeafe, as the china- root, the roots of foap-wort, burdock, &c. as alfo the wood of guaiacum and fafafras ; but as none of thefe have been found to pof-. lefs virtues fuperior to thofe already mentioned, we fhall, for the fake of brevity,pafs them over, and fhall conclude our obfervations on this difeafe, with a few general remarks«concerning the proper ' management of the patient, and the nature of the infeaion. * The ftsblimate may be piven in diflilled water, or any other liquid that the patent choofes. 1 commosly order ten grains to be diffolved in an ounce of the fpirit of wine, for the convenience_of carriage, ard let the patient take twenty or thirty drops of it night and morning in half a glals of braady orother fpirits. Mr. Dtbta, an ingenious chymiit of thia 1 plsce, informs me, that he piepares a fait of mercury much more mild and gentle in ita- operation than the fublimate. though equally efficacious. t See Appendix, DtcShon cf SjrfapariHa, ... +Th<>u&i- w« -re ftill-very much in the dark with regrrd to the method of curing thi« difeafe among ihe natives of Amerca, yet it is generally affirmed that they do cure it with fpeed, lafety and fuccefs, and that without the leaft knowledge of mercury. Hence it become! art object otconfideratole importance to dikover tferir method of cure. This might furely be done by ma-cing trials of the various plants which ar-. tound in thofe parts, and particularly ot fuch as tne nat-ves are known to make ufe of. All people in a rude ftate take their med. icmes < niefiy from the vegetable kingdom, and are often poiTefled of valuable fecrets with re- garo to the virtues of plants, of which ^o.e enlightened nations are ignorant. Indeed we »««£*£ Z„h £VfflTf ?i™ 0t °-r own erowth»were Pr°Per P»',ns ukent° difcoverthem, would be round as efflcecu, in cur.ng the venerea) difeife as thofe in America, it muft however be remembered, that what will cure the ven=r#,< difeafe ia on* country, will not al- ways be found to have eq>-a fuctefs in another. " "ut *" OF A CONFIRMED LUES* 341 GENERAL OBSERVATIONS. THE condition of the patient ought always to be confidered previous to his entering upon a courfe of mercurv in any form. It would be equally rafh and dangerous to adminifter mercury to a perfon labouring under any violent acute difeafe, as a putrid fever, pleurify, peripneumony, or the like. It would Iikewife be danger- ous in fome chronic cafes; as a flow heaic fever,br the hft ffege of a confumption. Sometimes, however, thefe difeafes proceed from a confirmed lues ; in Which cafe it will be neceffary to give mercu- ry. In chronic difeafes of a lefs dangerous nature, as the afthma, the gravel, and fuch like, mercury, if neceffary, may be fafeiy ad- miniftered, If the patient's ftrength has been greatly exhiufted by ficknefs, labour, abftinence, or any other caufe, the ufe of mercury muft be poftponed, till by time, reft, and a nourifhing diet, it can be fufficiently reftored. Mercury ought not to be adminiftered to women during the menftrual flux, or when the period is near at hand. Neither mould. it be given m the laft ftage of pregnancy. If, however, the woman be not near fhe time of her delivery, and circumftances render it ne- ceffary, mercury may be given, but in fmaller dofes, and at greater intervals than ufual; with thefe precautions, both the mother and child may be cured at the fame time ;• if not, the diforder will at leaft beitept from growing worfe, till the woman be brought to bed, and fijfficiently recovered, when a more effeaual method may be pur-, filed, which, if fhe fuckles her child, will in all probability be fuffi- cient for the cure of both. Mercury ought always to be adminiftered to infants with the greateft caution. Their tender condition unfits them for fupport- ing a falivation, and makes it neceffary to adminifter even the mild- eft preparations of mercury to them with a fparing hand. A fimi- lar conduct is recommended in the treatment of old perfons, who have the misfortune to labour under a confirmed lues. No doubt the infirmities of age muft render people lefs able to undergo the fatigues of a falivation; but this, as was formerly obferved, is never neceffary ; befides, we have generally found, that mercury had much lets effea upon very old perfons, than on thofe who were younger. Hyfteric and hypochondriac perfons, and fuch as are fubjea to an habitual diarrhoea or dyfentery, or to frequent and violent attacks of the epilepfy, or who are affliaed with the fcrophula, or the fcurvy, ought to be cautious in the ufe of mercury. Where any one of thefe diforders prevails, it ought either, if poffible, to be cured, or at leaft palliated, before the patient enters upon a courfe of mercury. When this cannot be done, the mercury muft be adminiftered in fmaller dofes, and at longer intervals than ufual. The moft proper feafons for entering upon a courfe of mercu- ry, are the fpring and autumn, when the air is of a moderate warmth. If the circumftances of the cafe, however, will not admit of delay, we muft not defer the cure on account of the feafon, but muft adminifter the mercury; taking care at the fame time to keep 342 OF A CONFIRMED LUES. the patient's chamber warmer or cooler, according as the feafon ot the year requires. # The rext thing to be confidered is the preparation neceflary to be obferved before we proceed to adminifter a courfe of mercury. Som* liv g**eat ftrefs upon this circumft^.nce, obferving, that by pre- %i -ufly relaxing the veffels, and correcting arry. diforder which may happen to prevail in the blood, not only the mercury will be dif- p-i"edtoaamore kindly, but-many other inconveniences- will be prevented. We have already recommended bleed'og and gentle purges, previous to the adminiftration of mercury, and fhall only now add, that tnefe are always to be repeated according to the age, ftrength, , conftitu'ion, and o*her circumftances of the pnient. Afterwards, if i- can be convenfeirly done, the patient ought to bathe once or tw e a-day, for a few lavs, in lukewarm water. His diet in the mean time muft be light, m >ift, and coolinf. Wine, and all heat- ing liq-.nrs,alfo v;olent bodilv exercife, and all great exertions of the mi°d, °re carefully to be av.ided. A proper regimen is Iikewife to be obferved' by fuch as are und.-r a courfe of mercury. Inattention to this not only endangers the patient*s l;fe, but often alfe difappoints him of a cure. A much f-riailer quantity of mercury will be fufficie. t for the cure of a per- fon wh lives low, keeps warm, an 3 avoids all manner of excsfs^ tha- cf one who cannot endure to put the fmalfeft reftraint upon h;s appetites; indeed it but rarely happms that fuch are thoroughly cure.f. There is hardly any thing of more '™.p stance, either for pre- venting or r.m ving venereal inf ai n, than cleanlinefs. By an early att ^ ti m to this, the infeaion might often bs prevented from ' entTi -g the body; and, where it has already taken plao% its effect may b? grei'ly mitigated. Whether this difenf • at fpft took its rife from 'irtin-fs, is hard to fay ; but wherevT tvat prevails, the in- feaion is found n its greateft degree of virulence, which gives grou-i^ to beli-ve that a ftria attention to-cleanlinefs would <go far toward? px«irpa'"'nir it ^together.* *l have not onty oft.nf en a recent infection earned off in a fewday^by means of clearili- nef , viz. bathifiR, fomentations, irrjecVnms. &c. but have Iikewife found it of the greateft ad* '' vantage in the mo.e advanced «ages of the oifrafe. Of this 1 had lately a very remarkable jn<t.nee, in a ram who<<- p*n;s was almoft wholly confumed by venereal ulcers • the matte* had f.een allowed to c nfi«ue ..n the f res. without anv ca-ehavinp been taken to' clean them, tl'., n twrthftandlng the ufe of mercury and o *-. medic nes, it had produced the effr&s ii£\n?£r?''*e ."'-ered WVm m'lkin6 ™\eJ 1° bc inJ'aed three or f™T «'»« »-"«»v. into all thefinuou< ulcers, n order to wafh out all the matter ; after which thev wer* fluff- ed with dry l,nt to abforb the f fh matter as it was generated. The patient at theTame tirne took every day half a grain of rhe corrofive fublimate of mercurv, diffolved fn an ounce of brandy, and rtrank an Enpl.fh quart of the decocTon of farfaparilla. fly this treatment m wawiu^.;e:^:;nCr^tedPerfeaiy C"ed ; «* Wh" ™ -y^W, ^ZSSS^Z the Weluf Scotland ^o fi?h * aCCoVnt <* a *>«'« * the ««• venerea which prevails in tne Welt ot Scotland, to which the nat.ves give the name of Sibbins or iW« The Doe to, obferves, that the fpreading of this difeafe is chiefly owing to the ncelec? of c'l nlineia and feems to thu*. thatby 4ue attention to that virtue, it might be e" tirpated! The S i)F A CONFIRMED LUES. 34J When the venereal d; feafe has been negfeaed, or improperly treated, ft often becomes a diforder of the habit. In this cafe the cure muft be attempted by reftoratives, as a milk diet, the decoc- tion of f rf iparilla, and fuch like, to which mercury may be occa- fionally added. It is a common praaice in North-Britain to fend fuch patients to drink goat whey. This is a very proper plan, pro- vided the infeaion has been totally eradicated before hand ; but when that is not the cafe, and the patient truics to the Whey for fmifhing his cure, he will often be difapp ointed. I have frequent- ly known the difeafe return with all its virulence after a courfe of goat whey, even when that courfe had been thought quite fufficient for comple*-ing the cure. One of the mift unfortunate circumftances attending patients in this difeafe, is the neceffity they are often laid under of hurry- ing the cure. This induces them to .take medicine too faft, and to le?.ve it off too foon. A few grains more of medicine, or a few days longer confinement, would often be fufficient to perfea the cure ; whereas, by negfea of thefe, a fmall degree of virulence is ftill left in the humours, which gradually vitiates, and at length contaminates the w^ole mafs. To avoid this, .we would advife, that the patient fhould never leave off taking medicine immediate- ly upon the disappearing of the fymptoms, but continue it for fome time after, gradually lelfrnirg the quantity, till there is fufficient ground to believe that the difeafe is entirely eradicated. It :s not only difficult, but abfolutely impoffible to afcertain rhe exaa degreeof virulence that may attend the difeafe; for which reafr n it w'll nlways be a much fafer rule to continue the ufe of med;cin * too bug, than to leave it off too fern. This feems to be the leading max:m of a modern praaitioner of fome note for the venereal difeafe, who always orders his patient to perform a quarantine of at leaft forty days, during wh:ch time he takes forty battles of, I fuppofe, a ftrong decoaion of farfaparilla, or fome oth=r anti-venereal fimple. Whoever takes this method, and aads a fufficient quantitv of corrofive fublimate, or fome other aaive preparation of mercury to the decoaion, wiil feldom fail to cure a confirmed lue*. It is peculiarly unfortunate for the cure of this difeafe, that not one in ten of thofe who comr? a it, are either able or wilfng to fubmit to a proper plan of regimen. The patient is willing to take medicine ; but he muft follow his bufinefs, and, to prevent fufpi- cions, muft eat and drink like the reft of the family. This is the true fource of nine tenths of all the mifchief arifing from the vene- real difeafe. I never knew the cure attended with any great diffi- culty or danger wh^re rhe pitient ftriaiv followed the phyficiar/s advice ; but a volume would not be fufficient to point out the dreadful confequences which proceed from an oppofite condua.— Scirrhous te'acles, ulcerous fore throats, madnefs, confumptions, carious bones? and a rotten progeny, are a few of the bleffings de- rived from this fource. ment of this difeafe is fimilar to that of a confirmed lues or pox. The yaws, a difeafe nr>w very common both in America and the Weft-India illamds, may aifo be cured in the fame manovt 344 DISEASES OF WOMEN. There is a fpecies of falfe reafoning, with regard to this difeafe, which proves fatal lo many. A perfon of a found conftitution con- traas a flight degree of the diforder. He gets well without taking any grear care, or ufing much medicine, and hence concludes that this will always be the cafe. The next time the difeafe occurs, though ten time more virulent, he purfues the fame courfe, and his conftitution is ruined. Indeed, the different degrees of virulence in the ftnall pox are not greater than in this difeafe, though, as the learned Sydenham obferves, in fome cafes the moft fkilful phyficians cannot cure, and in others the moft ignorant old woman cannot kill the patient in that diforder. Though a good conftitution is always in favour of the patient, yet too great a ftrefs may be laid upon it.— It does not appear from obfervation, that the moft robuft conftitu- tion is able to overcome the virulence of the venereal contagion, after it has got into the habit. In this cafe a proper courle of medicine is always indifpenfably neceffary. Although it is impoffible, on account of the different degrees of virulence, &c. to lay down fixsd and certain rules for the cure of this difeafe, yet the following general plan will always be found fafe, and often fuccefsful, viz. to bleed and adminifter gentle pur- ges with diuretics during the inflammatory ftate, and as foon as the fymptoms of inflammation are abated, to adminifter mercury, ia any form that may be moft agreeable to the patient. The fame medicine, affifted by the decoaion of farfaparilla, and a proper regimen, will not only fecure the conftitution againft any further progrefc of a confirmed pox, but will generally perform a complete Cure. CHAP. L. DISEASES OF WOMEN. \V OMEN in all civilized nations, have the management of domeftic affairs, and it.is very proper thev fhould, as Nature has made them lefs fit for the more aaive and laborious employments. This indulgence, however, is generally carried too far; and females, 1 inftead of being benefitted by it, are greatly injured from the want of exercife and free air. To be fatisfied of this, one need only com- pare the frefh and ruddy looks of a milk-maid, with the pale com- ' plexion of thofe females whofe whole time is fpent within doors. Though Nature has made an evident diftinaio.'i between the male and female with regard to bodily ftrength and vigour, yet fhe cer- tainly never meant, either that the one fhould be always without, or the other always within doors. The confinement of females, befides hurting their figure and complexion, relaxes their folids, weakens their minds, and diforders all the funaions of the body. Hence proceed obftruaions, indigeft- ion, flatulence, abortions, and the whole train of nervous diforders. Thefe not only unfit women for being mothers and nurfes, but often render them whirafical and ridiculous. A found mind de- fends fo much upon a healthy body, that where the latter is want- ing, the former m rarely to be found. OF THE MENSTRUAL DISCHARGE. 345 I Trre al^irays obferved that women who were chiefly employ- eel withou*- d'.nrs, in the different branches of hufbandry. garden- ing, and the l:ke, were almoft as hardy as their hnflynds, and that their children were Iikewife healthy. But as the bad effeas ci confinement and inaaivity upon both fexes have been ulrcdy fhewn, we fhell proceed to point out thofe crcumftances in the ftruaure and defign of females, which fubjea them to peculiar dif- eafes ; the chief of which are t'eir monthly evacuations, pregnancy, ard thild-bearing. Thefe indeed ca :not properly be celled difeofes, but, from th. del;cacy of the fex, and this being often improperly man- aged in fuch fituations, they become the fource of numerous calam- ities. " OF THE MENSTRUAL DISCHARGE. FEMALES generally begin to menftruate about the age of fifteen, ai.d l^ave it off about fifty, which renders thefe two periods the moft critical of their lives. About the firft appraran.ee if this difcharge, tne conftitution undergoes a very confiderable change, generally indeed for the better, though femetimes f r the wcife. The greateft care is now neceffary, as the future htdth ad happi. nefs of the female depends in a great meafure upon her condua at this period.* If a girl about this time of life be confined to the houfe, kept eonftaatly fitting, aad neither allowed to romp abou"-, nor employ- ed in any aft ve bufinefs, which gives exercife to the w; ole body, fhe becom s weak, relaxed, and puny ; her blood net being duly prepared, fhe looks pale and wan; her he.dvh, fp:rits, and vigour decline, and fhe finks into a val -tudinarian for life. Such is the fate of numbers of thofe unhappy females, woo, either from too much indulgence, or their own narrow circumftances, are, at this critical period, denied he benefit of exercife ar.d free air. .A hzy indolent difp .fitbu proves Iikewife very hurtful to girls at this period. One feldom meets with complaints frcm obftruc- tions among the more aa:ve and induftriou? p^rt of tee fex ; whereas the indolent and lazy are feldom free from them. To.efe are in a manner eaten up oy the chlorofis, or green fickneft, :nd other dceafrs of this nature. We would therefore recommend it to all who wifh to efcape thefe calami ies, to avoid indolence -and inaaivity, as their greateft enemies, and to be as much abroad in the open air as poifirlc. Ano1 h?r thing which pr'vesveryhurtf.il to g:rls about this period of life, is unwhol^f me food. Ford of all manner of tr?.fh, they often, indulge in it, till <h :r whofe humours are qu:te vitiated. Hence enfue indiireftions, wact of appetite, and a r.u-rerous train of evils. If the fluids be not duly prepared, it is utterly impoffible * It is the duty of mefhers, ar.d thofe who are Intruded w'th the education of g'rls, to inftruQ th--m early in tie conduit and managtmentof themfelves at this critical period of theirlivea. Falfe medefty, inattention, and ignorance of what is benefic.ai or hurtK.) a' this time, a<e the fource* of m ny difeaies *nd mhlortunes, in lire, v.hich a lew lenfib e ..H na from an experienced matron might have prevented. Nor is care 'cfs receffary in fhe lub- fequent returns of this difcharge. Taking improper food, violent afteftions ot the m-nd, ojr catching cold at this period, is often fuffic:ent to ruin the heaiih, cr to raider the female ever after incapable of procreation. (23) 346 OF THE MENSTRUAL DISCHARGE. that the fecretions fhould go properly on. Accordingly *e find that fuch girls as lead an indolent life, and eat great quantities tf trafh are not only fubjecT to obftruaions of the menfes, but likgwifj to ^andular obftruaions; as the fcrophula, or king s evil, &c. &" A dull difpofition is alfo very hurtful to girls at this period. It Is a r-re thing to fee a fprightly girl who does not enjoy good health, while the grave, moping, melancholy creature, proves the very prey of vapours and hyfterics. Youth is the fealon for mirth and cheerfulnefs. Let it therefore be indulged. It is an abfolute duty. To lay in a ftock of health in time of youth, is as neceffary a piece of prudence, as to make provifion againft the decays of old age- While, therefore, wife Nature prompts the happy youth to join in fprightlv amufements, let not the fevere diaates of hoary age tor- bid the ufeful impulfe, nor damp, with ferious gloom, the feafon deftined to mirth and innocent feftivity. Another thing very hurtful to females about this period oi life, is ftrait clothes. They are fond of a fine fhape, and foolifhly imagine that this can be acquired by lacing themfelves tight— Hence, by fqueezing the ftomach and bowels, they hurt the digef- tion, and occafion many incurable maladies. This error is not in- deed fo common as it has been; but, as fafhions change, it may come about again : we therefore think it not improper to mention it. I know many females, who, to this day, feel the direful effeas of that wretched cuftom which prevailed fome years ago, of fqueez- ing every girl into as fmall a fize in the middle as poffible. Humaa invention could not poffibly have devifed a praaice more deftrua'rye to health. After a female has arrived at that period of life when the menfes ufually begin to flow, and they do not appear, bu , on the contrary, her health and fpirits begin to decline, we would advife, inftead of fhutting thewoor girl up in the houfe, and dofmg her with fteel, afafcetida, and other naufeous drugs, to place her in a fitua- \ ion where fhe can enjoy the benefit of frefh air and agreeable com- pany. There let her eat wholefome food, take fufficieut exercife, 2nd amufe herfelf in the moft agreeable manner; and we have little reafon to fear, but Nature, thus affifted, will do her proper work.— Indeed fhe feldom fails, unlefs whiere the fault is on our fide. This difcharge in the beginnmg is feldom 16 inftantaneous as to furprife females unawares. It is generally preceded by fymp- toms which foretel its approach ; as a fenfe of heat, weight, and dull pain in the loins; diftenfion and hardnefs of the breafts ; head- ach ; lofs of appetite ; laffitude; palenefs of the countenance; and fometimes a flight degree of fever. When thefe fymptoms appear about the age at which the menftraal flux ufually begins, every thing fhould be carefully avoided which may obftrua that neceflary. cad falutary evacuation ; and all means ufed to promote it; as fit- ting frequently over the fteams of warm water, drinking warm di- luting liquors, &c. A After the own/knave once begun to flow, the greateft. c?.re Ciould be taken to avoid every thing that may tend to obftrucT them Fermles ought to be exceeding cautious of what thev eat OP THE MENSTRUAL DISCHARGE. 347 §r drink at the titne they are out of order. Every thing that is cold, or apt to four on the ftomach, ought to be avoided ; as fruit, butter-milk, and fuch like. Fifh, and all kinds cf food that are hard of di?eftion, are alfo to be avoided. As it is impoffible to mention everv thing that may difagree with individuals at this time, we would recommend it to every female to be very attentive to what difagrees with herfelf, and carefully to avoid it. Cold is extremely hurtful at this particular period. More of the fex date their difeafes from colds, caught while they are out of order, than from all other caufes. This ought furely to put them upon their guard, and to make them very circumfpea in their conduft at fuch times. A degree of cold that will not in the leaft hurt them at another time, will at this period be fufficient entirely to ruin their health and conftitution. The greateft attention ought Iikewife to be paid to the mind, which fhould be kept as eafy and cheerful as poffible. Every part of the animal economy is influenced by the paffions, but none more fo thin this. Anger, fear, grief, and other affeaions of the mind, often occafion obftruaions of the menftrual flux, which prove abfo- lutely incurable. , From whatever caufe this flux is obftruaed, except in the ftate of pregnancy, proper means fhould be ufed to reftore it. For this purpofe we would recommend fufficient exercife in a dry, open, and rather cool air; wholefome diet, and, if the body be weak and languid, generous liquors ; alfo cheerful company and all manner of amufements. If thefe fail, recourfe muft be had to medicine. '~ When obftruaions proceed from a weak relaxed ftate of the folids, fuch medicines as tend to promote digeftion, to brace the folids, and affift the body in preparing good blood, ought to be ufed. The principal of thefe are iron and the Peruvian bark, with .other bitter and aftringent medicines. Filings of iron may be infufed in wine or ale, two or three ounces to an Englifh quart, and after it has flood for two or three weeks it may be filtered, and about half a wine glafs of it taken twice a-day ; or prepared fteef may be taken in the dofe of half a drachm, mixed with a little honey or treacle, three or four times a-day. The bark and other bitters may either be taken in fubftance or infufion, as is moft agreeable to the patient. When obftruaions proceed from a vifcid ftate of the blood ; or from women of a grofs or full habit, evacuations, and fuchj»ed- icines as attenuate the humours, are neceffary. The patientmrthis cafe ought to be bled, to bathe her feet frequently in warm water, to take now and then a cooling purge, and to live upon a fpare th'n diet. Her drink fhould be whey, water, or fmall beer ; and fhe^ ought to take fufficient e?sercife. A tea-fpoonful of the tinaure ot black hellebore may alfo be taken twice a-day in a cup of warm water. When obftruaions proceed from affeaions of the mind, as #rief, fea-, anger, &c. every method fhould be taken to amufe and divert the patient. And that fhe may the more readily fcrgetw the «ufe of her affliction, fhe ought, if poffible, to. be removed rronj 34* 0F THE MENSTRUAL DISCHARGE. the cbfie w*ere it happened. A change cf place, by prefentiiM* the rni"nd with a variety cf new otic^s, has often a very happy influence in relieving it from the deepeft diftrefs. A .toothing, )<ind, and affable behaviour to females in this iituaLoa, is alfo of 'the firft imparlance. . An ot ftruaion cf the menfes is often the efwra cf other mala- ' dies. When this is the cafe, inftead of giving medicines to force that difcharge* which might be dangerous, we ought bv r.H means to endeavour to reftore the patient's health ?nd ftrength. Whea that is elfeaed, the other will return of courfe. But the menftrual.flux ir.riv be too great as well as too Mr all. When this happens, the patient becomes weak, the c: lour pale, the , appetite ziv' digeftion r.re bad, and ccdematcus fwdl;n -* of t'e feet, dropfies and confumptions often enfue. Thi? frequently happens to women about the ?.ge cf forty-five or fifty. ?nd is very difficult to cure. It rar.v proceed frcm a fedentary life ; a full diet, con. fifting chicflv'of falted, hiph-feafbned, or acrid focd; the ufe of fpir- ituouS liquors ; exceffive fatiaue ; relaxation ; a diffolved ftate cf the blood; violent pillions of tl e mind, &c. The treatment of this difeafe muft be varied according to its' caufe. When it is occafioned bv any error in the patienrs reg:-t . men, an cppofte* courfe to that which induced the diforder muft b^ '„. purfued, and fuch medicines taken as have a tendency to retrain, the flux, and counteraa the morbid affeaions of the fyftem fronf- whence it proceeds. ■ To reftn;in the flux, the patient fhould be kept quiet and eafy both in body and mind. . If it be very violent, fhe ought to l:e in bed, with her head low; to live upon acool and flender diet, as veal} or chicken broths with bread; and.to* drink dec--aions of nettle- roots, or the greater comfrey. If thefe be not fafficient to ftop the flux, ftronger "aftringents may be ufed, as Japan earth, alum, elixir cf vitriol, the Peruvian bark; &c* The uterine flux m-iy often d in quality as well as in quantity. What is ufuolly called '.kefluor albus, or whites, is a very common difeafe, and proves extremelv hurtful to delicate women. Thi< difchaige, however, is not always white, but fomefimes pale, yel- low, gr^en, or of a blackifh colour ; fometimes it is fharp and cor- rofive, fometimes foul and foetid, Sec. It is attended wi'h a pah complexion, pain in the back, lofs of appetite, -fwelling of the feet, and other figns of debility. It generally proceeds frcm a relaxed- fta»e ©f the body, arifing from indolence, the exceffive ufe of tea, coffee, or other weak and watery diet. To remove this difeafe, the patient muft take as much exercife as fhe can bear without fatigue. Her food fhould be folid and.. nourifhing, but of eafy digeftion ; and her drink rather generous, as red port or claret mixed with Pyrmont, Jfriftol, or lime-watery** •Two drachms of alum and one of Japan earth may be pounded together, and divided iflto e'ght or nine dofes, one of which may be taken three tim.s a-day. Perfons whofe rtomachs cannot bear the alum may take two table-fpoonsful of the tinc- ture of rofes thr«e or four times a-day, to each dofe of which ten drops of Maud2m»m. may be-.*' added. • j If theft fhou'd fail, hi'f a drachm of the Peruvian bark, in powder, with ten drops of tne elisir of vitriol may be taken in a glafs of red wine, fou? times a-day. OF PREGNANCY. 349 Tea and coffee are to be avoided. I have often known ftrccg broths have an exceeding good effea, arid fometimes a milk diet alone will perform a <mre. The patient ought not to lie too long a-bed. When medicine is neceflary, we know none prefei able .to the Peruvian bark, which in this cafe ought always to be taken in wbftacce. In v. arm weather the cold bath will be of confiderable fervice. > That period of life at which the menfes ceafe to flow, is Iikewife very critical to the fex.. Tne ftoppage of any cuftomary evacua- tion, hcv/ever fmall, is fufficient to diforder the whclr? frame, and often to deftroy life itfelf. Hence it ccmes to pafs, that^ fo many women either toil into chronic diforders, cr die about this time.— Such of them, however, as furvive it, without contr?afng any chronic difeafe, often become more healthy and hardy than they w are before, and enjoy ftrength and vigour to a very great age. ^lf the menfes ceafe all of a fudden in women of a full habit, they ought to abate fomewhat of their ufual quantity of fcod, efpecially of the more nourifhing kind, as flefh, eggs, Sec *! hey ought Iikewife to take fufficient exercife, aid to keep the bedy open. This may be done by taking, o.ce or twice a-week, a lifife rhubarb, or an infufion of hierapicra in wine cr brandy. it often happens '.h-t women of a grofs habit, at this pericd of life, have ulcerous fores break out about their ancles, cr in other paris of the body. Such ulcers ought to be confidered as critical. and fhould either be fuffered to continue open, cr have ariificial drains fubftituted in their fteal. Women, who will have Inch fores dried up, are cften foon after carried off by acute difeafes, or fall into thofe of^a chronic nature. OF PREGNANCY. THOUGH pregnancy is not a difeafe, yet that ftate is often attended with a variety of complaints which merit attendee," and which fometimes require the affiftance of medicine. Some women indeed are more healthy during their pregnancy ihnnat any oiher rime ; but this is by no means the general cafe : moft of them breed [mfe row, and are frequently indifpofed during the whele time or pregnancy. Few fatal difeafes,' however, happen during that period ; and h.irdly any, except abortion, that can be called dan* gerous. We fhall therefore pay particular attention to it, as it proves generally fatal to the child, and fometimes to the mother^ Pregnant womeri are often affliaed with the heart-burn.-*- The method of treating this complaint has been already pointed out. They are likewiie, in the more early periods of pregnancy, often harraffed with ficknefs and vomiting, efpecially in the mor- nin,z. The method of relieving thefe complaints has alfo been fhewn. Both the head-ach and tooth-ach are very troubleforce fymptoms of pregnancy. The former may generally be removed by keeping tne body gently open, by the ufe of prunes, t^s, roafted apples, aad fuch like. "When the pain is very violent, bleeding may >e nec-ifiry. For the treatment of toe latter, we muft refer to that article. Several other complaints iad;e.it to pregnant 35o OF PREGNANCY. women migttf be mentioned, as a cough and difficulty of breath- ing, fuppreffion and incontinency of urine, &c.; but as all of thefe have been taken notice of before, it is needlefs to repeat them. Every pregnant woman is more or lefs in danger of abortion. This fhould be guarded againft with the greateft care, as it not only weakens the conftitution, but renders the woman liable to the fame misfortune afterwards.* Abortion may happen at any period of pregnancy, but it is moft common in the fecond or third month — Sometimes, however, it happens in the fourth or fifth. If it hap- psns within the firft month it is ufually called a falfe conception ; if after the feventh month, the child may often be kept alive by prcper care. The common caufes of abortion are, the death of the chiid ; weaknefs or relaxation of the mother; great evacuations ; violent exercife; raifing great weights ; reaching too high ; jumping r>r ftepping from an eminences vomiting j coughing ; convulfion fit; blows on the belly ; falls ; fevers ; difagreeable fmells ; excels of blood ; indolence ; high living, or the contrary ; violent paffions or affeaions of the mind, as fear, grief. Sec The figns of approaching abortion are, pain in the loins, or about the bottom of the belly ; a dull heavy pain in the infide of the thigh ; a flight degree of coidnefs, or fhivering ; ficknefs, palpi} ■ tat ion cf the heart; the breaft s become flat and foft; the bellf*^ tails; and there is a difcharge of blood or watery humours from the womb. To prevent abortion, we would advife women of a wea^ or relaxed habit to ufe folid food, avoiding great quantities of tea* and other weak and watery liquors; to rife early and go foon to bed ; to fhun damp houfes; to take frequent exercife in the open air, but to avoid fatigue ; and never to go abroad in damp foggy weather, if they can fhun it. Women of a full habit ought to ufe a fpare diet, avoiding ftrong liquors, and every thing that may tend to heat the body, or increafe the quantity of blood. Their diet fhould be of an open- ing nature, confifting principally of vegetable fubftances. Every woman with child ought to be kept cheerful and eafy in her mind. Her appetites, even though depraved, ought to be indulged as far as prudence will permit. When any figns of abortion appear, the woman ought to be .laid in bsd on a mattrefs, with her head low. She fhould be kepti Qviet, and her mind foothed and comforted. 6he ought not to be Igept too hot, nor to take any thing of a heating nature. Her food fhould confift of broths, rice and milk, jellies, gruels made of oat- meal and the like, all of which ought to be taken cold. U fee be able to bear it, fhe fhould lofe at leaft half a pound. of blood from the arm. Her drink ought to be barley-water fharp*: ened with juice of lemon ; or fhe may take half a drachm of pow-" * Every mother who p?r;Ures an abortion does it at the hazard of her 1'fe; yet t are not a tew waojun this ruk merely ^prevent the trouble .of'.caring and biinaing up thilut-a^ Jtu uiely a mod unnatuial crime, and eannot, even in the moft abandoned, be «fe*ed wuncuthciror; but in the decent matron, it is ftill more unpardoflable.-Thoie Lvcry mother who p?r;«res an abortion does it at the hazard of her 1'fe; yet there t a tew w.ioiun this ruk merely ^prevent the trouble .of'.caring and biinaing up ;a/# Jtu uiely a mod unnatuial crime, and eannot, even in the moft abandoned, be I wuncuthciror; but in the decent matron, it is ftill more unpardoflable.-Thoie OF CHILD.BIRTH. & deed nitre, in a cup of water-gruel, every five or fix hours. If the woman be feized with a violent loofenefs, fhe ought to drink the decoaion 3f calcined hartshorn prepared. If fhe be affeaed with vomitting, let her take frequently two table-fpoonsful of the faline mixture. In general, opiates are of fervice; but they fhould always be given with caution. Sanguine robuft women, who are liable to mifcarry at a cer- tian time of pregnancy, ought always to be bled a few days before that period arrives, oy this means, and obferving the regimen above prefcribed, they might often efcape that misfortune. Though we recommend due care for preventing abortion, we would not be underftood as reftraining pregnant women from their ufual exercifes. This would generally operate a quite contrary way. Want of exercife not only relaxes the body, but induces a plethora, or too great a fulnefs of the veffels, which are the two principal caufes of abortion. There are, however, fome women of fo delicate a texture, that it is neceffary for them to avoid almoft every kind of exercife during the whole period of pregnancy. OF CHILD-BIRTH. MANY difeafes proceed from the want of due care in child- .. bed ; and the morehafdy part of the fex are moft apt to defpife the neceffary precautions in this ftate. This is peculiarly the cafe with young wives. They think, when the labour-pains are ended, the danger is over; but in truth it may only then be faid to be begun. Mature, if left to herfelf, will feldom fail to expel the frtus; bflt proper care and management are certainly neceflary for the re- covery of the mother. No doubt mifchief may be done by too much as well as by too little care. \ Hence females who have the greateft number of attendants in child-bed generally recover worft. But'this is not peculiar to the ftate of child-bed. Exceffive care always defeats its own intention/ and is generally more dangerous than none at all.* During aaual labour, nothing of a heating nature ought to be given. The woman mav now and then take a little panada, and her drink ought to be toaftand water, or thin groat gruel. Spirits, wines, cordial-waters, and other things which are given with a view to ftrengthen the mother, ancl promote the birth, for the moft part, tend only to increafe the fever, inflame the womb, and retard the labour. Befides, they endanger the woman afterwards, as they often occafion violent and mortal haemorrhages, or difpofe her to eruptive and other fevers. • though the management of women in child-bed has been pra&ifed as an employment fince the earlieft accounts of time ; yet it is ftill in moft countries on a very bad footing— Few women think of following this employment till they are reduced to the neceflity cf doing it for bread. Hence not one in an hunHred of ther\i havs any education, or pioper knowl- edge of their bufinefs. When the labour proves tedious and difficult, to prevent in- fhmmati.. ns, it w 11 be proper to bleed. The paffage ought to be gen ly rubbed with a lit le hft pomatum or frefh butter, and cloths wrung out of warm water applied over the belly. If nature feems to fink, ;ind the woman is greatly exhaufted with fatigue, a draught of jrenersiis wine, or fome other cordial, may be given, but cot other- wife. Thefe dreaions are fufficient in natural labours ; and in ali preternatural cafes, a fkilful furgeon, or man-midwife, ought to be coiled as foon as poffible. After delivery, the woman ought to be kept as quiet and eafy a? poffible.* Her food fhould be light and thin, as gruel, panada, Sec. and her drink weak aad diluting. ' OF CHILLT-BIKlJtf* 3<? nation to go frequently to ftool j a heat, and fometimes total fup* preifion of urine. This mutt be treated like other inflammatory diforders, by bleeding and plentiful dil.ticn. A fuppreffion cf the lochia, or ufual difch-.rges after delivery, and the io,ilk-fev*r, muft be treated nearly in the lame manner as an infl immation of the womK In all thefe cafes, the fafeft courfe is plentiful dilution, gentle evacuatione, and fomentations of the parts affeaed. In the milk-fever, the breafts may be e.nbrocatei., witn a little warm linfeed-oil, or the leaves of red cabbage may be applied to them. The chid fhould be often put to the breaft, or it mould be drawn by tome ether p.rfon. Nothing would tend more n prevent f e milk-fevrr than put- ting the child early to the oreaft. The.cuftom of not allowing children to fuck for the firft two cr three days, is contrary to Na- ture, and common fenfe, and is very hurtful both to the mother and child. .,,.,. n Every mother who has milk m her breafts, ought ei'ber to buckle her own child, or to have her breafts frequently drawn, at leaft for the firft month. When an inflammation happens in thebre-ft, attenfed with rednefs, hardnefs, and ether fymptoms of fuppuration, the fafeft application is a pculdce of bread and milk, foftened with oil or frefh butter. This may be renewed twice a-day, till the tumour be either difcuffed or broug t to fuppuration. The ufe of rep dlents, in this cafe, is very dangerous ; they oftea occafion fevers, and fometimes canors ; whereas a u.ppuraticn is feldom attended with any danger, and his often.the moft falutary elk as. When the nipples are fretted or c'r.apt, they may be anointed with a mixture of oil and bees-w?x, or a little p wdered rum aribic may be fprinkled on them. I hove feen Hucg?ry water applied to the nipples have a very good effea. Should the comprint prove obftinate, a cooling purge may be given, which ge-erally removes it. Th? miliary fever, is a dileafe incident to women in child-bed; but as it has been treated of alrerdy, we fhad take no further notice of it. The celebrated Hoffman obferves. that this fever of child-bed women might generally be prevented, if they, during their pr.gnancy, were, regular in their diet, ufed moderate exercife, took now and then a gentle laxative of manna, rhubarb, or cream of tarar ; not forgetting to bleed in the firft months, and t~ avoid all fharp air. When the labour is coming on, it is not to be haft. ened with forcing medicines, which inflame the blood and humours, or put them into unnatural commotions. Care fhould he taken, after the bdrth, that the naturil excretions proceed regularly ; and , if the pulfe be quick, a little nitrous powder, or fome other coding medicines, fhould be adminiftered. 3^4 OF CHILD-BIRTH. The moft fatal diforder confequent upon delivery is the puerpe- ral, or child-bed fever. It generally makes its attack upon the fecond or third day after delivery. Sometimes indeed it comes on fooner, and at other times, though rarely, it does not appear before the fifth or fixth day. t It begins like moft other fevers, with a cold or fhivering fit, which is fucceeded by reftleffnefs, pain of the head, great ficknefs at the ftomach, and bilious vomiting. The pulfe is' generally quick, the tongue dry, and there is a remarkable deprellion of fpirits and lofs of ftrength. A great pain is ufually felt ia the back, hips, and region of the womb ; a fudden change in the quan- tity or quality of the lochia alfo takes place ; and the patient is fre- quently troubled with a tenefmus, or conftant inclination to go to ftool. The urine, which is very high coloured, is difcharged in fmall quantity, and generally with pain. The belly fometimes fwells to a confiderable bulk, and becomes fufceptible of pain from the fligh'eft touch. When the fever has continued for a few days, the fymptoms of inflammation ufually fubfide, and the difeafe ac- quires a more putrid form. At this period, if not fooner, a bilious or putrid loofenefs, of an obfthate and dangerous nature, comes on, and accompanies the difeafe through all its future progrefs. There is net any difeafe that requires to be treated with more "(kill and attention than this ; confequently the beft affiftance ought to be obtained as foon as poffible. In women of plethoric conftitu- tions, bleeding will generally be proper at the beginning; it ought however to be ufed with caution, and not to be repeated unlefs where the figns of inflammation rife high; in which cafe it will alfo be neceffary to apply a bliftering plafter to the region of the womb. During the rigour, or cold fit, proper means fhould be ufed to abate its violence and fhorten its duration. For this purpofe the patient may drink freely of warm diluting liquors, and, if low, may take now and then a cup of wine whey; warm applications to the extremities, as heated bricks, bottles or bladders filled with warm water, and fuch like, may alfo be ufed with advantage. Emollient clyfters of milk and water, or of chicken water ought to be frequently adminiftered through the courfe of the difl eafe. Thefe prove beneficial by promoting a difcharge from the inteftines, and alfo by aaing as a kindly fomentation to the womb and parts adjacent. Great care however is requifite in giving them on account of the tendernefs of the parts in the pelvis at this time* To evacuate the offending bile from the ftomach, a vomit is generally given. But as this is apt to increafe the irritability of the ftomach, already too great, it will be fafer to omit it, and to give in its ftead a gentle laxative, which will both tend to cool the body, and to procure a free difcharge of the bile.* .. T„he medicine which I have always f yud to fucceed beft in this difeafe, is the faline draught. This, if frequently repeated, will otttn put a ftcp to the vomiting, and at the fame time leffen the cMu'be^ to women in moarmmlaentda:*,- b, aftf0R< r^l^^^^^^^^ ** OF CHILD-BIRTH. 355 violence of the fever. If it runs off bv ftool, or if the patient be reftlefs, a few drops of laudanum, or fome fyrup of poppies, may occafionallybe added. If the ftools fhould prove fo frequent as to weaken andexhauft the patient, a ftarch clyfter, with thirty or forty drops of laudanum in it, may be adminiftered as occafion fhall require; and the drink may be rice-water, in every Englifh pint of which half an ounce of gum-arabic has been diffolved. Shcfald thefe fail, recourfe muft be had to Columbo-root, or fome other ftrong aftringent. Though in general the food ought to be light, and the drink diluting, yet when the difeafe has been long protraaed, and the pa- tient is greatly fpent by evacuations, it will be neceffary to fupport her with nourifhing diet and generous cordials. It was obferved that this fever, after continuing for fome time cften acquires a putrid form. In this cafe the Peruvian bark mull be given, tither by itfelf, or joined with cordials, as circumftances may require. As the bark in fubftance will be apt to purge, it may be given in decpaion or. jufufion mixed with the tinaure of rofes,. or other gentle aftringents; or a fcruple of the extraa of bark with half an ounce of fpirituous cinnamon-water, two ounces of common water, and ten drops of laudanum, may be made into a draught, and given every fecond, third, or fourth hour, as faall be found neceffary. When the ftomach will not bear any kind of nourifhment, tUfc patient may be fupported for fome time by clyfters of beef-tea, or chicken-water. To avoid this fever, every woman in child-bed ougrTt to be~ kept perfefily eafy ; her food fhould be light and fimple, and her bed-chamber cool, and properly ventilated. There is not any thing more hurtful to a woman in this fituation than being kept too warm. She ought not to have her body bound too tight, nor to rife too foon from bed after delivery ; catching cold is alio to be avoided ; and a proper attention fhould be paid to cleanlmefs. To prevent the milk-fever, the breafts ought frequently to be drawn ; and if they are filled previous to the onfet of a fever, they fhould, upon its firft appearance, be drawn, to prevent the nvlk frcm becoming acrid, and its being abforbed in this ftate. Co/' ive- nefs is Iikewife to be avoided. This wil Ibe beft elfeaed by the ufe of mild clyfters and a laxative diet. We fnall conclude our obfervations on child-bed women by recommending it to them, above all things, to beware of cold— Poor women, whofe circumftances oblige them to quit their bed too Icon, often contraa difeafes from cold, of which they never recover. It is a pity the poor are not better taken care of in this fituat;on. • But the better fort of women run the greateft h?.z rd from tr.o much heat. They are generally kept in a fort of bagnio for the firft eight or ten days, and then drtfled out to fee company. The dan- ger of this conduct muft be obvious to every one. The fuperftitious cuftom of obliging women to keep the houfe till they go to church, is Hkewife a very conyncn caufe of catchrog 3e6 QF BARRENNESS. col;'. OF BARRENNESS. BARRENNESS may be very properly reckoped among the difc-af scf females, as few married women who have pet children enj'.y a good f!ate of health. It may proceed from varicus amies, as4 igh l.vi-rg, grief, rclax2tion, &c. butitis chiefly owing to an ob- Jb-uaion cr irregularity of '.he menftrm1] flux. u is very certain that high living vitiates the humcur?, and prevents fecundity. We feidom find a bo.rren v oman among the labouring poor, while nothing is more common among the rich and i-ffluen*. The inhabitants cf every country are prolific in propor- tion to their poverty; and it would be an eafy matter to adduce many inftances of wemen, who, by being reduced to live entirely upon a milk and vege.obfe diet, hove conceived and brought forth chil. crtn, though they never had any before. Would the rich \ii"e the fame fcrt of food and exercife as the better 'fort of peafants. they would feldcm ! ave caufe to envy th ir poorvaffals and dependants,^ the Uefficg cf a numerous and healthy offspring, w hile they pine in forrow for the want cf even a fingle heir to their extenfive do- mains. Affluence begets indolence, which not only vitiates the hu- mours, but induces a general relaxation of the folids; a ftate highly unfavourable to procreation. To remove this, we wculd recom- mend tne following courfe : Firft, fufficient exercife in the open air; f ccndly, a diet confifting chiefly of milk and vegetables ; * thirdly, the ufe cf aftringent medicines, as fteel, alum, dragon's I food, elixir cf vitriol, and theSpaw or Tunbridge waters,Peruvi- an bark, &c.; and laftly, al cv? all, the cold bath. Bairennefs is often the confequence of grief, fudden fear, .•n.very, or any of the p ffions which tend to obftrua the menftru- al flux. When barrennifs is fufpeaed to proceed from affeaions cf ihe mind t•.e perfon ought to be kept as eafy and cheerful as poffible ; all difagreeable objeas are to be avoided, and overy n cthod uk-m to amufe and entertain the fancy. CHAP. LI. DISEASES OF CHILDREN. Wf.ISER.ABLE indeed is the lot of man in the ftate of ii> frrey * He comes into the Wv rid more helplefs than any other ani- mal, and ftands much longer in need of the proteaion and care of us parents; but, alas ! this care is not always beft owed upon him; 'Dr. Chfvre avers, that want of children is oftener the fault of the male than of the female, a^d ftror.Jy r. commends a m'.'kand vegetable diet to th- former as well as the latter ; H.irr, '... r his: fr end Dr. Taylor, whom he calls the Miik-doaor of Croydon, had brought tundry opulent ramifies in his neighbourhood, who had continued fome years atter mama** *k;hoLt pro-:rnv, to have feveral Gne thildron, fey iteeping both paisati for aconiidetabt Rme, r.n -,i■'.!.' ^;o referable diit jjidhstssLd ut L.HILDREN. 3£7 and when it is, he often fuffers as much from improper management as he would have done from n?«i;le&. Hence the officious care of parents, nurfes, and midwives, oecoraes one of the moft fruitful Sources of the diforders of infents.f It muft beobvousto every attentive perfon, tint the firft dif- eafes of children arife chiefly from their bowels. As thefe fymptoms evidently arife from fomewhat t'-af :rri- tates ihe inteftines, doubtlefs the proper method ofcuremn^ be to exp 1 it as foon as p ffibh, The moft fafe and effectual method of d:iog tois is by gentle vomits. TV-fe who are willing to ufe the emeih" tar- tar, may give fix or feven drops of the antimonial wine, in a tea- fpoonful of water or thin gruel. Should this however not hap- pen, and if the child be coftive, fome gentie purge will oe neceffary; for this purpofe, fome mannn and pulp of caffi 1 m?y bo did :lved in boilrs water, and given in fmall quantities f;ll it operates ; cr, wh:t wi'i aofw-r nther betor, a few grains of magnefia alba may be-mix. d in r.nv kind of food t at is giveo. to the child, and con- tinued till it has the defired effea. If thefe msdirines b? properly admin'ftered, and the child's b.Tly and limbs frequently roobeo with a warm hand before the fire, they will feldom fail "ro reiieve- thofe aff aions of the ftomach and bowels from which infants fuf- fer fo much. Thefe general direftions include moft of what can bedo:e for relieving toe internal diforcfers of infants. Thefe, as was formerly obferved, are princp-illy owing to too hot a regimen, and co/aquently will be moft eieJtuoily relieved by gentle evacuations.
