This includes a great number of the male, and almoft all the female inhabitants of great cities. Yet evm thofe perfons ought to be cautious in ufing the cold bath. Nervous people have often weak bowels, and may, as well as others, be fubject to congeftions and obftructiuns of the vifcera ; and in this cafe they will not be able to bear the effects of cold water. For them, and indeed for ail delicate people, the beft plan would be to accuftora themfelves to it by the moft pleafing and gentle degrees. They ought to begin with the temperate bath, and gradually ufe it cooler, till at length the cold proves quite agree- abfe. Nature revolts againft all great tranfitions; and thofe who do violence to her dictates, have often caufe to repent of their temerity. ^ Wherever cold bathing is practifed, there ought Iikewife to be tepid baths for the purpofe mentioned above. Indeed it is the practice of fome countries to throw cold water over the patii£t as loon as he comes out of the warm bath ; but though this may not injure a Ruffian peafant, we dare not recommend it to the inhabit- ants of this country. The ancient Greeks and Romans, we are told, when covered with fweat and duft, ufed to plunge into the ri- vers, without receiving the fmalleft injury. Though they might often efcape danger from this imprudent conduct, yet it was cer- tainly contrary *o found reafon. I have known many robuft men throw away their lives by fuch an attempt. We would not how- ever advife patients to go into the cold water when the body is chilly ; as much ex-rcife, at leaft, ought to be taken, as may excte a gentle glow all over the body, but by no means fo as to over- heat it. * The late celebrated Dr. Smollet has indeed faid, that if he were perfuaded he had an ulcer in che lunjjs, he would jump into the cold bath j but here the Doctor evidently ihewj more courage than discretion ; and that he wu more a man of wit than a phyfician, every To young people, and particularly to children, cold bathing is "Of the firft importance. Their lax fibres render its tonic powers peculiarly proper. It promotes their growth, increafes their ftrength,* and prevents a variety of difeafes incident to child- hood. Wh -re infants early accuftomed to the cold bath, it would feldom difagree with them ; and we fhould fee fewer inftances of the fcrophula, rickets, and other difeafes wnich prove fatal to ma- ny., and make others miferable for life. Sometimes indeed, thefe diforders render infants incapable of bearing the fhcck of ccld wa- ter ; but this is owing to their not having been early and regularly accuftomed to it. It is however neceffary here to caution young men againft too frequent bathin?; as 1 have known many fatal confequences refult from the daily practice of plunging into rivers, and continuing there too long. The moil proper time of the day for ufing the cold bath is no doubt the morning, or at leaft before dinner ; and the beft mode, that of quick immerfion. As cold bathing has a conftant tendency to propel the bl©od and other humours towards the head, it ought to be a rule always to wet that part as foon as pcffitle. By due attention to this circumftance, there is reafon to believe, that vio- lent head-achs, and other complaints, which frequently proceed from cold bathing, might be often prevented. The cold bath, When too long continued in, net only occafions an exceffive flux of humours towards the head, but chills the blood, cramps the mufcles, relaxes the nerves, and wholly defeats the inten- tion of bathing. Hence, by not adverting to this circuirftance, expert fwimmers are often injured, and fometimes even lofe their lives. All the beneficial purpofes of cold bathing are anfwered by one immerfion, at a time ; and the patient ought to be rubbed dry the moment he comes out of the water, and fhould continue to take exercife for fome time after. When cold bathing occafions chillnefs, lofs of appetite, liftlefl- nefs, pain of the breaft or bowels, a proftration of ftrength, cr vio- lent head achs, it orght to be difcontinued. Though thefe hints are by no means intended to point out all the cafes where cold bathing may be hurful, nor to illuftrate its extenfive utiliry as a medicine ; yet it is hoped they may ferve to guard people againft fome of thofe errors into which, from mere inattention, they are apt to fall, and thereby not only; endanger their own lives, but bring an excellent medicine into difrepute. OF DRINKING THE MINERAL WATERS. THE internal ufe of water, as a medicine, is no lefs an object of the phyfician's attention than the external. Pure elementary water is indeed the moft inoffenfive of all liquors, and conftitute:: a principal part of the food of every animal. ,But this element is often impregnated with fubftances of a very a&ive and penetrating nature; and of fuch aninfidious quafty, that, while they promote * The celebrated Ga'en fays, that immerfion in cold water is fit only for the young of iions and bears; and recommends warm bathing, as conducive to the growth and ftrengtii of- infants. How egreeioufly do the greateft men err whenever they lofe aght of fa4}S, uid lUB.'t-ijjir. te>f ning in phyfic in pla«.e of obfervation and experience. .27) *i0 OF DRINKING MINERAL WATERS. certain fecretions, and even alleviate fome difagreeable fymptoms* they weaken the powers of life, undermine the conftitution, and lay the frundation of worfe difeafes than thofe which they were ^employed to remove. Of tris every practitioner muft have feen .£ -inftances ; and phyficians of eminence have more than once declar- * ed that they have known more dileafes occafioned than re noved by the ufe cf mineral w.-ters. This doubtlefs' fv-s proceeded fr^m the ?bufe of :hefe powerful medicines, which evinces the neceffity cf ufing them with c uiion. > Bv examining the contents of the mineral waters -which are moft ufed in this country, we fhall be enabled to form an idea of the danger which may arife from an improper application of them either ex.erndly or internally, though it is to the latter of thefe that the prefent obf.rvntions are chiefly co::fined. The waters moft in ufe fer medical purpofes in Britain, are thofe impre nated with falts, fulpher, iron, and mephitic air, either feparately, or vari ufly combined. Of t^efe the m-ft p werful is the filine fubhuv-niv water f Harrowgate', of which I h.-ve had mere occ -.lion to obferve the penici: us confequence.'?, when impro- perly ufed, than of any < their. To t is, therefore, the following re- marks will more immediately relate, hough they will be found ap- plicable t all the purging waters in the kingdom which are ftrong enough to merit attention.* The errors whrh fo often defeat the intention of drinking the purgative mineral waters, and whic fo frequently prove injurious to the patient, proceed from fe manner cf ufing them, the quantity taken, the regimen purfued, or ufing tlv-m in cafes where they are not proper. A very hurtful p-"j:idice ftill prevails in this country, that all difeafes muft b 2 cured by medicines taken into the ftomach, and that 'he more vlTmtly thefe medicin°s p> rate, they are the more likely to h?ve the defired eff tt. This opinion has proved fatal to thoufands, a-'d will, in all probability, deftroy many mor- before it can be whcliy erad'eated. Purging isoifn ufeful in acute dif- eafes, and in cnronicM cafes mav pave ti e way fer the operation of other-med'does; out it vill feldom pert rm a cure ; and by ex- haufting the ftrer.gth cf me patient, will cften leave him in a worfe condition t an v. found him. That this is frequentiy the cafe with regard to 'he mere aciiv-3 mi end wat-.rs, every perfon converfant in thefe 11 at'ers will readily allow. Strong fliv mlams applied to the ftomach and bowels for a length of time, muft tend to weaken and'deffroy their energy; and what ftimulan'F are more active than felt and fulphur, efpecial- ly whe- thefe fubftances are intimately combined, and carried through tve fyft?m by the penetrating medium of water ? 1 hofe bowels muft be ftrong iudeed, which can withftand the daily • Th? greateft clafs *f nrneral waters in this country is the chalvbeate. In many parts of Britain thefe are to be found n almoft evev firl.t ; but thofe chiefly in ufc, for medical purpol' • are the purgiog cha!yb;ate«, as the vvatersof Scarborough, Cheltenham, Thorp Arch, Ncv'l Holt, &c. Of thofe which do not purge, the waters of Tunbridge ftand in the higheft repute. The Saline purging waters, sn thofe of Aclon, Epfin, Kilborn, &c. are alfr. in very general efteem ; but the fountains moft frequented by the fick in this country, ■„,■ -•"- to which the minerals impart a certain degree of heat, as Bath, Briftol, Buxton, tee OF DRUSKING MINERAL WATERS. 41 r operation of fuch active principles for months together, and not be injured. This however is the plan purfued by moft of thofe who drink the purging mineral waters, and whofe drcumftances will permit them to continue long enough at thofe fafhionable^jll places of refort. Many people imagine that every thing depends on the quan- tity of water taken, an■'! that the more they drink thev will the fooner get well. T is is an egregious trror ; for while the un- happy patient thinks he is by this means eradicating his diforder, he is often in fa£t undermining the powers of life, and ruini: j h:s conftitution. Indeed nothing can do this fo effectually as weak- ening the powers of digeftion by the improper application, of ftrong ftimulants. „The very effence of health depends on the di- geftive organs performing'her due functions, and the meft tedi- ous maladies are all connected with indigeftion. Drinking the water in too great quantity, not only injures the bowels and occafions indigeftion, but generally defeats the intention for which it is taken. The difeafes for the cure of which mineral wafers are chiefly celebrated, are moftly of the chronic kind ; and it is well known that fuch difeafes can only be cured by the flow operation of alteratives, or fuch medicines as act by inducing a gradual change in the habit. This requires length of time, and never can be effected by medi- cines which run off by ftool, and operate chiefly on the firft paffages. Thofe who wifh for the cure of any obftinate malady from the mineral waters, ought to take them in fuch a manner as hard- ly to produce any effect whatever on the bowels. With this view a half-pint glafs may be drank at bed time, * and the fame quantity an hour before breakfaft, dinner, and fupper. The dole, however, muft vary according to circumftances. Even the quan- tity mentioced above will purge fome perfons, while 01 hers will drink twice as much without being iu the leaft moved by it. Its operation on the bowels is the ody ftandard for ufing the wa'er as an alterative. No more ought to be taken than barely to move the body ; nor is it always neceffary to carry it this length, provided the water goes off by the other emunctories, and does not occafion a chilhefs, or flatulency in the ftomach or bowels. When th? wa- ter is intended to purge, the quantity mentioned above may be all taken before breakfaft. I would ot only caution patients who drink the purging min- eral waters ever night, to avoid heavy fuppers, but alfo from eating heavy meals at any time. The ftimulus of water, impregnated with falts, feems to create a falfe appetiie. I have feen a ddicats perfon, after dri iking the Harrowgate waters of a morning, eat a breakfaft fufficient to have ferved two ploughmen, devour a plentiful dinner of flefh and fifh, and, to crown all> eat fuch a fup- per as might have fiatisfied an hungry porter. * \V ,e:> 1 fpeak of drinking a glafs of th$ w3'er ovar-night, I muft beg leaVe to c?utiort thofe who fal ow this plan aganft eating heavy luppirs.^ The late Dr. Daultry of York, who was th; firft that brought the Harrowgate waters into repute, uied to adviie his pa- rents to drink a. glafs before they went to bed ; the confequence of which was, that having eat a flefh fupper, and the water operating in the nigi.t, they were cften tormsitejl y<ij£ I :"pes and obliged to cajl for medic*! all1.ft.yjc5. 412 OF DRINKING MINERAL WAlERS. All this, indeed, the ftomach feemed to crave; but this crav- ing had better remain not quite fatisfied, than that the ftomach fhould be loaded with what exceeds its powers. To ftarve pa- ftients was never my plan; but i am clearly of opinion, that in the ufe of all purging miner .1 waters, a light and rather di- luting diet is t e moft proper; and that no perfen, during fuch a courfe, cught to* at to the full extent to what his ap- petite crave?. To promote the operation of mineral waters, and to carry tlvm t\ rough the f/i.em, exercife is ind fpenfably neceffary. -This may be taken in a y manner that is moft agreeable to the pr tient; but he ought never to carry it to excefs. The beft kinds of exer- cife are t ofe conne£bd with amufement. Every thing that tends to exhderate the fpirits, not only promotes the operation of the waters, "out rets as a med'eine. All who refort to the mt'.eral wa- ters ought ther:fore to leave every care behind, to mix with the ' company and t*> make themfelves as cheerful and happy as pof- fible. From this conduct, affifted by the free an.' wholefome air of thofe fafhioraole plac s of refort, and alfo the regular and early hours which are ufually kept, tne pa!iect often receives more ben- efit than from ufing the waters. But th° greateft errors in drinking the purging mineral waf ers arife from their b-ing ufed in cafes where they are abfolutely im- , , proper, and adverfe to the nature of the difeafe. When people \ hear of a wonderful cure having been performed by feme mineral / water, they immediately conclude that it will cure every thiDg, and accordingly fwallow it d; wn, when they might as well take poifon. Patients cught to be well informed, before they begin to *•* drink the more actve kinds of mineral waters, of the propriety of the courfe, ard fhould never perfift in ufing them when they are found to aggr ivate the diforder. In all cafes where purging is indicated, the faline mineral wa- ters will be found to fulfil this intention better than any ether medicine. This operation, if taken in proper quantity, is gen- erally mild ; and they are neither found to irritate the nerves, nor debilitate the patient fo much as the other purgatives. As a purgative, thefe waters are chiefly recommended in difeafes of the firft paffages, accompanied with, or proceeding from, inactivity of the ftomach and bowels, acidity, ,indigeftion,- vitiated bile, worms, putrid fordes, the piles and jaundice. In moft cafes cf this kind they are the beft medicines that can be ad- minjlered. But when ufed with this vieT?, it is fufficient to take then twice, or at moft three times a week, fo as to move the bo- *>y th-re or four times ; and it will be proper to continue this courfe for fome weeks. Bu' tne operation of the more active mineral waters is net' corfi *ed to the bowels. They often promote the difcharge of the urine, and not unfrequentiy increafe the perfpiration. This fhews that tney are capable of penetr th g into every part cf the body, and of ftimulating the whofe fyftem. Hence arifes their efficacy in removing the moft obftinate of all diforders, ol^ruclisns <f the OBSERVATIONS CONCERNING DIET, Wc. , 413 glandular and lymphatic fyftem. Under this clafs is comprehended the fcrophuhi or King's evil, indolent tumours, obftructions cf the liver, fpleen, kidnies, and mefenteric glands. When thefe great purpofes are to be effected, the waters muft be ufed in the 2d-- ual manner mentioned above, and perfiftco in for a length of time. It w'll be proper however new and then to difcontinue their ufe fr a few days. The next g*-eat clafs of difeafes where mineral waters are fcund to be beneftVal, are thefe of the fkin, as the itch, fcab, tet- ters, ringworms, fcaly eruptions, leprcfits, blotches, foul ulcers, Sec.' Tnough thefe may feem fupexficial, yet they are often the m ft obftinate which the phyfician has to encounter, and not un- frequeniy ft h's fkillat defiance : but they will fometimes yield to the appllcni- .>n of mineral waters for a fufficient le g h of time, and in moft c?fes of this kind thefe waters deferve a trial. The faline fulphureous waters, fiich as th ;fe of Mcffat in Scot- land, and Harrowgate in England,' arc- the m dt Ilk lv to fucceed in difeafes of the fkin ; but for t; is purpofe it will be neceffcrv not only to drink the waters, but Iikewife to ufe them externally. To enumerate m^re particul?rly the qualities of t''e different mineral *-aterp, to fpecify thofe diljeafes in which they are refpect- ively indie ted, and t) pc int out their prop r m des of appii ation, would be an ufeful, and by no means a difagreeable employ- ment ; but as the limits prefcribed to thefe remarks will not allow m° to tre t the fubj ct at more length, I fhall conclude by obferving, thst when ver the mineral waters are found to exhauft the ftrength, deprefs the fpirits, take away the appetite, excite f-vers, diftend the bowels, or occafion a cough, they ought to be difcontinued. CHAP. LWL VESERFATIONS COHCEXHING THE DIET OF THE COMMON PEOPLE, XECC:,-- MENDING A METHOD OF liriXG LESS EXPENSIVE, AND MORE C9NDVC1VE to health than the present. XLXPERIENCE proves that not a few of the difeafes in- cident to the inhabitants of this country, are owing to their mode of living. The vegetable produ&ions they continue, fall confider- ably fhorf>f the proportion which they ought to bear to the animal part of their food. The conftant ufe of bread, and animal fub- ftances, excites an unnatural thirft, and leads to the immoderate ufe of beer and other Simulating liquors, which generate difeafe and reduce the lower orders of people to a ftate of indigence. To teach the poor man how to live cheaper and better, is the defign of the following pages. 1 "hough the common people of this country live at a greater $xpenfe than any where elfe, it dees act follow that they live bev 414 GENERAL OBSEi\?~A~j. IQNv Giv /ii,ii«xji»i. ter.- They are ftrong indeed, but by no means heakny ; and it is . found f>at, from an attachment to a particular mode of living, they are more liable to difeafe and death in foreign climes, than the in- haoitants of any other country. 'w It is certainly proper that a poor man fhould be inftrufted in , every thing that can make his littie earnings go as far as poflible, \ or which can add to the comfort of himfelf and family. Nor can ceconomy in living, be deemed trivial, in a country where the rich- es depend on the cheapnefs of lab ->ur. it is alledged that the Englifh are fo much attached to their own modr.s of living, that no argument will induce them to make \ .the fmalleft change. Habits are indeed obftinate things, efpecial- ly thofe wrrch relate to diet; but there are proofs that the Erjglifh \ are not inflexible even in this matter. The mode of living among the lower orders has beeu greatly changed in my time, and I am , forry to fay, not f«~r the better. The people cf England have too much good fenfe not to liften to reafon provided due care were taken to inftruct them. But here the people rr.?y be truly faid " to penftjfor.want of knowledge." No means have been ufed to give them proper inftruction. Hurtful ■ cuftoms have been fuffered to prevail, till they have ftruck fuch deep roots that it will not be an eafy matter to eradicate them. The difficulty, however, is not infurmountable. A few experi- •' ments of reform would have the effect to render it as agreeable as it is falutary. - '" ,• Adults have many old prejudices to overcome, but the cafe is different in regard to children. They may be taught to ufe any I kind of food, and what they ufe when young (hey will love when d| old. If I can introduce a different method of feeding ch'ldren, my purpofe will be anfwered. This alone will, in time, effect a total change in the general mode of living. { The la* t diftrefs cf the poor has called fcrth many publications * intended for their relief. Moft of them, however, were adapted only for the particular occanon, and not calculated to prevent the return of like evils. The following obfervations, it is hoped, will have a m re permanent effect. Trey are intended to recommend '\ a plan of living, which will render the people lefs dependent on bread and animal food for their iubfifter.ee, and consequently not fo liable to fuffer from a f Scarcity cr dearth of either of thefe articles in future. Particular attention has been paid to the fubftitutes for bread, as the fcarcity of this article proves peculiarly diftreffmg to the poor, it will appear from the following pages, that bread is by no means fo much a neceffary of life as generally imagined, and that its place may, in many L ftances, be fupplied by a variety of other farinaceous fubftances. GENERAL OBSERVATIONS ON ALIMENT. NO creature eats fuch a variety of food as man. Intended for an inhabitant of every climate, he devours the productions of them all; and if they do not fuite his palate, or agree with his fto- GENERAL OBSERVATIONS ON ALIMENT. 415' mach, he calls in the aid of cookery, an art peculiar to himfelf; by which many tnings that, in a crude ftar.', woul! prcve hurtful, or even poifonous, are rendered wholefome and falutary. The odious divifion offx>d is into animal and vegetable. ^ To fay ti'.af'm^r. w:s intended by nature for uf ng either the < ne or t^e ether al me, would be abiiird. His ftructure and appetite prove that he was form d for both. Judgment, however, is requi- fite in a 'jutting the due prep;rtions of each, fo as to avoid the in- conveniences, arifing from an extreme on either hand. 1 hong animal fo >d is more' nourifhing than vgetable, it is not fafe to live nn that alone. Experience has fhewn lhat a diet confifting folely of animal food, co;cites thirft, and naufea, occa* fions putrefcence in the ftomach and bowels, and finally brings on violent griping pains with cholera and dyfentery. Animal food is lefs adapted to the fedentary than the labori- ous, and leaft of all to the ftudi us, wh fe diet, ought to confift chiefly of vegetables. Indulging in animal focd rend, rs men dull, and unfit for the purfuits of fcience, efpecially when it is accompa- nied with the free ufe f ftrong liquors. The plethoric, or perfons of a full habit, fhould eat fparingly of animal food. It yi<Ids far m re blood than vegetables taken in the fame quantity, and of courfe m \y induce i. flammatt ry difor- ders. It acts as a ftimulus to the w .ole fyftem, Dy which means the circulation of the blood is greatly accelerated. I am inclined to toink, that conlumptions, fo common in Eng- land, are In part owing to the great ufe of animal food. Though the Phthifis Pulmonalis properly fpeaking, is not on inflammatory difeafe, yet :t generally begins with fymptoms of inflammation, aDd is often accompanied with them through its '& hole progrefs. But me difeafe moft common to this country is tee fcurvy. One finds a clafh of it in almoft every family, and in fome the . taint is very deep. A difeafe , fo general muft have a general caufe, and there is none fo obvious as tne great quantity of animal food devoured by the natives. As a proof that fcurvy arifes from this caufe1, we are in poffeffr. n of no remedy for that difeafe equal to the free ufe cf fiefh vegetable?. By the uninterrupted ufe rf animal f )od a putrid diathefis is induced in the fyft^a, which predifpofes to a variety cf diforders. I am fully convinced that many of thofe obftinate con:pl ints for which we are at a lofs to account, ard find it ftill more difficult to cure, are the effe&s of a fcorbutic taint lurking in the habit. Improp' r ^iet affects the mind as well as the bo ;y. I he chol- eric difpT.fi ion of the Eaglifh is almoft proverhi.d; Were I to af- fign a < aufe, it would be, their living fo much on animal food. There is n© doubt hut ih*s indue s*a ferocity of ten pf r unknown to men whofe food is chiefly taken from the vegetaf le kiogdom. Though thefe aod fimilar confequences may arife from t^e ex- cefs of animal diet, we are far from difcouraging its ufe in moder- aticn. In all cold countries it is certainly neceffary ; but the ma- jor part of the aliment ought neverthelefs to confift of vegetable fubftances* There is a continual tendency in animal food, as well 4i<5 OF BREAD. as in the human body itfelf, to putrefaction, which can only be' counteracted by the free ufe of vegetables. With regard to th proportion of vegetable food to that of ^animal, great nicety is by no means required. It muft yary accor- . fcing to circumftances, as the heat of the weather, the*warmth cf . '• the climate, and the like. . The vegetable part, however, where nothing forbids, cught certainly to preponderate, and I think in the proportion of at leaft two to one. The ex effive confumption of animal food is one great caufe of the fcarcity of grain. The food that a bullock affords bears but a fmall proportion to the quantity of vegetable matter he con- fumes. v I am no enemy to good fruit, as an article of diet; but the* greater part of what is ufed in this country, by the lower orders of the people, is mere trafh. Fruit fhonld be eateu in the early part of the day, when the ftomach is not loaded with food, andrit never ought to be eaten raw till it be thoroughly ripe. OF BREAD. BREAD, or fomething refembling it, makes a part of the diet of all nations. Hence.it is emphatically denominated theftaf %>f life. It may, however, be ufed too freely. The late Dr. Foth- ergiil was of opinion, and I perfectly agree with him, that moft people eat more bread than is conducive to their health. I de not mean to infinuate that bread is unwholefome, but that the beft things may prove hurtful \phen taken to excefs. A furfeit of bread is if ^re dangerous than of any other food. Ormtis repletio mala re- pleizo panis peffima. The French confume vaft quantities of bread ; but its bad effects are prevented by their copious ufe of foups and fruits, vhich have little or no fhare in the diet of the*common peo* pie of England. One important ufe of bread is to form a mafs fit for filling up the alimentary canal, and carrying the nutritious juices along that paffage in fuch a ftate as to render them fit to be afted upon by the lacteal'abforbents, which take up the nourifhment and convey it to the blood. In this light bread may be confidered as a foil from whence the nourifhment is drawn. I do not fay that bread contains no nourifhment, but that its ufe, as an article of diet, does not folely depend on the quantity of nutriment it contains, but in fome meafure, on its fitnefs as a vehicle for conveying the nutritious particles through the inteftinal tubes. Hence it follows, that the fineft bread is not always the belt adapted for anfwering the pur- pofes of nutrition. •■ The richeft i\r* will n^t noufefh an animal, unlefs the ali- mentary canal is fufoe'eotly diftended. A dog has been fed on the richeft brcn, yet conk' not be kept alive; while another, which had only th.-: meot boiled to chip and water, throve very well. This fhews tl e f lly of attempting to uourifh men on ali- mentary powders and other concentrated food. The great art therefore of preparing food, is to blend the nu- tritive part of the. aliment with a fufficient quantity of, fome ligh: 4i? farinaceous fubftance, in order to fiil up the canal, without over- charging it with more nutritious particles than are neceffary for the fupport of the animal. This may be dons either by bread, or other fari iaceous fubftances, of which there is a great variety, as ' will appear from the fequel. Bread is one of the moft expenfive modes of tiling grain, and not adapted to the narrow circumftances of the lower or fers cf the people, as it is burthened with too heavy add"tional charges, in pafling though the han 1 s of both the miller and the baker. Be- fides, the former often grinds down extraneous matter with the wheat, and the latter as frequently bakes it up *./:th tne addition of lime, chalk, alium, and other pernicious fubftances. Since the articles of diet hive become branches of manufacture, the public neither know what they eat nor what they drink. People imagine, as the fineft flour .contains the greateft quan- titv of nourifho'ent, that it muft therefore be the moft proper for making into bread ; but this by no means follows. The fineft flour comes the neareft to ftarch, w ich though it may occafionally prove a good medicine, makes bad bread. Houfehold bread, which is made tty grinding down the whole gram, and only feparating the coarfer bran, is without doubt the moft wholefome. The- belt houfehold bread I ever remember to have eat,was in the county of York. It was what they call meflin bread, and ccn- fifted of wheat and rye ground together. I am not quite certain as to the proportion ; but I think there might be two parts of the former to one of the latter. This bread, when well ferment- ed, eats light, is of a pleafant tafte, and foluble to the bowels. After ufing it for fome years, I found that bread made entirely of flour was neither fo agreeable to the palate, nor fo conducive to health. Bread is often fpoiled to pleafe the eye. The artificially whitened, drying, fluffing bread, though made of the heir; of ! he- wheat, is in reality the worft of any ; yet this is the bread which moft people prefer, and the poorer fort will eat no other. , AH the different kinds cf grain are occafionally made into i bread, fome giving preference to one and fome to another, aroord* J ing to early cuftom and prejudice. The people of South Britain generally prefer bread made of the fineft wheat flour, while thofe of the northern counties eat a mixture of flour and oanneaS, or • \rvemeal, and many give the preference to bread made cf oarmeal alone. The common people of Scotland aho eat a mixed bread, but more frequentiy bread of oatmeal only* ' In Germany the common bread is made of rye, and the American labourer thinks no bread fo ftrengthening as that which is made of Indian corn ; nor do I much doubt but the Lapland.r thinks his bread, made of the bones of fifties, is the beft of any. Bread made of different kinds^ cf grain is more wholefome - than what is made of one only, as their qualities ierve to cormfe one another. For example, wheat flour, efpecially the finer kind, being of aftarchy nature, is apt to occafion. conJtipation. Bread made of ryemeafe on th*? otljer fc?.i}d. proves, of tea too Oippery , 41-8 Ox* ±>i\mtu. for the bowels. A due proportion cf thefe makes the beft bread. For tne more active and laborious I would recommend a mix* ture of rye with the ftronger grains, as peas, beans, barley, o.ts, Indian corn, and the like. Taefe may be blended in many & different ways ; they make a hearty bread for a labouring man, and to ufe his own language, they h° longer, on his ftomach than \ bread made of wheat flour only. Barley bread pafles too quickly \ through the alimentary canal to afford time for conveving the proper nourifhment; but bread made of barley mixed with peas is verv nourifhing. When potatoes, or boiled grain, are ufed, bread ceafes to be aneoe.ffary article of diet. During the late fcarcity of brtad, I made it a rule not to eat above one half t.e quantity i ufed to do, and I found no inconvenience whatever from the cha ege. Nay,; fame told m?, that for a confiderable t'rae they had left off" the ufe of brea&altogether, without experiencing any change in the ftate of their heal h. A great port cf fhe bread confumed in this cou try is by" children, it is. always ready, and when .childr n call f r food, a pi ceof bread is put into their han^s, to fave t e trouble of dr fling a. y ether kind of victuals. Of many children this is the principal food, but it is fr from being the meft proper. Children are often troubled wkh acidi i s of toe ft mach and bowels.; and it is wellkn wn that bread mixed with water, and kept in a decree of he tt equal to that of the hum in ftomach, foon turns four. • . During the late fcarcity, many of the labouring men, and even artificer;-, could not earn as much money as v».s fufficient to keep their families in the article of bread only, it is cer- tain, " however, that on a different plan, fuch families might . have lived very comfortably. Many of fhe articles of diet are cheaper than bread, and equally whtlefome. Above one half of xoe expenfe of living might be faved by a- due felection cf th? articles'of diet. The Englifh labourer lives chiefly on bread, which being accompanied win other dry, and often fait food, fires his blood, and excites an unquenchable thirft, fo that his perpetual cry is for driok. ISut the greateft confumption of bi\ ad is occafioned by tea. It I is faid thai the fubjects of Great Britain enfume a greater quanti- *; ty of that hero, than the whole inhabitants of all the other nations of this quartf r of the globe. The poor ft wonun in England muft have her tea, an \ the children generally fhare it win her. As tea contains no nourilhm nt, either for y ung < r Id, there muft of courfe be breed aod V.tt-r to eatal ng with it. The quartern ie/af will net go far anv.ng a family of hungry children, a d if we add tr.ecoit f t a, fugar, butter, and milk, th; exprnfe of one meal will be moreth n would be fufficient to fill their bellies with whck-lbme food three times a day. There is reafon to believe that one half the bread confumed in England is ufed to tea, without one hearty meal ever being made OF BOILED GRAIN. 4*9 of it. The higher ranks ufe tea as a luxury, while the lower or- ders make a diet of it. I had lately occafion to fee a ftriking in- ftance of this in a family that was r prefeoted to me as if! diltrefs for want of bread. I fent them a little monev, and was inf rmed that they ran away with it directly to the 'ea-fhep. To a heavy, flugg.fh, phlegmatic man, a moderate ufe of tea may not prove pernicious; but where there h a debilitated ftomach and an irritability of fibre, it never fails to do much hm t. With many it has the effect to prevent fleep. Tea will induce a total change of conftitution in the people of this country. Indeed, it has gone ?. great way t w?rds effect- ing that evil already. A debility aod confequ oot irritability of fibre, are become fo common, teat not only women but even men, are affected with them. T. .at clafs of difeafes which, for want of - a better name, we call nervous, has madr- aim it a complece n- queft of the one fex, and is making hafty ftrides towards vanquifh- ing the other. Did women koow the train of difeafes induced by debility, and how difhgreeable thefe difeafes render ihem to the other fex, they would fhun tea as the moft deadly poifon. ^ No man can love a woman eaten up with vapours, or wafhed down with difeafes arifing from relaxation. It is not tea taken as a beverage after a full meal, cr, in a crowded affembly, that I fo much condemn, though I think fome- thing as elegant and lefs pernicious might be fubftituted in its place. The mifchief occafioned by tea arifes chiefly from its being fubfti- tuted for folid food. This is fo much the cafe at prefent, that, had I time to fpare, I think it could not be better employed than in writing againft this deftruct've drUg. OF BOILED GRAIN. THOUGH farinaceous fui.fiances, of one kind or another, make a neceffary part cf the food of man, yet there can be no reafon why fuch fubftances fhould always affum.- the name and form of bread. M.my of them are more wholefome, and not H.s agreeable in other f .roes. Bread is effenufed merely to fave the trouble of cookery ; and being portable, is the moft convenient article of diet for carrying abroad. It does not, however, admit of a doubt that more grab* is eaten b iled, though not in this com.try, than is made ioeo bread ; and that this mode of cookery is the moft wholefome. Simple boiling precludes all adult-ration, and is an operation iruch lefs laborious and artificial than baking. The moft general article of diet amorg mankind,:» rice. Tin* may he made into a vori. tv of dlfhes ; bi-t fimple boiling is all that is required, to render i<" a proper fubftituie f-.r bread. It may either be eaten alone or with milk. In the Eaft it is ufed with meat, in the fame manner as we do bread. The people of this cowntnTbelleve that rice proves ..injurious to the eyes, but this feems to be without foundation; ir has no fuch effect en thofe, who make it the principal part oi* their food, 42o OF BOILED GRAIN. Many other kinds.of grain will, when boiled, make good fub- ftitutes for bread. Even thofe. which make a harfh and unpleafant fort of bread, are often rendered very palatable by boiling. This is the cafe with all the leguminous clafs of plants, as pea?,- beans, &c. Even oats and barley are more agreeable, as well as more wholefome, when boiled, than made into bread. All allow that peas and beans boiled, when young, are a great luxury. But when old, they are equally wholefome, and, when properly cooked, bv no means unpleafant. There are few who do not relifh peas-pudding, and even prefer it to bread. Beans are not fo fit for this purpofe; but they moke an excellent ingredient in the poor man's broth, and whoever eats this broth, will lied lit- tle occafion for bread. Peas and beans contain a" equal quantity of fugar with wheat, oats, -r barley, and at the fame tune a greater proportion ef oil, conf quently are more nourifhing. This fact is confir ned*by daily experience. On thr fe ferns where poas and bea^s are r ufed in great abun- dance, the labourer»are much fed on that fort of gra-n ; but when removed t forms where t ^ev are fed v/ith other kinds of grain, ""> they foon ooaipl an of a di -inution of ftrength. and requeft a fup- ply of peafe meol as for erly. Nature feems to have pointed out the propriety of the exten- sive ufe of peafe and beans, it being a tact, that when crops of that "kind arr* duly alternated who crops of wheat, barley, or oats, the fertility of the f il may be maintained wirhout ret or manure, for many years i gether. whereas, if the latter be raifed on the fame foil for feveral years fucceffively, they render ic barren, fo that, without reft or- manure, its fertility cannot be preferved. The people in England are but little accuftomed to the ufe of boiled groin, though in many countries it is eaten as a luxury. B tied barley is a great favourite with the Dutch, and is eaten with milk, butter, or mlaffes. 1t is the principal food of the Dutch failors, who in general, are both healthy and robuft. parley is one of the beft ingredients in foup. Count Rumf rd fays it poffeffes the quality cf lithing, or thickening foups, in a fuperior degree to any ether grain. We have reafon, however, to believe, that grits, or coarfe oatmeal, will anfwer that purpcfe ftill better. Oatmeal is frequently made into bread; but it is a much mere wholefome, as well as agreeable food, when made into hafty pud- ding, and eaten with milk. The peafants in many parts of Bri- tain make two meals a day of it, while their children almoft whol- ly fubfift on it; and it is well known that both old and young who are thus fed, are healthy and robuft. The opinion of oatmeal being heating, and occafioning fkin difeafes, is wholy without foundation. Bread made of oatmeal, wh n vt. leavened, will fometimes occafion the heartburn • butthfe is no pro: f of its heating quality. Unleavened bread' of wheat or soy other grain, produces the fame effect on a debill itafed ftomach. Oatmeal thoroughly boiled feldom gives theheart- burn. OF BOILED GRAIN. • 421 Perfons who are fed on oatmeal tread, or h?fty pudding, are not more fubject to difeafes of t;e fkin than th fe who live on wheatmeal. Cutan ous diforders proceed more from the want of cleanlinefs,than fr'many particular lLient. .The French, fo far from thinking that oatroeal is veatng, fr> ak of itas p^ffeffed ofa cooling qualry; mdeven the Englifh give <■ atmeal. or grit gruel, to lying-in women, and,fick people of ev^ry befcription, which fhews th*t th y are inconfiftent wifh themfelves, in alledgicg that the blood is fired by the ufe of oatmeal. Alieut rant of the army, refiding at a coun ry villagewithin a few miles ofEdnburgh, with a wife and ten coilore , having nccther income than his half pay, fed the whole of his children with hafty puddmg and butter-milk only, from a conviction that it w«s the mot wholefome and full diet that fell within the reach of his nar- row circumftances. They grew rpnce, and it *was the univerfal remark of the neighbourhood, that they were as fprigatly, healthy, and robuft as otoer children, and at the fame time perfectly free from all fkin difeaies. Children are feldom well, unlefs when their bodies are gently open. But this is more likely to be the cafe when fed on oatmeal and rrolk, than when their bellies are crammed with a ftarchy fubftance made of the fineft flour; yet this in Engbnd is the common food of children. I have feen an infant fluffed four or five times a-day wi:h this kind of food. There needs no conjuror to tell the confequences. A late Author, a man of learning, but the dupe of prejudice, has by a ridiculous definition, endeavoured to reprefent oats as proper food for horfes only. I wifh the horfes in England de- voured a fmaller quantity of that grain, and the people more. Few things would have a greater tendency to leffen the expenfe of living. The oats in N^rth Britain are of a fuperior quality, and I hop° the people will long have the fenfe to ufe them as an article of diet. Indian corn is Iikewife faid to make the beft food when boif- ed. Count Rumford obferves, that rf all t' ings it makes the beft pudding, and that he has made a >rearty meal of it, fauce included, for five farthings. What makes good puddings will make gocd dumplings, and thefe will, at any time, fupply the place of bread. The C*nmt alfo remarks, that the co-gr^fs in America prefer Indian corn to rice ; and that t e Bavarian peafants prefer it to wheat; that it might be imported from Norte America at abour four or five fhill*egs/vr bufhel ; thatwhe- made into flour, it would coft only one penoy farthing per pound ; and that it is highly nutritious, and th? cheapeft food known. During the hie fcarcity a brg - quantby of t i ■ grain was imported ; but fuch is the averfion cf the common people of this country to every fort of food to which they are n t accuft med, that they refuted to purchafe it, and the inero,"ant<? wrere very great lfers by the importation. On the fame principle the Germans, till within a few years, would not be induced to eat potatoes, though now they are become- e*< tsremely fond of tnem. 422 OF BUTTER. The American, the Italian, and t^e German, all cook Indian corn the fame way as the North Briton does his oatmeal, by making it into hafty pudding. It may be eaten in a variety of ways. Some eat it with a fauce compofed of butter and brown fngar*, or butter and m laffes. O.hers eat it with milkonly. In either way it makes a good, cheap and wholefome diet, by no means difagreeable to thofe who are accuftomed to it The only other grain we fhall mention as beft when boiled, is buck-wheat: It is cf a very mucilaginous nature, and of courfe highly nutritious. In feveral parts of Europe, k conftitutes a prin- cipal part of the food of the lower poople. In former times it was eaten in Ruflia, not by the lower claffes only, even the nobili made ofe of it. Boded and then buttered,it wasfo great a favorite of the great Czar Peter, that he is faid to ha^ feldom flipped on any thing elfe. OF BU1TER. IT has been faid that the Englifh have a thoufand religions, and but one fance. It muft be allowed that they ufe butter with al- moft every kind of food. B itter, though a good article of diet may be ufed too tn=°ly, and in tMs country, I am convinced that is the cafe. To weak ftomachs it is hurtful, eyen in fmall quantities^ and when ufed freely, it proves preju 'cal to the ftrongeft. Butter, like other things of an oily nature, has a conftant . tendency to turn rancid. This procefs, by the heat of the ftom-» \ ach, is greatly accelerated, infbmuch that many people, foon after \ eating butter, complain of its rifing in their ftomachs, in a ftate ) highly difagreeable. Oils of eveiy kind are with difficulty mixed with watery fluids.. - This is the reafon why butter floats on the ftomach, and rifes in fuch an unpleafant manner. Perfons afflicted with bile fhould ufe butter very fparingly. Some fceptical authors doubt wheihtr or not aliment of any kind has an effect on f e b'le. One thing, however, is certain, that [ many patients afflicted with complaints which were fuppofed to be occafioned by bile, have been completely cured by a total ab- ftmence from butter. The moft violent bilious complaints that I ever met with, were evidently occafioned by food that became rancid on the ftomach, as the cholera morbus and ne like. Nor can fuch com- plaints be cured, till the rancid matter is totally evacuated by vomit'ng and purging. But tuppofing butter did not poffefs the quality cf heroming rancid on the ftomach, it may nevorthelefs, prove hurtful to digeftion. Ols of all kinds are cf a relaxing quality, and tend to impede the action of digeftion. Hence the cuftom of giving rich broths and fat meats to perfons v/ho have a voracious appetite. The free ufe of butter, and other oily fubftances, not only rends to relax the ftomach, and impede its action, but to induce a debility cf the folids, which paves the way to many maladies. In a country where two thirds of the inhabitants lead fedentary lives OF BUTTER. 4*2? a debility of fibre muft predominate. Whatever encreafes that debility Ought to be aw ided. C iidren, wi*h ut ex-"prio-, -redifpofed to difeafes arifing from refex_ti -n. Bu'ter c.f courfe, ought to be given to them w'rh a fp'ring w nd. But is this th.-: c.f ? By no means. Bread and butter ronp ltu»e a great part of the food of children, and I am cotiv.noed that th -grofs humours with which they are frequently troubled, are partly wi g to thisfoOd. As children abound wi moifture, - read alone is, g-nerally fpeaking, better for them than bread and butter. I ave been aft 'nifhed t fee the quantities of butter eaten by grofs women wo lead fedentary liv-s. The r tea bread is general- ly contrived fo ?.s to fuck up butter lik^ a fponge. Wh t quantities of crumpets ard muffins trey will dev air in a m ru:ng, foaked with (his oil; aod a1 er-wards complain r.f indigeftion, when they have eat^n what wr.ul t o\ erloae the ftomach cf a ploughman. Dr. Fothergill is of opi u- »n, that butter produces the nervous or fick head-ach, fo c mo on among the w men of this couetry. As a proof of this, it is of en cured by an emetio. Oils, in certain quantiti?s, excite naufea, and even vomiting. They muft of courfe prove unfriendly to digeftion. A Dutch failor, we are told, can digeft train oil. So may an Enghfh tailor; but it would be very improper food for a London lady. To fome of the leaner farinaceous fubftances, as the potato© and the like, butter makes a very proper addition; but eating it to flefh and fifh of almoft every defeription, is certainly wrong. The flefh eaten in tr is cou^ry is gererally fat enough without the ad- dition of butter, and th. more oily kinds of fifh, as faimon or her- J rings, are lig'ter on the ftomach, and more eafily dig.ited when 1 eaten without if. Butter is rother a grofs food, and fitter for the athletic and la- borious, tho.n the fedentary and delicate. It i3 fefs ! urtful when eaten frefh thao falted. Salt but er cer tally tends to induce ikin d.feafes, and f am inclinet t thi ok, the free uf- of it at feamay have fonu fhare in bringing otht dreadful malady fo deftructive to our brave failors, the fea fcurvy. There is a method f rendering felt butter lefs hurtful,, but it . feems not to be kn nvn in England. What I mean is to mix it with an equal quantity of honey, a'd keep it for ufe. In this way it may be given to children with greater freedom. In N r h Britain this method of mixing butter with honey is well kn wn, and from a common prover -. I take uV cuftom to be very ancient. But er, in itfelf, is not fo hu'tful, as when combined with certain o her things For example: bread made wi.h butter is almoft i odh-fti le, and paftries of every kind are little better : yet many people almoft livj upon paltry, and it is univ..rfally given to children. It is little better, h-wever, than poif n, and never fails to diforder their ftomachs. rhe food m % her cannot pafs a paftry fhop, without treating h r 'arl'mg boy with fome of the dainties, and then wonders how he got the cuigh, cr.--h Jic. I have known a man feemingly in perfect health, who by eat^ 424 OF FRUITS, AND R001S. ing a penny-worth of paftry, as he paffed along ftreet, was feized j with fuch an afthmatic fit, that he was obliged to be carried home, and had nearly loft his life. This occurred whenever heinadvert- j ently ate any thing baked with butter. Every thing that proves very injurious to health ought, as , far as poffible, to be prohibited, by laying a high duty upon it. A . duty on paftry would be ferving the public in more refpects than one. It would fave many livis, ani leffen fome tax on neceffaries. Cheefe, as a diet, is Iikewife injurious to health. P fhould never be eaten but as a deffert. It occafions conftipation, fires the blood, and excites a conftant craving for drink. It is. very improp- er for the fedentary, and hardly to be digefted even by the athletic. If men will live on dry bread, poor cheefe, fait butter, broiled bacon, and fuch like parching food, they will find their way to the . ale-houfe, the bane of the lower orders, and the fource of half the . beggary in the nation. OF FRUITS, AND ROOTS. FRUITS and roots form a large clafs of fubftitutes for bread. The latter, being produced under ground, are lefs liable to fuffer from the inclemency of the feafons than graim Men who y wifh to inflame the minds of the multitude may inveigh againft the fubftiiut s for bread ; but reafon and found fenfe fay, the more fubftitutes for bread the better. When one fails, recourfe can be had to another. • ' In warm climates the inhabitants have many fubftitutes for i ■bread, and as their feafons are more uniform than ours, they can . generally depend on the plant, or whatever it is, proving product-1 lve. The plantain-tree, commonly called the Indian-fig, which has from time immemorial been cultivated in South-America, bears fruit of a fweetifh tafte, which will diffolve in the mouth without chewing. It is eaten either raw, fried, or roafted. When intended to fupply the place of bread, it is gathered be- i fore it is ripe, and eaten either boiled or roafted. The banana is nearly of the fame nature, but its fruit is greatly fuperior both in afte and flavour. t The inhabitants of the South Sea, or Ladrone iflands, are fupplied with bread from a tree which has been lately imported into our Weft India iflands, and will it is hoped, be found to an- fwer the fame purpofe there. It has a flight degree of fweetnefs, but not much flavour. It refembles new bread, and requires to be roafted before it is eaten. Thofe who have tafted it fay, that it is in no refpect fuperior to the potatoe. In lbme of the Weft India iflands the inhabitants fupply the place of grain by making bread from the root of a fhrub called the caflada or caflava. Though, to my tafte, this bread is very infipid, yet the natives are fond of it to fuch a degree that I have known fome of them eat it, during their refidence in England, in preference to the fineft London bread. But the moft general fubftitutes for bread in the Weft-Indies ^re the yams. There are three different fpecies of this plan*: the. OF FRUITS AND ROOTS. 4*| roots of which are promifcuoufly ufed for bread. They are faid to be very nutritious, ofeafydigcfticn, and, when properly dreffed, are by fome preferred to the beft of wheaten bread. The tafte is fomewhat like the potatoe, but more lufcious. The negroes gen- erally eat them boiled, and beaten into a mafh. The white people- have them ground into flour, and make bread and puddings of them. They can be preferved for feveral feafons, without lofing any of their primitive goodnefs. Of all the fubftitutes for bread in Europe, the potatoe is the moft extenfively ufeful. This plant is a native of Peru, and has been in Europe abA ut two hundred years. Like moft other im- portant clifcoveries, it made but a flow progrefs, and is ftill far from being fo generally cultivated as it deferves. to be. It is in- deed known in moft parts of Europe, but its culture is beft undef- ftcod in Ireland and the northern parts of England. A* Harwich, however, the preference is given to the Dutch potatoes, brought over by the packets between that place and Helvoet Sluys. There is a light fandy foil in Holland very favourable to the culture of that ineftimable root. As this plant thrives in every foil, and feldom fuffers frcm the inclemency of the feafons, we muft blame ourfelves if we fuffer a famine to exift. Indeed no fuch thing can ever be, where due at- tention is paid to the culture of putatoes. A far greater quantity of farinaceous food can be raifed on an acre of ground planted with potatoes, than fown with any kit d of grain. It is not un- common to have a return of forty for one. They art not fo hearty a food as corn, but no man will ever perifh for hunger who can have potatoes. Potatoes abound with an infipid juice, -which indures fome to think that they are not very nutritious. Facts, however, are agrinft this opinion. Some of the ftoiiteft men we know, are brought up on milk and potatoes. Dr. Pearfon* who has beftow* ed fome pains in analiziog this root, foys, that potatoes and water alone, with common fait, can ncurifh men completely. They differ in colour and confiftence, but not materially with regard to their nutritive qualities. Some think toe firm kind are the moft nutritious; but the Irifli, who muft be good judges, give the pref- rence to the mealy-. The difference, however, depends much on the mode of Cooking them. More than half the fubftance of potatoes confifts of water, and experience fhews, tat the mode cf cooking, which moft di- minifhes their moifter, is to be preferred. In London th?y are drenched in water and wafhed before they are brougot to market, which accounts, in a great meafure, Tor the bad quality of the London potatoes. = They are*Jreffedin a varicy of ways, but fimple boiling cr roafling feems to be all the cooking they require, to render them a proper fubftitute for bread. Some are fond of making bread of them. This, in my opinion, ho marring both. Why man- ufacture anv thing into bread, which requires only the aid (28) 426 OF FRUITS AND ROOTS. of the fire to make it fuch ? Nobody thinks of making dough of me bread fruit; but the potatoe bright with as great pro- priety be called the bread root, as it is made into bread by the fame procefs. Stewed mutton and potatoes make not only a nouriftv.ng bat a very palatable difh. The excefs of fat of the mutton, which when otherwife cooked fuftains great lofs is thus preferved, by b ing abibrb-ed by the potatoes. It is, however, to be obferved, that when potatoes-are ufed in broth or.ftews, they ought previoufly to be boiled, and the water thown away, as it contains fomething deleterious. Simple boiling or roafting is fufficient to prepare po- tatoes to fupply the place Of bread, but when they are intended to verve as a meal, vhey require fomething of a foftening nature, as milk, butter, or both. What a treafure is a milch cow and a potatoe garden, to a poor man with a large family, who lives in tjie country ! Yet, with a little attention from landlords and farmers, almoft every man might be fo accommodated. What a fcurce of real wealth and population ! Men would multiply, and poverty, unlefs among the profligate, be unknowB. Horfes are fometimes fed with potatoes, and become very fond of them. Wit" the ad- dition of a fmall quantity of hay, they are found to be fufficiently nouruhiog. I w >uld beg leave to recommend, both to landlords and farm- ers, a careful perufal of Earl Winchelfea's excellent letter to Sir John Sinclair, on the advantages of cottagers renting lands. This humane Nobleman takes up the matter in a truely patriotic light, and fhews that farmers, inftead of leffehing the number cf ppor, do every thing they can to multiply them ; and I am lorry to fay, that, fo far as my obfervation goes, it agrees entirely with his lordfhip's. Some think that the potatoe, unlefs it is made into bread, will not keep. An accident taught me the contrary. Many years ago a friead of mine fent me a potatoe, after it had bsen roafted- in an oven, c.n account of its fingular figure. I laid it on a fhelf among fome other things of the like kind, and was furprifed, on removing them many years after, to find the potatoe quite frefh though as drv as a bone. On grating it down it was perfectly fweet ; and as fit for making foup as the day it was roafted. I ap- prehend that nothing made into bread would have kept fo long. Poberity will nardly believe that a fcarcity of bread could be felt in Britam, at a time when it was known that a fufficient quantity of farinaceous food could be raifed in one county for the inhabitants of the whole ifland. Let proper encouragement be given to the culture of potatoes, and let -famine at defiance. Mony other domeftic roots, fprouts, Sec. are very wholefome, and may ocrafionally fupply the place of bread. Of thefe Mr. Bryan of Norwich, reckons above forty; but we fhall only take notice, by way of fpecimeu, of the moft ufeful and pro- ductive. ' It is worthy of remark, that no nation can be very populous, which does not draw a great part of its food from un- der ground. OF tJZUITS AND KOOTST^ 427 The Jerufalera artichoke is a native of Brazil; but, having been long cultivated in this country, it is too well known to need any defcripdon. Frcmjts tafte, which is like that of artichoke * bottoms, it Would feem to be nutritious?, and is far from being unpleafant to the palate. Some reckon it windy, but this may be corrected in the cocking, by warm fpices, and as the plant, is very productive, we would racommend it to be, ufed in the feme manner as potatoes and the other farinaceous roots. . ■ Of the efculee.t roots in this country, the parfnip is reckoned the moft nourifhing. It is Iikewife of eafy; digeftion, and is agreea- ble to moft palates. Some indeed difllke it on account of itsfweet- nefs; but that is a proof of its nutritive quality, fugarbeing tie moft nourifhing thing in nature. We are told th.-t, in the north of Ire- . land, the poor people make beer from this root. Tnere is not any plant that affords a more ftriking proof of the benefits of culture than the turnip. In.its wild ftate it is good for little or nothing; but, when properly cultivated, it not only affords wholefoxme nourifhment for man, but furnifhes the principal winter food for cattle. There is a fpecies of this plant which grows in .N-irth Britain, called the yellow turnip, which is fweet and of a fuperior quality to thofe produced in the fouth, particularly about London, which are bitter and ftringy. The yellow turnip is the moft nourifhing, and alfo the moft hardy in fuftaining the winter. Jt is eaten with milk to cure the confumption aud fcurvy. Mar- graaf fays, he could extract no fugar from the turnip, which affords ground to conclude, that it is not fo nutritive as certain other y roots. Not only the root of the turnip, but the tops, when young, make very pleafant greens. • The fprouts, if gathered when very tender, make excellent fallad. The carrot, like the turnip, is good for Utile in its natural ftate, being fmall, tough and ftringy. Manured, ft grows large, fuccul- ent, and of a pleafant flavour. It ought, however, to be eaten young, ctherwife it lies on the ftomach, and is hard of digeftion'. It is an ingredient in feveral foups, and being folid, may in fome meafure fupply the place of bread. Salfafy, fkirrets, and the feveral kinds of beets, are all pleaf- ant and nourifning* They are Iikewife of eafy digeftion, and may be dreffed in a variety of ways, Margraaf has by experiments difcovered, that both ikirres and beets contain a confiderable quantity of fugar. Though the extracting a fecoharine fait 'from thefe plants may be no object while we poffefs the Weft- India iflands, yet it ferves to fhew that they poffefs a quantity of nutritious matter, fufficient to give them a rank among the articles calculated to fupply the place of bread. The onion, we are told, was a great favourite in Egypt four thoufand years ago, and Dr. t^afielqueft fays, it is -not to be wondered at, for whoever has tailed the onions of Egypt muft allow, that none can be better in any part of the globe. There, he fays," they are fweet, though in many countries thev atfe ftrong and naufeous. There they are foft, whereas in eonherq * ai$ OF FRUITS AND ROOTS^ countries they are hard ; aud their coats fo compact, that they are difficult to digeft. This very quality may however recom- mend ' them in countries where food is icarce. The Doctor ob- ferve:, that the Turks eat them roafted with their meat as we do bread, and are lo fond of them that they wifh to be indulged with this difh in paradife. From the Doctor's account one would be induced to believe, that the onion ufed in Egypt was of a different fpecies frcm ours; but I am rather inclined to think it may depend on the mode of culture, as well as on the warmth of climate and the difference of foil, as we find in the fcuthern parts of Europe they are milder than in the more northerly. In Spain they are very mild, and a root weighing two pounds will grow from a fingle feed. Onions are dreffed in a variety of ways, but, in regard to wholefomenefs, there is no method better than fimple boiling. By this method of cooking, they are rendered mild, of eafy digeftion, and go off witout leaving any difagreeable heat in the ftomach • or bowels- Many fhun them on account of the ftrong difagree- ; able fmell th°y communicate to the breath. Mr. Bryant lays, this may be remedied, by eating a few raw parfley leaves im- mediately after, which will effectually overcome the fcent of the onions, and Iikewife caufe them to fit more eafy on the ftomach. The leek is generally reckoned among pot-herbs; but as the root is the part chiefly ufed, the confidcration of it comes under the prefent head of difcuffion. Indeed, it is ar properly a root as the ohion, which grows chiefly above ground. Xhe leek, , as well as the onion, is faid to be a con?ant difh at the tables ' of the Egyptians, who chop them fmall and eat them with their meat. The leek is ufed as a pot-herb in moft parts of Britain, efpecial- ly in Wales, wh;re the narives are faid to be fond of it. In Scot- lrnd a full grown fowl and a fmall piece of fait beef, flewed with a large quantity of leeks, is a very favorite difh. In my opinion the leek'i? not fo generally ufed any where as it deferves to be. Tl ^re is no ingredfent goes into foup ,that is more wholefome, or that gives .': a better flavour, than leeks. They are in many re- fpeft? cnedirinal, and to my tafte, as an ingredient in foups, they are grea'ly foperior to the onion, cr any other pot-herb whatever. It is a fact worthy of obfervation, that the boiling of vege- table fubftances thoroughly,extricates a confiderable quantity of air, and rakes them lefs liable to produce flatulency. I could mention a great many more efculent plants which might occafio^ally fupply the place of bread, but the above fpeci- mei is fufficient to fhew how liberal nature is in fupplying man with food, provided he will take the trouble of cultivating and cook- ing it. Mr. Bryant, in his hiftory of efculent plants, enumerated above four hundred and fifty, each of which affords a wholefome nourifhment, and may occasionally be ufed in place of brt-ad. OF BROTHS AND SOUPS. 4^9 THESE may Iikewife be confidered as fubiiitutes for b^ead. Jf properly made they will ferve both for bread and driok. Though broth is a difh of the greateft antiquity, and may be confidered as extremely delicious, yet it is not a favourite in this country. Here the people are fond of what they call folids; yet thofe very folids they make into broth by fwallowing as mu h drink Tter them as they can get. The only difference is, the foreignpr make's his broth in a pot, and the Englifhman makes his in the ftomach. A very fenfible anonymous writer obferves, that b England a pound of meat makes fimply a pound of food ; whereas m any other country in Europe, tnat quantity of anneal food, when ftew- ed down with vegetables and Scotch barley, will produce an a:f- ple meal f r half a dozen people. Hence he juftly infers that among the v. rety of fchemes which may have been devifed by the humane for relieving the diftreffes of the poor, a better and, more extenfive char ty cannot be devifed than that of inftructing tfiem in a new mode of cookery. . The fame author adds that the refult of his experiments on this fubject had exceeded his moft fanguine expectations, and that eacii day gave hici frefh proofs of the excellency,-.of his plan for teaching the. poor and needy to find themfelves in a wholefome and palatable diet, at the cheapeft rate, in which little or no bread was required. He concludes by afferting that there is fcarce a place in this 'kingdom where twenty perfons may not have a wholefome, hearty, and palatable meal for three fhillings. The writer who has paid moft attention to the improvement of cookery for the benefit of the poor, is Count Rumford. In his economical and philofophical eflays, he has given fuch a variety of f rms for making wholefome, cheap and ncurifhing foups, ftews, and other dijfhes for common ufe, that little more feems neceffary to be faid on the fubject. 1 fhall only obferve that the mode of living on broths, foups, haity-pudding, and fuch like, fo warmly aud juftly recommended by the Count, has bten practiced in the northern pans of this kingdom from time immemorial. There the J>-od ot the common people is hafty-pudding with milk for break- fall and fupper, and broth, with vegetables and meat for dinner. i he poorer tort often make broth without meat; but they all ufe vegetables in great abundance, and fometimes they fupply tie place of meat with butter. As the haftv-puddicg and milk make a complete meal, no bread is neceffary either at fupper or breakfaft ; nor is much required at dinner, as th the broth is made thick with barley, cabbage, and a variety of other yeg» ta- bles or pot-herts. Cabbage is a favourite ingredient in the Scotchman's broth. It is feldom made without this article, which is not eaten fo early as in England. It is there fuffered to grow to maturity, and when that is the cafe there is no plant more product tive. This the Germans know well, and vcake it into sour crout,one of the beft antidotes againft the fcurvy with which we are acquainted. This kind of diet not only faves bread but drink. The la- bourer who lives on hafty-pudding and. foups, feldom has occafion 430 > OF BROTHS AND SOUPS. \ for drink ; while he who is burnt up with dry bread and cheefe, or fait meat broiled, has a continual thirft, and lpends the greater part of his earnings in liquor. This, by acting as a powerful Aim- ' ulous, may make him do more work for fome time, but it generally cuts him off iii the middle of his days. The Englifh labourer, who U works hard and drinks hard, feldom lives long, and is an old man » when he fhould be in his prime. The roafting of meat is a wafteful mode cf cookeiy, which ' ought to be avoided by the poorer fort of poople, as much cf the fubftance, and the moft nutritive parts, are loft by fcorching, and 2y off by evaporation. ^ . . I know it will be faid, that I recommend flops in place of, folid food. They are fuch flops, however, as the greateft heroes \ of antiquity lived upon; and though I have vifited moft parts ' pf the ifland, I know of no better men than thofe who live in the , manner defcribed above, nor are the people any where more 1 healthy, cr longer lived. j Broth is not only a difh of great antiquity, but one that can be J made in a great variety of ways. It receives into its competition, animal and vegetable fubftances of every kind that are ufed in diet, * and it may be feafoned fo as to fuit every palate. Indeed, people early accuftomed to eat broths properly made, are generally fond of tnem for their whole lives. It would be difficult to aflign a reafon why the inhabitants j of South Britain fhould diflike a difh fo much relifhed by other J nations. Cuftom, no doubt, fettles all thefe things ; but how J cuftoms arife is not fo clear a matter. If any alteration iidietj is to be introduced with effect, it muft begin with children. What* 1 ever men are accuftomed to eat when young, they generally pre* ] fer for the reft of their lives. Were the children in South Britain I taught to eat hafty-pudding, with milk, fo^breakfsft and fupper ; * and broth with vegetables and meat boiled in i% for dinner, they would relifh thefe dithes as long as they lived, would find little oc- I cafionfor bread, and ftill lefs for.drink ; and. would thrive belter J than on their prefent food. J What parents love themfelves, they generally give to their oj children, without any regard to its being proper for them or not. I have feen a father who was fond of ftrong beer, make his fon, an ^ infant, guzzle it at every meal; and the mother who delights in ] tea, does not fail to give it to her daughter whenever fhe takes it to \ herfelf. By this conduct, the fon becomes a tippler, and the daugh- ; ter fips tea ia the place of folid food, until fhe is eaten up with va- v: pours and other nervous diforders. c Count Rumford fays, brown foup is the common breakf>ft of the Bavarian peafants, to which they cccafionaily add bread, • This heavers is infinitely preferable in all reflects to that per- nicious wafh, tea, with which the \o\vt*r clafles cf inhabitants of this ifland drench their Stomachs, and ruin their conftitutions. He adds, that a fimple infufion of this drug, drank boiling hot, as the poor generally drink it, is certainly poifon, which, though, it be fometimes flow in its operation, never fails to produce fatal effects, *: *-mmLUsioN. tw even in the ftrongeft conftitution", where the free ufe of it is continu ed for a confiderable length of time. ; The German on his polenta, the American on his mufh, and the North Briton on his hafly pudding, can make a hearty breakfaft for a tenth part of what a tea-breakfaft would coft, while it is infinitely .more wholefome. It has Iikewife the advantage that no bread is neceffary. < I have been often told, when recommending foups to the poor, that they had not time to make them, and that they could n. -t afford fuel on account of its price, as it is dear in great towns. They can however, find fuel twice a day to boil a tea-kettle, and time to make tea which is a more tedious operation, by far, than making a mefs or hafty-pudding. For a great part of the year even the pooreft perfon muft have a lii^lelire; and it would require no more to make a comfortable mefs of foup, which is always beft when made with a fbw fire. The mode of living that I would recommend to the lower orders of the people, with a view tq fave expenfe and improve their health, is to fubftitute occafionally other farinaceous fubftan- ces in tiie place of bread, as potatoe, &c. to give up in a great meafure the ufe of roafted, baked, and boiled meat?, and to fupply their place with broths, foups, ftews, and fiich like, made wi'h a little meat and plenty of vegetables ; to give to chtdren, and to grown people who will eat it, for breakfaft, milk-porridge, or hafty-pudding, with milk, fmall beer, or molaffes.^ This will be found a more wholefome breakfaft than tea, while it is much cheaper and requires no bread.* CONCLUSIONS AFTER a long attention to the cure of difeafes, it i? morti- fying to reflect how mach this arduous province is infefted b\ a race of ignorant and. fhamelefs empirics, who are daily tampering with the public credulity, to the deftructian of numbers cf lives. It may Dfely be affirmed, that a confiderable part ot the annual deaths are occafioned"by the profligate temerity of thefe unprincb pled itnpoftors. There is hardly a news paper that does rot teem § with the audacious falshoods, and pompous pretentions, of this impaling clafs of mercenary, and yet (1 ufe not too harfh an expreflion) tolerated murderers. What man who is converfant * The celebrated Dr. Huffland. in his Art of prolonging Life, fays, a moderate ufe of foups is certainly not hurful ; and it is finjulaf that people fliojild imagine it tends too much to relax the ftomach. Does not altour drink, even though cold, become in a few mirnres a kind of warm foup in the ftomach ; and does not the ftomach retain ihe fame temperature during the whole day ? Be careful only not to ufe it hot* in too «reat quantity at one time, or too watery. It is attended even with great advantages.^ It Supplies the plrfce cf^dnnk, partict and i here ' our iu drynefc'and rigidity in the body ; and therefore tne t»e;t nourjiament lor oiq peop.c, *,.« thofewho are of an arid temperament. It even fupplies the place or medicine. Alter catching cold, in nervous head-aches, cholics, »nd different kinds of cramp in the ftomachr warm foup is of excallent fervice. It may ferve as a proof of the utility, or at leaft-harm- lefl'nefs of foup, when I remark that our forefathers, who certainly had more ftrength than wc have, ufed foup ; and that it is ufed by nifties, who are ftill ftronger than thofe in refined life ; and that all the old people with whom 1 ever w« acquainted were great friends to it. 'Vj J 2 COI. u .u W w X ^aV . with phyfic can perufe without indignation tl*e public advertife- pnents of thefe quacks, in which every one arrogates to himfelf the pofTeffion of fuperlative knowledge, and afcribes to his refpedtive noftrum fuch contradictory and inconfiftent qualities as were never yet united in any one medicine in the world ? To the difgrace, however, of the public credulity, not a few of thefe impoftors attain to a degree of opulence that is feldom acquired even in the fcientific and legitimate profecution of medical practi.e. The artifices which they employ to dAkide the multi- tude are well known to many. Having picked up the name of fome extremely active medicine, the bold and indifcriminate ufe of which muft therefore be proportionably dangerous, they immedi- ately refolve on converting it into a noftrum, and endeavour to difleminateits unrivalled praifes either by advertifements or hand, bills. But being themfelves totally iilirerate, they have, for this purpofe, recourfe to fome other perfon, whom they engage for a Stipulated reward to fabricate the pernicious illufion. A hyper- bolical panegyric on t e wonderful remedy is accordingly vamped lip, and preparations are made for commencing a lucrative trade with the puofic. Should the channel of communica'i-n be the public papers, it is a fettled point, that if daily or frequent advertife- ' ■pients can be fupported for the fpace of fome months, the fame of I the medicine, whatever be its real character, is eftablifhed. The \ bet er to promote th\s purpofe, innumerable authorities in favour Of the noftrum are afferted in general terms; venality is again ex- erted to furniih fpecific teftimonials in its fupport; and if, among the number of unfortunate purchafers, or patients, there exifts any perfon who has not only taken it with impunity, but even witn fome advantage (and what extremely powerful medicine may not fometimes by chance have good effects ?) the fortuitous incident is immediately blazoned with all the oftentation of interefted zeal and affected popularity ; and a reference to uncorrupted teftimony re- founds through every channel of information. 5y a ft range aflbci- ation, truth now is confidently adduced in fupport of falfehood ; and the recovery of one or tw . perfons is rendered the unhaapy means of draining the purfe, undermining th! health, and deftroying the lives of rhoufands. Such, in fact, is the general progrefs of empiricifm. Were the talk not invidi us, and the objects too d*fpicable for any other than Juridical cognizance, woich they merit in a fuperlative degree, the reprefentation here given might be fupported by 'unquestionable authority. It is hoped, however, that enough has been faid to influence the minds of the judicious with refpect to this iniquitous practice, which bee-mes every day more alarming, and threatens the more credulous part of the community with the moft fatal eff cts. This country, through the blefling of Providence, has been exempted from the horrors of famine, and for years the fword j but the infatuation of a numerous body of the people has fub- je#ed it to the ravages of another public calamity, which, though/ generally more flow in its operation than any of the former, is ■teqyally deftruftive io the end. Humanity* fhudders at the horri* 1 ble depredations committed on the human conftitution by this empirical tribe, who fubfift by public delufion, and riot, where they can, in the irreparable ruin of thofe whom they intice into their fnares. What, confumptive vifages, what enfeebled frames, what mutilated bodies, and what palfied limbs, are the miferable monuments of that ignorance and criminal temerity by which they are actuated! In certain difeafes, it is doubtlefs an object of importance to the unfortunate patients, that their cure fhould be conducted with fecrecy, and lik.-wife to many, at the fmalleft poffible expenfe ; but they do noi confider that, while they are economical in this ar- ticle, they are fatally prodigal of health. They grafp with eager- nefs tne pili-box or the phial, which they are affured contains the el xir o*- fp. e 'y and effectual convalescence ; but, alas ! the flatter- ing hope proves of fhort duration. They may tcel perhaps, for a , littie, a fuppreflion of the fymptoms of their difeafe ; but the de- l ftructive embers arc mothered, not extinguifhed ; and while prey- ing upon the vitals, are acquiring a malignity which will again bieak form with redoubled violence. it is n-t, however, in one difeafe only, nor in the lower clafs of the people, that this infatuated credulity operates ; we find it prevail even amongft thofe from whofe fuperior fituations in life more difcernment might be expected ; hut who have neverthelefs become voluntary dupes to the meimeft artifices of empiri ifm. Witnefs the fuccefsful impofture practiced with regard to the in- fpsction of urine ; the v'fionary notion of charms, &c. But it is time that fuch chymerical doctrines fhould be con- , figned to the regions of barbarifm, and flourifh no longer in a foil where almoft every other p >yfical prejudice has been rooted up and explored by the progrefs of fience. To effect this' falutary purpofe, nothing can have a more powerful tendency than the view which has been given, in th preceding pages, of the caufes and. cure of difeafes. By remov.ng the mytterious veil which for a long time concealed this ufeful branch of knowledge from the eyes of the public, it ought, on one hand, to preclude forever all refources to empirical iuipoltors, and on the ctht-r, to fhow in what cafes it will be proper to call in fhe afiiftance of a phyfician. Within the bounds prefcribed by this limitation any perfon of an ordinary ca- pacity may act in conformity to 'he rules which have been deliver- ed.
