But, if a woman has been unhappily accuftomed to luxuries, or if foups fhould difagree with her, fhe may be indulged with a little fifh, a little boiled veal or chicken, and bread pudding. Kvtry fucceding day will render fuch indulgence fafer. Hot fpices, however, and ardent fpirits in any form or mixture, are to be abfo- lutely prohibited during the whole time of lying-:n. Wine- itfelf o- liable *.«> do numb 'n.:ir*h:ef :•■'! evry *""" np'ors ot -ever or irflam- 534 'jiDVICE TO MOTHERS, mation difappears ; and, even then, fhould be very fparingly ufed* not more than a glafs or two being allowable at the principal meat only. &ut though quietnefs, repofe, the admiffion of frefh air, ftrict cleanlinefs, and a temperate cooling regimen, muft contribute very much to prevent fever, and to promote a woman's fafe and fpeedy recovery from childbed ; yet '.11 thefe prudent meafures will often fail, without her own faithful difcharge of one of the moft facred duties of a mother, that of fuckling her infant. Unlefs the milk, which is ready to gufh from her nipples, finds the proper vent, it will not only dif tend and inflame the breafts, but excite a great de- gree of fever in the whole fyftem. Every attempt to difperfe it by iirtificial means, being an act of flagrant rebellion againft nature, is as dangerous to the mother herfelf, to fay nothing of her child, as an attempt to procure abortion. The evident determination of the blood to the breafts, for the wifeft and moft benignant purpofe, c?rn never be repelled with fafety. It is either depofited upon fome Other p?.rt, there to produce inflammation : or, if purgatives and fudorifics are ufed to carry it off by different outlets, the violence of their action muft be attended with dangerous fhocks, even to the firmeft habit. It may be faid, that there are inftances without number, of mothers who enjoy perfect health, though they never fuckled their children. I pofitively deny the affertion ; and maintain, on the contrary, that a mother, who is not prevented by any particular weaknefsor difeafe from difcharging that duty,cannot neglect it with- out material injury to her conftitution. The fame midwives who would affift her in procuring a mifcarriage, if fhe wanted it, may now alfo undertake to difperfe her milk with the utmoft eafe and- fafety. Let her not truft to the wicked delufion. The inifchief is not the lefs certain from its being perhaps unperceived at the time; and cruelty to one child, often deftroys the power of procreating another. If we take a view of all animated nature, it is {Tracking to find, that woman fhould be the only monfter capable of withholding the nutritive fluid from her young. Such a mooft r, however, does not exift among lavage nations. They cannot feparate the idea df bringing forth a child, from the neceflity of giving it fuck. The wives of the American favages are faid to extend this mark of motherly tenderaefs and folic! tude even to infants that die upon the breaft. After having beftowed upon them the rites of burial, they come once a-day for feveral weeks, and prefs from the nipple a few drops of milk upon the grave of the departed ftrkling. I have feen a drawing taken from nature by a gentleman at Botany Bay : it reprefented a female of that country, after having openea one of her veins, and made an incifion in the navel of her fickly child, en- deavouring to transfufe her blood into its body, and hoping there- by to reftore its health, and to prolong its exificnce. Obfervation and experience had taught her, that the umbillical cord, or naval- ftriog, was the medium through which thefoetus, while in the womb, received nourifhmeat from its mother; fhe fancied, therefore, tha! ADVICE Ta MOTHERS. 53^ (he coiod transfule her blood through the famechanflel, and reno- vate a hfe which was dearer to her than her own i Let the mother in civilized fociety, who, from motives of felfifh eafeand imaginary pleafure, denies her infant the vital ftream with which fhe is abun^ dantly fupplied for its fuftenance, think of the poor fava'ge, and ftart with horror at her own unnatural depravity. It is alfo a great miftaka on the part of fuch felfifh mother*, (0 fancy that they can take more pleafure by abandoning their infants to the caie of hirelings. Some of them may be callous to ali re- proaches of confidence for the frequent difeafes of thofe" children ; but leaving moral fentiment and natural tendernefs cut of the queltion, pleafure is infcparahlyconnected with the enjoyment of health ; and 1 have already fhewn how much this is endangered by a mother's unwillingnefs to become a narfe. I need not repeat what I faid of the inflammation and fuppuration of the breaft; but my hint on barrennefs, as one of the probable cemfequerices of an attempt todilperfethe milk, may be farther enforced by obferving, 1 hat the womb is the part moft likely to be affected in fuch cafes :' the repelled humour has often been depofited on that delicate or- j>ar., and has there produced deep-feated and frequently incurable ulcere. Many inftances of this fort, as well as of other diforders arifing from the fame caufe, and equally fatal to fecundity, gave rife to my former affertion, however harfh it may found in the ear of fafhionable perrerfenefs. But I can with equal confidence affure the fond parent, faith- ful to her truft, and eager to cherifh her infant with the vital fluid which nature has kindly given her for that purpofe, that nothing elfe can fo effectually promote her recovery from childbed, the fpeedy return of good health, and the long continuation of that in- valuable blefling. Befides, all nurfes concur in declaring, that the aft itfelf is attended with fweet, thrilling, and delightful fenfations of which thofe only who have felt them can form any idea. I have already admitted, that a mother may be prevented from giving fuck, by fome particular weaknefs or diforder ; and is touching on the fame fubject in another work, I obferved that women of delicate conftitutions, fubjeft to hyfteric fits, or other nervous affections, made very bad nurfes. I^eft that remark might give too great a fcope to excufes, on the ground of pretended weak- ness or delicacy, 1 added, t^iat every mother who could, aught c=r- ta:nly to perform fo tender and agreeable an office. I now go farther, and maintain, that every woman, who is not able and willing to difcharge the duties of a mother, has no right to become one. The fame psrlonal defect, or conftitutional infirmity, which nay difquulify her for nurfing, ought to be confidered as an equally ftrong difqualification for marrying. But if, after marriage, any fubfequent difeafe cr accident fhould render the difcharge cf a mother's firft duty impracticable or dangerous, ihe is, in fuch cafes only blamelefs for calling in the aid of another to fuckle her child. In the next chapter, I fh^tlj. have occjUron to ipeak of the faiu- rarv effects of the mother's aoik on the new-born meant. The aim of mvprefert obfrTvatio.ts is'« coii'V"^ ; ^ ng 0*. women,'that 536 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. the free and natural difcharge of that precious current is effential to their own health and fafety. But as fome young mothers, how- ever well-inclined, may be difcouraged from perfevering in their attempts to give fuck, by the difficulty? awkwardnefs, or pain, at- tending the firft experiments, I fhall fubjoin for their direftion in fuch cafes, a few rules laid down by the moft approved writers on midwifery. The firft advice given by thofe eminent practitioners is, to put tne child to the breaft as foon after delivery and due repofe as the ftrength of the mother will permit, care having been previouiiy taken to wafh the breafts with a little warm .milk and water, in ox;der to remove the batter, vifcid fubftance, which is furnifhed round the nipple to defend the parts from excoriation or. i'oreaejk.- When the woman has.never nurfed before, the nipples at firft are fome- times not fufficiently prominent to afford a proper hold for the child. The ends alfo of the fmall tubes through which, the milk pafles, are contrafted, to prevent its flowing out fpontaneoufly.— From thefe circumftances, a3 well as from the inexpertnefs both of the mother and the infant, fome pain and difficulty may arife. But the common practice of having the breafts drawn by an old child, or fome ^rown perfon, is deemed improper, .becaufe the degree of violence ufed on thefe occafions will often irritate and inflame the parts, and frighten the woman againft the renewal of fuch painful experiments. Much gentler means will produce the defired effeft. The breafts fhould be fomented with flannels dipt in warm water ; and then a glafs or ivory cup, mounted on a bag of eiaftic gum, • onght to be applied in fuch a manner to the nipple, tha*fc it will draw it out gently and gradually, while by moderate preffure on the fides of the breaft with the hands the milk will be pufhed forward. In obftinate cafes, inftruments of more attractive power may be ufed, though with caution, for fear of injuring the breaft. If the difficulty be not owing to a tlatnefs of the nipple (apon the principal caufe of which I before hazarded a conjecture) but to a little rigidity of the milk-veffels, nothing more ;s neceflary than the warm fomentation above recommended. The ftiffnefs or con- ductions of the ends of thofe fine tubes will gradually yield to the natural efforts of the infant. They will foon become ftraight, fo as no longer to impede the egrefs of the milk, which is drawn into them by the fuftion. Impatience, or exceffive eagernefs, in thefe cafes as in all others, defeats its own end. The attempts at firft fhould neither be too often repeated, nor too long continued ; and when the child is put to the breaft, the mother ought to be fup- ported by pillows in bed, in a reclining pofture, and with due pre- cautions againft catching cold". Such are the dictates of enlightened practice, of which I am happy to avail myfelf, as an additional incitement to mothers not to fhrmk from their duty. A little pain is eafily iurmounted, and is followed by tailing pleafure. I muft not omit another caution given by the fame writers, in cafe of any par icular forenefs of the nipples, always to procure the beft medictil afiiftance, as-the mcdes of treatment purfued by ignorant perfons are, in thefe inftances more efpecially, no lefs injurious than abfurd. ADVICE TO MOTHERS. 53% CHAP. IV. OF THE NURSING,AND REARING OF CHILDREN. VERY thing is perfect, fays Rofeau, as it comes out of the Is of God ; but every .thing degenerates in the hands of man. Th-s is particularly true of the human fpecies. If the mother du- ring pregnancy, has not fuffered any injury from accident, or from her own imprudence ; and if, after the acceffion of labour, neither fhe nor the midwife has difturbed or impeded the efforts of nature ; the offspring of ftrong and healthy parents is fure at the birth to be well formed, healthy and vigorous.' Any inftances to fm» contrary are fo rare and extraordinary, as almoft to leave fome doubt of the poffibility of fuch an event; yet it appears from the- beft calculations, that at leaft one half of the children born, die before tney are twelve years ol/j. Of the furviving half at that period, how many perifh before they attain to maturity! How many others are ftinted in their growth, diftorted in their figure, or too much enfeebled ever to enjoy the real fweetsof life 1 What a train of ills feem to await the precious charge, the moment it is taken out of the hands of nature ! But as the moft of thefe calam- ities are the confequences of mifmanagement or negieft, I fhall en- deavour to fhew how they may be prevented by tender and ration* ai attention. \ SECTION I. Of the influence of Air on the Health and Lives of Children. THE firft want of a new-born infant is clearly manifefted by- its cries, not arifing from any fenfe of pain, but from a ftimulus or impulfe to expand the lungs, and thereby open a free pbuTage for the circulation of theblood, and for admiffion of air, fo effential to the exiftence of every living creature. While the child lay in the womb, its lungs were in a*collapfedcr fhrivelled ftate; it received? all its fupplies through the medium of the naval-ftring. But at its birth a very obvious change takes place. The pulfation or throbbing of this cord firft ceafes at the remoteft part, and then by flow degrees, nearer and nearer to the child, till/the whole firing be- comes quite fixe id, all circulation being confined to the body of the infant. It is then that the cries of a healthy child are heard ; in coniequence of which the air rufhes into the lungs ; their tubes: and cellul ir ipaces are dilated ; the bofom heaves ; the cavity of the cheft is enlarged ; and the blood flows with the utmoft .eafe.—. But as the air paffes out, the lungs again collapfe, and the courfe of the bl od receives a momentary check, till a frefh influx or in- ip: radon of air in concurrence with the action of the heart ancl arteries, renews the former falutary procefs, which never ceafes duri -g life. t he air thus inhaled, after imparting its vital properties to the whole frame, takes up the perfpirabie matter conftantly ifluing fr. ia the interior furfaca of the lungs, and carries off, oa itsexpui- (35) 25S ADVICE TO MOTHERS. fion, a confiderable part of the noxius and fuperfluous humours of the body. Its purity is of courfe deftroyed, and, in confequence. of being frequently breathed, it becemes unfit for refpiration. In a confined place, therefore, it is not air we inhale, but our own effluvia ; and every other caufr, which tends to wafte or pcllufe the air, renders it in fome degree injurious to the ftrength and health of thofe who breathe it. In this account of one of the moft important of the vital func- tions, I have avoided the minute details of anatomical Hence,w^:ch •would indeed, have made it more accurate, but lefs intcl'igible to the generality of my female readers. I thought it far b^tn** to explain to them, in as familiar language as I could, the caufe of an infant's cry at the moment of its birth, with toe hope of rendering them attentive to the purity of what nature fo brecuc ufiv demands. The quality of the air we breathe, io of much greater confeqiteoce than our food or drink, at every period of life, but particularly in infancy, a ftate of the utmoft defiCacy and weaknefs. Good air Braces.,'bad air relaxes, the tender frame ; the former is a fource of health and vigour, the latter of infirmity and difeafe. ;;,. It fhould therefore be the firft object of a .pregnant woman's1' care, tofecure, at le.;ft ft,r the time of her lying-in, a wholefome^ fituation. Inftead of flying from the country to town, as many do/ fhe fhould fly from town to the country. If her circumftances will£ not admit or this, file muft fix her abode in as open and airy a ftreet as fbe can, and at as great a diftance as pdffible from noife,T from tumult, and from thofe nui faeces which contaminate the at-f mbfphere of great, cities. Let her apartments be lofty and fpacious,i, dry rather than warm, and expofed to the fun's morning rays. I have already explained the importance of cleanlinefs, and or occa- fionally letting down the upper fafhes of the bed-room windows in fine Weather, to admit frefh air, and to prevent fever. An atten- tion to thefe points is not lefs neceffary on the new-born infant's account, than on his mother's. Let not the firft air he breathes be foul from confinement, too much rarefied by heat, or charged with any noxious exhalations. The mild temperature to which he has been ufed "m fhe womb, renders it very proper to preferve for fome*' time the fame moderate degree of warmth in his new place* of refi- denee. But he is not on that account, to be rcafted before a great: fire, or kept panting in fteam and pollution. If the room be kept properly ventilated and free from impurityj the infant will foon get hardy enough to be taken cut into the open air, not only without the leaft danger, but wich the greateft 4 advantage ; provided always that the feafon cf the year, and the" ftate of the weather, encourage fuch early experiments. A month ; fpent within doors, is confinement long enough in almoft everyJ cafe *, and t^te nurfery is-then to be frequently exchanged for green/; fields and ftmny eminences. - There your chid will drink, as it' wete, the Vital ftream pure from its fource; he will draw in at every breath frefh fupplies of ftrength and alacrity; while the-bra*" cing action of the air on the furface of his body, will give a degree1"' of firomefs unattainable by any other means. ,1 ADVICi TO MOTHERS. S& In the courfe of a few months, the ftate of the weather need not be much regarded ; and its unfavourable changes, unlefs the heat or cold be intenfe, muft not operate as a coeck on thofe daily excursions from the nurfery. Our climate is very fickle ; we fhall puffer much from its, rapid variations, if we are not freely expofed to them in early life; do not therefore facrifice the future comfort and fafety of the grown man, to miftaken tendernefs for the infant. If your child be accuftomed from the cradle to go cut in all wea- thers, he will have nothing to fear from the bleak north, or the ful- , try fouth, but will bear every change of feafon, of climate, and of atmofphere, not only without danger, but without pain or inconve- nience. What is here faid of the importance of frefh air, and of fre- . quent expofure to all forts of weather, in early life, mult derive ad- ditional weight from a confideration of the bad eff-fts of confine- ment and of unwholefome air upon children. This part of the fub- jeft is pretty fully difcuffed in my " Domeftic Medicine." I there explained the reafon why fo few of the infants that are put into hofpitals or parifh work-houfes live. Such places are generally crowded with old, fickly, and infirm people, by which means the air is rendered fo extremely pernicious, that it becomes a poifon to young children.. I alfo took notice of one of the worft afflictions of poverty in great towns, where the poorer fort of inhabitants live . in low, dirty, confined houfes, to which the frefh air has hardly any '-accefs. Though grown people, who are hardy and robuft, may live in fuch fituations, yet they generally prove fatal to their oft- fpring, few of whom arriye at maturity, and thofe who do are weak and deformed. While I was confidering the hard lot of the poor, ffic-ft of whofe children perifh becaufe the wretched parents are not in a condition to take them often out into the open air, I could not but obferve that the rich were without any excufe for neglecting fo efjential a part of their duty. It is their bufinefs to fee that their children be daily carried abroad, and that they be kept in the open air |or a fufficient time. This will always fucceed better? if the mQther goes along with them. Servants are often negligent in thefe matters, and allow a child to fit or lie on the damp ground, inftead of leadiag or carrying it about. The mother furely needs air as w$ as her children, and how can fhe be better employed than in attending them ? Io the fame chapter, i had farther occafion to cenfure a verey bad, though a very prevailing cuftom, of making children fleep in fmall apartments, or crowding two or three beds into one cham- ber. ' Inftead of this, thenurfery and the fleeping-rooms ought al- ways to be the largeft and moft airy apartments in the houfe.— When children are fhut up in fmall rooms, the air not only becomes unwholefome, but the heat relaxes their folids, renders them deli- cate,, and difpofes them to colds, and many other diforders, partic- ularly of the convulfive kind. All medical men, who have had much practice in the treatmept, of children, agree in opinion, that convulfion-fits, of which foraariy infants die, are to be chiefly alcn- j** ADVICE rd MOTHERS. bdifo a confined and impure air. I wifh to imgrefs-this truth oil the minds'cf mothers and nurfes, to make them fenfible of the dan- ger of frtiall or clofe rooms, and of the pernicious folly of covering kn infant's face in bed, or the front of its cradle, and thereby ma- , king it breathe the fame air over and over all the time it fleeps. It rhay be of no lefs confequence t? repeat and enforce my •> cautions to p rents againft fending their children,'while very young,'«! or indeed at any age, to crowded fchools, the atmofphere of which . is really a floating mafs of putrid effluvia. The breath and per- ' fpirationoffo many perfons in a room, even fuppofingtheni all to > be in go*>d health, muft wafte and corrupt the air, deftroy its vital ** properties, and of courfe render it wholly unfit for the. fupport a' animal life. But fhould any one child happen to be difeafed, alio* the reft are very likely to catch the infection. ' When I fee a poor baby, before it can well walk, carried in a nurfe's arms to fchool, I really feel ftronger emotions of pity, and of alarm for its fafety, than if I had feen it conveyed to a peft houfe/ In thejatter place, . \ children Would be kept feparate, and proper means would be ufed j; to prevent the fpreading of contagion ; in the former, all are thrown '*' together, and thefe remain with relaxed lungs, open pores, and ^ • ftetiming bodies, fo as tb render it almoft impoffible for any to *- efcape. ,; < As thoufands of children die every year the victims of difeafe^ caught at fchools, and as the health and conftitutions of ftill greater*j numbers are irretrievably ruined by the confinement and the bad air' ■■■« of fuch places, parents muft not be offended at the Teeming harfh- * rlefs of ray language in reprobating fo-abfurd, fo cruel, and fo un- <♦ natural a practice. .'1 know, that as foon as. children begin to run about, they require the moft watchful care to prevent mischief.— Will any mother urge this as a reafon for being tired of them, and for confining, as it were in flocks, that reftlefs activity which is*' wifely defigned by nature to promote their growth and vigour ?— . Will fhe, from a wifh to fave herfelf fome trouble, or fo gain time for other bufinefs infinitely lefs important, fend her little babes'; to fchool, under the filly pretence of keeping diem out of harm's way ?■ I hope what I have already faid is fufficient to convince perfons of common underftanding, that they cannot be expofed to greater • Harm, than by being fixed to a feat in the midft of noxious fteam . for fix o*r feven hours a day, which fhould be fpent in the open air ^ and cheerful exercffe. . . , ^ . " Should it b^ alledged, that children are fent young to fchool. from a becoming zeal for jheir early improvement, I need only reply, tnat learning, however defirable, is too dearly bought at the * essence of the conftitution. Befides, learning can never be ac- quire I byjuch prepofterous means. Confinement.and bad air are , not lefs injuria us to the mind, than to. the body; and nothing fo effectually prevents the growth of the intellectual faculties, as pre- • . mature application. • Sending a. child tq fchool in his nurfe's arms, is the fure way to make him an idiot, or to give him an unconquera- ble ofguft,to books : the only book he fhould then look at, is the great volume of nature. This is legible at every age, and Is as grat- A * ADVICE TO MOTHERS. C41 ifying to a child as to a man : It abounds with the moft delightful and moft ufeful, information : it is equally conducive to pleafure, >: health, and knowledge. «**■» A thoufand abfurdities in the fafhionable modes of education prefent themfelves now to my view ; but I moft only take notice of /.errors in the phyfical treatment of children ; an ! furely no error of •, J this fort can be more reprehenfible, than that which 1 h^ve been * jaftjJefcribing. Debility of body and mind is the certain confe- quence of fending very young children to fchool ; and of fending them, at any age, to crowded or confined fchools. The terms of inftruftion are in general fo low, that a matter or raiftrefs < of a fchool is obliged to take a great number of fchclars, in order: to get a living; and can feldom afford to rent a fpacous room in an open * and elevated fituation. Yet not only tl is is as abfolutely Eeceffary for health, but a large play-ground alfo, where even day-fcbolars * fhould be permitted to go out frequently to tafte the frefhmfs- of the vit^l breeze. The plants of genius and of manhood cannot flourifh; but by frequent expofure to the enlivening rays of the. fun*. SECTION II. Of warm and cold Bathing*. JN obferving the regular fucceffion of an infant's wants, after the fupply of air procured by its firft cries, its., feeming uncleaob* • nefs attracts our notice. The fkin appears covered with a flippery glue, which foon dries and forms a kind of fcurf. This fhould be ,, -waflied off very gently with a foft fpunge and warm water, having a little foap diffolved in it. Nurfes, in general, are as eager to re- move every fpeck of it, as if it was the moft offenfive impurity, though it is perfectly harmlefs, and will eafily come away in three or four wafhings, without the danger of hard,rubbing, cr the aid of improper, and fometimes very injurious, contrivances. Oint- ments or greafy fubftances cannot fail to fill up the little orifices of ihe pores, and to put a ftop to infenfible perfpiration Sp;rits cib any kind are ftill worfe, on account of their inflammatory efftft.— Even Galen s advice to fprinkle the child's fyody with f ft, that the glutinous matter may be more effectually rubbed off, is a* beft un- neceffary. I fave ho particular objection to the modern improve- mtnt on that hint-, which confifts in diffolving fait in the "warm bath, with a view of giving it the agreeable ftimulus, as well as the clean- fing and bracing properties of fea-water ; but I would not encour- age any folicitude in this refpeft, as the eafieft and fimpleft mode pf proceeding will fully anfwer the defired end* fn the hardy ages of antiquitv, we are told that.the Germans -ufed to plunge their new born infants into the freezing waters of tfye Rhine, to inure them betimes to the fevere cold.pf their native country. I need not take any pains to point out the danger of foi- bwing fuch an example in our times, when mothers and nurfes are .'too apt to run into the oppofite extreme of unnerving effeminancy. In, this, as in every thin& elle> ihe golden mean is the line of wii- •4o'm—theUjretofeepurtajby^auoaalrffe^on. It would be SA2 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. extrtmefy hazardous to dip the tender body of a child, reeking fr m the womb, in cold water, and to keep it there during the ne- ceffary operation of wafhing ; but 'he ufe of the cold bath may be Yafely brought about by degrees in five or fix months after the birth; and will then be found not only one of the beft means of promo- ting health and ftrength, but of preventing alfo many of the moft diftreffing coipplaints to which children are fubject. The following method 1 can confidently recommend, having had frequent oppor- tunities of obfervicg its falutary effefts. 'Ihe temperature of the bath proper for a new-born infant, ft uld appr ach nearly that of the fituation which he has juft quit- ted. It is proper to acquaint thofe who may not have an inftru- ment to afcertain the degree of heat, that abfolute precifion m that refpeft is by no means neceffary ; their feelings will inform them with fufficient ex'.ctnefs when the water is ratljer warmer than new milk ; a little folution of foap, as I before obferved, is all that is warted to increafe its fbftnefs and its purifying effect. The ope- ration of wafhing fhould be performed in a veffel large enough to allow room for the expanfion'of the infant's limbs, and for eafily difcovering any defect in its ftrufture, or any accident which may have happened to it during laboiu*: either may be often remedied by timely care, tut may become incurable through delay or neg- lect. Ihe child fhould not be kept in the bath longer than five or fix minutes; and the moment it is taken out, it fhould be wrapped up in a foft warm blanket, and there kept for a few minutes in a Kate of gentle motion. I would not have any difference tnade, either in the tempera- ture of the bath, or the time of the infant's continuance in it, for the firft month. Ihe uncleanlinefs of young children renders fre- quent wafhing neceflary. It fhould be the firft object of attention in tbe morning, and the laft at n:£*-t; but it fhould not be perform- ed with a full ftomach, eve^ when the child receives all its fupplies fro e the breaft. 1 his is the only caution which need be added to thofe already given concernirg gentlenefs in the manner, of wafh- ing, fpace enough in the bathiog-veffel, and ftrict care to wipe the child dry, and wrap it warm the inflant it is taken out of the bath, when expofure to cold would be doubly dangerous from the natu- ral delicacy of the infant, and from the immediately preceding warmth, aud the cpennefs of the pores. After the firft month the warmth of the water may be leffened, but almoft imperceptibly fo as to guard againft the rifk of fudden changes or too rafh experiments. The mildnefs of the weather aad the evident increafe of the child's ftrength, muft be taken into confederation ; for, though cold water is very ferviceable in bracing weak and relaxed habits, yet, if tried too foon, its ftimulns on the furfaceihay be too ftrong, and the powers of reaction within too weak,, fothat tjie worft confequences may follow. Thefe will be prevented by a gradual diminution of the temperature of the water, and by clofe attention to its effects, wheo reduced nearer and ftill nearer to a ftate of coidnefs. If immerfion in the bath be quickly followed by a glow ail oyer the body, and a perceptible liveKnela ADVICE TO MOTHERS. 543 in. the child, we may be fure that the water has not been^ too cold for his conftitution, and that we have proceeded with due care.— Bat fhould «t produce chillnefs, evident langcur, and depreflion, we muft make the water a little warmer next time, and not venture; upon the cold bath tiH we are encouraged by more favourable ap- pearances. It would-tend rather to increafe than to clear up the doubts of mothers and nurfes, were I to enter into a detail of all the infirmi- ties and difeafes, in which the cold bath would be ferviceable or in- jurious, not only during infancy, but at a more advanced period of life. There are many nice diftinctions in a variety of complaint^ where the greateft medical fkill.and experience are neceflary, to de- eiJe on the propriety or impropriety of retorting to lb powerful, but at the lame time.fb hazardous, a remedy. I muft, however, forbid its ufe in complaints of the bowels ; affeftions of the lungs ; erur> tions on the infant's.ikin ; and in cafes of extreme weaknefs, indi- cated bv the before-imentioned fymptoms of chillnefs aad apparent lofs of ftrength and fpirits after immerfion. < With fuch reftraints on iodifcreet rafhnefs, it is hardly poffible that a woman can do wrong in purfuing the plan which 1 haye pointed out, for reducing the;warmth of the water by very flow, and almoft imperceptible degrees, till it can be employed quite cold with fafety and benefit. There is no doubt but a great deal of mifchief ,has refulted from the too early and injudicious ufe of the cold bath.. I per- fectly agree with Dr. Untierwasd in his equally fenfible and humane .remark, tuat " to fee a little infant, three or four days old, thea£ fpringperhaps of a delicate mother, who has not ftrength even to fuckle it, wafhed up to the loins and breaft in cold water, expofed for feveral minutes, perhaps in the midft of winter (when cn'-kbom are more inclined to difeafe than thofe born in fummer) itfelf in one continued ftream, and'the fond mother-covering her ears coder tne bed-clothes, that fhs may, not be diftrefled oy its criet .."eever ftruck me as apiece of unneceffary feverity, and favours 0?;» le or kindoefs, as p'u 'gbe<r an icfaof- a fecond or t*tird (imeiroo a odp of ^pater, with its mouth open acd gafping for breath, in tboj eld-L: on- ioned mode of cold bathing: b.th of which efter. induce cramps and pains in the bowels, and weaknefs of the lower extremities,, but rarely an increafe of ftrengh. I hope the advice which I have given refpefting the ■ proper temperature,ofthe bath during the firft months, of infancy will ope- rate as a check on the "• unneceffary feverity" fo juftly cenfured in the firft.par-t of this obfervation. But the error pot ;ted cut in the .old-faihioned modeof cold bathing, may n< t be fo eafily corrected, unlefs fome ftrong and clear reafons are affigned for dffcontinuing the dangerous part of fhat practice. Women fhould therefore be informed that the immedia'e effect of immerfion in cold water, at any age, is a fudden contra fticn of the pores "and blcod-veffels of the fkin,N and a general repulsion or throwing back of the fluids towards- the internal parts. Tiie chill- ing fenfation excites the moft vigorous efforts of the organs of life* particularly the, heart ao&arteries, to increafe' .the heat with"* tfie j44 ADVICE Tit Mu^HaRi. fcody, andrefiftthe fhock given to the fnrface. This is what is called action and re-action, the degree of the latter beirg always in }»cportion to the violence of the former, and ta the ftrength cf the conftitution. Hence arifes that delightful glow, which follows the firft impreffion of cold ; and, fo far, the full play of the vial organs is as pleafant as it is falutary. But, as the increafed heat foon pafles off from the body, if it be continued in the water, or taken cut and directly plunged into it again, the animal powers are liable to fee exhaufted by inceffant cr repeated efforts to produce mere heat and to overcome the action of the external cold. Grown perfons have often experienced the fatal confequences of too long a ftay in the water. What then muft the effefts of a fecond and third dip be tipon the tender and delicate frame of an infant, whofe vital powes is proportionally feeble ? Befides the rifk cf extinguifhing 'the faint iparks of life, an accumulation of humours in the head, ftagnations of the blood in other parts, and convulfion fits, are very likely to take place. But though none of thefe meiahdu-\y circumftances fhould happen at the moment, a ftoppage of growth, and a puninefs of habit, muft certainly fellow fo inconfiderate an abufe of the very means beft calcul ted, under proper management, to promote health, expanfion, and vigour. In cafes of previous indifpofition, or difeafe; where the cold bath may be prefcribed as a remedy, the danger to a poor infant muft be ftill greater from an injudicious mode of proceeding. I took no fmall pains in my " Domeftic Medicine," to expoie the whims and prejudices of nurfes in this refpect. They would be objefts of ridicule, were they not often attended with tjie mcft ferious confequences. I fhould finiley for inftance, at the remains of fuperftitious weaknefs, in believing that the whole virtue cf the water depends upon its being confecrated to a particular faint, were it not that moft of thofe holy wells, as they are called, are ver> unfit for bathing, and, what Is worfe, that the child is kept too long in the water> and that due attention is not paid to friction and warmth afterwards. Some of thofe filly women pkice their cenfi* deuce in a certain number of dips, as ihree,leven, crnine, though every dip after the firft, at each time of bathing, net only defeats the hope of benefit, but increafes the ftrong probability of much anifchief. This may indeed be avoided, by dipping the infantonly once at a time ; but even in that cafe, the magieal number of dips is very infufficient for any defirable purpofe. I have alfo known nurfes who would not dry a child's fkin after bathirg, left it fhculd deftroy the effefts of the water ; others will even put cloths dipt in the water upon the child, and either put it to bed, or fuffer it to go about in that condition. This is fometimes done with impuni- ty by grown perfons,. who refort to the famous fpring at Malvern m Gloucefterfhire, for the cure cf particular complaints cf the cu- taneous clafs ; but it would be little fhort of frenzy to make inch an experiment upon children. The only w&y of fecuring to an infant all the falutary effefts of the cold bath, without the leaft pofijbiiity of harm-, is to prepase him for it ia the flow and cautious manner before recommended.— ADVICE TO MOTHERS. 545 This may. be accomplifh«-d, under favourable circBmitances, in fiv<» or fix months. R^In or river water is "fitter for the purpofe of bathing, than, pump or fpring water ; though the latter, io cafe cf neceflity, may be ufed, after-having been expofed for fome hours to the fun or the atmofphere. The child muft not be dipped when its body is hot, or its ftomach full, and fhould be put. only once under the water at each time of bathing. All the benefit, as before obferved, depends upon the firft fhock, and the re-acti n of the fyftem i In order to prevent a fudden and ftrong determination of the blood fo the head, it is always advifeable to dip the ch'ld with this part foremoft, and to be as expeditious as poffible in wafhirg away all impurities. I have been already fo particular in my direc- tions to have the young bather inftautly wiped dry, and wrapped up in a foft warm blanket that I need not repeat them; but I muft add another injunction, whico is, not to put the child to bed, but to keep it for fome time in gende motion, a'od to accompany the whole prccefs with lively fingmg. It is of far greater importance than moft people may be aware of, to affociate in early life the idea of pleafure and cheerfnlnefe with fo falutary an operation. During the ufe of the lukewarm bath, the whole body is to be immerfed in it every night as well as morning. But when recourfe is had t© cold bathing, it muft be ufed in the manner above prefcri- bed in the morning only. At night it will be encugh to wafh the lower parts ; and even for this purpofe a little warm water may be added to the cold in fevere weather. Every danger will thus be avoided; everv benefit will befecured; and the habit of perfonal cleanlinefs being rendered familiar in childhood, will be retained through life, and v.td contribute very much to its duration and en- joyment. SECTION III. Of Children s Drefs. THERE n not any part of my profefliona} labours which I review wita greater pleafure, than my exertions in early Ufe to ref- cue infants from the cruel tortures of fwathing, of rollers, and of bandage?. When 1 firft ventured to take up the fubject, about half a century ago, it certainly required the ardour, the courage, the enthufiafm of youth, to animate my oppofition, not only to the prevalence of cuftom and the ftubbornefs of eld prejudices-, but to the doctrines of the Faculty themfelves. Abfurd as we may now think the practice of twaddling and wrapping up a child, till it was *s ftiff as a log of wood; the arguments in favour of a loofe and eafy drefc, which 1 made ufe of in my Inaugural Differtation* were vehememly combated by the moft eminent men, who at that time ^taught medicine in the JJniverfity of Edinburgh, the reform Vhich has fince taken place, though »ot carried to the extent that it ought to be, is an encouragement to ufe lefs referve in condem- ning the remains of fopermcious a fy to It/snoot be deemed, a matter ot aftomfhment, while medical tmen declared fchem&lve* advocates for fuch a mode of cioathing, * -* DeN^apttua vita coafervMuJa* 546 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. that it fhould be carried to the moft dangerous excefs, by ignorant, bufy, or felf-conceited women. They fancied that the fhape, beauty, and health of the infant depended wholly on the expert* nefs of the perfon employed in dreffing it. The mid-wife was to new-mould the head, and to fhape, every limb, according to her own fancy, and then to retain the parts, in the ft >rm fhe gave them, by cbfepneffure. Her ftupid prefumption was farther encouraged by the vanity of parents, who, too often defirons of making a fhow of the infant as foon as it was born, were ambitious to fee it made up in perfect trim, and to have as much finery heaped upon it as p Jlible. Thus it came to be thought as neceffary for a midwife to excel in bracing and dreffing an infant, as.for a furgeon-to be ex. pert in applying bandages to a broken limb ; and the poor child, as foon as it came into the world* had as many rollers and wrappers applied to its body, as if every bone had been fractured in the birth; while thofe cruel ligatures were often fo tight, as not only to gall and wound its tender frame, but even to obftruft the motion of the heart, lungs, and other organs neceffary for life. In the progrefs of folly and vice, when the influence of de» praved fociety had extinguifhed in the breafts of many mothers every fpark of natural affection, and had prompted them to abandon their children to the care of hirelings, the mercenary nurfe was glad, for the fake of her own eafe,to follow what phyficians taught, and midwives practifed. The infant was kept fwathed in the form of an Egyptian mummy, as incapable of motion as the latter, and almoft as deftitute of every fymptom or indication of life, except its unavailing cries. Though dwarfifhnefs, deformity, difeafes, pr death,,muft have frequently been the confequence, yet the nurfe efcaped all blame, as the bandages prevented any limbs from being broken, and the poor viftim bound hand and foot, might be thrown any where, and there left with the utmoft indifference, while fhe attended to her private concerns. The only thing relating to the drefs of infants which feemed to arife from any tendernefs, was a regard to its warmth: Unfortu- nately this was carried too far: and children fuffered from the quantity, as well as from the tightnefs of their clothes. Every child has fome degree of fever after the birth; and if it be loaded with too many clothes, the fever muft be increafed, often to fuch a de- gree, from the concurrence of other, caufes of heat, as to endanger the life of the infant. Even though no fever fhould be excited,. the greateft debility muft be the confequence of keeping a child in * ftate of perpetual wafte by exceffive perfpiration. Befides, in fuch a condition, a child is liable to catch cold upon expofure to the leaft breath of air ; and its lungs, relaxed by heat* and never fuffi- ciently expanded, are apt to remain weak and flaccid for life, fo that every cold will have the moft alarming tendency, and probably terminate in an afthma, or a confumption. All the former evils, ariang from the fallacy of medical theo- ries, from the prefumption of midwives;, the folly of parents, the un- willin^nefs of lome mothers to do their duty in becoming- nurfes, thefelfifh views of hirelings, and the quite oppofite, though no lefs ADVICE TO MOTHERS. 547 fatal fuggeftions of mifguided tendemefs, were farther aggravated by the imperious dictates of fafhion. Reafon, experience, and true tafte, would have long fince triumphed over fillv fpeculations, ig- norance, and caprice, had not every confideration been facrificed to prevailing forms ; fo that from the infant to its fwaddling-clothes, to its grand-mother in her fhroud, drefs muft be wholly regulated by the etiquette of fafhion. Againft this fpecies of hitherto un* fhaken tyranny, I fhall therefore point the chief force of my argu- ments ; after a few more ftriftures on the abfurdity and perni- cioufnefs of the other caufe—of tight and oppreffive cloathing, which has really inflicted deeper wounds en population, than fam- ine, peftilence, and the fword. To begin with the error of phyficians: it is almoft inconceiva- ble, how any fet of men, who profeffed to be the admirers and fol- lowers of nature, fhould have been fo totally blind to her obvious mode of proceeding in the prefervation of infant life. She forms the body foft and flexible to facilitate its future growth ; fhe fur- rounds the fatus in the womb with fluids, to prevent its receiving any injury from unequal preflVe, and to defend it agaiDft every thing that might in the leaft cramp or confine its motions; fhe a^, dapts the fame means to the fafe delivery of the child, all whofe bones are fo griftly and eiaftic as to yield with furprifidg pliancy to every obftruction in the act Of labour, and afterwards to refume their proper form, unlefs reftrained or diftorted by the bufy inter- ference of man. Yet people of pretended fcience have been bold enough to affert, that a child, when it comes into the world, is filmoft around ball ; and that it is the nurfe s part to affift nature in bringing it to a properfhape. We fhould rather fay, let the meddlirtg hand be amputated, which dares to <offer violence to t^e works of nature, if, through the inexpertnefs or impatience of the midwife, any of the child's delicate limbs have been fractured cr pur cut of joint, they will require immediate care and prcper bandages ; but let not prefumptuous follv attempt to mend what nature has made perfect, or perverfely confine what was formed for the utmoft free- dom of m< >tion and expaniion. I have often had occafion to obferve, that the inftinft cf brutes is an unerring guide in whatever regards the prefervation of animal life. Do they employ any artificial means to mould the iimbs of their young, or to bring them to a proper fhape P Trough many of thrfe are Extremely delicate when they come into the world, yet we never find them grow weak or crooked for want of fwaddling-bands. Is nature lefs kind or lefs attentive to the human fpecies ? Surely not; but we take the bufinefs out of nature's hands, and are juftly punifhed for our arrogance and temerity. This argument mav be rendered ftill more unanfwerable by an appeal to the conduft of thofe nations that approach neareft to a ftate of nature. They have no idea of the neceffity of rollers or bandages to ftrengthen the imaginary weaknefs, or to bring to aprop- • er fhape the imaginary' deformities of their infants. They allow . them from their birth the full ufe of every organ; carry them abroad in the ©peiv air j wafh dfeir bodies daily in cold water ; and give -4g ADVICE TO MOTHERS. them no other food or phyfic but the truly mediein?! and nutritive fluid with which the miners are benignantly fupplied by nature. Such management tends to render their children fo ftrong and har- dy that by the time our puny infants get cut of the nurfe's arms, theirs are able to ftiift for themfelves. I referve fome remarks en • r^e perfect fhape of thofe lavages for a diftuoft chapter, in which, - I mean to centraft it with thedwarfiihnei« and deformity of civili- zed nations. ' ,, . ', 4 Inftead of eonfidericg a child, at its birth as a round ball, which j ought to be brought to a proper fhapeby a midwife.' s or a nurfe's af- \ fiftance, I would have both thefe delcriptions of people, look upon } its little body as a bundle cf foft pipes, replenifhed with fluids in continual motion, the leaft ftoppage of which is attended with im- , minent danger, Tight prtffure always weakens, and may Corner - ; times fufpend, with deadly effect, tne action of the heart, the lungs, and all the vital organs ; it impedes the circulation of the blood^and the equal diftribution of nourifhment to the different parts of the body ; it diftorts the pliant bones, cramps the mufcular powers, prevents growth, and renders the whole frame equally feeble and misfhapea. - Even were reafon Glent on thofe points, and were we unwarn- ' ed of the bad effefts of fwathes and fillets by paft experience, hu- «y inanity cught to reftrain us from putting a helplefs innocent to the ■■ moft cruel torture, fqueezing its tender body into a prefs at the in- ftant of its releafe from former confinement, and loading it with chains as the firft mark of our attention. I have cften been af- tonifhed at the infenfibility pf mid wives and nurfes to the cries of infants while dreffing—cries that feldom ceafed till the powers of the poor creatures were exhaufted. Yet fo far from feeling any emotion ofpity, it is ufual for the midwife cr the nurfe to fmile at fuch cries, and to endeavcur to perfuade the mother, if within hearing, that the violence of the fcream is a fubject of joy, not of borrow, as it proclaims the child's fceakh and V'gour. 1 have al- ready explained the caufe and important purpofe of a new-born in- fant's firft cry, to promote refpiration ar.d circulation. 1 he loud- Pels of that cry is indeed a proof of the ftrength of the child's lungs; but every fubfequent cry is- the language of pain, the expreftive tone of irritaticn-and fuffering;. If you do net inftantly attend to it, you may be guilty of murder. Think of the immenfe number of children that die of convulfions foon after the birth ; and be af- fured, that thefe are much oftener owing to galling preffure, or fome ^external iojury, than to any inward caufe. I have known a cojkl - feized with convulfion fits foon after the midwife had done fwad- dling it, and immediately relieved by taking off the rollers and ban- dages. A loofe drefs prevented the. return of the difeafe ; and though this Ai'ill not always cure fits produced by tight cloath.ing, as the effeft of the injury may continue after the removal of - the - caufe, yet it is one of the neceffary means of relief, it being impoffi- ble that a patient can recover, as long as the caufe which firft gave* rife to the diforder continues to act. It may .be proper in th$ place, to ^?e as octear, fimple, and ADVICE TO MOTHERS. C4Q eoncifean account as I can of the nature of convulfions, that ttiidV wives and nurfes may learn to fhudder at the idea of occafioning, ' by their mifcOndoct, the moft fatal, as well as the moft frequent difeafes incident to childhood. The heads of infants being pro- portionally larger, and the nervous fyftem more extended, than in grown perfons, their nerves are more fufceptible of irritation ; and convulfion fits are the confequences of keen irritation, however ex- '. cited. The great Boerhaave was of opinion, that moft of the dif- orders of children might be raffed Under fhe clafs of convulfions. It is certain that all the different caufes of uneafinefs to a child . form but one general or undiftiaguifhed fenfation of pain, which he has alfo but one way of expreffmg, namely, by his cries-; and if -, thefe are not attended to, and no relief is or c^n be given, acute an 1 unmitigated pain commonly produces a fit. If any ftronger reafon need be urged for immediately attending to an infant's cries, it is that they are almoft always owing to mifmacagement. I admit, that the moft incurable convulfions are thofe which proceed from fome original fbult in the ftrufture of the brain itfelf, whence the nerves iffue. But fuch cafes feldom occur, though the brain has unqueftionably been*often injured, and convulfions occa- fioned, by a midwife's prefumptuous attempts to model the feull of t the new-born infant. I have already hinted at this deteftable practice, and fhall prefently make fome farther remarks on its bane- ful prevalence, and its horrid effefts* Children are alfo fubjectto convulfions from cutting the teeth ' with difficulty*, or from a feverifh irritation of the fyftem at the ap- ' proach of fhe fmall-pox, meafles, and other eruptive difeafes, I am ■ftr from being difpofed to blame nurfes for What they cannot pre. tent; though I-believe that the dangerous fymptoms, Which often attend teething iU particular, are chiefly, if not Wholly owing; to the previous improper and enervating treatment of the •child. The Other convulfi ms here alluded to ^generally go off as the eruptive difeafe, of which they may be called the forerunners, makes its ap- pearance. There is another caufe of coftvulfions,' for which midwives aod nurfes flatter themfelves that they are not in any fort blameable, I mean acute pain in the ftomach or bowels. But whence does this pain arife ? either from the tight preffure of thofe parts ; from t?he relaxing effect pf a hot and impure atmofphere ; or from fome acrid fubftance in thefhatieoffood cr phyfic conveyed into the ftoHiach, and Irritating thealimentary cailal. If you attend to the directions before given on the fubjfjft of air, wafhing, and cleanlinefs;—-if you pour nothing down th& infant's throat but the wholefome unviti- ated juice, defigned for him by nature;—if you flacken, inftead d bracing your wrappers round his body ; you may depend upon it that his ftomach and bowels will never be difordered as to cccafioa convulfions. . The Only part of an infant s drefs or covering which may be applied pretty clofe, is a broad piece of thla flinnel -round the navel, to guard againft any protrufion there, from the accidental violence oftheduldWfes. But take care not to'make tie priflure too wo 'ADVICE TO MOTHERS. tight, or you will not only hurt the bowels, but, perhaps, caufe ia another place a much worfe rupture than that to which your pre- caution is directed. This is what happens in many fimilar cafes} when people act from narrow or contr fted views of the' fubject!-, and in their eagemefs to prevent fome trifling and mer ly poflible J inconvenience, too often occafion irreparable mifchief. "' Again," then, let me caution midwives and nurfes againft retaining any part^ of the old fyftem of tight fwathing, as the injury it muft do is cer-'' tain, and the good or inconvenience, to which it may feem adapted, 13 imaginary. I am now fpeoking of its immediate "bad effefts, in fqoeezing the infant's delicate body; fretting his tender fkin, keep- ing his little limbs in a ftate of painful confinement, exciting his cries, and, by all thefe caufes of nervous irritatkm, throwing bian in- to convulfions. The female who1 can hear and fee tfa fe effects* of her own folly, and will yet perfift in it, afer \t is pointed out, cer- tainly does not deferve the name of a mothe". ; k But the moft cenfura ble part of the ufual con Juft cf midwives*! and nurfes ftill remains to be minutely examinedanl reprobated.—■ " It is not enough for them to keep up the fh/>w f helping nature, as they call it, during the procefe of a labour, though fhe has been truly faid tofdfdain and abhor affiflance; but t aey pretume to mend her work after delivery, and to give a more proper farm to the heads of new-born infants. The midwife will tell you, that the foft bones of a child's fcull are often fo difplaced an! fque zed together in com- ing into the world, that the head would be fhapelefs and frightful, were it not for her improving touch .s. Another reafon' is affigned * by the nurfe for foe- meddling:. She takes ahrm ^t the imperfect in- ' dentation of the bones on the crown of the her*, and u tooly ftrives to prefs them clofer and to br ic-- them by means of fillets, but is careful to keep the head warmly covered, t^orevent the poor baby,'/. asflie fays, frojti eatcMtg his death by the: expofure of thofe Open' parts to the air. • Deformity is the leaft of the evils that attend fuch * acts ofaftonifhing infatuation. The delicate texture of the brain is peculiarly liable to be affected; and though neither convulfions nor any other perceptible coMphint may immediately follow, yet a' weaknefs of underftanding, or a diminution *>f the m nttl powers, is often the confequence, and defeats all the efforts of the beft educa-f' tion afterwards...... l * V The oflificationor growing hardnefs of the bones of a child, ' and particularly thofe of the fcull; is incomplete • in the womb, to ' favor the purpofes of eafy and fafe delivery. In confequence of ' their foftnefs and pliancy, they admit of being fqu*ez-d together, and even of lapping over without injury, fo ast^ make the head conform to the fhape and dimenlions or the parts through which it is to be expelled.' They will foon refume their proper place, if left - to the kind management of nature, and not tampered with by the7" profane finger of a conceited midwife or a filly nurfe. As to the opening or imperfect indentation rf the bones of &ei}x fcull, it is owing to the fame caufe, and defigned for the fame impor- ! tant purpofe, to facilitate the birth of the infant. The free action of the external *ir is then neceffary to promote the firmmefs and ADVICE TO MOTHERS. 5S1 compaftnefsof thofe boaes, and to make them prefs into each other, and form futures for the perfect defence of the brain, not only againft blows and brnifes, but colds and defluxions. Warm and tgnt covering directly counterafts all thefe benignant intentions of nature, and renders the fcull a very weak fhield for the fecurity of its precious contents. The curious diftinction made by Herodotus, in the field of bat- tle, between the fculls of the Egyptians and the Perfians, has oftea been quoted toilluftrate and confirm this doftrine. That lliftorian having vifited the fcene of action, where the flain of thofe two na- tions had been feparated, fays that 021 examining their remains, he found the fculls of the Egyptians fo firm that the largeft ftdnes could hardly crack them, while thofe of the Perfians where fo thin and weak as to be eafily fractured by a fmall pebble. After ftating the faft, he accounts for ft by obferving, that the Egyptians, were ac- cuftomed from their infancy to go bareheaded ; whereas the Per- fians, on the contrary, always wore thick tiaras. Thefe were like the heavy turbans which they ftill ufe, and which fome travellers think the air of the country renders neceflary. I believe with Rof- feau, that the generality of mothers will pay more regard to the fug- geftion of fuch travellers, than to the remark of the judicious hilio- rian, and,will fancy the air of Perfia to be univerfal. In oppofition, however, to filly conceits and prejudicies, I muftaffure my female readers, that there is no part of the human frame which fuffers more from heat and preffure than the headj and none of courfe which ought to be kept cooler and lefs encum- bered. A .thin, light cap, flightly fattened with a bit of tape, fhould conflitute the whole of an infant's head-drefs, from the moment of its birth till the increafed growth of the hair renders anv other protection unneceflary. As foon as nature fupplies your child with this belt of all coverings, never think of any thing more, even when you take him out into the open air, unlefs rain or intenfe heat or cold fhould make the occafional ufe of a very light and eafy hat ad- vifabfe. I muft alfo forbid the ufe of ftay-bands to keep the poor infant's head as fixed and immoveable as if it were placed in a pil- lory. One would fuppofe that our heads were fo badly fecured by the Author of our bejngy that they would fall off if they were not held faft by thofe pernicious contrivances. If. is ftrange that women fhould be fo blind to the importance of letting the head move freely in every direction, in order-to facilitate the difcharge of tiie fluid excretions voided at the mouth! It is not neceffary to enter into minute details refpefting the other parts of ah infant's drefs. Any nurfe of common fenfe and doqility will eafily catch the fpirit of my former arguments on the futyeft, and will pay due regard to the following general, direction, with the writer's very plain and fenfible remarks; " Rational ten- derriefs," fays this author, " fhews itfelf in making the drefs light, Jmpk,vn& loofe.. By being as light as is confiitent with due warmth, it -will neitherencumber .the intaat, nor caufe any wafte of his pow- ers,;—in confequence pf its firaplicity, it will be readily and eafily put on* fo as to prevent many <*'m and tears, an object of infinite ADVICE TO MOTHERS. importance :—and its loofenefs will leave full room for moving and ftretchiog thofe Htde limbs which have been long heaped together, and for the growth and expanfioo of the entire frame." 1 before defired the nurfe to have always a loft warm bjanket m readinefst to wrap up the infant on being taken out of t e bath. In that wrapper the child ftVuld be kept for at leaft ten minutes, in gentle motion, ind then dreffed. A piece of fine flannel r. und the navel, a linen or cotton fhirt, a flannel petticoat, and a linen or cotton robe,' are foon put on ; and where faftenings are requifite, they fhould confift Of tape, without the dangerous ufe of pins. Their puoc- tures and fcratches are very irritating ; and I believe the f*& men- . tioned in my '* Domeftic Medicine" is far from being fingular or extraordinary, where pins were found flicking above half an ioch into tne body of a child, after-it had died of convulfion fits, which in all probability proceeded from that caufe. bio part of an infant's drefs fhould hang down above two or three inches lower than the feet. Long robes and long petticoats ferve only to conceal the nurfe's inattention to cleanlinefs, and are, - eveo on that account, very improper, as well as cumherfome. ihe night clothes fhould be much lighter than thole worn by day, from a due regard to the fituation of the infant, who fhould at all times, • either in bed or out, experience nearly the fame degree of warmth. Every moifture or impurity fhould be inftantiy removed, and as ■ thofe parts of the drefs which are next the fkin are conftantly im- biDing perfpirable matter, they fhould be changed frequently. In- deed, the lame clothes ought never to be kept on for many cays together.- Away with finery ; but take care that the child is al- ways clean and dry. 1 wifh 1 could here clofe my remarks on drefs, without having; any juft caufe to apprehend a ftronger refiftanceto all my precepts from fafhion, than from folly, ignorance and prejudice. Folly may be laughed out of its errors ; ignorance may be inftrufted; and even tiie ftubbomnefs of prejudice maybe borne down by the krefiftible force of argument. But fafhion bids defiance to the combined efforts T ridicule and reafon. The only favourable cir- cumstance is, that, being fickle as well as imperious^ it may, in its changeful whims, fometimes fall in with the dictates of true tafte, and give both eafe and elegance to the human form. This has been happily the cafe in the difcontinuance of fame of the moft painful, aukward, and disfiguring articles of female drefs; I taean the high- heeled fhoc, and the whalebone ftays, which, I hope, will never again mase their frightful appearance. ' Buttnough fafhion has lately carried the loofe and light attire of our fair country women almoft to the ex reme of nudity, yet it cruelly and abfurdly retains too much of the bracing method in' childhood and youto, when the ten' er and growing frame requires the utmoft eate and freedom. It is true, we no longer fee the once familiar fpeftacle of a mother laying her daughter down upon a carpet, then putting her foot upon the girl's back, and bie. king half a dozen laces in tightening rer ftays, to give her a il-oder waift. L . • i . i rr- . n 555 My former remarks on the tight huffar dreffes of little boys, who ought to be kept much longer in pettieoats, and on the diago- j nal braces of young ladies, who are thus cramped and diftorted, | preclude the neceflity of any farther cbfervations concerning the I due degree of eafe which fhould always be confulted in the body- I clothes of both fexes. But it is proper to fay fomething of the I changes in point of warmth, which may be requifite in different 1 ft.ite* of the weather. I have met with feveral plaufible arguments iti favour of an uniformity of drefs in all feafons ; and the example of the great Newton has been urged, to induce us, like him, to wear j camlet in winter as well as in fummer. But though that iiluftri- . ous philofdpher made himfelf immortal by his amazing difcoveries, yet his natural life did not greatly exceed the ordinary period of • threefcore years and ten. He cannot therefore be mentioned as a i remarkable inftance of longevity; and even had he Ived manv f years longer, the number of his days might be more reofonably af- cribed to his temperance, his regularity, the habitual fweenefs of H his difpofition, and the exquifite pleafure arifi tg from his fuccefsful refearches, than to the unvarying famenefs of his drefs. It does not therefore imply the fmalleft diminution of our rev- erence for the great Newton, if we look upon nature as a more un- erring guide than any philofopher. Obferve h iw kindly fhe varies the covering of animals, according to the temperature of the cli- mate, and the difference of the feafons. Their hairy coat is longer \ and thicker in cc!d countries than in hot; and its growth and '- warmth are evidently increafed, at the approach of winter, in the I chilly regions of the north. Her care of the feathered race is dif- [ played ia a different manner. She gives theinftinftive fmpulfe that makes them anticipate the rigours of winter,and wing their way to milder climates. Though men cannot fhift their places of abode at every feafon with the facility of birds of paffage, they can profit by the example of nature's kind protection of other animate, and can accommodate their cloatiiing to the fenfible changes of the fea- fon and weather. Let it not be fuppofed that I am for recommending thofe peri- odical fafhions of drefs which are regulated by tne dates of an al- manac, in fuch a country as ours, where the weather is fo change- able, where the clofe of autumn is frequently fevere, and where, • not only in the fpring, but even after the commencement ot the r fummer months, we may-fay in the words of the poet, That; winter oft at eve refunds the breese. Chills the pale morn, and bid* hi« driving fleets Deform the «ay delightiels. I fhould be ftill lefs inclined to encourage a ridiculous attention to every little change of the air and weather, as indicated by b.tr- * ometers and thermometers. Our fenfes will afford us all neceffary information on this head, without the aid of mathematical inftru- ments It is only in cafe of confiderable tranfitions from heat to cold, or the contrary, that our feelings will direft us to guard againft danger, by fuitable changes of apparel. Poor nervous, del- icate beings, are affected bv a pafkag cloud, or a (hiftrag breath of 55& ADriCE TO^MOTHERS. j wind. But my rules are intended for healthy children, habituated to the cold bath every morning', and thus prepared to bear with- j cut uneafinefs or injury, any flight variations of the weather which may take place in the courfe ofthe day. Yet even fuch childrtn , are not to be expofed in thin cottons to the keen aftion of the win*4 ter's cold, nor fmothere.l with wooliens in the fultry heat of fum-*« mer. Both thofe manufactures, which are carried to great perfec- tion in England, are well fuited to the different feafons. But I muft obferve, that fine linen is at all times the nioft proper covering next the fkin on every part, except the feet ard legs, ft r the reafons', before mentioned ; it fufficienty increafesdnterred warmth, with- out any unneceffary ftimulus, or difogreeaole friftion. Particular infirmities, or a defective perfpiration natural in old age, may ren- der flannel or fleecy hofery advifable; but Mm n next the /kin is beft . fuited to early life, and requiresvlittle trouble to ke-p it always clean. ■* , The upper parts pf the human, frame do pot ftartd in need of much covering. Nature takes care of tie head ; fo that even the thin cap, recommended at an infant's birth, becomes wholly un- neceffary, either by day or by night, in three or four months.— When children are taken out, according to my f rner intimation,. a light, eafy hat, made of ftraw or beaver-fur, is very proper; and" if the under-fide of the brim be died grew, it vill afford a pleafing. relief to the eyes, and prevent the injurious effefts of too glaring a light. The pink or vivid colours, fometimes ufed by females in the linings of their hats or bonnets, may give for the moment a feem* iag frefhnefs to the cheek, but muft very much impair the fight by their dazzling brilliancy. We fhould not apply any covering whatever to the necks of young perfons of either fex. When they grow up, in order to avoid oeing pointed at for Angularity, they may preferve fome lit- tle fhow of conforming to fafhion, but without checking the circu- lation info dangerous a part. ' Females maft never be induced to wear tight necklaces ; nor muft males brace their collars, or ufe any ftiffening in their flocks or cravats, through weak compliance with' the whim of the day. Even keeping the neck very warm, though without any clofe preffure, increafes its delicacy, or rather its fenfi- bility, and renders it fufceptibls of cohl upon the leaft expofure. .The fleevcs of frocks, gowns, and coats, fhould be made loofe, t to leave the motion cf the arms perfectly free and unconfined.— Though gloves are imneceffarv except in very cold wea'her, yet I have not any ftrong objection to their ufe, provided they flip on eafily, and are made of porous materials, to facilitate the evapora- tion of the perfpirable matter. I fhall conclude thefe obfervations on drefs, with a picture of famionable'abfurdities given in the laft edition of my " Domeftic Medicine." It is riot from an over-weening fondnefs for my own remarks, that I occa(jonally refer to, or make extrafts from that work; but as fome of the points there touched upon are*here more fully difcuffed, it would be the effeft of falfe delicacy on my part; ADVICE TO MOTHERS. 157 to oipprefs now any thing ufeful or pertinent which then occured to me. * After having applauded th ■ judicious reforms of female drefL for their attention to health, fi ■ n!i:iry, and realelegince, I expreff- ed fome concern at uot being able to pay my own-fex the fam- ; compliment: ■" An affectation,'" I cbkrved, :" of what is called •military fmartnefs, feems to hive converted their whole apparel in- to a fyftem of .bandages. The hat i> as tl^ht as if it was intended for a helmet, or to defy th" fury of a hurric.the.
