Blotches and eruptlor.s in the face, head-ac'o, apoplexies, and fad- den deahs, riay be often traced to to.is onife ; and if we view its effefts in another lhit, we fhali rot be furprifed at any inconfift- encv in the language or conduct of people, who take fo much pains to fufpend all interccurfe between the head and the heart.'* " The clofe preffure,'" 1 added, " of the other articles of drefs is equally reprehenfi le. Narrow fleeves are a great check upon the mufcular exercife of the arms. The waiftcoat, in its prefent form mav be very properly termed a firait one, and no doubt is, in many inftances, an indication of fome mental derangement. The wrifts and knees, but more particularly the latter, are braced with ligatures or tight burtouing ; and the legs, which require the ut- moft freedom of motion, are ferewed into leathern cafes, as if to [ convey an idea that the wearer is fometimes mounted on horfe- | back. To complete the whale, and in order that the feet may be Jcept in as tight a prefs as the head, when ihoes are to be worn,Jhe fhape of the foot and the eafy expanfion of the toes are never con- fuite*d,-but fafhion regulates the form of the fhoe, fometimes iquare toed, more frequently pointed, and always fure to produce crimps and corns, fhe keen, the fenfible announcers of every change cf the weather. 1 have ib long employed lerious argument upon thefe. fuhj-cts in vain, tl at I am now" accuftomed to view them with ple«lantry ; anJ when I meet with fuch figures difguifed, and ren- dered truly aukward b}tfc in .their motions and appearance, I can- noyt help thinking with Shakespeare, "that fome cf Nature sjour- ne men had made them, and not made them well, they imitate humanity fo abominably.' SECTION IV. , Of the Injury done to Children by the too early and unnetejary life of Med- icines. Of all the abfurdit;ts that prevail in the treatment of infants, there is none fogrofslv repugnant to common feidevas the frenzy et giving them phyfk before we give them food. Tmey fcarcely be- gin to breathe, when fome purgative ilop is for|ed down their throats, and the tender ftomach and bowels are t!»wn into a ftate of the moft unnatural irritation. It ofte^^pearM to me very 55* ADVICE TO MOTHERS. ftrange, how people came to think that the firft thing given to a child fhould be drugs ; but after duly ccnfideriog the matter, I 1 perceived it to be tie effect of fuperficial knowledge. The more € examined this point, the more I was ftruck with the truth of the phiiofopher's remark, that mere ignorance hath never done any material . injury ; that error alone is deftrucliye ,• and that we do not err in things we. ' arc profeffedly ignorant of, but in thofe which we conceive we know. To begin with niedicines at the birth, is a ftrong illuitration of the mif- , chief of conceited fkiU. It would never enter into the minds of perfons wholly unac- quainted with medical fcience, that evacuations ought to precede the fir ; fupplies of nourifhment. But a little fmattering of phyfic gave rife to the idea of cleanfing the firft paffages at foon as poffible, in or 'er to bring away the black, vifcid, fyrup-like fubftance con- tained in the inteftines of a new-born infant. The fallacy of fuch a fuggeftion can ody appear upon a more accurate and compre^ henfive view of the fubjeft. In tne firft place, the meconium, as it is called, generally paffes foon after the birth* without any excitement but the mere effort of . nature. When this does not take place, every defirable purpofe is fure to be effefted by the thin, waterifh, and purgative quality ofi the mother's milk. Do yosi fuppofe that any chemical procefs can equal this ? Or do you imagine that the retention of the meconium for a few hours, can do half as much mifchief, as your oils and your fyrups, your indigeftible or your acrimonious trafh, muft oc- cafion ? But it was enough for midwives and nurfes to hear phy- fidans, who knew very little more of the matter than themfelves- |£ prefer bine; things of an opening nature to purge off the remains of the meconium. This acquifition of imaginary fcience was too flat- tering to female vanity, not to be difplayed upon every occafion ;\ acd many a fevere twinge have poor infants fuffered, from a mid-" wife's defire to fhew her profound fkill in'phyfic. I was once fent for by an intimate friend, to look at a new- born infant who appeared to be in great agony. 1 foon difcovered that tne complaint was the belly-ache, caufed by fome injudicious purgative. As the midwife was prefent, I remonftrated with her on the raftmefs of thus tampering with an infant's delicate confti- tution. She repfed iu a tone of felf-fufficiency and furprife, " Good God ! Doctor, I only gave the proper phyfic to bring away the economy." I fhould have fmiled at her* affectation of medical cant, and het ridiculous attempt to catch at the found of the word meconium, had not the ierious mifchief fhe had done fuppreffed every emotion of laughter, T reprimanded her in very pointed terms, •aud made her feel the bumitg blufhes of confufion, when I fhew- ed that poifon was as likely to be ufe 1 for phyfic, as economy for the word meconium, from the fame impulfe of conceited folly. But the whole blame in fuch cafes is not, as I have already in- timated, to be hid to the charge of midwives and nurfes ; the fac- ulty themfelves have paid too little attention to the medical treat- Trent of children ; and, in confequence of their fuperficial knowl- edge of thefe important fubjefts, haye f anftioned errors of the moft ADVICE TO MOTHERS. -559 fatal tendency. I once heard a medical profeffor of great celebrity fay, that he had met with a cafe, where the meconium was not brought away for three months after the child's birth, and then only by means of ftrong draftic purges. Though one of the firft anatomifts in Europe, he was led into this miftake by the blackifti colour of the child's ftools, which, for want of practical obfervation ao0 experience, he could not account for but by aicribing it to the fuppofed remains of the meconium. There is nothing fo abfurd, fays an ancient writer, wlvich has not been uttered by fome philofopher. 1 am f rry to add, that a fimilar affertion might be made with ftill greater truth concerning the profeffors of phyfic It would be well, however, if the idea^oft e neceflity of giving medicines to children, was confined to one op " ing dofe to purge off the meconium. Unfortunately, the error committed at the birth is repeated again and again ; and feldom ceafes but with the poor creature's life. Opiates are deemed neceflary to make it fleep ; carminatives to expel wind, or to cure the gripes ; laxatives and emetics to cleaHfethe ftomacli; and ten thoufand other unavailing and pernicious contrivances to relieve complaints, which are entire- ly the effefts of bad nurfing, and which admit of no remedy but by a complete reform in that department. When a medical man is fent for to attend an infant, his firft (, duty is to inquire into the conduct of the nurfe; and if there are faults, to have them rectified. He will feldom find occafion to prefcribe any thing elfe. There cannot be a greater error than to fuppofe that the faults of nurfes may be repaired by drugs. Medi- cine, however fltillfully adminiftered, cannot fupply the place of proper nurfing ; and when given without fkiU, which I fear is too often the cafe, it muft be productive of much mifchief. The fol- * lowing fafts will place this matter in the cleareft light. About forty years ago when I undertook the charge of a laige branch of the. Foundling hofpital at Ackworth in Yorkfhire, I found that the children at nurfe had till then been attended by the country apothecaries, who, fure of being paid for their drugs, al- ways took care to exhibit them with a liberal hand. Every cup- board and every fhelf in the houfe was filled with phials and gaUi- oots. Under fuch treatment, half the children died annually. As it was evident to me, that this mortality could not be natural, 1 fuggefted to the governors, that the children had little or no occa^ fion for medicines, and that with proper care they would thrive and do well. A new arrangement took place. The nurles were tor-- bidden, at their peril, to give any medicine but what ihould be -ordered by me ; aod were advifed to rely more on the faithful dil- *fcharge of their duty than on dofes of phyfic. The coniequence was, that the expenfe for drugs did not amount to a hundredth part of what it had been before, and that not above one in fifty pf the children died annually. An opportunity of ^.kin&.e^e^er^ on fo extenfive a fcale feldom occurs. I had at that nme the fole fuperintendance of an immenfe number of chi dren fpread over a fine healthy country, where the nurfes found it their mtereft to do in every refpeft what I defired, as they loft their appointment m 56» ADVICE TO MOTHERS. 1 cafe of the leaft neglect. The happy refult of the plan left no doubt j of its pr priefy. It was theory verified by praftice. j A little r flection would foon fatrfy an attentive obferver of fl nature, that fhe never defign^d the young of any fpecies to be .! brought up by the aid of medicine. Other an'mals, following the • r1 guidance of inftioft, oeverfail in-this important bufinefs ; but man ,- becomes in all things the creature of art, and is milled by it. I ^ have frequently met with inftances of families who had loft every , child while they trufted to phyfic and employed the faculty, but who at length becoming wife through defpair, and confi Bering that their offspring could only die, left off the ufe of medicine altogeth- * er, and from that time rie.A loft a fingle child. If we wiih for a more general illuftrark>n of the effefts of thofe two different modes > of treatment, we^fljjift'iiad it in that part of the ifland where 1 was born (i>Jor?h Brita: -■) an) wh§re .'ae common people have a ftrong and very juft avrfi n t% ^rig thfir children medicines. The fruits of thnr g* tr i fenfe -u e-.<\ fplayed in a mmerous and healthy progeny. But pu-ir-ti, jiekneis, and death, find their way, in company with the a* i'eneiatt rhe houfes of parents of higher rank. As the children of the latter are often obferved not to thrive, the common remark is, No wonder I they gave the poor things phyfic. It is indeed -poffible, that cafes may occur to juftify the ufe of *o medicines ; butfhisv^y feldom happens when children are.prop-- * erly nurfed, unlefs "the poor creatures may have inherited debility frcm the enervated conftitutions of their parents. 1 may go farther and aflert, that even when the frequent or continued ufe of medi- • cines is deemed neceffary, a child kept in exiftence by the help of , drugs has little reafon to thank its parents for preferving its life.— It lives o^ly to be a burthen to fociety ; and never can be faid to t enjey life fo much as to render the pofl flion of it a blefling. lo all other cafes of flight and accidental indifpc lition, I do not hefitate * to give a decided opinion, that medicines do injury leaft twenty times for once that they do good. A late writer on the management of children (Nelscn) thinks ' it a matter'of regret that they can feldom be brought to take phyfic with nit force. When I confider the almoft infinite number" 6f young martyrs to medicine, inftead of lamenting the circumftarice here ftated, 1 rejoice at it, from the fulleft conviction, that if chil- dren had no reluftance. to fwallow drugs, we fhould lofe a great many more of them. I know it is a common practice with many mothers, to lay a child on its back, to ftop its nofe, and force the medicine down its throat. This is adding the danger of fuft'ocation, , and the certainty of difguft, to the hazard of a dofe too often in it$» own nature Injurious. Bribing and coaxing children, as foon as they become fufceptible of fuch impreflions, are almoft equally bad. Telling a child, that if it will take its phyfic, it fhall have a reward, is informmglt before-hand, that the potion is unpleafant; and, after that, the child is fure to refufe it, be it rendered ever' fb pala- te le. V here medicine is abfolutely neceffary, which, as I faid before, is very feldom the cafe, it may be fo contrived as to make a part of the child's food. Befides, a child fhould be accuftomed very • ADVICE TO MOTHERS. c6i early to refufe nothing'; and it will not refufe to take medicine.— It will aft from habhual fubmifli m to authority, not from the : cruel impulfe of force, or the pernicious allurement of a bribe. I cotild here point out raanv eafy contrivances to mike chil- dren take phyfic, were I not af/ired that they are already too often #<poifoned by it. If drugs do not direftly produce infirmity, dii- eafes, or death, thefe are-fure to be ultimately the confequence of fubftituting medicyte in the place of proper nurfing, and fooiifidy fuppofing that the f srmer c n fupply the defects of the latter. Art opens all her ref urces in vain; nor can the greateft efforts of hit- man iofee- uity make amends f r the want of g--xH air, cleanlinefs, v * healthy breaft eoilk, wh< lefome f od, and proper exercife. The ne- ; gleft of any of thefe effential points is attended with irreparable mif- chief; and 'ii the contrary, a due attention to thefe precludes the neceffity of any medical aid. Whenever any Co2 ADJ ICE ivy Juiyi;ii:AO. of their children appear indifpofed, or do not feem to thrive, which \ muft be frequently the cafe where they are fo badly nurfed, away the mothers run to the apothecary. His candor is too often check- ed, and even his judgment is liable to be biaffed by his immediate \ intereft. He derives his fupport from the fale of his drp*gs, and will feldom refill the temptation to fend large fupplies wh re he knows the parents are in a condition to pay. Phyfic, in a variety of forms, is fubftituted for the only rational means of reftoring the child's health, fome neceffary change of air, exercife, cloathing, or diet; the mifchief begun by the nurfe is completed by the doctor ; and death comes fooner or later to put an end to the fufferings of the tortured victim. It gives me pain to write any ftrict-ares on the interefted views and reprehenfible practice of even the lower orders of the faculty; but the evil is of fuch magnitude, and fo truly alarming, that it cannot be paffed over in filence, nor mentioned without burfts of . ftrong indignation. The weaknefs and the fears of mothers bring m "the apothecary ; and it requires an effort, to which not one in a thoufand is equal, to get him out again. A bold bufy man of that profeffion wants only a few timid mothers to make his fortune.— But, mercy on the poor babes who, to make his chariot roll, muft fwallow drugs every day I Yet, fuch is the infatuation of mothers, that, if this be not done, they think their children neglected, and difmifs one apothecary to make way for another, who adminifters' * medicines with a more liberal, or rather a more deftruftive hand. If the apothecary be a dangerous man, the quack is ftill more fo. Yet I hardly ever knew a mother or nurfe who had not by her the noftrum of fome quack, with which fhe every%ow and then kept doling the infant. Were the boafted fpecific, like the anodyne necklace, a mere chip in porridge, it would do no harm to the child, and would ferve only to amufe the mother, and to levy a contribution on her credu- lity. But it is very often made up of aftive ingredients, which ought to be adminiftered with the greateft circumfpecticn. Moft of thenoftrums given to children are ftrong opiates or purgatives, of a nature very different from the innocent efficacy of a good nurfe's lullabies. They may quiet or compofe the infant, and feem to give it eafe for a time, but they never fail to deftroy the powers of digeftion, and to induce univerfal debility, with all its baneful confequew es. There is, however, another clafs or defcription of quack medi- cines, which, though they cannot kill with greater ultimate certain- ty than the former, are more fudden and violent in their fatal mode of operation, I mean the cakes and powders, and various other compoiitions, which are advertifed for the pretended cure of worms. A child's pale fcoks frighten the mother into a belief that worms are the caufe; aud fhe goes immediately to the wormUotlor, who adminifters his draftic dofes, without the leaft regard to the delica- cy of the patient's conftitution. His fole aim is to expel worms; and if any appear, he triumphs in the fhow cf fuccefs, though al- ways attended with great danger, and fometimes with death. I ADVICE TO MOTHERS. , 563 have known a noftrum of this kind to kill in twenty-four hours;— but that *as nothir.g to the quack ; he had fold his medicine ; and he gave himfelf little concern about the injury it might do in par- ticular cafes. 1 would not have faid fo much of th's fhccking indifference to murder, had I not feen proofs of it, and in fome too, who preteoded to eminence in that line. I once told a lady, that her daughter was in a deep confumption, and that fhe ought to go into the country, to take exercife on horfeback, drink affes' milk, and ufe a light . reftorative diet. But inftead of following this advice, fhe took her daughter to a very celebrated v/orm dcftor, who foon relieved her from all her trouble. Here I cannot help lamenting that confidence in worm-powders or worm-cakes is not confined to the'*weakef t of the fair fex, but is edifcovered even in men of rank and'talents. I have feen, though with heart-felt concern, names of the firft refpeftability fubfcribed to the certificates of the efficacy of fome of thefe noftrums. I am ,,. far from queftioning the integrity of the perfons who figned fuch papers; but they certainly knew not what they did. They fancied ; they were only attefting a plain matter of fact, though the thing was far beyond the poffible reach of their judgment or knowledge. They had feen a quack medicine given to a child, and had alfo feen worms afterwards voided by that child. What then ? As the fame effect might be produced by very dangerous poifons, how cculd '.. people, wholly ignoranr of the ingredients, tell whether the-operation afcribed to them was not at the rifk of the child's conftitution, or» » of its life ? Even fuppofing that fome apparent good and no percepti- ble mifchief attended the experiment in cne cr two cafe?, are they fufficient grounds for the general recommendation of any fecret medicine, to which the lives of thoufands of children yet unborn may be facrificed ? I hope thefe remarks will prevent men of charac- ter from rafhly giving a fahction to the poffible deceptions of quack- ery, and will alfo leffen the refpect which individuals or the public at large may feel for fuch inconfiderate teftimonials. To refume now my detail of various inftances of maternal weaknefs ; I have to obferve, that the ftrangeft, and not the leaft mifchievous infatuation of all, confifts in giving medicines to chil- dren in good health, with the filly view of preventing difeafes. The fpring and fall are the periods confecrated to phyfic ia the calender of mothers and nurfes. At thofe feafons, if children are ever fo well, they muft have a dofe or two of what is falfely called an in- nocent purge, to keep them pure and hearty. Thus they are made really fick, for fear they fhould become fb ; and their conftitutions are enfeebled by the perverfe means employed to ftrengthen them. I have already faid fo much on the bad confequences which muft refult from the ufe of laxatives, efpecially in childhood, that no far- ther diffifefives againft fo abfurd a practice feem neceflary, except this one remark ; that purging, like bleeding, induces a habit which cannot be left off with fafety. Every purge paves the way for •m th^r rill the bowels are deftroyed. Such medicines tnerefcre fhould never be adminiftered but in cafes of actual lllnels. end to expel fome greater poifon than themfelves. cj5:\ ADVICE TO MOTHERS. As this is a point which cannot be too ftrongly enforced, I » ihalllay before the reader Mr. Locke's fentiments on the fane fub- '«Y ;eft. They derive double weight from his medical fkill, and from. Jj the extraordinary precifion of his manner cf reafoning upon any ropic. As he was regularly bred to phyfic, he i? exempt frcm the lutpicion iocured by fome later phiiofophers, of having written under the influence of prejudice agamft the ficulty. " Perhaps," fays ho, " it will be exoefte 1 that I fhould give fome directions of phyfic, to prevent difeafes ; for which I have ot.ly this -one, very facredly to be thftrved^ never to give children any phyfic for prevention. i he obferva- tion of what i have already advifed will, i fuppofe, do that better, than the ladies' diet drinks, or apothecaries medicines. Have a f.reat care of tampering that way, left, inftead of preventing, you draw on difeafes. Nor even upon every little iodifpofition in phyfic to be coven, or toe phyfician to be called to children, efpecially if he be a bufy man, that will preiently fill their windows with gallipots, and their ftomachs v/ith drugs. It is fafer to leave t:>em wh-My to nature^ than to pat them into the hands of cne forward to tamper, rr that thinks children are to be cured, in ordiuary diftempers, by any thing but diet, or a method very Utile differing from it; it feemirg fuitable both to my reafon and experience, that the tender conftitutions of children fhould have as little done to them'.as is pcffible, and as the abfolute neceflity of the cafe requires." ' To add any thing by war cf comment or illuftration to lan- guage at once fo clear and fo forcible, would betray the greateft weaknefs. It is.enough for roe to quote fo unexceptionable an authority in fupport of my favourite deftrine. The chief defign of the prefent treatife is to Otpereede the ufe of medicines in early life, and to fhew how health may be effectually preferved by go :q. nurfing alone. An attention to the rules here laid down is the only method of preventing difeafes, with which 1 am acquainted.—- A child ufed to the cold bath, and to the full enjoyment cf frefh air, • cannot be liable to coughs, cold?, fore eyes, or defiuxiens. A clean dry fkia,' never relaxed by fculiiefsor heat, will favour the efcape of noxious or redundant humours, while exercife will net fuffer the feeds of corruption to lurk in any part cf the frame. Inftead of baneful phyllc, let your infant have the aliment prepred for him by nature ; and you may be fure that the milk of a healthy, temperate nurfe, will never give him the gripes or the colic; it will ncurifh, but not inflame him ; it will keep the habit pure; the action of the blood regular, and the furface cf the whole body free frcm blotchfs or eruptions-. Indeed, 1 knew of no difeafe againft which a child may hot be fecured by the rational conduct of his nurfe. The predifpoun£\ caufes of all the complaints of infants, are the weak- nefs of all the digeftive powers, and tl^e irritability of the nervous fyftem. Both are obviated by the method I propofe. Tte ftomach is fupplied, but not overcharged, with fuch food as is furred to its ftrength; and every thiog that may irritate the nerves, or give rife to convulfions, is averted with all poffible care. Even in the midft cf contagion, or of epidemical dftempers, the purity of a well- nurfed child's habit will corrcft the malignity of the infection, and difarm ir of its ufual terrors. ADVICE TO MOTHERS. $6$ The earneftnefs with w^ich I have recommended inoculation in another wr>rk, may feem a little inconfiftent with the doftrine here laid down ; but it is becaufe very few children are nurfed according to my plan, that I think it advifeable to guard them all igainft the poflible danger of catching the fmall-pox by accident. Befides, it is cf importance to be able to command time, place, and circum- ftances, particularly as I have fhewn in my " Domeftic Medicine," with what eafe and fafety the operation may be performed by mothers and nurfes without the leaft occafion for any farther medi- cal advice or afiiftance. SECTION V. Of the Food proper for Children. THE pernicious follv of making phyfic precede food at an in- fant's birth is, I hope, fufficiently expofed in the former fection ; and notice is there taken of the admirable manner, in which the thin diluted, and gently opening properties of the mother's milk, are adapted to every medicinal as well as alimeotary purpofe.— Nature does notafford, nor can art contrive, any effectual fubfti- tute for that delicious fluid. By degrees the milk acquires confift- ence, aud affords greater aourifhment to the child, as he becomes more capable of digefting it. At length, his bodily ftrength in- creafing, aud his teeh burfting through the gums, he can take more folid and fubftantial food, which requires ftill greater powers of digeftion. . Thefe changes are fo obvious, that they cannot be miftaken. Ignorance is pleaded in vain, and the leaft deviation from fo plain a road to health, is punifhed with lafting injury.— The infant, after having derived its whole fuftenance and growth* while iu the womb, from the mother's juices,,cannot without the greateft danger have its fupplies totally altered at its birth. It muft ftill be fed from the fame congenial (ource, or the fhock of a fud- den and unnatural change will prove very trying to its tender con- In my advice to mothers at the time of lying-in, I endeavour- • ed to convince them of the imminent danger to their own health, wh'ch would arife from their neglect of the moft facred of allcmties* that of fuckling their children. It is an obligation fo ftrongly en- forced by nature, that no woman can evade the performance of it with impunity. But cheerful obedience to this icvereign law is at- tended with thefweeteft pleafure of which the human heart is fuf-- centible. The thrilling fenfations, as before obferved, that ac- company the aft of giving fuck, can be conceived only by thofe who havefeltthem, while the mental raptures of a fond moth-r at fuch moments are far beyond the powers of defcription or fancy, she timsalloenfures the fulfilment of the promues made by the beft wri ets on this fubjeft-fpeeaV recovery from cmid-bed the firm Xhlifhment of good health, the exquifite fenfe of wedded joys, tit^cS^of bSring more children, the fteady attachment of her hufband theefteem aiid refpeft of the public, the warm returns of Son and gratitude from the objects other tender care, and • a£r Xthefftisfaftiontofeeherdaugh.ter3 follow her example and recom nend it te others. e66 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. Though I expreffed myfelf pretty fully on this head in the place above referred to, yet when I confider it, nev/ ideas arife in my mind, and I am more and more impreffed with a fenfe of its importance. People have been often amufed with illufions of uni- verfal remedies. Long experience has almoft deftroyed my faith in the efficacy of even the beft fpecifics. But were I called upon to point out any one remedy for the greateft part, not only of the dif- eafes, but of the vices alio of fociety, I would declare it to be the ft*, ift attention of mothers to the nurfmg and rearing of their chil- dren. " Wc uld you have mankind return all to their natural du- ties," fays the eloquent Rousseau, in one of his fine fallies of fen- timental enthufiaftn, " begin with mothers of families: you will be aftonifhed at the change this will produce*. Almoft every kind of depravation flows fucceflively from this fource ; the moral order of thiugs is broken, and nature qui e fubverted in our hearts; home is lefs cheerful and engaging ; the affecting fight of a rifing family no more attaches the hufband, nor attracts the eyes of the ftranger; the mother is lefs truly refpeftable, whofe children are not about her ; families are no longer places of refidence ; habit no longer enforces the ties of blood ; there are no fathers, nor mothers, chil- dren, brethren, nor fitters; they hardly know (how fhould they love) each other ? Each cares for no one but himfelf; and when home affords only a melancholy folitude, it is natural to feek diver- fion elfewhere. " But," continues he, " fhould mothers again condefcendto NURSE their chiIdrmn, manners would form themfelves ; the fenti- ments cf nature would revive in our hearts ; the ftate would be re- peopled ; this principal point, this alone, would re-unitt every thing. A tafte for the charms of a domeftic life, is the beft anti- dote againft corruption ot manners. The noife and buftle of chil- dren, which is generally thought troublefome, becomes hence agreeable ; they render parents more neceffary, more dear to each other, and ftrengthen the ties of conjugal affefti< n. When a family is all lively and animated, domeftic concerns afford the moft de- lightful occupation to a woman, and the moft agreeable amufement to a man. Hence, from the correction of this one abufe, will foon refult ageneral reformation ; nature will quickly re-affume all her rights ; let wives but once again become mothers; and the men will prefently again become fathers and hufbands." To this fketch, drawn by the pencil of fo great a mafter, I fhall only add, that the happy confequences of fuch a reform would be no lefs ftriking in a medical than in a moral point of view. A ftop would bs put to the cruel ravages of death in early life. The long catalogue of infantile afHiftious would almcft become a blank, or contain nothing to excite alarm. Every child, invigorated by his mother's milk, would, like the young Hercules, have force fufficient to strangle in his cradle any ferpents that might affail him. Occafional illnefs would be to him only part of a neceffary courfe of difcipliue, to enure him by times to bear pain with manly for- titude. In fhort, health, ftrength, and beauty, would take place of puninefs, deformity, and difeafe ; fociety would be renovated; ADVICE m MOTHERS. & and man, inftead of dwindling away, as he now does, by a grad- ual degeneracy, would foon rife to the original perfection of his nature. If you entertain any doubt of the truth of what is here ad- vanced, look at other parts of the animated creation, and your doubts will immediately vanifh. Wild animals never degenerate ; they bring forth and rear their young with undiminifhed ftrength. And why ? Becaufe the females, obediet t in every thing to the impulfes of nature, nurfe their offspring, and watch over them with the moft tender folicitude, till they can provide for themfelves.— Not only the inhabitants of the howling wildernefs, the fhe-wolf and the fell tygrefs, but even the monfters of the great deep, draw out their breaft and give fuck to their young. Will woman then fuffer herfelf to be ftigmatized as the only unfeeling monfter that can defert the iffue of her own womb, and abandon it to the care of another? Will fhe alone entail the curfe of her unnatural con- duct on her haplefs pofterity ? But let me vindicate the female character from fo foul a re- proach. It is not fo much the fault of the women, as of what is improperly called civilized fociety. In its ruder ftate, this never happened. It never happens among favage nations. I have already mentioned fome remarkable inftances of thoir parental tendernefs. The influence of fo ftrong a priociple can be weakened only by the prevalence of vice, and of artificial refinement. Wherever an in- nocent fimplicity of manners prevail, the children are not brought up by proxy; the women are not fatisfied to be mothers by halves, as an old writer expreffes it—tobring forth, and then eaft off their offspriig. They think with him, that nothing can be more contrary to nature, than fuch an imperfect fort of mother, who, after having nourifhed in her womb, and with her blood, fomething which fhe did not fee, refufes now her breaft-milk to what fhe fees living, be- come a human creature, and imploring the afiiftance of its parent \ In the polifhed, or rather the depraved circles of focial life, thofe fentiments are either unfelt, or difregarded. Women, ener- vated by luxury, allured by a falfe tafte for miftaken pleafure, and encouraged by fhamelefs example, are eager to get rid of their chil- dren as foon as born, in order to fpend the time thus gained from the difcharge of their duty in diffipation or indolence. Let not hufbarids be deceived; let them not expect attachment from wives, who, in neglecting to fuckle their children, rend afunder the ftrongeft ties in nature. Neither conjugal love, fidelity, modefty, chaftity, nor any other virtue, can take deep root in the breaft of a female that is callous to the feelings of a mother. I am aware of the li tie tricks t*-.at are fo often played off by new-married women to keep up the fhow of a wifh to nurfe their children, while every engine is fecretly employed to make the deluded huiband conjure her torelinquifh her defign, for fear of the injury it might do her conftitution. if fhe has not injured her health by vice, nurfing will not leffen, but increafe her ftrength ; and if any con&tutional de- fect renders her wholly unfit for fuckhng her child, we ought to abftain from procreation. The woman who cannot difcharge the 56« ADVICE TO MOTHERS. duties of a mother, ought again and again to be told, that fhe has no right to become a wife. # » In cafes of accidental injury or difeafe, where it maybe impof- fible for the mother, or highly improper on her part, to give the child the breaft, fhe is to be pitied in being thus deprived of the greateft pleafure of life, the pleafure of feeding and rearing her own offspring. But the number of thofe women who really cannot fuckle is very fmall, compared with thofe who will not. The latter excite our ind:g tation—not our pity ; they ftiffe every emotion of ten- dernefs ; they are deaf to the voice of nature ; they facrifice the moft important duty to vicious purfuits; and madly bnrter joys that willpleafe on every reflection, for fuch as never can bear to be recalled. Littfe do thofe diflipated mothers think of what their poor in- fants are likely to fuffer, when committed to the care of oirelings.— Ought thev not to confider, that the woman who parts w;th her own ba'oe to fuckle one of theirs, unlefs fhe is imp lied by the keeneft diftrefs, gives a proof in the firft inftance of her not bemg a gcod mother ? How then is it to be expected that ftV fhould become a good nurfe ? Even fhould fhe acquire, in time and from habit, a tender affection for her fofter-child, ought not a inr.ther of any fen- fibility to take alarm at the idea of having that child's love trmf- ferrea from herfelf to a ftranger r* Indeed, the claims of the nurfe who does her duty faithfully, are greatly fuperior to thofe of the parent who neglects her's. It was a faying of Scipio Afric anus, that he took her to be more his.mother -who had nurfed him for two years, though fhe had not brought him forth, than her who, after fhe had brought htm into the world, deferted and abandoned him. But I am ftill better pleafed with the anecdote related by Van Swieten, of a Queert of France, who gave her fon fuck, and would not defift from fd< doing even when fhe was taken ill of an intermitting fever. It happened during one of the fits, that another matron gave her1 breaft to the thirfty and crying child ; at which the queen was fo much difpleafed, that fhe thruft her finger into the child's mouth, in order to excite a vomiting, being unwilling that another fhould parform any part of a mother's office. I fhall not enlarge any farther on this fubjeft, I hope I have faid enough to excite good mothers to the moft affiduous obser- vance of their duty, and to warn others of the evds infeparabie from thenegleft of it. Such as may refolve to obey the dictates of nature and reafon, will find the following directions of fome ufe in the profecution ©f fo laudable a purp fe. The mother, after delivery, fhould be indulged with a few hours fleep, to recover her from the fatigue which fhe has lately undergone, and to allow due time for the fecretion of the milk, be- fore the infant is put to the breaft. The child can fuffer no incon- venience from this delay. Being replete with blood and juices, he has not the leaft cccafion for any frefh fupply of nutriment, till the mother is prepared by neceffary repofe to give him the grateful and fpontaneous beverage. I before pointed cut the means to be employed when the nipples are not fn^ciently prominent to afford ADVICE TO MOTHERS. 569 a proper hold. But whatever thefor.o of the nipples may be, they ihould be wafhed with a little warm milk and tfater, in order to remove tae bitter vifcid fubftance which is furuifhed round them to defend the tender parts from excoriation. I would rlfo .adviletne mother, during the whole time of her nurfing, to wafh the nipples, immediately after giving fuck, in warm water. When- ever this can be conveniently procured ; and, in cafe the fupplies of tne nutritive fluid are very copious, or feem to exceed the infant's wants, ihe may always prefs out a little of the milk before the child is put again to the breaft, as the firft drops iffuing from the foun- tain at evary treat are the moft liable to fournefis and putrefcency. I need not urge a fond mother freely to give her child what .nature freely produces. The only check in this rcfpeft: is not to fuffer the infant to fleep at the breaft, or to fuck till vomiting enfues. But any attempts to entice the baby to the ufe Of fpoon-meat are ftill more improper. This is a common praftice, not only with • hired nurfes, but even with affectionate inotmrs, from a foolifh though prevalent idea of leflcningthe demands en the breaft, or of ftrengthening the child with additional nourifhment. If the nurfe be not irregular in her own manner of living, fhe need not fear having a plentiful fupply for the infant; and flie may reft affured that her, milk is far better fuited to his young ftomach, and will afford a gueater quantity of nutritious chyle, than any preparation *. which art can devife. • . Another error no lefs prevalent, and more injurious than the former, is the idea that a woman, when nurfing, cannot eat and drink too heartily, as it is termed, to fupport her own ftreng'h and that of the infant. On the contrary, the tainted ftream of intem- perance muft enfeeble and diforder toe child, while the nurfe really lefiensher own power of giving fuck, and invites the attack* of a fever by her thoughth fs indulgence. The ceding rejrimpii before recommended muft be ftriftly complied with for the fife: week after delivery ; and though a mere liberal diet may then be allowed, yet this allowance muft net extend to grofs meats or heating liquids.— A pint of porter or ale twice a-day for at leaft a fortnight more, will be quite.fufficient, and snimal food fhould be very fpariegly ufed for a much longer period. Indeed it would be happy for the children, as well as for their nurfes, if the latter would confine themfelves, without painful reftraint, to the falutary varieties cfa milk and vegetable diet. It is a great miftake to fuppofe, that a nurfe is better fitted for her office by living on animal fubftances ; the reverfe is the truth. The milk of wemen who live wholly on vegetables, is m re abundant in quantity, will keep longer, rd is far fweeter and more wholefome than what is prepared from ani- mal food, which, befides its inflammatory tendency, muft fubject the children to gripes and worms. Thefe remarks are merely defigned to correct feme vulgar errors refpefting the quantity and quality of the aliment moft prop- er for nurfes, but not toimpofe upon any woman theneceiftdy < t a total change from her former and ufual manner of living. I would 1'ave her continue fhe temperate ufe of what fhe has fr.und by *x- fto ADVICE TO MOTHERS. perience to be moft conducive to her health ; and that will alfo agree beft with her child. Her natural appetite may be fafely in- dulged ;buv»glut tony muft be repreffed, and a depraved tafte for u? fpirituous liquors, or high-feafened food, muft never be gratified* It has been juft hinted that the breaft>milk of a woman in good health is abundantly fufficient for an infant's fupporh Nothing elfe fhould enter his lips for at leaft three Or four months after the birth. A little thin pap or panada may then be occafionally^ in- troduced, with a view of familiarifmg it to the child's tafte, and thereby leflening the difficulty and danger of a complete and fud- den alteration at the time of weaning. But no fpices, no wine, no fugar, fhould at any time be mixed with his food or drink. Thefe and the like contrivances of filly women to make an infant's fpoou meat what they c>H palatable an.I nourifhing, are fure to vitiate his - natural tafte, to inflame his blood, and to fill the ftomach with flime and acidities. Sugar, in particular, has another very bad effeft : its frequent ufe noc'only gives children a difrelifh for a wholefome Simplicity, but entices them to fwallow more than t>ey otherwife would, or than they want, and thus makes gluttons of them even before they can be ftriftly faid to eat. Infants are commonly deprived of the breaft too foon. What < people call folid food is fuppofed to contribute more to their growth and health. But, in the firft place, milk, though a fliud** is imme- diately converted into a folid fubftance in the ftomach, where it is-' foon after digefted, and then affords the* beft nutriment poffible.— It aMb appears contrary to nature to put folid fubftances into the mouth of a child, before it is furnifhed with teeth to chew them.— I fhould therefore look upon the previous cutting of the teeth as the fureft indication of the proper time for weaning children. I do not mean to lay this down as an invariable rule. The ftate of the nurfe's health, as well of the child's, fhould be duly confidered. It > feems only that the cutting of the teeth gives a fort of hint of the ufe to which they may be applied. It is farther remarkable that, during the continuance of this ufually fharp and painful operation, children, as it were inftinftively, carry every thing that is put into their hands up to their mouths. Give them on fuch cccafions crufts of bread, pieces of bifcuit, dried fruits, or, frefh liquorice* •' root, wfrch ihey may fuck and chew. Corals, glafs, and the like hard bcdies, are verv improper, as they will eith°r bruife the gums and caufe an mflaramaticn, or make them hard and callous by continual rubbing, fo as to render the cutting of the teeth ftill j more difficult, and the pain more acute and lafting. I A few weeks before the intended time of weaning-, that is to fay, in the interval between the firft fymptoms of cutting the teeth and the appearance of at leaft four of them, fpoon-meat fhould be given more frequently, and in grea'er quantity, reducing in the like degree the proportion of breaft-milk, till the gradual increafe of the one nd diminution of the other render, the change almoft imper- ceptible. The beft fpoon-meat that I knowy confifts of bread and milk, prepared in the manner pointed ouUin my Domeftic Medi- cine ; that is, firft boiling the bread in water, afterwards pouring the ADVICE TO MOTHERS. tft water off, mod then mixing with the bread a proper quantity of new m"'lk uncded. I there obferved, that milk ufed this ,way was mo-e wholefome and nourifhing than when boiled, and was lefs liable to occafion coftivenefs. **■ it is not neceflary, however, to confine children, after they are weaned, to one particular fort of food. The bill of fare may be 'gradually enlarged with the child's growth, provided always that it ^con^ft of an innocent- variety. He may have bread and milk at one time, bread puddiog at another, and bread fliced in broth, or in the gravy of roaft meat, diluted with water, now and then, till •atlength his teeth being properly grown, and fit to chew meat it- felf,-he may be allowed a little of it at dinner, with a due pr por- tion of bread and of wholefome vegetables. But I muft forbid ia - thetnoft pofitive manner any artificial fweetening of his food, all Jpicesor feafbning, except felt, all forts of paftry, butter in every form,unripe fruits, and fermented liquors. As 1 h eve great reliance on the difcretion of good mothers, f when well informed of their duty, I fhould be forry to tire them , by too manv details, or to fetter them by unneceffary refrraints ; ■ 1 fhall theref re only add one caution more on this part of the fub- "" jeft,and that is, not to adopt the pernicious cuftom of giving food or drink to children during the night. Even in the courfe of the > day* they fhould not be crammed every hour, and trained up in , tNerhabits of early gluttony. Temperance is that fure order vative pf health, which they cannot be taught to practice too foon. Let them eat freely at proper intervals; and the longer they are kept 'irom the tnings already forbidden, the more rapidly will taey thrive, and tiie greater number of difeafes will they efcape. c As I have admitted that cafes may occur, in wmch it would beimpoflible or improper for a mother to fuckle'her own child, I fhall flipped a few hints on the choice of a nurfe, and th^ remain- ing duties cf the parent. Whtn 574 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. y-u take him into the fields, which you fhould do every clay in fine we ther, iet mm roll upon the dry grafs; and, when in the nur-"] itry; upon the carpet. He will foon learn the ufe of his legs, with- oe t the leaft poffipility of making them crooked by the preffure of'-' fo ught a body. When he begins to walk, you muft help him a •( littk in r is firft experm.enls ; lead him about with the fupport * of '- your hands, and then by the finger only, till you perceive he can do without your affiftat>ce. Go-carts and leading-firings not only retard he increafe,cf a child's activity, and produce an awkward- ntis of gait very hard to be corrected afterwards, but often affect the cheft, lungs, and bowels, in fuch a manner as to pave the way for nabitual indigeftion or coftivenefs, and for afthmatic or confump- live complaints. . » Nothing can be more ridiculous than the numberlefs contrv' vances of mothers to teach their children to walk, as if it was a thing tb be learned by their inftruction ; and to keep them propped up by wooden machines, or fufpended by back-firings, as if their. live* and limbs were to be endangered by the leaft tumble. Thtu are too near the ground, and too light tonurt themfelves by falling. Befides, the oftener they fall, the fooner they will learn, when down to get up again; and the only way to make them fure-footed, is to accuftom them bet'mes to truft more to the proper manage- ment of tneir own legs, than to any artificial fupport. As to the beft time for exercife during infancy, it admits of a very fimple regulation. That fort of paffive exercife, which con- fifts of agreeable motion in a nurfe's arms, muft never' be omitted after the ufe ofthe bath in the morning, and cannot be too often repeated in the courfe of the day. But when the child is abfe to take exerc fe himfelf, it will be eafy to manage matters fo as to let fnm have as much as he ljkes before meals, and never to rouz- him into aftion upon a full ftomach. A healthy thriving child fleeps more than two-thirds of his time for a few weeks after ms.birth. Nuries, indeed, are too apt, for their own eafe, or to gain time for other concerns, to cljer fo the ileepy difpofition of infanis, and to increale it by various tbmgs of a ftupefactive quality. Galen mentions the propriety of placing .Chiloren to fleep in IcBulis pendentibus, or hanging little beds; and the reafon for fuch' a contrivance is thus exolained with great clearnefs " and fimpkcity, by Van Swieten .- 6i As thefoetus,^ fays this accurate obferver of nature,u hang- lly fha.ken this way hence it has been rea- in fuch a vibrating motion. ^ Tney have therefore been hid in cradles, that they might enjoy this gentle exercife, and. be more and more ftrengtheced.— ^ oDiilyexperience teaches us, fiat the worft-tempered children are *.- foothed by this motion, and at laft fink into a fweet fleep. But the *• fhaking or the cradles fhould be gentle and uniform ; on which ac- count, thofe cradles thai hang by cords are the beft of all, as they may by a >. flight force be moved equably, and without any noife. At the ^dfame time, the motion communicated to thefe cradles is impercepti- bly diminifhed, and at laft ceafes without any fhock." •';,. In England, as wTell as in moft other parts of Europe, cradles ^ fixed upon wooden rockers, have been in ufe from time immemorial. No evil could arife from tneir continuance, while in the hands of eareful and affectionate mothers ; but, when left to the manage- „ ., ment of impatient nurfes, or of giddy boys and girls, the delicate texure of an infant's brain would often be expofed to great dan- f ger. The agitation of a cradle by fuch perfons has been compared !"'* to the jolting of a ftage-coaeh bafket; and I believe that a poor . child would luffer as much from the one as from the other, were he not a little more confined in the former. It is poflible to con- ceive a more fhocking object than an ill -tempered nurfe, who? in- ftead of foothicg the accidental uneafinefs or hdifpofition to fleep of her baby, when laid down to reft, is often worked up to toe higheft pitch of rage ; and, in the excefs of her folly and bru:a!ity, endeavours, by loud, harfh threats, and the impetuous rattle cfthe ' cradle, to drown the infant's cries, and to force him into flumber ! ' She may fometimes gain her point, but never till the poor victim's '.* ftrength is exhaufted. „."'', ,. „ To guard againft this evil, the tranfition from rocking-cradles to fixed bedfteads was not neceflary. ri he gentle motion before " ' defcribed, at once fo natural and fo pleafing to infants, may be given them with eafe and fafety in little bafkets fufpended by cords, as ufed in the Highlands of Scotland und-r the name of creels, or in the more elegant contrivances of fuming cots, which are now r-{' coming into fafhion. 1 am forry to fee any of she Fitter furround- *' ed with clofe curtains, which have almoft as bad an effeft as confin- ing the infant in a room of the fame diraenfions. CHAP. V.. OF DWARF1SHNESS AND DEFORMITY. . X HE chief caufes of defects in the fize and form cf Chil- dren, have been occafionally touched upon in the preceding chap- ters ; but t e prevalence of fuch evils, and the lamentable confer quences with which 'hey are followed, require to be more fully and diftinftly, c; nfidered. I muft not weaken th" ioflnence of im, portant truths by fupprefling any part of them, or by leaving them two widely fca'tered. I muft fhake offthe reftraints of falfe deli- cacy, and by candidly poir ting out the grand fource of fo many private and public calamities, endeavour to prevail on parents to adopt th- moft effectual remedy. Let not the faireft part of the cre-tior. be offeoded with me for faying, that, in all cafes of dwarf ifhoefs and deformitv, ninety-nine out of a hundred are owing to the foily, mifcenduct or neglect of mothers. The following re- marks are act written in the fpirit of reproach, but with a view to the moft dcfirable reform. h would be d;ffi- ult to mention any thing in which-.-fociety is fo -Veplv 'ot-rffted, as in the proper union of the fexes. This has cfter engoo'd ' e ettention of leciflators, and irarriages have been prohibited in various difeafes and perfonal difqualifications. We have even an inftance upon record, where the community interpo- fed, when d gener«cy in the royal line was likely to be the confe. quefice of their king's mjudicious choice of a wife. Hiftory tells *fc*j that the Lacedaemonians condemned their king Archidamus for jl2Jrj[CE^TO MOTHERS^ 577 paving married a weak, puny woman ; " becaufe," faid they, " in- r 4«ad ot propagatiog a race of heroes, you will fill the throne with U progeny of changelings." t 1 am aware that any checks on the liberty of individuals in their matrimonial contracts, would be deemed inconfiftent with the freedom of the ]Briti(h conftitution ; and indeed, it is ftrange that r. laws fhould be cec, flary to convince men, that health and form i ,are, or ought to»be, p. werfulconfi derations in the chciceof a wife. Every part of animated nature proclaims aloud, that like begets like s tand though a puny, dwarfifh, or distorted woman, may become a ^" mother, it will often be at the rifk of h- r own life, and always with a certainty of tranfmitting fome of her infirmities to' her innocent and ill f. ted offspring. But the inheritance of parental weaknefs arid deformity is one of thofe curfts which argument or expoftulation cannot avert.— The voice of reafon is difregarded, aud objects of natural defire are ,overlo ked,' by avarice and pride. I fhall therefore confine my ob- \fervations to fuch evils as may be prefumed to admit of a cure, becaufe they arife rather from error and folly, than from depravity : or wilful perverfenefs. It feems to be the natural wifh of every pregnant woman to bring forth ftout, healthy, and beautiful infants. Yet, Mr. Locke did not hefitate to afferr, that, if mothers had the formation of their own children in the womb, we fhould fee nothing any where but deformity. The fstus is happily placed in better hands, and under ," the guardian care of nature. But topugh it cannot be new-moul4.- •ed, altered in its fhape, or disfigured by the mere fancies and capri- cious delires of the mother, it may fuffer no lefs injury -from her ignorance, her folly, or mifconduft. 1 hope I made it fufficiently evident in my cautions to women during pregnancy, thou the foetus may not only be checked Ukits growth, but marked alfo and .dif- torted by tight or heavy preffure on the womb—by ftays, girdles, or the like improper ligatures. . In vain does nature provide for the eafy and gradual enlargement of the embryo, if her benignant pur- pofes are counteracted by the bracing reftraints cf a filly mother's * After the birth, rs I before obferved, fill grea'er danger awaits the infant from attempts to mend his fhap.—to keep his head and hmbs in proper form—and to fecure him asamft accident. The worft accident that can befai him is far lefs alarming than the certain confequences of fuch prefumptuous improvements ar.d ill- directed care. He becomes puny, ftunted, deformed, difeafed ; and though perhaps eaft " in nature's happieft mould," is fure to be fpoiled by the disfiguring touch of man. I have already explained ihe fatal effefts of meddling with the foft bones of an infant's fknll at the birth ; of confining them by anv check ; or covering them too warmly. I fhewed how wonder. fuiiv the pliancy of thofe bones was contrived toyie d to obftruc, tions for the purpofe of promoting eafy and fafe delivery and af- terwards to refume of themfelves their proper place and form ir they had been fqueezed together in the ad of parturition, 1 alio £7S ADVIUFTTO MOTHERS. infifted on the importance of a thin and light cap, that the air may -^ x*ft upon them freely, to render them hard and compact, and of i courfe fitter to defend the braio from cold or any external injury. .? But while midwives aod nurfes are fuffered to purfue a . contrary^ 3 plan, we need not wonder at meeting with fo many inftances cf . early convulfions, of idiotifm, and of heads misfhapen, infirm, or ftilceptibleofc'Id upon exp~fureto the leaft breath of air. 1 was no left earneft in my-cautions againft the ufe of banda- ges, or of opprefiive covering for any other part of the tender fraoae. I did not m ;goify the danger, but fimply ftated the refult of frequent obfervation. ). rever knew a fingle inftance of a child's attainment to fall fize and vigour, after having been cruelly con- fined during inftmcy in fwathes or fwaddllng-cloihes. How, in- deed, is it poffible, when the aftion of the heart, the lungs, the ar- teries, and of all the vital organs, is cramped and enfeebled?--* when tee free circulati n of the blood and fecretion of the humours m-e preveired; and when the impatience of reftraint urges the in* fant to w vfte all his ftrength in continual but unavailing efforts to burft hi? fetters. ♦ As I km< w that external objefts were more likely to make .^ fome imprefiLn on the minds of my female readers, than argu- ments drawn from the ftruftureof the human frame, I endeavoured to fix their attention firft on the young of brute animals, many of , N which, as kittens, puppies, &c. though very de'icate when brought into the world, never want to be Strengthened, kept in due form, or preferved from accidents, by means of fwaddling-bands. Chil- dren have as little occafion for any fuch defence againft danger. In reply to the idle objections of mothers and nurfes, founded on the difference in point cfalertnefs between kittens and infants, it has been admitted, that tl-e latter are certamly heavier than* the for- mer,,but they are more feeble in the fame proportion r.they are in- capable of moving with fufficient force to hurt themfelves, and if their limbs get into a wrong fituation, the uneafinefs they feel foon induces them to change it. Is it not abfurd to put them to real pain by g?dliog ligatures for fear of imaginary bruifes; and to dif- tort thsir tender bodies effectually by iqueezing them into a prefs, left thev fhould grow diftorted from being left at liberty to ftir ? While 1 was writing on this part ofnhe fubjeft laft autumn, I c-mld not help being ftruek with another illuftration of it, which pre- sented itfelf every day to my view. Above three hundred cattle were p.razing in a field before my window- all of them nearly of the fame fize, well-formed and vigorous, without the leaft mark of fe- -blenefsor diftortion. They .had'not been kept panting, when young, in tight and cumberfome wrappers, nor had they been itunted in their growth by improper management. They might be truly colled the offspring of nature, reared and brought up in xon- formity to her laws. How painful and humiliating did I feel the contraft, when 1 compared them with the fofter-children of art, with bipeds, of various fhapes and fizes—with the hunch-backed, crooked-legged, lame^ ricketty, diminutive, and deformed human beings, whom I often faw walking through the fame field ! * , ADVICE TO MOTHERS. -579 J&,"',.-. ^ou!^ it be alledged, that inferences drawn from a fpecies fo ^'differ, nt from cur .wn are not concluiive, let us text turn our eyes to wh-t takes place in lavage natkeos, who are 11 known to be tall, mouft. or* Weii prop; rtioned. Almoft every body has at leaft heard, that the Apollo Belvidere is one of the moft beautiful and exquiLite pieces of f atuc ry in the world. *■ I muft net here omit Buffon's account of the method cf bringing up their young, purfued by other nnpolifhed nations, as ' we pr c udly call them. *' The ancient Peruvians," fay s he," in loofe- ly fwethiog the:r children, left their arms at full liberty. When thev threw afide this drefs, they placed them*at freedom in a -hoi- low, dug in the earth, and Jined with clothes. Here their children, . unable to get out and era .vl into danger, had their arms quite locfe, and c mid move their heads and bend their bodies, without the rifk of falling or hurting therofelves. -As foon as they were able to 1 ftand, the nipple was fhewn them at a diftance, and thus they were enticed to learn to walk." Th- fame writer obferves, " that the young negroes are cftea in a fituation in when it is with more difficulty they come at the bre .ft. They cliog round the hip of the mother wiih their kr.ees and feet, and by that means ftick fo clofe, that they ftand in no need cf being fupported, while they reach the breaft with their hands, and thus continue to fuck, without leiting.go their hold, cr " beinc* in any danger of failing, no'wi-Minding tbe-varions motions of tne mother, who all the while is employed ia her ufual labour. 'Thefe children begin to walk at tor end of the fecond month, cr rather to fnuffle along on their hands and knees ; an exercife that gives them ever afterwards a facility of running alm:ft as fwift iu that manner as oa their feet." , „. *-*v* To this very interefting defcription, I can ace, upon -tne tef.i- '■'s mony ofa friend of mine who had been feveral years on the coaft ' of Africa, that the natives neither put any clothes on their children, -ner apply'to their bodies bandages of any kind, but lay them on a pallet and fuffer them to tumble about at pleat ufe. \ 11 they are * all ftraight, and feldom have any complaint. Good health, as well as a eood fhape, is the confequence of their free, uncenfined motion durfcinfancy ; while, among us, on the contrary, reftramt, cr. •wl^tis the feme tKn£, tight preffure, checks growth, diftorts the 5ac ADVICE TO MOTHERS. frame, and renders it at once diminutive, unfightly and infirm.-— There is always a clofe and very natural connection between de- formity, weaknefs and difeafe. The more we enlarge ourfurvey of the human fpecies in vari- ous parts of the world, the,lefs doubt fhall we entertain of the prin- cioal caufe of dwarfifhnefs and deformity. We fhall find that man- kind are Hunted and diftorted in proportion to their degree of civilization ; that people who go almoft naked from their birth, and live in a ftate of rature, are well-fhaped, ftrong and healthy—and that among others who boaft of higher refinements, the greater at- tention that is paid to drefs,thenearer are the approaches to the feat- ure and to the weaknefs of p'gmies. Sterke, who knew fo well how to enliven the moft ferious fubjects, reprefents himfelf as ftruck with the number of dwarfs he faw at Paris. I am very forry to obferve, that we need not go fo far as Paris to be convinced of the lamentable effefts of tight clothes, b?d nur- fing, and confined impure air. Many ot thefe matters are not much better ordered in the Englifh metropolis ; every narrow bne in London fwarms with ricketty children ; and though we cannot fay of the people whom we meet with in the ftreets, that every third man is a pigmy, yet we may with fir ft truth afl'ert, that many of the women are evidently ftunted in tfieir growh, and, both in fize and robuftnefs, are below the ftandard of mediocrity. With re- gard to females, indeed, born and bred in this city, as more atten- tion is unfortunately paid to the tightnefs of their drefs, acd to the artificial moulding or pretended improvement of their fhape when, young, the far greater part of them muft be of a diminutive, feat- ure, and numbers are diftorted either in body or limbs. CHAP. VI. BAKEFUL EFFECTS OF PARf.NI'Jl TSNDURNESS, OK OF WHAT MAY BE CALLED AN E.\rRSMEl.r DLLiCAtB ANH LUSRVAriNG KDUCATION. JtiAVIKG repeatedly had occafion to point cut the evils that muft arife from the inattention of mothers to any part of their duty, and efpecially from abandoning their children to the manage- ment of hired nurfes. I fhall now proceed to explain the bad con- fequences of the oppofite extreme. Too much care operates in the fame manner ?s too littie, and produces fimilar effefts. A cafe or two, felefted from many which haveoccured to me in the courfe of practice, will fufficiently illuftrate the truth of this affertion. The grand rule of life, which reafon and experience concur to recommend, is always to purfue the golden mean ,• to fteer a middle courfe between dangerous extremes; and to take care, in avoiding any one vice or folly, not to run into its oppofite. Mothers are too apt to forget this admirable leffon, in nurfing and rearing their chil- dren. , They do not feem toknow the proper medium between.cru- el negleft or indifference on the one hand, and the fatal exc^Jes of anxiety and fondnefa oa the other. In giving way to the ftrong im* ADVICE TO MOTHERS'. 5ft pulfes of natural affection, they commonly go too far, and do as much mifchief to their offspring by mifguided tendernefs, as by to- tal lnlenfibihty. It is not my intention to combat thofe fine feelings of mothers, without which the human race would foon be extinct. I only wifh to lee t*em kept a little more under the control of reafon. I wifh to lee re moft amiable of all paffions, maternal love, difplayed in promoting the health and fortifying the conftitutions of children— not in relaxing them by every fpecies of foftnefs and effeminacy. Wnen ths paffion is carried beyond the proper bounds, it ceafes o be love : it becomes a fort ot blind infatuation, always injur-.5, and often deftroys tae object of its regard. Mothers fhould never for- get the fable of the monkey fnatchingup one of its your g in a mo- ment of alarm, and, iu order to fave it from danger, fque zmg it with fo clofe an embrace as to occafion its death. Waat a juft pift- > ure of darling children fo frequentiy killed by kindnefsf Nature provides for the helplefs -ftate of infancy in th* ftrong attachment of parents. A child comes into the w-rld chiefly de- pendent on the mother's care for the prefervation of its hemtr. She is tremblingly alive to all its wants. Every tender office (he per- forms increafes her fond felicitude, till at length it gains the full pof- feflion of her affeftions, and her fole wifh is to make it happy — What a lamentable thing it is that fhe fhould fo frequently railkke the means! Indeed there cannot be a greater miftake than to imagine that extreme tendernefs or delicacy of treatment will promote the health, the growth, the prefent or the future hoppinefs of a child. It muft have quite a contrary effeft. I- ftead of fupplying the real calls of nature, it creates a thoufand artificial wants: inftead cf guarding the infant from pain and difeafe, it renders him much more fufe p- tible of both, and lefs capable of enduring either : inftead of happi- nefs, it enfures mifery in ev^ry ftage cf his exiftence, as the infirm- ities )fbody and mind, which are contract 3d in the cradle, will fol- low him with incurable obftinacy to the grave. The vi riter, whom I quoted on the fubjeft of fuckling, is na lefs forcible iu his cenfure of ma tern d fondnefs. He lays, the ob- vious paths of nature are alike forfaken by the woman who gives u? the carecf her infant to a hireliug, or, in other words, who neglects the duties of a mother ; and by iter who carries thefe duties to excefs ; who makes an idol of her child ; increafes his weaknefs, by preventing his fenfe of it; and, as if fhe could emancipate him froin the laws of nature, hinders every approach of pain cr diftrefs ; without thinking that, for the fake of preferving him at prefent from a few trifling inconveniences, fhe is accumulating on his head a diftant load of anxieties and nisfortunes—without thinking, that it is a barbarous precaution to enervate and indulge the child at the expenfe of the man. He the:: begs cf mothers to attend to nature, and follow the track fhe has delineated ;—" fhe continually exercifes her children and fortifies t-eir conftitution by experiments of every kind; in- uring them betimes to grief aad pain. *n aitt;07 fteir teerx tVy" 5g2 ADVICE TOItfOTHERS. experience the fever; griping colic? throw them into convulfions; the hooping-cough f uffocat-s, and worms ttrment them ; furfers corrupt their blood ; and the various fermentations ta which their humours are fubjeft. cover them with troublefome eruptions ; al- moft the whole period of childhood is ficknefs and danger. But, in paffmg through tois courfe of experiments, the chyld -gar.hers ftrength and fortitude; and, as foon as he is capable of living, the principles of Ufe become lefs precarious. " Tois," he adds, " is the law of nature. Why fhould you aft contrary to it ? Do you not fee that, by endeavouring to cor- rect her work, voujfpoil it, and prevent the execution of er defies? Aft you from without, as fhe does withio. This, according to vou, would increafe the danger ; on the cootrary, it will crwte a diverfion, and ldfen it. Experience fhews, that cnld-en delicately educated die in a greater proportion than others. Provided you do not make them exert themfelves beyond their powers, lefs rifk is run in exercifing, than indulging them in eafe, Imir? them there- fore by degrees to thofe inconveniences which they muft ooe day fuffer. Harden their bodies to the intemperature of the feafons, climates, and elements ; to hunger, thirft, and fatigue." As the philofopher was aware that the latter part of his advice would ftir up all the fears and alarms of fond mothers,- he takes fome pains to convince them that it may be followed with perfeft fafety. He very juftly obferves, that, " before the body has ac* quired a fettled hahat, we may give it any we pleafe, without dan- ger ; though when it is once arrived at full growth and confiftence^ every alteration is hazardous. A child will bear thofe vicffitudes* which to a man would be infnpportable. The f ?ft and pliant fi- bres of the former readily yield toimpreffion ; thofe of the latter are more rigid, and >re reduced only by vk lence t^ recede from 'he forms they have affumed. We may ' herefore," he concludes* »; 'J bring up a child robuft and hearty, witoout end ngerlog either its life or health ; and though even fome rifk were run in this re- fpect, it would not afford fufficient caufe of hefitation. Since they are rifks infeparable from kimaa life, can w^ do better, than to run them during that periodof it wherein we take them at the leaft disadvantage ?"—I. leave this queftiontobe duly confidered by every mother who is not blind to thecleareft evidence of truth, or wilfully deaf to the moft commanding tones of eloquence and argu- ment. The familiarity of any object hffens our furprife at ir, or there are few inftances of human folly wVich would aftonifh us more, than thatof a fond mote er,.who, in order to protect her child from a little pain or uneafinefs while he is young, multiplies.his fufferiugs when he comes to maturity. Strange infatuation J to facrifice the man to the infant, and, through over-folicitude for'a year or two ' after his entrance into life; to fhorten its natural extent, and to fill up that contrafted fpan of exiftence with weakrefs, irritability, and difeafe! Did any body ever think of rearing an oak plant in a hot* houfe, thence to be removed to the bleak mounta n ? And is the puny, enervated nurfling better prepared to endure the tranfition s-irirv; rev MOTHERS. 5% from the lap of foftnefs to all the accidents of a rugged and'a ftor- my world ? < As ftrong examples often make fome imprefficn where other modes of reafomng fad, I fhall here beg leave to introduce' the hif- tory of a young gentleman, whom 1 attended at a very early period of my practice, and who fell a viftim to the txc-ilfive fo.-drefs of an in lufeent mother. With every wifh to promote her fon'?. health and happmefs, fhe was as"fa*- as reflected intention, th; vivocmthuX. •"abfolate caufe of totally deftroying both. She brought 00 relaxa- tion and debility, by her mifguided endeavours to avert peon ; and" while fhe hoped to prolong the life cf an only fon, the means whicii fhe made ufe of for that purpofe, not only abridged its duration., * but precluded his p >\ver\of*er joying it. Though he was buried at the age of twenty-one, he might be laid to have died in his cradle* for life has been wed defined, not to confift in merely reathing, x but in making a proper ufe of our organs, or fenfes, our faculties, and of all thofe parts of the human frame which contribute to the I confcioufuefs, of our ex ftence. That he uever attained to thij ftate of being, will fully appear from the following narrative. Edward Watkinfon was the only fon of a country clergyman, of amiable manners and found learning, but of a reclufe turn of mind. The mother was a daughter of a London tradefman, and had been educated with extreme delicacy. She naturally purfued the fame line of conduct towards her own child ; and her fond hufbaod was too much under the influence of the like fatal weaknefs. Many a child is fpoiled by the indulgence of one parent; in the cafe now before us, both concurred to produce that en-ervathg effect. For fome time after his birth, mafter Neddy was re:koned a promifing boy. When I firft faw him,-he was about eighteen teeth quite dec yed—his nofe and chin almoft touched each other— his'Veaft narrow and prominent—his body twitted—his legs like Tpinnles—his handsand fingers approaching nearly to the form of birds claws—in fhort, his whole figure exhioited the truly pitiable appearance of a very old man, finking under the weight of years and infirmities i.~ to the grave. ,. It was at'Midfummerl paidmyfirftvifit. Ithen found him wrap- ped up in clothing fufficient for the rigours of a Lapland winter, and fo clofely/mufHed that one could hardly fee the tip of h>s ncfe. - He wore feveral pair of ftockim-s; his gloves were double, and reached his elbows ; and, to compleat the abfurdity of his drefs. he was tight laced indtays. Though armed in tms manner at ad poinds, he feldom peeped out of doors except in the dog uays, and then ventured no father than the church wirch was only forty pa- ces from his father's houfe.. I believe this was the moft diflant ex- curfiorLheever made; and the extraordinary attempt was always accom^&ied with peculiar care, and many additional prefervanves %GmThedeyeof his parents might be truly fed d to watch over hi* cg4 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. cot only by day, but by night alfo, as he flept in the fame bed with { th^ra, having never been permitted to lie alone, left he fhould throw _ the clothes off, or feel the want of any immediate afiiftance. It did not once occur to his father or mother, that all the inconveniences j which they fo much dreaded, could not be half fo injurious as the relaxing atmofphere of a warm bed, furrounded by clofe curtains, and impregnated with the noxious effluvia from their lungs and His food and his drink were of the weakeft quality, always adminiftered warm, and by weight and meafure. When I recom- mended a more nourifhing diet, and a little generous wine, I was told that the ftrongeft thing mafter Neddy had ever taken was chick- en water, and that they durft not venture on wine or animal food for fear of a fever. Thus was the poor lad reduced almo^: to a fkeleton, through the filly apprehend ion of a difeafe, of which he was not fufceptible. Nature was in idm too weak to fpread a hec- tic fiufh even for a moment over his countenance, which had ac- quired the colour of a par-boiled chicken. Ali his vital powers were languid r and even his fpeech refembled the fqueaking of a bird, more than the voice of a man. When I fpeke of exercife, I was told he took a walk every fine day in the hall, and that was deemed fufficient for one of his deli- cate conftitution. I mentioned a horfe—the mother was frighten- ed at the very name of fo dangerous an animal. On telling her that I owed the firmnefs and vigour of my own conftitution to riding every day, fhe began to taink there might be fomething fpe- cific in it; and fhe therefore ccnfented to the purchafe of a little horfe. But tame as the creature was, it did not quiet the mother's alarms. Mafter Neddy, though placed upon the poney's back,was net entrufted with the reins. Thefe were given in charge to a maid-fervant, who led the horfe round the orchard, while the cau- tious rider faftened both Iwnds on toe pommel of the faddle: and the father walking on one fide, and the mother on the other, ield him faft by the legs, left he might be brought to the ground by any fudden ftart of his high mettled racer. This exhibition was too ridiculous not to excite the laughter of the neighbours ; which foon put an end to mafter Neddy's equeftrian exercife. The timidity of a youth thus brought up is more eafily con- ceived than defcribed. Fearful of every thing, he would run from the moft inqffenftve animal, as if he had been purfued by a lion or a tiger. His weaknefs in this refpeft: being known to the village boys, it was a common practice with them, whenever they faw him peeping through his father's.gate, to frighten him into the houfe by calling to the pigs to bite him. This fpcrtive alarm had thefame effeft as the fudden rufh of a mad bullock. • With fuch exceffive weaknefs both of mind and body, mafter , Neddyh&d fome good points about him. His parents reprefented him as a perfect model of morality ; and I had no right t* doubt the truth of their reprefentation, though I did not give him quite fo much credit on that fcore, becaufe he did not poffefs fufficient for. e of cocftnution to be capable of any kind of vice. But I viewed, fWm\ snjn^B. TO MOTHERS. 585 w^1?wi%™0tT^adiniwi0Pand P^, fome proofs'of fe-rn- Xd ^iltl-S W£rh he left' ehind h™- * was the m re fur. Ihe father had perceived his owj error, but not before it was t0<L-/le,j .V9 reading my inaugural differtation, which was then j publiihed1 in Latin, uoder th- title already mentioned, he fent for i-* me, and beg/ed I would endeavour to fave his fon.
