The father on his death-bed ^conjured me to tranflate my differtation into Engrfh, as he th ug. % ■ it might be of infinite fervice to. mankind. "My compliance with j hisjequeft gave rife to the " Domeftic Medicine," of w ich'that r" ft c£ the "aeaas tif preferving the lives of children, conftitutes the firft, and, in my opinion, the beft chapter. £* r ^ ^e above relation may to fome appar romantic: but did I fuppofe any one capable of queftioning my veracity, I could nme feveral perfons of t,!e firft refpefta*llity, who know, the, fo far from being heightened, it fells fh rt of the truth, indeed I might go farther, and affert, fr >m my own too fr qu.mt obfervation, hat a matter Neddy is not fo fingular a phenomenon in many other ftim- ilies, and tiiat the evils of parental folly ar? much oftener entailed upon favourite heirs, than the power of fully eojoying the eftates ■*•* wnich defcend to them. But it is in toe female world, more, efpecially, that maternal fondnefs fpreads its fatal ravages. Girls remain 1 ng« r than beys coder the immediate and almoft exclu.hve care of their mothers £ and when the latter are more guided by love than reafon* by the impulfesof a tender heart inftead of he dictates of an enlightened mind, the former are doomed to weaknefs and mifery. 1 fhall not offend my fair readers by a repeftion of the remarks already made oa the acquired defects and infirmities of too many of our young women ; nor fhall 1 attempt to defcribe the 1 »ng train and almoft endlefs variety of nervous difeafes, from which fo few of them are exempt: I fhall now confine myfelf to a fiogle inftance of tte ef- fects of extreme delicacy in the education cf a daughter, as the counterpart of my ftory of a fon cut oft* by the like oeans. ' . Ifabella Wilfon was in early life a very promifing child, and the object of her mother's adolatry. This go ^d woman had no .idea i.hat hcuth and beauty were more likely to be deftroyed than im- (38J* 496 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. , * proved of preferved by exceffive care. In the choice of diet, clothe, exercife, &e. the delicacy of her fweet girl was always the ruling ' idea. It is eafy, indeed, to render the human frame more delicate £ i but to makeitmore robuft, requires a very different mode of pro-'^ ceeding. As the child did not feem afflifted with anye particular "5 complaint, the doting mother exulted at the happy ejects of her0 own management, and never thought that the taper form, the frs^ limbs, and the languifhing foftnefs, which fhe. fb mnch admirdjj- were the fure fymptoms of debility and of latent ^difeafe. *• Ifibella's mental improvement, in which fhe furpaffed mar.yO' other young girls of her age at the fame.fchocl, was no lefs flatter- ing to her miftaken parents. But fhe had fcarcely attained her fourteenth year before the fond illuficn vanifhed*, and the regular?-. functions of both mind and body were fufpended Jby a fit of the ; moft extraordinary nature. I cannot avoid making one remark;'! here, which' may be of great practical utility. .: It is, that' fits^l though thev go by different n-onco, and areafcribedto a great vari?" ety of caufes, may all be ranked under the general appellatipn ot"'* nervous affeftions, and are alnr ft always the confequence of bad£, nurfing or injudicious treatment l« childhood. Few children, prop-7 * erlynurfed, have, fits; and of thofe who are improperly managed* * few efcape them.' Poor Bell Wilson was one of the unfortu-^ nate clafs. On my being fent for to attend this young woman, who was then fixteen, 1 was informed that fhe had been fu jeft to fit3 for about three years, and had taken a great deal of medicine by the advice of feveral of the faculty, but without having experienced any.. benefit.. Though the perfon who gave me this account made mejj of the ward Jits, I foon found that ftriftly fpeakirg, it was only en*.* ft, that affumed two different forms or ftates, which followed one ahother in cooftant fucceffion during the whole of the above period. - 1 In order t-"» give a precife idea of this fr gular kind of §t,,I fhsll call its firft ftate active, and the fecond paffive. During the former^ the y oung woman made ufe of the moft violent exertiens, fpringing* up, throwing her arms about, and ftfiking them againft every thing'1 wr.ich came within her reach. At the fame time, fhe-uttered a fort of noife, confuting of three notes, which was more like the cry of fome wild beaft than any thing human. An univerfal fpafin fucceeded thofe ftrange agitations, and every limb became as ftiff and inflexible as if it had been fuddenly petrified. Her whole appearance was that of a ftatue made of Parian marble. Ia this ftate of rigidity fhe continued fometimes for one hour, fometimes two, and often three or four, but the mo- ment it was over, ftie began with the cry and motion above defcri- bed. Theaftiveconvulfionnever lafted fo long as the rigid ftate ;-'•' but it was the only time at which any thing could be got down her throa*-. If any creature can ex- ma ft teoi greater wretch"dnefs, or is aju ,er object of com- vuderation ih^ .-i -t-oy who 'is f rcr-d to clean chimneys in this coentry, 1 am very much mftaken. I have Witnefled ftill triiier crtieity : 1 nave mure than once feen a Doy* whsn the ADVICE TO MOTHERS. 593 ** chimney was all in a blaze, forced down the'vent, like a bundle of » wet rags, to extinguifh the flame. On the very day (the twen-y-feccnd cf laft October) when I was come to this part Of my fubjeft, an indictment f r cruelty to a r young chimcey-Iweeper happened to be tried at the Weftminfter P lellions. The wretched fufferer had been decoyed in to the houfe of a woman who carried on fads horrid bufinelV, bu: who promifed \ to employ him only as an errand-boy. He /ad not been Ion? I \: there, however before he was put to learn the trade, as it is called!". s Some domeftic leffo; s were deemed neceffary to prepare him for , ^public exhibition. The child, not being able to clmb with the , readinefs expefted, ufed to be ftripped naked by the foreman, and k ', r whipped round the room with birch reds. His body, legs and j .arms, were feverely hruifed by the beatings he had received." This was not all. Though his knees and elbows had been rendered foar * by repeated trials, yet when the poor creature could not mount - v quick enough, his cruel instructor ufed to goad him (while in the chimney) in the legs and thighs, by a needle put into the end of a flick. .. It alfo cime out in the courfe of the evidence, that unfortunate children of this for* are taught to climb by being taken to the porch of St. George's church, where, at the rifle of their lives, they are obliged to mount the perpendicular wall. I am always happy .'. • to feejuftice temp.red with mercy, efpecially when the punifkment is at the difcretion of the ju ige or magiftrate; but after a culprit lad been fuliv convicted of thofe atrocious acts, I could not help thinking that lenity towards him was carried too far in fentencing . him only to fix months imprifonraent. I am ftill more grieved to think, that any bu' nefs which requires fuch dreadful rnodo^ of ?. traini. g, fhculd be tolerated. Perhaps I fhall be t Id, that boys fo trained are neceffary. I deny the affertion. Chimneys are kept clean, without fuch cruel and danger us means, not only in many countries on the conti- nent, but even in fome parts or our owa ifland, where the houfes are much higher than in London. In North Britain, for inftance, a bunch of furze or of broom anfwers the purpofe,and dees the bu- finefs muci cheap r and better. One man ftands ar the top and * auother at the bottom of the chimney, when a rope is let down by •* " means of a ball; and the Lunch of furze or broom, being properly v. • faftened on, is pulled up and down till the chimney is quite cleaned. j The little trouble and expenfe attending the operation are the * ftrongeft incitements to repeat it fo often as to preclude thcpofilboi- » ty of chimnies ever taking fire. Is this the cafe in Londor.,though hundreds of lives are ever? year facrificed to th- moft birbarour method of preventing dang-r ? How vain fhall we find the boafts that -'re made of mighty improvements, in tne metropolis of the Britifh empire, if wefairly confider that it is at leaft a century be- hind the meaneft village in the kingdom in almoft every thing that /" regards the prefervation of human life ! . n-r . - 1 have often heard the plea cf nee .duty urged to juftify doing ^rosg, but never more abfurdly than in toe employment of boys 5?4 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. , to clean chimnfes. Experience clearly proves that it can be much better done without them ; and fhall weij in perverfe oppofition to reaft>n and humanity,cont;nue a practice which is equally forbidden by both ? The abolition of the Have trade has Of late years become a very popular topic among us; and^the caufe of the' poor Afri- cans has been pleaded with lips of fire in our fenate. But while our pride i? flattered, by the idea of relieving flaves abroad, we make a fetch our feliow-fobjefts.at,hoaie infinitely greater flaves, aod far more miferable 1 This is fomething like the fafhicnable chimera of umverfal philanthropy, which pre?«nds to be alive to toe fofferings of the diftant Hottentots, but in reality i+ech the heart againft fpeftacles of much keener wretchednefs'in our own ftreets. My iar ■ worthy friend, J.nas Hanway, who literally went about, doing good, ufed all his influence to ameliorate the c'~>oe;r*;nn of ihofc unhappy creatures ; which, in a certain degree, he effected. %.. But, there are ferae customs, that can be thoroughly mended only by > beirg complete!v abolifhed. While boys are forced up ch'imnies, ihey muft be mifei able, whatever laws are mrde for their relief. A law prohibiting the practice altogether, would beat once laying the axe to the root cf tne tree j and the evil admits of no other re- medy. Had Mr. Hanway taken up the matter upon this ground, he had fpirit and perfeverance fufficient to hove carried it through, and to have obtained an act of parliament for the effectual relief of the moft wretched beings on the face of the earth. He confined hi? benevolent exertions to a partial alleviation of their mifenes, be- caufe it had never occurred to him,, that the climbing boys, as he calls them, were wholly unneceffary. What a pity he did not c»r- ry his views a little farther, as, in that cafe, he. ce-tainly would not have remained fatisfied with any thing fhort of their total emancipation from fuch cruel and ufelefs bondage \ Toe fituation of thefe children of miferyi-; now become more hopelefs, in confequence of the death of Lady Montagu*, who ufed to make fuch of them as could go to her houfe, happy for at leaft one day in the long aod lingering year. 1 often wifhed to fee her well known talents exerted in their favour ; they could not have had an abler or a better advocate. The amiaMenefs of her charac- ter wouid have given additional force to the impreffive productions of her pen ; and the legiflature might have been induced to inter- pofe its autnority in luppreffmg an employment at once fo deftruc- tive aad fo degrading to the human fpecies. But furely there is humanity enough in both houfes of parlia- ment to take up this fubjeft, without any other appeal to their feelings than, a bare reprefentation of facts. Many touches more would be neceflary to finifh the melan- choly piftureof the wretchodnefs of young chimney-fweepers. It is enough for me to fketch the principal outlines, in hopes that dome perion more at leifure may be induced to lay on the internal colouring. The wife of a peafant, who lived .„ in a ' cottage' at H ' no gfeat diftance, was gone out upon fome bufinefs, leaving an in- ^ fant in the cradle, under the care of three or four more of her :«^ young-family, one of whom gave a knife to the baby to amufe it. ' Duri.-g the mother's,abfence, a wolf, impelled by hunger, rufhed * *■ into the cottage, and made its firft.foap at the infant's arm, which, JA being extended with the knife*in a playful manner,- it entered the -^ throat of the ravenous animal, and proved its>death. -As the wo- & men in our happy ifland have no occaffou to-arm their''children £♦againft fuch enemies, I do not fuppofe that they a\ ill oe tempted by the defire of hearing their infants cried up as neroes, to in trull k- them with weapons, which are far more likely to hurt than to de- $i fend fhem. -^ » . But to return to more ferious and neceffary precautions : I hiuft next take notice of fome fatal accid no; wnlch treqiv -n ly occur, * though they may be eafily guarded againft. Wed ily hear of chil- '*< dren falling from windows nd being killed ; this muft be owing to'i '/* the want of proper prefervatives ; a few bars fixed acrofs the win-' *dow^ of thenurfery, or of any high apartments where children are fuffered to play about, would prevent fuch difafters. Yet eafy as jjthe remedy "s, we find it too often neglected. ? How frequently % have I feen, With much alarm, children hanging out of windows in a ftate of imminent danger, and no regard'paid to them by any- perfon within ! this is peculiarly the cafe with the children of tne ,poor in London, who commonly five in the'upper ftories, ar.d fel- dom or m ver have their windows fecured by, bars. Tg keep fuch 'windows conftantly fhut would exclude the'frefh air, fo nectffary. to health, and nven to exiftence; while, on the other hand, having ;»»' them open, without the guard or defence here recommended, is ex- 4 poling the giddy and thoughtlefs to certain mifchief. On the leaft - . ,. noife in the ftreet, a child is apt to run to the window, and, leaning JP"forward in eager gaze, is often dafhed againft the pavement. -'* In my former remarks on banging eats, my chief aim was to fhew how much faf-r and more conducive to fleep their gentile mo- r tion was than* the violent and dangerous rocking of-a cradle. I r' have now an obfervation of greater extent to make on bedsingen- eral, which is, that they often prove fatal 'o children, inftead of ■rvbeing places of eafy and fecure repofe. Either in Fr.mce or in Hclland, I am nol now pofitive which, there is a prohibitory law againft put- ting any child to fleep in the fame bed with its mother or nurfe.— Though we have no fuch law in England, maternal care ought to fupply the w^nt of it, efpecially as it is not an extraordinary, or a very uncommon thing to hear of a child's being fmothered by the accidental rollmg or preffuro of a grown perfon during fleep. Theufe of tum-up bedfteads is not lefs noxi-ms or dangerous. They exclude the ?.ir from the bed-clothes all day, a^drender them frouzy and uowholelbme. Childrea may alfo be inadvertently killed in them. Ihefervant, or perhaps the mother, turns up the bed in a hurry* without examining whether the child is in it or n^t; the infant incapable of making any noife in this fituation, is fmoth- ered before the miftake is found out; aid tears flow in vain to remedy what a little caution mi^ht hive pr vented, or what could never have happened in a pr p.r bed. Children fuffocated in 'his manner, as well as thofe accident-.', ally overlaid, might be fometimes reft r d to life. Yet I do not . recollect any inftances, except the one meoti ^ned io my " Domeftic Medicine," of its having been done ; t ough it muft be as practi- cable as in cafes of drowning, of fits, and various other cafualties, at- tended with a fufpenfion or feeming exti ftroi of all the vital pow- ers. It is,not my bufinefs, however, to fuggeft the ufe of very precarious reftoratives, when the means of prevention are fo eafy ^ and certain. I fhould never have done, were I to engage in a particular de- fcription of all the dangers to which chil.ren are expofed in our ftreets, partly through the want of a good medical police, and part- ly through the negl gence of their parents. The importance and the neceffity of the former, I may take f me other opportunity to enlarge upon ; my prefent wifh is, >oexcite ihe attention of moth- ers to the perils which await their children at almoft every comer, when they are fuffered to run a; out by themfelves, or intrufted to young people who have/neither fufficient ftreng'h nor experience to proteft them. It always g'ves me great uneafinefs to fee infants dandled by girls who are hardly able to hold them up, or led abcut ***• by others whofe giddinefs is more likely to .plunge tnem intodanger * than to keep them out of it. At leaft half tee accidents that befal children, both within doors and wit out, are owing to tie foRy, the cruelty, I had almoft faid the murderous criminality of leaving one childto the care of anot er, who, though a litrle older, is not o"'U lefs in want of a mother's or a nurfe's vigilance. ' Let me afk any parent of common fenfe and tender feelings*'''■■ < whether it can be fairly confidered as much fh rt of murder, to let*'j a little girl of feven or efeht years of age take an infant out la her'*"* arms, or lead about younger childreu than herfelf, in a city, wher° carts and carriages of every defcription are rattlin? along—where * horfesare galloping—bollocks furioufly driven—and crowds of peo pie conftantly rufhing with thoughtlefs impetuofi'y ? Is it a ^r] " der, then, to hear every day of children run over, their fegs q- ADVICE TO MOTHERS. Sox arms broken, their brains dafhed out, or their bodies crufhed to atoms, in the midft of thofe dangers to which they are fo inconfid- eraiely expofed ? Though the immediate authors of fuch difafters dei.rve in moft cafes to be hanged, yet I do not know how any jnry could confeientioufly acquit the negligent parent of fome parti- cipation in the crime* I/tt me therefore conjure mothers, in particular, never to per- mit their young children t ego alone into the ftreets, and never to rely upon one child's protection of another. It \i alfo a mother's . duty to make her children, as foon as they are capable of it, aware of danger, and to inftruft them how to avoid the numberlefs ene^ mies that befit them on all fides, and too often prove fatal to in- f an1 life. A good police would certainly go a great way towards the prevention of many of thofe difaiters which to frequently occur in '.-or ftr ets, but no laws can ever be made that will fupercede the mother's care and atteetiort in watching over the health and fafety A't)f ner children. CHAP. IX. QF FOUNDLING HdSFITALS. AND OTHER CHARITABLE INSTI- TUTION* FOR THE REAMING OF tQ*R OR DESERTED CHILDREN. JL HE proper qualifications and ir.difpenfible duties of mothers having fo far engaged our attention, 1 am not without , Tropes that the remarks which have been made on thofe important }Jf feibjects, will be of f >mefervice in the middling and higher ranks of r life. But ir gives me pain to thiok, that ihere are great numbers V of poor women, who do not want fo much to be taught, as to bt enabledto difcharge their duty. They would willingly devote all their time and care to the nurfing cf their children ; but the want of common neceffaries forces them to be otherwife employed.—* 1 hey fee their infants languifh; but the dread of famine is -n ob-. j.ft of ftill keener concern. They are not dead to the impulfes of natural affeftion ; but its' warm emotions in their hearts are foon chilled by diftrefs. It is poverty, whofe icy hand congeals the K'ftreams of maternal comfort, and whofe withering breath'bhfty v fome cfthe faireft buds of human life. . ■. *'" Veoity is more gratified than reafee, by the acknowledge - ment thar there h Uot any country in the known world whico is for ijjucri diftinguifhed as England for its charitabfe hoftitutions. How nvrtifving is it to add, that there is not any country in which char- ity is fo much abufed and perverted ! When. I look at fuch abmf? di'agas the Fr undling Hofpital, much more like a, palace than a nurfery for poor children—when I confider rhe vaft funis raid.our. "i ill railing that ftrufture, and the ftill greater foms wmeh have fmce V' been expended and continue to be expended,up n the moft pomp- '< Ous and ufelefs parts, of the eft ibliihment-1 carmot help-crying OUt with RoCHEFOUCAULT, that virtue or charity wzuld never go jo far-if pride did not keep her company. „ „, * > itts-miKhtobs lamented, that thejto tor th? rt.iifi both. <& -" < ,(39> 6*2 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. the old and the young, in everv pitiable fituation, which at differ- ent times have been encouraged by the people of England, fhould all be connected with fuperb buildings, profitable jobs, and a train ' of offices and employments, which prey upon the funds, and render theu ufe to the public very fmall, in comparifon to what it might otherwife have been. This grofs mifapr ''xation—this wafte of charitable contributions, is not the only thing to be found fault w;th in the management of the Foundling Hofpital. Never did any infbtution hold out fairer claims to the fupport of the benevofent ar ."' humane, and never was any more lihHnlly patrooifed ; yet, fr m ignorance and inexperience^ inftead of preferving the lives of children, ?' has too frequently ac.elsrated their de?th. The firft fuggeftion of a'Foundling Hofpital was certainly verv plaufible; its profefled objeft was to refcue from mifery and deftruction the innocent victims that would otherwife be doomed to borh by the deferlion of their parents. Thus many valuable lives would be faved to fociety, and fuch a refource would be open- ed to ut<fortunate mothers, as would remove, or confiderably weak- t. en, ariy tempta* >o to the commiflion of the moft unnatural afts. The pruda, the bigot, a' d th<- canting hypocrite, might, indeed, condemn foch a fuppofed encouragement to the illicit union of the fexes; bat humanity can never look upon the prefervation of a hu- man being as a crime. An undert king, therefore, of fo fpecious a tendency, could not fail to meet widi the moft generous fupport. Independently^ of all private contributions, fixty thoufand pounds a year out of the public money were for feveral years appropriated by parliament to fo laudable a purpofe. A palace, as I before obferved, irfftcad of a nurfery, foon reared its head. The doors were thrown open for the admiffion of deferted children from all parts, not to be nurfed d" and educated there, but to be fent down to the diftant counties, and in their conveyance numbers of the poor infantb perifhed. I am not here making a vague or an imaginary calculation ; 1 am ftating what foil.under my own notice, when I was phyfician to a very c^ifnerable branch cfthe Foundling Hofpital. I have elfewhere " defcrihed tke mortality which was occafioned by the improper ufe of medicines among thofe who had fm wved the fatigues of the ' j -urney. I haye aifo explained the very eafy means I made ufe cf; to-put ? ftop to fuch afflicting ravages, while the children and thsir' nurfeslwere under my infpnction&controul. Butallmy remenftrances a* dofthaviDgthemdraojeedawayfromthofenurfestoo foon,crowdfcd ' inoo hofpitals, confined in fchools, or put out to unhealthy employ- mem % were una ailing. I was told, that the eftablifhed rule? and * Cuffoms of. the hofpital could not be deviated from, though their evident effect was to deftroy health, and to enfeeble the minds as Well as the bodies of the unfortunate victims. , a haveofteuyi-wed with indignation the falladcris reports' cf * the numbers 'of children faid to be brought up aud provided for at "^ for!.. eda~fs. y a,n very confident, that were an accurate lift made - cut of thofe who perehe^ through ir feafonable removals, improper treatment, bad nurfing, difeafes"cccafioned by too early confine- * * .J- " * ADVICE TO MOTHERS. 6c > m?nt in fchools, as weU as by infection in hofpitals, and, laftly, through what I call murderous occupations, it would appear that Dt?P°,ne m ten of th~ infants received there,ever lived to become an ufeful member of fociety. But, befides the evils here hinted at, there is a fundamental ejroi" m the plan itfelf, not only of the Foundling Hofpital, but of all parochial and other charitable inftitutions for the maintenance and education of poor children. Every fcheme \Vvich tends fo fe- parate the parent from the child, whatever impofing appearance it may wear, is a bad one, and will eventually be ft u^d to do mif- chief. It is flying in the face of nature, a thing tha- never ^an he done wjth impunity ; it is rending afunder. the firft and the ftrongeft bondsi of fociety—parental and filial affection : it is perv.rlely at- tempting to blunt and deftroy the fineft feeling of the heart, moth- erly love, without which the human fpecies could not long xift.— All nature points out the mother for the nurfe of every thing that is brought forth alive; nor can her place be fupplied by any inftitu- tion. Thofe who make the experiment will foon be convinced of their temerity and folly. A few years ago I met with a letter addreffed to lord FitzwilU iam, then viceroy of Ireland, on the fubjeft of the proteftaut charity fchools in that country. As the author's fentiments agree with mine in many points, and particularly withrefpeft to rOepc-rmchus effefts of feporating children from toeir parents for the fake of edu- cation, I fhall here give them in his own words. Some people may think the language too ftrong ; but the nature of the fubject requir- ed the moft pointed energy. <; My Lord, I do requeft tkat you will take the trouble of reading the account of this iaveter«teiy illiberal inftitution in any common almanac ; and every line will, I rhink, carry its own rep- robation to an iagenuous mind. The children, as it is regulated ia their unnatural fyftem, are all placed in fchools rfciAote froa» their former ab«fes ; or, in other words, tlaey are. torn from all the i fweet aSpciations that attend the interemng idea *-,f home. This is, indeed, a charity which thrives en the extinction cf all the other Charities of life; sndthe feelings oj[nature muft be eradicated^ before^hey can become nurjlmgs cfthe flat e\ They are banifhed from their viein- age to a remote quarter of the kingdom, wh?rv all the traces and, ties of lti-.idredare loft and cut off; all habitudes of the-heart fmothered in tne cradle; and, when fent into tiie world, they know not tiie fp .t which gave them birth, the mother that bore them, nor the blood that flows in toeir veins. I think cf the fpeech of I o- gan, the Indian chief,when all his kindred were murdered^ Then t remains not a drop of my blsodia the veins of any living creature." The writer here takes occafion to direct his remark- to ths f pathetic, voung blood, and, placing the mind.in a ftate of fallen inflation, makes its reaction upon fociety rather dangerous than beneficial.— Tjje ties of kindred operate as a fort of external confcience upop 6k4 ADVICE TO MOTHERS, the conduft of men, deterring them from the commiffion of great crimes, for fear of the difgrace which would be reflected on their relations. There is a family pride, a domeftic honour, among the very poore't and loweft of the community, that guards, and fanc- tions, and is a fort of God for the littie houfehold. Even the high-, eft have fuch workings of nature. La iy Macbeth exclaims, hadj, he not refemblca my father when heflcpt, I had done it. The imagined1 countenance of her father was the only conference left, and c?me bet we n her and murder. But charter-fehool policy makes a fort of maflacre of all thofe domeftic moralities which operate upon character and conduct, without being able to put a higher and no* bier principle of aftion in their pface y and thus, I fear, the fame policy has bred up many victims for the laws, while they only thought of making pr. -foiytes to religion." Though the fifojeft is h :re confidered only in a moral light, yet I could not re!lft the 'emptatie»n to make a fhort extraft from fuch amafcerly letter ; audi do not think that any of my readers will be difpieafed at the peru'.ai of it. But I have ftill lefs occafion to make any apology for ftating the author's opioion of the moft* effectual means of remedying the evil, as the leading feature of his plan of reform exactly correfponds with what I recommended above thirty years ago. I was then lamenting that poverty often obliged mothers to neglect their offspring, in order to procure the necefla- ries of life. I obferved, that, iu fuch cafes, it became the intereft as well as tkecjuty of the public to affift them -% but I -contended. that ten thoufand times more benefit would accrue to the ftate, Ifoy enabling the poor to bring up their own children, than from all the hofpitals that ever Could be erefted with that view. In a fubfequent edition of thofe remarks, I added, that, if it were made the intereft of the poor io keep their children alive, we fhould lofe but very fewJ- cfthem ; and that a.fmall premium given annually to each p(X>r family, for eve*yltiiild they had alive at the year's end, would fave more infant lives than, if the whole revenue of the1 crown were ex- pended en hofpi;ah for that purpofe. It gave me great pfeafur© to find, that the writer juft now quoted had reafer.ed io the fame manner on the hundreds of thou- fhreds which had'beenexnended, merely, he fays, to create foundlings > the tenth part of which, if properly employed, would have been of infinitely more fervice to fociety. The proper mode of employing charitable contributions, he thinks, fhould confift chiefly in giving premiums to fueh pa-rents a$ have fhewn moft zeal and capacity in. educating their children. ♦ Thus, notwithstanding the different points of view in which we examined the abufe of fo important a part of public charities, the very means which I advifed for faving toe lives, and promoting the oo'dily health of infants, appear to him; the moft conducive alfo to thfcir intellectual and moral improvement.^ The prejudices in favor Sf old iiiftitutions muft be ftrong, in- deed, who they can refift the cleareft evidence of frets, and the pi in deductions of fair argument. Yet, in the queftion before us how the public, or the ftate, may moft effectually contribute to the nurfing and rearing'of poor infants, one would fuppofe that no ap< ADVICE TO MOTHERS. <5o^ peal to paft experience. on* mv g-eat depth of reafoning, were ne- ceflary to de o i e im.j)i\n.riotv rf feparatiog children irom their o re*"... ';. oture forms th; coain that binds them ; and, if poflible, it flioelvi n*er he broken, i have ihewn how the lives cf Ihe mother . me child are entwined, not only .-foriog preg- nancy^ but a! iter delivery. To p.o ukoa,is to eoeau gt r the heal h and exiftence of both; ftey are equally necelury to each tods well-heing ; and the longer they are kept together, the more fenfi- ble they become <f the dities they owe to one another, which* when faithfully difcharged, tend greatly to increafe the fura cf hu- man happinefs. But how are we ttfexpeft '.ha* any regard will be paid to thfe doftrine by narrowr-mioded unfeelh.g overfeers of the poor, who make a greater merit of laving a fhilhng to their parifh, than of fa- ■ ving fifty'lives to the community ? We now and than hear of'fuch perfons being dragg d into our courts of juftice,to anfwer for their eruelty to pregnant women ; but no account is kept, and of courfd no cognizance can be taken of the immenfe number of infants that are torn in cur parochial receptacles, from the arms cf their dif- treffed mothers, and are consigned to an untimely death, cr to c*r- taiu debility and protracted wretchednefs. Even the fhow of hu- manity in fome of thofe places, ferve* only to excite our ftronger in- Vdignation. Ins a cobweb, through w^ich the penetrating eye can 1,'^afilyfeewrivKUup "in letters cfblood, Children murdered hereunder the ' 'fanclion of Charity. Are we not always fhoeked at reading or hearing any accounts of the barbarous policy th?t prevails in Cftiaa, where avaricious as well as diftreffed parents are encouraged by the permiflion of the law, and by the force of example, t > deftroy their female children,'; in order to avoid the cxpeufe of rearing them ? And is there a father or a mother in Great-Britan, who would not join in a gene- ral outcry againft an aft of parliament for the imm&liate drowning^ of all infants taken to parifh work-houfes ?*Xet> humanity itielt muft acknowledge that inftant death is infinitely preferable to a lingering exiftence in a ftate of pain, of mifery, of continual gor- ing and difeafe. I do not,.therefore, hefitate to affert, that fuch policy as that of the Chinefe, or fuch an aft of parliament as I nave iuow mentioned, would, io reality be an aft of mercy, contralced with the prefent moft barbarous.moft inhuman, and moft deteltabie* method of taking care, as it is falfely called, qfpoor infants thrown upon the parifh. , 'Nothing was left undone by the^Iate Jonas Han-way to probe this fore to the botto*. He fpared neither time, pains, nor ex- penfe, to procure the fulleft information en the fubjeft, before he ppublilhed his " Plea for mercy to the children of the Poor, tie *H,there ftates, as the refult of his inquiries and calculations, that not more than one in feventy ofcthe children configned to the Pariin, ever attained to mature age; and that even that one ieldom De- came an ufeful member of the community. Among many mitances of the moft horrid nature, he takes notice of a memorandum he met with in &§ books o( a certain parifh, where the uames-cf patticuiar 606 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. purfes were inferted, with the remark of their being " excellent kill' ing nurfes." This teftimonhl of their expertnefs in murder, was deemed by the overfeers, who had tried them, the ftrongeft recom- mendation to conftant employment. Let it not be fuppofed, that I mean to involve all overfeers of the poor in one general charge of infanticide. I know feveral of them to be very ami ble and tender-hearted men, who would do every thing iu their power to promote the ends of true charity and t'\e real interefts of humanity, were tfeey not tied down by the eftab- lifhed rules of their office. That part of the inflitution which re- lates t. the nurfing and rearing of chil :r. n, being radically wrong, the moft zeal us exertions of any individual, during his inert con- tinuance in ■ ffice,will operate but as a temporary palliative of an evil inks wn nature hcurable. It is not a partial reform, but a total change of fyftem, that can in fuch cafes be productive of any go.;d effect. 'The fopporters of Foundling Hofpitals may imagine,, that a juft ification of their plan is implied even in my cenfures of parochia eftaolifhments for poor children. They may fay, that the laudable end of t.eir charity is not tofeparate infants from their mothers, but to provide for thofe from whom cruel and unnatural mothers have feparated themfelves. I before paid the juft tribute.of ap- planfe to the fpirit of fuch an inftitution, and to its profeffed objects; but I lamented the abufes which had arifen out of it, cr rather which were iofeparably connected with the execution of the fcheme." Its obvious tendency and its actual effeft have been ta create foundlings, to encourage the defertion of young children, Whom ma, y of theirparents would never have configned to a reccep- ta.de of that fort, but from cruel neceffity, and from a vain hope that due care would be taken of the poor creatures. I have fhewn thw extent of that care, the dreadful fweep of mortality which ac- companied it, and its confequent infufficiency to promote the de- fired end. 1 would not however have Foundling Hofpitals entirely abol- ifhed. I w old endeavour, in the firft place, to render them lefs neceflary, by ?» method which I fhall fully explain in the next chap- ter, and cf which the great object-will be to take away from poor movers all temptation to abandon their children. But as fome women may be prompted, by other motives than that of want, to deftroy their young, let there be a receptacle ever open for the refcu.? or fuch victim?, and for the prevention of fuch unnatural crimes. It mv plan for the relief of poverty, above hinted at, be carried mto full effect, the number of foun'dltr gs of the latter de- icnpupn w?l. iways b2 fmall, and will not require any very ex- penfive c.oabhfnm-t. There will be no occafion for fhowy buit- do- gs—no room for lucrative jobs, office?, or appointment s. Two cr three rruiiecs, without ialarv of emolument, aad actuated folely by humane and charitable motives, \rd\ be fufficient to'receive "the money, ant: to lay it out according to well-regulated and maturely confiwerediuftruftiei^. The prefent ahufes are very great and iisgrant $ but tiie reform is very eafy, if it be zealoufly undertakes ADVICE TO MOTHERS.- fa, anl perfevered in by men of talents and virtue. I take it for granted that fuch oerfons will alio poffels dignity of mind enough to defpifethe wretched fuggeftions of ignorance, of prejudice, of envy, malignity, fordid i.oereft, aod dhoppcmted vanity. The foregoing remarks are puioofely confined to fome of our moft popular eftabh foments for foe nurfing and rearing of children- It would lead roe too far to snterinto a detail of all the fcheroes which have affumed rhe like name of charitable inftitutions in the neighbourhood o*-London alone. The former have at leaft the merit of having been well i neoJed ; but nWt of the latter are founded in fraud, and have no other aim but to enrich fome artful projeftor, at theexpenfeof the public credulity. This is an inex- hauftable fund for any man who can invent a quack medicine, a new mode of faith, or the plan of fome fpecious charity. We have feen footmen in the firft department, coa1 -heavers in the fecond, and fwindlers in the third, driving their chariots with rival fplen- dor and fuccefs. But the viftims .,f the laft deception are moft to be pitied, becaufe they are poor, innocent, and helplefs children ; while the dupes of the two firft being people of mature years and experience, cannot lay claim to any compaflion for fuffering not only their purfes, but their very fouls aod bodies, to be fported; with by ignorant and audacious impoftors. CHAP. X. SKETCH OF A PLAN FOR THE PRESER VATION AND IM- v PROVEMENT OF THE HUMAN SPLC1ES. TW ERE this fubject to be difcuffed with a degree - of ex, tent fuited to its importance, it would require a large volume.— -But I mean to touch only on a few of the principal points, merely 1 *with the hope of exciting more general attention to a matter, which, though of the higheft concern, has hitherto-been very fbghtly con- fidered. I fnall not dwell upon truths which are obvious to almoft every perfon of common underftanding, that the refources and fta- bilitv of a ftate depend upon the number, vigour, and induftry cf its fubjefts ; and, on the contrary, that where little valuers let c\ the increafe of population, on the growth, health, or life otman, the political fabric, however fhowy, ftands on a rotten foundation, and muft fooner cr later fink into an abyis dug out of its own in- humanity. It is enough to mention thefe truths, in order to se- cure the'ready affent of aU thinking people ; but the practical in- ferences to be drawn from them may admit of- a great diverhty of emmens. The plan which I am about to propofe, whether ap- proved of or not, may be productive of one good effect at leait, that of exercifing the ingenuity of others, and perhaps of conduct- ing public and private charity to more uieiul purpcies than thole to which thsy are now applied. I hope I need not go over the old ground aga c, or ; --p-^ my former anrumr-ts, to prove that there ;*• n i any law oj eaonoo cte'fo foible, or fo facred, as toot ooooii ordams every mcuer 0o3 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. to be the nurfe of h.r own young. T - well-being of both, as I before obferved, depends on the faithful difcharge of this.duty.— Every attempt io divert the breaft-miik from its proper channels, end -gers toe mother's life ; and it is beyond the power of wealth to procure, or of art to devife, any nutriment fo congenial to tee conft'tution of the infant, as the very juices of whicn it is compo- fed an> i which have fo long fuftained it in the womb. It is equally impoflibie to fupply the tender care and uo wearied attention ot a parent; and the rich, who truft to hirelings, find by woeful expe- rience, that half their children perifh in early life. Is it a wonder, then, that a far greater mortality fhould await toe poor infants thr wn upon the parifh, or configned to. an hofpital, where no in- dividual is interefted in their prefervation ? I have faithfully ftated, as a matter wt ich came within thefphere of my own knowledge, ' that nine out often of the deferted foundlings died befor* they at- tained to maturity ; and it appears from Mr> Hanwafs unquestion- able account, that fixty-nine out of feventy of the poor parifh chil- dren perifh in the fame untimely manner. Can it therefore be called charity to perfevere in meafureS which are. fo deiiructive to the population of the country ? I before fuggefted what appeared to me the beft fubftitute for a Foundling Hofpital, confined in its objefts to the prefervation of fuch children as might otherwife perifh by negleft, and of fuch as may be deprived of their mothers at an early aoe by *he " hand of fate." Poor orphans and deferted infants being in the fame pre- dicament, though from different caufes,- muft be reared by ftran- gers. The only expedient in thefe unavoidable deviations from nature, is to place the children under the care of nurfes of unex- ceptionable character, in a healthy part ol the country, and not to take them away till of age to be put apprentices. This very cir- cumstance will prompt every nurfe to ufe her beft endeavours to rear a child whb is to ftay with her if he thrivs and dees well, till he is f urteen years of age. It is the only chance cf making a feran- ger acquire in tio;e a maternal affection for her nurfling. It is alfo the. only chance of a poor child's acquiring a good conftitution, and that kind of early education which is beft fuited to rural employ- ments. But for the relief of poor women, who in pregnancy may he expofed to numberlefs afflictions, ard who, after delivery, may of- ten be forced to part with «hur children, I would not recommend fit er hofpitals or p.rifh w rk-houfes. Thefe receptacles aref'ttle better than half-way h< m!cs, or conduftors.to the grave. In their room, 1 would have a fund eftablifhed to afford indigent mothers every neceffary comfort and afiiftance at their own habitations, du- ring pregnancy as well arm child-bed, and afterwards to enable them to nurfe and bring up their infants themfelves. By thefe means more lives would be preferved than by all the charitable in- ft ^utions now exifting in this country, without cofting one half of the money, or alienating the LenrtR of children frcm their m.rents. It is impoffible, without heart-felt forrow, to think of the Tm- (Qscfe cumber of fine children that are loft for want of a little timely ADVICE TO MOTHERS. && aid to mothers. How mariy of thefe poor women pine with their ofoiprin-: in obfcunty, and in unavailing {irugg'es for their fupport! The dread of ill treatment, of difeafes, of death, and (what is iiin ir re terrible to a delicate mind) the dread of (karc.e, keeps them from work- Jioules and hofpitals. Their groans are un! eard-—ti eir wants un- pitied—and they pafs like filent fhadowsto the grave ! How many others, no lefs tenderly attached to their young, aT€ driven by extreme diftrefc to leave them to the very i.ncertain care of others, and ft hire out their breafts, and their own diffracted atten. tion, to a ftranger } When a mother abandons her child, to fuckle that of another woman, one of the infants is alnriolt fure to die j and it frequently happens that both {hare the fame fate. > T heir is a third clafs of truly pitiable objects, the gh too often re-. gardedwith cruel indilference and contempt ; I mea- the poor wo- men whom we daily fee begging with two, three, or mc -. t children, and entirely dependent on lb precarious a refource frr a moiL'i ojc bread. While thefe can lie under hedges, and get fcr.>; s of food they may live ; but fhould a<evere winter overtake them, t, hen they muit cling to the rock for fhelter, they will all be loft. It i* not unlikely that many of thofe poor infants may be the iffue of men who have. fought for their country •, and that they were turned out of houfe and harbour, left they fhould become troublefome to the parifh/ ■ Can public or private charity be better employed than in prefer- ving fo many lives to the ftate ? And how are they to be preferved ? Not by tearing the poor children from the arms of their mothers, and fending them to hofpitals and work-houfes, "to be put under the care; of" excellent killing nurfes"~-b\it by enabling the mothers to nurfe them agreeably to'the defigns of nature, and thus rendering fertility, not what it enow is, a curfe to the poor, but the fource of the ftvcteifc pleafures, and the greateft of all bleffings. A very fmal! part or the vaft fums collected in this kingdom by taxes, under the title of poor > rates, and by voluntary contributions, would be fully fufficient for the propofed /und ; and I am perfuaded that the wifdoin.and hunnni- tv of "parliament and of government could not be better exerted, that* ty of-parliament in preparing, and carrying into effect either this, or feme other rrurw advifable plan, forfaving the lives of fuch an incalculable number of devoted victims. , , Vanity, as I before obfevve<J, has a very great feare m tec erec- tion would as w good women tion and fupport of alms-houfes'; or ihe rich^and the truly humane would readily difcover, in the hints now given, a much moro utcfol as well as a more charitable method of employing their fupcrmious ;ealth. I hope, however, that the confeious pleafure of dom x real ood, will induce many Jadics, blciled with ■allluencc, to a'ull poor romen to nurfe and rear their children in tbeir own little huts ov hao- ftations, though not inferibedon the outfiue with any vain component to the pride of a patronefs or a founder. Is not the fight of a. rumg family, who are indebted to you for health, and even for exilfee.ee, a, thoufand times more gratifying to the human heart, than we luiy oftentation or r.arade of a public charity? ..r It would imply a very unbecoming 4onl\ of the good ien.e ana +i9 ADVICE iu SAUinAK*. natural feelings of my readers, to dwell any longer en this head ; bu many of them may think the other, part of my plan, expreffed in the title of the prefent chapter, and having for its avowed object the im- provement of the human fpecies, a little romantic. Yet, I flatter myfelf that I fhall be able to prove, that there is nothing of fanciful or im- j-rafticable theory in the fuggeftion ; and tha; the improvement as v^ellae the prefervation of the human fpecies, may be effectually pro- moted bv the fame means—well-timed affiftance, and proper encour- Loanent to mothers. Iu the firft chapter of this work I threw out feme hints on the pxeper choice of wives and of hulbands, with a view to the procrea- tion of a healthy and vigorous iffue ; and I lamented, that the impulfes of natural inchnatior were too often checked in civilized fociety by tlu r^aeer paffions of avarice and falfe pride. I alfo took notice of cafes in win.*; marriage had been forbidden by the legiflators of differ- crii countries. But though it would be difficult to frame, and to en- fe-ce anv complete fyftem of jaws for regulating the union of the fcie'i; and though fuch leenJ reftraints on marriages would be incom- patible wi'h the liberty of individuals in a free government like ov.rs, Y?.-t it is in the power of every-ft ate to encourage the m-.rirg of fne c?.iidr-':i,by graoiir.g to every mother a premium annually, in propor- tion to tne ::ge and nurr.be/ of healthy children fhe brought up. The project of . liberal and honourable reward at the end of every year, v ou'.l encourage mothers to exert all their fkiilj and ufe every endea- vour e- rr«i- a numerous and healthy offspring. It would excite a gen- trai emulation among mothers ', and the-object of the virtuous- ftrng- f e would be, who fhould have the fineft children. The name of Cornelia, tha*-farn»u» Roman mother, would no longer ftand alone on tne r.-ctrds cf maternal affection : ButEnglith women, when requeft-^. t-Uooew tlc"<r jewels or their brighteft ornaments, would throw coenthc nuifery, aad exhibit a lovely family to the fpectators admi- Oeig ; aee. Let i t not be frivoloufly objected, that a fond mother cannot want, <or cemsvei feel a ftrceger ftimulus, than natural affection, to make her k'«<» -.arc ot fcer child. Poor women are forced by keen diftrefs to neglett *o.c-u mfaots, in ordor to earn a bit of bread. They require te <-core patent fupplics, and the affurance of a future reward ; net 'y to induce them, but in fact to enable them tot>eft»w more time ar i o.nticn upon this one important object. Again, then, I muft affrrjythat a p.rt of the public money, as well as of private charita- k'e c'f efriOutions, cannot be applied to a better purpofe, than to the e'tablifhment of a fund for the fupport and encouragement of fuch to, vthtrs. The good effects of this plan would far exceed any prefent eenie&ure or calculation. ,- The population of the country would in. arsatc v.-oh almoft inconceivable rapidity. Inftead of puninefs, de- foiir!ty,;«Hfeafcs, and early deaths, the rifing generation would bedt'f- ■Jiug-.ithrri for their health, beauty, and vigour: and we ftjerttid foonfee a . -ftout ard hardy race fpring up, to repay with e.fury, in valuable fervi- tos ro '" :e ftate, the fums expended in nurfing and rearing them.- I do no? know any one inftitutioa upon earth, in which humanity and en- fi-hecned policy wo aid"be found more happily united. Auntjy. <0 MOTHERS. 6u The effe£tsof premiums have beenproved in a variety of other in- ftances, fuch as the culture of vegetables, the growth of flax, of hen.p, pf potatoes, the planting of trees, and the improvement of the breed of cattle. Is it not a matter of juft furprife that no attention of this fort (le uld ever have been paid to the perianal or bodily improvement of the human fpecies ? We know that the mpft tender plant is not more fufceptible of any fhape or form than infant man: We know that his ftrength and figure are certainly as improveable as thofe of any other animal, were proper methods purfued for the acccmpiifh- roent of fuch defirable purpofes. Yet the breed of men is alone neglect- ed, while every effort of ingenuity is called forth, and the resour- ces of wealth are exhaufted, in experiments to improve the breed of fheep, of horfes, and of oxen ! I never met with more than one man who'took up this fubject oa a ferious ground. His plan was a good one had he poffeffed fuficient means to carry it into execution. He propofed to purchafe a fmall ifland, and to plant it with as many people of both fexes as it would very comfortably maintain. Of thefe, he meant to fuperintend the .diet, occupations, marriages, and the management of their children, with a view to try how far the breed might be improved. It was &■ ^peculation worthy of an enlarged mind. Were every perfon of land- ed property in the kingdom, of this gentleman's way of thinking, and could our country fquires in particular be induced to pay half as much attention to the breed of men as to that of dogs, horfes, and cattle, the progrels of the human fpecies to perfection would become more rapid, and more aftoniftiing, than the degeneracy fo often complained of in every fucceffive age. Nor would this progreflive improvement of man be confined to the body only ; it would extend itfelf alfo to the mind. Every thing great or good in future life, muft be the effect of early impreiiions ;■ and by whom are thofe impreflions to be made but by mothers who are moft interefted in the confequences ? Their inftrudlicus and ex- ample will have a lafting influence, and of courfe, will go farther to form the morals, than all the eloquence of the pulpit, the efforts of fchool-mafters* or the corrective power of the civil magiftrate, who. may, indeed.punifh crimes, but cannot implant the feed? of virtue.— If thefe .are not fown in childhood, they will never take deep root; and where they are not found to grow, every vice will fpring up with bane- ful luxuriance. In this view of the fubject I could eafily find a thoufand argu- ments to enforce the political importance of the plan which I have fuggefted •, but I undertook only to fhew that perfect health and growth, that perfonal beauty and vigour, were moft likely to be the fruits of the well-directed and well-encouraged care of mothers in the nurfing and rearing of their children. Other authors have enlarged on the.culture of the heart and the underftanding, the firft and chief part of which they all acknowledge to be the inconteftibie province of mothers. The eloquent writer whom I have repeatedly quoted, and who has taken fome pains to illuftrate this point, argui.-s wirh great juftnef?, that, if the early part of education, which concerns v.» n.oit, $i> ADVICE TO MOTHERS. iiadbeen deffgtttd for fathers, the Author of nature would doubtlefs have furnifhed them with milk for the nourifhment of their children. It is in concurrence with his opinion, that I have addreffed this little book of inftruction to females j and as he has very beautifully com- pared infant man to a fhrub expofed to numberlefs injuries in the highway of life, I fhall join him in calling on the tender and provident another, to preferve the rifing fhrub from the fhocks of human preju- dice I fhall fay to her, almoft in his words, Cultivate, writer the young plant before H die s f*fl>all its fruit be hereafter delicious to your teffte. jEreclan early fence round the difpofition of your child : others mw? defin- ite its extent; but it ipmai/lS-j»Nf yo(t mbf to raifb tiie bttfriW. m APPENDIX, WHEN t firft turned my attention to the fluffing and manage- ment of children, the late Dr. Cadogan's pamphlet on that fubjeft fell into my hands. I perufed it with great pleafure, but foo^n loft it; and though I have been in queft of it for above forty years, I never couU fet mycjcs upon it till within thefe few days ; which makes me con- clude that it is out of print. That fo valuable a fragment may not be loft, I fhall infert the principal part of it in this Appendix ; and I hope it will render the book more extenfively ufeful, and, at the fame time, tend to corroborate my fentiments concerning mothers. " In my pinion,"' fays the ingenious writer whom I am now quoting, " the bufinefs of nurfing has been too long fatally left to the management of women who cannot be fuppofed 'to have proper knowledge to fit them for fuch a talk, notw'thftanding they look upon it to be their own province. What I mean is, a philofophic knowledge of nature,to be acquired only by learned obfervation and experiencejand which therefore the unlearned muft be incapable of. They may pre- fume upon the examples and tranfmitted cuftoms of their great grand* mothers, who were taught by the phyficians of their unenlightened days j when phyficians, as appears by la!te difcoveries, were miftaken irt many things, being led away by hypothetical reafonings to entertain very wild conceits, in which they were greatly bewildered themfelves* and milled others to believe Iknow not what flrange unaccountable powers in certain herbs, roots, and drugs; and alfo in fome fuperfti- tious practices and ceremonies •, for all which notions there being no foundation in nature, they ought to be looked upon as the effects of ignorance, or the artifices of defigning^uacks, who found their account by pretending to great knowledge in thefe occult qualities, and impo- fing upon the credulous. The art of phyfic has been much improved within this laft century : by oblerving and following nature more clofely, many ufeful difcoveries have been made, which help us to ac- count for things in a natural way, that before fecmed myiierious and ma<tical, and which have confequently made the practice of it more conformable to reafon and good fenfe. This being the cafe, tfeere is '«»reat room to fear, that thofe nurfes, who yet retain man^ of tlhefe traditional prejudices, are capitally miftaken in their manoj&icnt-og children in general, and, fancying that nature has left a"£Rat deal to their fkill and contrivance, often do much harm where they intend to do good. Of this I fhall endeavour to convince them, by (hewing fcow I think children maybe clothed, fed, and managed, with much j,:fs trouble to their nurfes, and infinitely greater cafe, comfort, afi« j'af«tv,t'o-.the littie ones. 6"r'4 ADVICE iu Mvitinits. u When a man takes upon him to contradict received opinions and prejudices fanctified by time, it is expected he fhould bring valid proof of what he advances. The truth of what I fay, that the treat- ment of children in general is wrong, unreachable and unnatural, will in a great meafure appear, if we but confider what a puny val- etudinary race moft of our people of condition are chiefly owing to bad nurfing, and bad habits contracted early. But let any one who would be fully convinced of this matter, look over the Bills of Mortal- ity. There be may obferve, that almoft half the number of thofe who fill up that black lift, die under five years of age ; fo . that half the people that come into the world, go out of it again before they become of the leaft ufe to it, or themfelves. To me this feems to deferve ferious confideration; and yet I cannot find that any one man of fenfe and public fpirit has ever attended to it at all; notwith- standing the maxim in every one's mouth, that a multitude of inhab- itants \± the greateft ftrength and beft fupport of a commonwealth. The mifconduct, to which I muft impute a great part of the calamity, is too common and obvious to engage the idle and fpeculative, who are to be caught only'by very refined rcfearches ; and the bufy part of mankind, where their immediate intereft is not concerned, will al- ways overlook what they fee daily ; it may be thought a natural evil, and fo is fubmitted to without examination. But this is by no means the cafe ; and where it is entirely owing to mismanagement, and pof- fibly may admit of a remedy, it is ridiculous to charge it upon nature, >ind fuppofe that infants are more fubject to difeafe and death, than grown perfons ; on the contrary, they bear pain and difeafe much-bet- ter, fevers efpecially (as is plain in the cafe of the fmall,pox, gene- rally moft favourable to children) and for the fame ' reafon that a twig is lefs hurt by a ftorm than an oak. In all the other productions of nature, we fee the greateft vigour and luxuriance of health, the nearer they are to the egg or the bud ; they are indeed then mod fenfi- ble of injury, and itis injury only that deftroys them. When was there a lambda bird, or a tree, that died becaufe it was young ? Thefe are under the immediate nurfing of unerring nature, and they thrive ac- cordingly. Ought it not therefore to be the care of every nurfe and every parent, iiot only to protect their nurflings from injury, but to be well allured that their own officious fen-ices be not the greateft the helplefs creatures can fuffer. « In the lower clafs of mankind, efp-cially in the country, dif- eafe and mortality are not fo frequent, either among the adults or their children. Health and pofterity are the portion of the poor I " mean fkJjbonous. The want of Superfluity confines them more wl*H1HP,mits of nature ; hence they enjoy bleflings they feel not and are ignorant of their caufe. The mother, who has only a few ' rags to cover her caild loofely, and little more than her own breaft to feed it, fees it ooalthy and ftrong, and very foon able to drift for itfelf- while tne puny infect, the heir and hope 'of a rich family, lies lanI gUifmng under a load of finery that overpowers his limbs, abhorrm* and rejecting tne dainties he is crammed with, till he dies a *■ —.-„' to the mtftaken care and tendernefs of his fond mother. In tj>«j co'irfo"-* ADVICE T9 MOTHERS. 6*5 of my practice, I have had frequent occafion to be fully fatisfied .nt this; and have often heard a mothei anxioufly fay, the child h,:s not > been well ever fince it has done puking nd crying. Thefe complaints, though not attended to, point very plainly to their caufe. Is it not very evident when a child rids its ftomach feveral times in a day, that | it has been overloaded i—When it cries, from the incumbrance and confinement of its clothes, that it is hurt by them ? White the natu- ral ilrength Ialls, (as every child is born with more he^oh and ftrength than is generally imagined) it cries at or rejects the fup<:r- flujus load, and thrives apace ; that is, grows very fat, bloated, and Jil- tended beyond meafure, like a houfe-lamb. But in time, the >' e ©ppreffive caafe continuing, the natural powers are overcome, t-_ 05 no longer able to throw off the unequal weight ; the child nev. not able to cr/any more, languifhes and is quiet. The mis ^o tune .0, thefe complaints are not undcrftood ; it is twaddled and TTim.d onT till, after gripes, purging, ^c. it finks under both burdens ino a convulfion-fit, and efcapes any farther torture. This would be r ■; cafe with the lamb, were it not killed when full fat. " That the prefent mode of nurfing is wrong, one would chink needed no other proo'f than the frequent mifcarriages attending it, Jic deaths of many, and ill-kealth of thofe that furv^ve.**** What I ^ut '■ going to Complain of is, that chil dren in general are, over-clou.td aad. over-fedj and fed and clothed improperly. To thefe caufes 1 impute almoft all their difeafes. But to be a little more explicit: The firii great miftaieis, that they think a new-born infant cannot be kept too warm; from this prejudice they load and bind it with flafisds, wrap- j.*pcrs, fwathes, ftays, Sec. which altogether are almoft equalto its ova weight ; by which means a healthy child in a month's time is made Yo tender and chilly, it cannot bear the external air 5 and if, by'any ac- cident of a door or a window left carelefsly open too long, a refreil ing breeze be admitted into the fuffocating atmofphere of the lying-in *'" bed" chamber, the child and mother' fometimes catch irrecovctaLIe colds: but, what is worfe than this, at the end of the month, if things '* go on apparently well, this hot-bed plant is fent out into the country '.' to be reared in a leaky houfe, that lets in wind and rain at every quar- •Vter. Is it any wonder the child never thrives afterwards ? The truth is, a new-born infant cannot well be too cool and loofe in its drefs ; 'it wants lefe cloathing than a grown perfon in proportion, becaufe it is naturally warmer,-as appears by the thermometer, and would there- fore bear the cold of a winier's night much better than any aduir per- fon .whatever, i There are many inftances, both aecient and modern, of infants expefed and deferted, that have lived feveral day^jw it ,, was the practice in ancient times, In many parts of the world, to ex- pofe all thofe whom the parents did not care to be encumbt»-o*w:th ; thar were deformed, or born under evil ftars j not to mention the m uiy foundlings picked up in London ftreets. Thefe inftances may ferve to * fbe«-, that nature has made children able to bear even great hard- lbips, before they are made weak and fickly by their miftaken nurfes. f' Bui, befides the mifchief arifing from the wc^To: and he.t of thefe fwad<iling-clothes,they are put on fo right, and the child is fo cramp* ed by them, that its bowels have not room, nor the limbs sny liberty, ( 4i6 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. io act and exert themfelvei' in the free and eafy manner they ought.** This is a very hurtful circumftance : for limbs that are not ufed will never be ftrong, and fuch tender bodies cannot bear much preffure 4 the circulation reftrained by the compreflion of any one part, muft pro- duce unnatural fwelliflgs in feme other, efpecially as the fibres of in-. Fants are fo ea.ily diftended. ' To which, doubtlefs are owing the many diftortions and deformities toe meet with every where ; chiefly amoni women, who fuller more in this particular than the men. „ " If nurfes were capable of making juft obfervations they might fee and take notice of that particular happinefs, which a child fhews by all its powers of exprefhon, when it is newly undretfed. How pleafed, how delighted it is with this new liberty, when indulged for a few minutes with the free ufe of its legs and arms ! But this is not to laft long ; it is fwaddled up as before, notwithftandihg its cries and compfaints. " I would recommend the following drefs :a Httle flannel waiftj coat, without fleeves, made to fit the body, and tie loofely behind ; to which there fhould be a petticoat fewed, and pver this a kind of gown of the fame material, or any other that is light, thin, and flimfy. The petticoat fhould not be quite fo long as the child* the gown a few inches longer, with one cap only on the head, which may be made double, if it be thought not warm enough. What I mean is, that the whole coiffure fhould be fo contrived, that it might be put on at once,- and neither bind noT prefs the head at all; the linen as ufual. This I think would be abundantly fufficient for the day ; laying afide all thofe fwathes, bandages, flays, and contrivances, that are moft ridicu<-» .loufly ufed to clofe and keep the head in its place, and fupport the body. As if nature, exact nature, had produced her chief work, a human creature, fo carelefsly unfinifhed as to want thofe idle aids to make it perfect. Shoes and ftockings are very needlefs incumbrances* befides that they keep the legs wet and nafty if they are not changed every hour, and often cramp and hurt the feet ; a child would ftand firmer, and learn to walk much fooner, without them. I think they cannot be neceffary till if**funs out in the dirt. There fhould be a thin flannel fhirt for the night, which ought to be every way quite loofe. Children in this fimple, pleafant drefs, which may be readilyV ,put on and off without teaming them, would find themfelves perfect-* ly eafy and happy, enjoying the free ufe of their limbs and faculties, which they would very foon begin to employ when thus left at liber ty^ I would have them put into it as foon as they are bom, and continu- ed in it till they are three years old ; when it may be changed for any - ath^rjnore genteel and fafhionable ; though I could wifh it was not the cuftom to wear ftays at all,; not becaufe I fee no beauty in the v> fugar-foaf fhape, but that I am apprehenfive it is often procured at thd#! expenfeofthe health and ftrength of tfie body. There is an odd notion enough entertained about change, and the keeping of children clean. Some imagine that clean linen and frefh clothe? draw, and * rob them of their nourifhing juices -, 1 cannot fee that they do any thing more than imbibe a little of that moifture which their bodies exhale. Were it, as is fuppofed, it would be ©f fervice *e them . A&®VTC& TO MOTHERS. 6*17 fince they are always too abundantly fupplied, and therefore I think they cannot be changed too often, and would have them clean every day ; as it would free them from flinks and fournefs, which are not only offenfive, but very prejudicial to the tender ftate of infancy. ♦ " The feeding of children properly, is of much greater import- ance to them than their clothing. We ought to take great care to be ^ right in this material article, and that nothing be given them but what is wholefome and good for them, and in fuch quantity as the body calls for towards its fupport and growth ; not a grain more.— Let us confider what nature diredts in the cafe : if we follow nature; inftead of loading or driving it, we cannot err. In the bufinefs ot nurfing, as well as phyfic, art is ever deftructive, if it does not exact- ly copy this original. When a child is firft born, there feems to tee noprovifion at all made for it; for the mother's milk, as it is now managed, feldom comes till the third day : fo that, according to this appearance of nature, a child would be left a day and a half,- or two _days, without an^y food. Were this really the cafe, it would be a fuf- ficient proof that it%anted none ; as indeed it does not immediately \ for it is born full of blood, full of excrement, its appetites not awake, nor its fenfes opened ; and requires fome intermediate time of ab- ftinence and reft to compofe and recover the ftruggle of the birth, and the change of circulation (the bipod running into new channels) which always put if into a little fever. However extraordinary this might appear, I am fure it would be better that the child was not fed even aft that time, than as it generally is fed, for it would fleep the greateft . part of the time, and, when the milk was ready for it, would be very : hungry, and fuck with more eagernefs; which is often neceffary, for it feldom comes freely at firft. But let me endeavour to reconcile this difficulty, that a child fhould be born thus apparently unprdvid. ed for ; I fay apparently, for in reality it is not fo. Nature never intended that a child fhould be kept fo long falling, nor thot we ihould feed it for her. Her defign is broke in upon, and a difficulty ■t raifed that is wholly owing to miftaken management. The child, as foon as it is born, is .taken from ihe mother, and not fuffered to fuck till the milk comes of itfelf ; but is either fed with*'Grange and im- proper things, or put to fuck fome other woman, whofe mi'It flowing ; in a full ftream, overpowers the new-born infant, that has not yet learned to fwv.llow, and lets ir a coughing, or gives it a hickup ; the '■ mother is left to ftruggle with the load of her milk, unafllfted by the fucking of the child. "Thus two great evils are produced, the cue a •, prejudice to the child's health, the other the danger of the J-pBte^s life ; at leaft the retarding her recovery, by caufing what is called a fr milk fever ; which has been thouglit to be natural, but. fo far from if, that it is en.tirely owing to this mifconduct. I am confident from experience, that there would be no fever at all, were things^ managed ». rightily were the child kept without food of any kind till it was hungry ; whichit is impoffible it fhould be juft after the birth,and then applied to the mother's breaft : it would fuck with, ftrength enough, after a few repeated trials, to m?ke the milk flow gradually, >n due ' proportion tothe child's unexercifed faculty of-fwaHowing, and enc call (40) *!8 XDVICE TO MOTHERS. ot its ftomach. Thus the child would not ohly provide for itfelf the beft of nourifhment, but, by opening a free paffage for it, would take off the mother's load, as it increafed, before it could opprefs or hurt -y her: and therefore effectually prevent the fever, which is caufed only - by the painful difteufion of the lacteal veffels of the breafts, whenthe milk is injudicioufly fuffered to accumulate. Here let me defcribe a- cafe of pure latere, in order to illuftrate this material point yet far- ther. When a healthy young woman lies in of her firft child, before th:- operations of nature'have been perverted by any abfurd practices, her labour v. ould be ftrong, and, as i have chofen to inftance in the' e ife of a firft child, perhaps difficult; but in a few minutes after her deliver v, (lie and her child, if it be not injured, wouid fall imo a fweet fleep of fix or feven hours j the mother, if no poifonous opiate has been unnecellkriiy given her, would awake refrefhed, the cluld hungr)'. A little thin broth with bread or fom« fuch light food, fhould be then given her ; and foon after the child be put to fuck. In one hour or two the milk would infallibly flow ; and, if nothing elfe be given it, the child would grow ftrong, and fhe recover perfectly in a few days. This is the conftant courfe of nature, which is very little attended to, and never followed. The general practice is, as foon as the child is ' born, to cram a dab of butter and fugar down its throat, a little oil/ panada, caudle, or 'ome fuch unwholefome mefs. So that they fet out Wrong, and the child ftands a fair chance of being made Tick from the firft hoirr It ie the cuftom of fome to giye a little roaft pig to an in- fant, which, it feemt, is to cure it of all the mother s longings, *M ucb nonfeufe has been propagated, and believed, about women's longings, without any foundation in truth and nature. I wifh thefe matters were a little more inquired into for the honour of the fex, to which many imperfections of this kind are imputed, which I am fure it d«esftotlic under. " Hence I may be afked, what is to be done with a child born* fick, that inftead of fleeping, cries inceffantly from the birth, and i& hardly to be quieted by any means ? Let good care be taken that it is not hurt by the dreffing, or rather let it not be dreffed at all, but wrap- ped up in a loofe flannel. If, notwithftanding this precaution, it ftill continues crying ; inftead of feeding it, for it is certainly a prepofte- rous thing to think of feeding a child becaufe it is fick, though poffi- bly this may ftop its mouth for a little while, let it be applfed to the mother's breaft ; perhaps it may bring the milk immediately, which would be the beft medicine for it in fuch a cafe j or the nipple in it» mpmh may quiet it, though it does not bring it. And it is certainly, better it fhould be quieted without food than with it, which muft ne- cefferily make it worfe. Sometimes indeed the child.may be fo very. ill, that it will not even attempt to fuck. In fuch a cafe, which I think can happen but rarely, let the phyfic I (hall recobimend a little farther on, where children are unavoidably • to be dry-nurfed, be1 given, a little every hour, till it takes effect, (till attempting to brinfff at to fuck the mother's milk, which is the beft phyfic or food it can? take. "When a child fucks its own mother, which, with a very few e*r 1 ceptioasi would be beft for every child and etery mother, nature ADVICE TO MOTHERS. *IO has provided it with fuch wholefome and fuitable'nomifliment, fuppo- fing her a temperate woman that makes fome ufe of her limbs, it can hardly do amifs. The mother would Iikewife, in mofthyfterical ner- vous cafes, eftablifh her own health by it, though fhe were weak and fickly before, as well as that of her offspring. For thefe reafons I could wifh, that every woman that is able, whole fountains are not greatly difturbed or tainted, would give fuck to her child. I am very fure that forcing back the milk, which moft young women 1. uft have in great abundance, may be of fatal confequence ; fometimes it endangers life, and often lays tne foundation ef many incurable difeafes. The reafors that are given for this practice are Very frivoIous,and drawn from falfe premifes, that fome women are too weak to bear fuch a drain, which Would rob them of their own nourifhment. Tliis is a ve y miftaken notion ; for the firft general caufe of moft people's difeafes is, not wart of nourifhment, as is here imagined, but too great fullnefs and redun- dancy of humours ; good at firft, but being more than the body can employ or confume, they ftagn^te, degenerate, and the whole mafs be- comes corrupt, and produces many difeafes. This is confirmed by she ; general practice of phyficians, who make holes in the fkin, perpetual * blifters, iffues, &c, "to let out the fupetfluity. I would therefore leave it to be confidered, whether the throwing back fuch a load of humour as a, woman's firft milk, be moft likely to mend her conftitution,cr make her complaints irremediable. The mother's firft milk is purgative, and cleanfes the child of its long-hoarded excrement; no child,,therefore, can be deprived of it without manifeft injury. By degrees it changes its property, oecomes lefs purgative, and more nourifhing ; and is the beft and only food the child likes, or ought to have for feme time. If I could prevail, no child fhould ever be crammed with any unnatural mixture, till the provifion of nature was ready for it; nor afterwards fed with any ungenial alien diet whatever, at leaft for the firfi three l monthsi for it is not well able to digeft and affimilate other aliment* fooner. I have feen very healthy fine children, that never ate or drank any thing whatever but the mother's milk for the firft ten or twelve i* months. Nature feems to direct this, by giving them no teeth till ' about that time.. There is ufuatly milk enough with the firft child; fometimes more than it can tske -, it is poured forth from an exuberant* overflowing urn, by a bountiful hand that never provides fparingly -. The call of nature fhould be waited for to feed it with any thing more fubftantial, and the appetite ever precede the fodd; not only with re- gard tothe daily meals, but thofe changes of diet, whLh opening, in«* creafing life requires. But this is never done in either cafe, which is one pf the greateft miftakes of all nurfes. Thus far nature, if fhe be i not interrupted, will do the whole bufinefs perfectly well ; and there feems tt/be nothing left for a nurfe to do, but to keep the child clean and fweet, and to tumble and tofs it about a good deal, play wiiair* and keep it in good humour. " When the child requires more folid fuftennnce, we are to inquire What, and how much is moft proper to give it. We may be well affu- red there is a great miftake either in the quantity or quality of children s food, or both, as it is ufually given them ; becaufe they are made azk- by it ; for to this miftake I cannot help imputing nine m ten of all their difeafes. As to quantity, there is a moft ridiculous error in the cqmmon pradice ; for it is generally fuppofed, that, whenever a child cries, it wants victuals j and it is accordingly fed ten, twelve, or more 6lt ADVlCl TO MOTH^io. times in a day and night. This is fo obvious a mifapprehenfion, that I am furprifed it fhould ever pievail. If a'child's wants and motions be- diligently and judicioufly attended to, it will be found that it never cries but from pain \ ncwthe'firft fenfations of hunger are not attended with pain ; accordingly a child (I mean this of a very young one) that is Lunerv, will make a hundred other figns of its want, before.it will cry for feod If it be healthy and quite eafy in its drefs, it will hardly evr crv at all. Ir/.eed thefe figns and motions I fpeak of are but rarely to be obferved ; becaufe it feldom happens that children are ever fuffer- ed to be hungry. In a few, very few, whom I have had the plea-' fure to fee reafon*bly nurfed, that were not fed above two or three rimes in four and twenty hours, and yet were perfectly healthy, aftive, and happy, I have feen thefe fignals, which were as intelligible as if they had fpoken . , , . " There are many faults in the quality of their food; it is not Gtnrfe enough. Their paps, panadas, gruels, &c. are generally enrich*. ed with fugar, fpke and fometimes a drop of wine, neither of which thev ought ever to tafte. Our bodies never want fnem ; they are what luxury only has introduced, to the deftruction of the health of man- kind It is not. enough that their food be fimple, it fhould be alfo light. Several people I find" are miftaken in their notions of what is light ; and fancy that moft kinds of paftry, puddings, cuftards Sec. are light, tha is. light of digeftion. £ut there is nothing heavier in this fenfe 'than unfermented flour and eggs boiled hard which are the^ chief in- *gtedients of thofe preparations.
