It is not common for people to complain of ails they think hereditary, till they are grown up -, that is, till they have contributed to them by their own irregulari- ties and exceffes, and then are glad to throw their own faults back upon their parents, and lament a bad conftitution when they have fpoiled a very good one. Indeed, when we find them affedled with fcrophulous or venereal complaints, we may reafonbly conclude the taint to have been tranfmitted to them ; but tnefe cafes are very rare, in comparifon of the many others that are falfely, and without the leaft foundation, imputed to parents ; when the Teal caufe is either in the complainants themfelves, or bad nurfing that has fixed them early in bad habits. In one fenfe many difeafes may be faid to be hereditary, perhaps all thofe of malformation by which I mean not only deformity and diftortiont but all thofe cafes where the fibres and vefldls of one part are weaker in proportion than the reft ; fo that upon any ftrain of the body, whether of debauch or too violent exercife the weak p-trt-f ails firft and diforders the whole. Tills complaints may be produced fimilar to thofe of the ■■parent, owing in fome meafure to the.fimilitude of parts, which poffibly is inherited like the features of the face j but yet thefe difeafes might never have appeared, but for the immediate acting cauf-, the violence done the body Moft diftempers haye two caufes : theone, a particular ftate of the? folids and fluids of the body, which difpofe it to receive Certain infections and irnpulfes ; the other, the infetlion or impulfe it- felf. Now what I contend for is, that though this predifponent ftate or habit of body be heritable, htx. the difeafes incident to thefe wretch- * ed heirs may be ayoided by preventing the aclive caufe •,'which may be done in m.iny cafes by a due attention to the non-naturals as they lire called ; in plainer w*rds, by a temparate, active life ; in children, by good nurfing. Therefore I conclude, that, inftead of indulging and enfeebling yet iiore by^the common methods, children fo unhappily born, what I am recommending, together with the wholefome milk of a healthy nurfe, is the beft, the only means to remedy the evil, and by which alone they may by degrees be made healthy and ftrong. And .thus,.ina_g;neratin or two of reafonable temperate perfons, every taint and infirmity whatever, the king's evil and madnefs not excepted, would be totei/ worn out. " Th" plain natural plan I have laid down is never followed, be- eaufe moft mothers, of any condition, either cannot, or will not un-' demke the troublefome taflc of fuckling their own children ; which is troublefome only for want of proper method ; were it rightly managed, there would be too much pleafure in it, to every woman that can prc- yail upon herfelf to give up a little of the beauty of her' breaft to feed her.offspring ; though this is a miftaken notion, for the breafts are not fpoiled by giving fuck, but by growing fat. In my opinion, a man of fenfe cannot have a prettier rattle (for rattles he muft have of one kind or other) than fuch a young child. I am -quite at a lofs to account for the general practice of fending infants out of doors, to be fuckled or , dry-nurfed oy another woman, who has not fo much underftanding, nor can have fo much affeaion for it, as the parents; and how it comes to pafs, that people of good fenfe and eafv circumftances will not give themfelves the pains to watch over the'health and welfare of their children, but are fo carelefs as to give them up to the common methods, without conlidering how near it is to an equal chance that they are deftroyed by them. To fuch only I would addrefs my- felf, and earneftly recommend it to every father to have his child nur- fed under his own eye ; to make ufe of his own reafon and fenfe, ia fuperintending and directing the management of it; nor fuffer it to be made one of the mylteries of the Bona Dea, from which the men are to be excluded, i would advife every mother that can, for her own fake as well as her child's, to fuckle it; if (he be a healthy woman, it will confirm her health ; if weakly, in moft cafes it will reftore her. It need be no confinement to her, or abridgment of her time ; four times in four-antf-twenty hours will be often enough to give it fuck j letting it have as much as it will fuck out of both breafts at each time. It may be fed and dreffed by fome handy reafonable fervant, that will fubmit to be directed ; whom, Iikewife it may fleep with. No other woman s milk can be fo good for her child ; and dry-nurfing I look upon to be the moft unnatural arei dangerous method of all ; and, ac- cording to my obfervation, not one in three furvives it. To breed a child in this artificial manner, requires more kuowledge of nature and the animal oeconomy, th.au the b^ft nurfe was ever rniftrefs of, as well as more care aud attention than is generally bellowed on eoil- fdred ; the (kill of a good phyfician would be neceffary to manage it rightly." ******** 'fhe Docloris here led to ftate his opinion as to the precautions. neceffary to be taken in the choice of hired nurfes, and his reafons why the children entrufted to their care (hould be treated fomewhat differently from thofe who are nurfed in a more natural way, and fuck their own mothers. He does not deem it enough that hired nurfes fhould be clean and healthy ; he looks upon their age as a material con- fideration. " Thofe,"' he fays, t« between twenty and thirty are cer- tainly of the beft age ; becaufe they will have more mil* thm the very young, and more and better than the old. Bnt what," he thinks, " of the utmoft confequence is, that great regard fhould be had to the time of their lying-in, and thofe procured, if poffible, who have not been brought to bed above two or three months." He juftly ob- ferves, th;rt "nature intending a child thould fuck about a twelve- mouth, the milk feldom continues good much longer ;" and he adds, 624 ADVICE TO MOTHERS. with a ftill greater degree of evidence, " that if a new-bom infant be deprived of its own mother's milk, ^t ought undoubtedly to have what is moft like it: the newer it is, the moie fuitable in all refpe&s tvts tender nature." ******* After cenfuring a very common practice with poor women, who, if they can get nurfe-children, will fuckle two or three of them fuc- ceffrvely with the fame milk, he proceeds thus : l< A nurfe ought to have great regard to her diet: it is not enougR that ihe be fober and temperate; her food fhould confift of a proper mixture of flefh and vegetables : fhe fhould eat one hearty meal of unfalted flelh-meat every day, with a good deal of garden-fluff, ard a little bread. Thin broth or milk wouid be beft for her breakfaft and fupper. Kt r drink fhould be fmall-becr, or miik and water ; but on no account ftiould flie ever touch a drop of wine or ftrong drink, much lefs any kind of fpirituous liquors; giving ale or brandy to a nurfe is, in effect, giving it to the child ; and it is eafy to conclude what would be the confequence.'* *** 1 his equally candid and judicious writer does not enter upon his promifed defcription of the treatment proper for children put out to nurfe, without again reminding his readers, that the plan, which he would lay down, could he prevail, would be that of nature, excluding art and foreign aid entirely. "But," he adds, " when this is broke in upon, a little adventitious fkill becomes indifpenfibly neceffary ; that, if we are not perfectly right in following clofely the defign of nature, we may co-operate a Jittle, and not be totally wrong in coun- teracting it, as is too often the cafe. "What I mean is, that every child, not allowed the mother's firjt milk, whether it be dry-nurfed or fuck- led by another woman,(hould be purged in a day or two after the birth, and tins, purging continued for fome time ; not by regular dofes of phyfic that may operate all at once, but fome lenient laxative fhould be contrived, and given two or three times a-day, fo as to keep the child's body open for the firft nine days, or fortnight ; ieflehing the quantity infenfibly, till it be kftoff. It fhould be fo managed, that the operation of the artificial phyfic may refemble that of the natural. This is fo material, that, for want of it, moft children in the firft month break out in pimp^s all over •, the nurfes call it red-gum, and look upon it to be a natural thlnsr, and that the children w"'Jl be un- healthy who have it not. So indeed they will be in all likelihood ; and it is better that thefe foulneffes, which become acrid and hot by remain- ing too long in the body, fhould be difcharged through the fkin. than not at all ; or that they fhould be lodged in the bldod, or fall upon the vitals^ to lay the foundation of numberlefs future evils; but it is chief- ly owing to the neglect of this method at firft. ' A child that fucks its own mother, Unlefs it be greatly over-fed, or kept too hot, will never be troubled with this humour at all." ***** . .. ■ The following is the form of the gentle purgative which the Doctor recommends to fuch infants as have been deprived of the fal- utary operation of their mother's milk: . "Take manna, pulp of caffia, of each half an ounce: diffolve them in about three ounces* of thin broth. Let-the child take two fpfconsiu! three times a-day, varying the quantity according to the ADVICE TO MOTHERS. 625 effect j which, at firft, ought to be three or four ftools in four-and- twenty hours." Among other rules for the conduct of hired nurfes, this experi- enced phyfician particularly enjoins filch women " to keep the chil- dren awake by day, as long as they are difpofed to be fo, and to amufe and keep thfem in good humour all they can -, not to lull and rock them to fleep, or to continue their fleep too long ; which is only done to fave their own time and trouble, to the great detriment of the chil- dren's health, fpirits and underftanding." .***** Here he re- fers to his former obfervations on the changes to be gradually made in the diet of children, when they come to require more folid fufte- nance than breaft-milk j and he takes occafion to introduce the fol- lowing remarks : " A child may be allowed any kind of mellow fruit, either raw, ftewed, or baked, roots of all forts, and all the produee of the kitchen- garden. I am fure all thefe things are wholefome and good for them, and every one elfe, notwithftanding the idle notion of their being windy, which they are only to very debauched ftomachs ; and fo is milk : but no man's blood wants the cleanfing, refrefhing power of milk, more than his, whofe ftomach, ufed to inflammatory things of high relifh, will not bear the firft chill of it. To children, all this kind of food, taken in moderation, is perfectly grateful and falutary. Some may think that they carry into the ftomach the eggs of future worms ; but of this I am not very apprehenfive ; for I believe there are few things we eat or drink that do not convey them. But then they can never be hatched irr a healthy infide, where all the juices are fweet and good, and every gland performs its office ; tiie gall, in pai- ticular, would deftroy them j bullock's gall has been found to be a good and fafe vermifuge. It is my opinion, we fwallow the e^gs cf many littie animals* that are never brought to life within us, except where they find a fit,neft or lodgment in the acid phlegm or vitiated humours of the ftomach and bowels. Were thefe totally Wcharged every day, and the food of yefterday employed in nourifhment, and the fuperfluity thrown off to the laft grain, no worms could ever breed or harbour in our vitals. As foon as the children have any teeth, at fix or eight months, they may by degrees be ufed to a little flefh-meat : . which they are always very fond of, much more fq at firft, than of any confectionary or paftry wares, with Which they fhould never debauch their tafte." I have elfewhere enlarged on the fatal effeas of thefe palatab.e poifons ; and I am not without hopes that tender and rational mothers ' **ill pay fome little attention to my warnings. A reform in this article alone—the total dif ufe of paftry in the diet of young children—will go a great way towards preventing many of the worft complaints to , which they are fubject. • , , From the above remaiks on the proper food of infants, the Doc- tor makes*a very natural tranfition to*.the confideration of their dii eafes He begins with expofing the abfurdity of popular errors — popular prejudices with refped to teething. « Breeding teeth' fays " has been thought to be, and js fatal to many children ; but I and he' (fatf ADVICE TO MOiHBRS. am confident this is not from nature, for it is no difeafe, or we could not be well in health till one or two and twenty, or later. Teeth arc breeding the greateft part of that time ; and it is my opinion, the laft teeth give more pain than the fiift, as the bones and gums.they are to pierce are grown more firm and hard. But, whatever fever, fits, or other dangerous fymptoms feem to attend this operation of nature, healthy children have fometimes bred their teeth without any fuch bad attendants -, which oughtto incline us to fufpedl the evil not to be natural, but rather the effect of too great a fulnefs, or the corrupt hu- mours of the body put in agitation by the ftimulating pain the tooth cauies in breaking its way out. This I believe, never happens with- out fome pain, and poflibiy a little fever ; but if the blood and juicc6 be perfectly fweet and good, and there be not too great a redundancy of them, both will be but flight, and pafs off imperceptibly, without any bad confequence whatever. The chief intention of the method I am recommending is, to preferve the humours of the body in this ftate, and therefore, if it fucceeds, children fo managed will breed their teeth with lefs pain and danger than are commonly obferved to attend this work of nature.'* ia fupport of this opinion, I can ftate from my own experience, that I have never known cutting the teeth, as it is called, attended with any pain of an alarming nature, excfpt in cafes of previous difeafe, mifmangement or bad riurfing. Fevers, convulfion-fits, and other dan- gerous fymptoms, <are always, upon fuch occafions, th- confequences of an extreme fulnefs of the habit, a vitiated ftate of the blood and juices, fome conftitutional weaknefs, or a great irritabdity of the ne.- veus fyftem. The ufe alfo of corala, and the Hke hard fubftances, by rendering the gums callous, muft oppofe additional refiftance to the burfting tooth, and greatly nucreafe the acutenefs of the pain. But ttu Doctor's text requires no comment. I fhall therefore refume my quotation from his valuable pamphlet. " As I have faid," continues he, *' that the £*ft and general caufe of moft of the difeafea infants are liable to, is the acid corruption of their food, it may not be amifs juit to mention an eafy and cen.am r m- edy, or rather preventative, i/given timely, at the firft appearance of predominating acid ; which h vr.ry obvious, from the crude white or g'-cert itools, gripes and purging* occafioned by it. The common mtthod when thefe fymptoms appear, is to give the peatl-julep, ciabs- (', s, and :hc teftaceous powders, which, though they do abforb tee acidities, have this inconvenience in their effect, that they'are apt to loo^e in the body, and bring on a coftivenefs very detrimental to in- f-.i.ts, and therefore require a little manna, or fome gentle purge, to be go.■■: frequently to carry them off. Inftead of thefe, I would re- cc-'T.cru1 a certain fine infipid powder, called magnefia alba, which, at th.. <amc time it corrects and fweetens all fournefs rather more effect- ui y than the teftaceous powders, is Iikewife a lenient purgative, and kops the body gently open. This is the only alkaline purge I know of and wh rh cur difpenfatories have long wanted, I have taken it m fe'.f, o id given it to others, for the heart-burn, and find if to be the .nett. iad moft effe.otual 'remedy for that complaint. It may be given tr, he-v: ..n froiv. one to two drams a-day, a little at a time in all their food, .till .th$ acidifies be quite overcome, and the concomitant fymp. ADVU.H 10 MOTHERS. 621 toms difappear entirely. I have often given it with good and great. effea, even when the children have been far gone in difeafes firft brought on by prevailing acid. *« It is always eafier to prevent difeafes than to cure them; and as neither children, nor indeed grown perfons, are ever feized with chronic difeafes fuddenly, the progrefs ot decaying health being per- ceptibly gradual, it is no difficult matter for a phyfician of common ikill to obferve the firft ftep towards illnefs, and to foretell the confe- quence, in all thofe whole habit of life is well known .to him. But to parents and nurfes in general, thefe obfervations may not occur, I will therefore point out a few certain figns and fymptoms, by which they may be affured, that a child's health is decaying, even before it appears to be fick. If thefe are neglected, the evil increafes, grows from bad to worfe, and more violent and apparent complaints will fol- low, and perhaps end in incurable difeafes, which, a timely remedy, or a flight change in the diet and manner of life, had infallibly prevent- ed. The firft tendency to difeafe may be obferved in a child's breath. It is not enough the breath be not offenfive •, it ftioiuld be fweet and fragrant like a nofegay of frefh flowers, or a pail of new miik from a young cow that feeds upon the fweeteft grafs of the fpring; and this as well it firft waking in the morning,as all day long. It is always to with children that are in perfect delicate health. As foon, therefore, as a child's breath is found to be either hot, or ftrong, or four, we may be affured that digeftion and f urfeit have fouled and difturbed the blood, ;.nd now is the time to apply a proper remedy, and prevent a train of impending evils. Let the child be reftrained in its food ; eat lefs ; live upon milk or thin broth for a day or two -, be carried, or walk if it is able, a little more than ufual in the open air. Ltt a little <;f this pow- der, or any other proper phyfic, be given ; not 'that 1 would advife phyfic to be made familiar ; but one dofe adminiftered now, would prevent the neceffity of a great many that might afterwards be prefcri- bwd with much lefs good effect. " If this firft fymptom'of approaching illnefs be overlooked, tie. child, who, if it was healthy, would lie quiet as a log all night, will have difturbed fleep, reftlefs, terrifying dreams ; will be talking, ftart- ing, kicking, and tumbling about; or fmiling and laughing, as is cora- re.on with very young children when they are griped ; 3nd the nurfes fay they fee and converfe with angels. After Tiis will follow lofs of appetite and connection, check of growth, decay of ftrength, cough, confumption, or elfe colics, gripes, worms, fits, &c. difeafes that re- quire all the (kill of a good phyfician ; and happy for them, if the ut- moft he can employ will reftore them to auy degree of tailing health. " There is one thing more which I forgot to mention in its prop- er place, and therefore 1 muft take notice of it here : tie ' is, the de- gree of exercife proper for children. This is of more confequence | th?n all the reft ; for, without it, all our care in feeding and clothing will not fucceed to our wifhes ; but when by due degrees a child is brought to bear a good deal of exercile without fatigue, it is incon- ceivable how much impropriety and abfurdity in both thefe articles jt will endure unhurt. A child, therefore, {houjd be pufhed forwards, ; <aS ADVICE TO MOTHERS. and taught to walk as foon as poflible. An healthy child a year old will be able to walk alone. This v.e may call the sera of their deliver- surce; for this great difficulty furmounted, they generally do well, by Vetting out of the nurfe's hands to fhiftfor themfelves. And here I tiuft endeavour to correct a great miftake, which is, that moft people^ think it wrong to put weakly children upon their legs, efpecially if they are the leaft bsnt or crooked : but whoever will venture the experi- ment will furely find, that crooked legs will grow in time ftrong and ftraight by frequent walking, while difufe will make them worfe and worfe every day. As they grow daily more and moreable, let their walks be gradually increafed, till they can walk two miles on a ftretch without wearinefs; which they will be very well ahle to do before they are three years old, if they arc accuftomed to it every day. To , fcad them fuch a walk, fhould be impofed as an indifpenfible talk upon their rn oris, for to them it will be the hightft pleafure ; fo far from a burthen to'them, that if they perform the daily duty, they will, from :.hc impeife of their own active vigour, be found running, leaping, and playing, all day long. Thus, a dull, heavy childomay be made play- £ie andfprightly, a weakly one healthy and ftrong, and confirmed in good habits and perpetual health. . "There are fome other little niceties that,were they obferved in the linrfmg of children, would be of fome ufe to them; fuch as making tocrn lie ftraight in the bed. I do not mean extended like a corpfe, bur that t*.dr limbs may be free and e^fy. I have fometimes feen children a year or two old He doubled up in bed as in the womb, efpe* cia;?y in cold weather ; and from the conftraint of their pofture, fall into profufe fweats- This will be prevented if they are laid ftraight j and iletp relaxing all the mufcles of the body, the knees will naturally be bent a litt!.. They fhould be taught to ufe both hands alike; for employing dc mere than the other will not only make the hand and arm fo ufcct|but alfo that tide of the body bigger than the other. This io fometir:i>s the caufe of crookednefs. It would Iikewife not be amifs to fe>rward their fpeaking plain, by fpeaking plain difUn<5t words to them, inftead of the namby-pamby ftyle, and giving them back their own broken inarticulate attempts ; by which means, I believe, fome children ftarcely fpeak intelligibly at kven years of age. . I think they cannot be made reasonable creatures too foon." ****** As this etfay was written in the form of a letter, the Doctor con- cludes it with an apology to the gentleman to whom it was addreffed, for the loofe manner in which the thoughts were laid before him. The writer very candidly confeffes that he had " neither time nor patience ' lo think of form and order, or fupporting them by affected demonftra- tions taken from mechanical principles and powers. " All I have en- deavoured,^ fays he, " is to be intelligible and ufeful; and therefore I have avoided, as much as poflible, all terms of art; together with lear- ned quotations, as often produced out of vanity, and to fhew deep te d-nc, ^s for the fake of proof. * * * * * I fhall only add by way of oerfja.rve to thofe who may be inclined to make a trial of the meth- od i r.ncmmeod, that I am a father, and have already practifed it with the moft deftrable .fuccefs." In a poftfcript to the tenth edition of this pamphlet, dated ]vSif 1*% 1769, the author expreffes himfelf in the following manner : ■*" It is now above twenty years fince I wrote the foregoing effay ; and though I have made a few alterations, it was only to explain thofe paffages that contained any apparent difficulty or obfcurity: 1 have never yet found caufe to alter cffentially anyone opinion delivered in it. I have through the whole induftrioufly laboured at the greateft piain- nefsand fimplicity ; and yet my meaning has been much miftaken.-— Some have very ftrangely expected to find in it the geneial cure of chiW, dren's difeafes, though it be profeffedly written only to prevent them- by eftablifhing good health ; a very differeht thing (wjiaiever pcop.e may think) from the cure of difeafes. Sick ot weak children,* wheth- er fuch by nature, or made fuch by bad nurfing, cannot perhaps e brought immediately into^he habits here recommended, but muft f. .1 be cured of their maladies by a fkilful phyfician; who, if he be alfo a * honeft man, will introduce thefe, or fimilar habits of management tfr continue them in health and ftrength. But in treating their difeafes,, as well as in nurfing them, I am very fure many capital errors are cor.v mitted. I object greatly in particular to the frequent ufe of antimoni- al and mercurial medicines ; which, though they give fometimes a, little tempcrary relief, by difcharging crude and phlegmatic humours killing worms, Sec. I am very confident a repeated ufe of them break* the blood, relaxes the fibres, and is every way deftrudtive to the confti- tution of children. Prefentrelief feems to be all that is defired, and therefore all that is intended by medication ; the flow, but perman. r& effects of good habits few have patience to expect. Others have ne- glected effentials, to lay ftrefs upon trifles. A lady of great fwaf among her acquaintance told me long ago, with an air of reproach* that fhe had nurfed her child according to my book, and it died. I afked, if Ihe had fuckled it herfelf r No.—Had it fuckled any othe? woman ?—It was dry-nurfed.—Then, madam, you cannot impute. your misfortune to my advice, for you have taken a method qu;k contrary to it in the moft capital point. O I but, according to mxy directiorr, it had never worn ftockings. . Madam, children may oi,* whether they do or do not wear ftockings." A ftronger iliuftrauas could not be given of the folly of attending only to .trifles, and a&in- diametrically oppofite to the dictates of reafon and expsr;ouce in mat tsrs af the greateft moment.
